topics.title: Determination of viscosity of lubricating oils and fuels and its variation with temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL507 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-1
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of viscosity of lubricating oils and fuels and its variation with temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 тАФ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Viscosity

**Viscosity** is the measure of a fluid's internal resistance to deformation (flow) under an applied shear stress. It quantifies the friction between adjacent fluid layers moving at different velocities.

For a fluid obeying Newton's law of viscosity:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (N/m┬▓)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity (Pa┬╖s)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to flow direction (sтБ╗┬╣)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Key Viscosity Terms (KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate)**
> - **Dynamic Viscosity (╬╝):** Resistance to flow due to internal friction.
> - **Kinematic Viscosity (╬╜):** Dynamic viscosity divided by fluid density, representing the fluid's resistance to flow under the influence of gravity.
> $$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$
> Units: Dynamic тАФ **Pa┬╖s (SI)**, **poise (P)**, **centipoise (cP)** ; Kinematic тАФ **m┬▓/s (SI)**, **stoke (St)**, **centistoke (cSt)**.
> Conversion: 1 poise = 100 cP = 0.1 Pa┬╖s ; 1 stoke = 100 cSt = 1 cm┬▓/s.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a tray of marbles across a rough surface versus across a tray filled with honey.

- The marbles (low viscosity) slide easily because layers slip past each other without resistance.
- The honey (high viscosity) resists because each layer drags the adjacent layer along with it through molecular cohesion.

In engines, lubricating oil behaves like this honey тАФ its viscosity must be just right. **Too thick**, and the engine wastes energy pumping oil. **Too thin**, and metal parts grind against each other. Temperature drastically changes this "thickness," which is why we must measure how viscosity shifts with heat.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world relevance in Thermal Engineering Lab:**
> Lubricating oils used in IC engines, turbines, and compressors are graded by viscosity. Engine oils like **SAE 20W-40** or fuels like diesel have strict viscosity limits at standard temperatures (40┬░C and 100┬░C) defined by **ASTM D445**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 тАФ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Viscometers Used in the Lab

The experiment typically employs **capillary-type viscometers**, which measure the time taken for a fixed volume of fluid to flow through a calibrated orifice under gravity.

| Viscometer | Used For | Orifice Size | Standard Volume |
|------------|----------|--------------|-----------------|
| **Redwood No. I** | Lubricating oils (lowтАУmedium viscosity) | Small (1.62 mm) | 50 mL |
| **Redwood No. II** | Heavy fuel oils, bitumen | Large (3.8 mm) | 50 mL |
| **Saybolt Universal (SUS)** | Lubricating oils | Small | 60 mL |
| **Saybolt Furol (SFS)** | Heavy fuels, residual oils | Large | 60 mL |
| **Engler** | General oils | Standard | 200 mL |

### 2.2 Theoretical Principle тАФ HagenтАУPoiseuille Law

For steady, laminar, incompressible flow through a vertical capillary under gravity, the dynamic viscosity is given by:

$$\mu = \frac{\pi \cdot r^{4} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot t \cdot h}{8 \cdot V \cdot L}$$

where:
- $r$ = capillary radius
- $\rho$ = density of fluid
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity
- $t$ = time of flow
- $h$ = effective head
- $V$ = collected volume
- $L$ = capillary length

This forms the theoretical foundation for all empirical Redwood/Saybolt/Engler equations.

### 2.3 Empirical Redwood Formula (KTU High-Yield)

Since the simple HagenтАУPoiseuille law ignores **kinetic energy corrections** and **entrance effects**, empirical constants are introduced. The kinematic viscosity in centistokes is:

$$\boxed{\nu = A \cdot t - \frac{B}{t}}$$

- For **Redwood No. I**: $A = 0.0026$, $B = 1.72$ (valid for $34 \le t \le 100$ s); for $t > 100$ s, use $\nu = 0.0026 t$.
- For **Saybolt Universal**: $A = 0.0022$, $B = 1.8$ (valid for $t > 100$ s).
- For **Saybolt Furol**: $A = 0.0216$, $B = 2.64$.
- For **Engler**: $\nu = 0.00147 \cdot t - \dfrac{3.74}{t}$.

### 2.4 Variation of Viscosity with Temperature

As temperature rises, molecular spacing increases and cohesive forces weaken тАФ viscosity **decreases exponentially**. The most accurate empirical correlation is the **Walther Equation (ASTM D341)**:

$$\log \log (\nu + 0.7) = A - B \log T$$

where $A$ and $B$ are constants unique to each oil, and $T$ is absolute temperature (Kelvin).

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative Correlations**
> - **Arrhenius-type:** $\mu = A \cdot e^{B/T}$
> - **ASTM ViscosityтАУTemperature Chart:** A graphical chart with viscosity (cSt) on the y-axis (log scale) and temperature on the x-axis. Oils produce straight lines on this chart тАФ the flatter the line, the higher the **Viscosity Index (VI)**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Relation | Units |
|-----------|-------------------|-------|
| Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu = \tau / (du/dy)$ | Pa┬╖s |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | m┬▓/s or cSt |
| HagenтАУPoiseuille | $\mu = \pi r^{4} \rho g h t / (8 V L)$ | Pa┬╖s |
| Redwood No. I | $\nu = 0.0026 t - 1.72 / t$ | cSt |
| Saybolt Universal | $\nu = 0.0022 t - 1.8 / t$ | cSt |
| Saybolt Furol | $\nu = 0.0216 t - 2.64 / t$ | cSt |
| Engler | $\nu = 0.00147 t - 3.74 / t$ | cSt |
| Walther Equation | $\log\log(\nu + 0.7) = A - B \log T$ | тАФ |
| Viscosity Index | $VI = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$ | тАФ |
| Unit Conversions | 1 cP = 0.001 Pa┬╖s ; 1 cSt = 10^{-6} m┬▓/s | тАФ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Engine lubrication:** Ensures hydrodynamic film formation between piston rings and cylinder walls.
- **Fuel atomization:** Diesel viscosity affects injector spray droplet size.
- **Hydraulic systems:** Predicts pressure drop and energy losses.
- **Pipeline transport:** Crude oil pumping depends on viscosityтАУtemperature data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 тАФ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Kinematic Viscosity from Capillary Flow (HagenтАУPoiseuille Foundation)

**Step 1 тАФ Setup:** Consider a vertical capillary tube of length $L$ and radius $r$. A volume $V$ of oil flows through under a constant hydrostatic head $h$.

**Step 2 тАФ Driving pressure:** The pressure difference across the capillary due to gravity is:

$$\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 3 тАФ Apply HagenтАУPoiseuille law** for laminar flow through a circular pipe:

$$Q = \frac{\pi r^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate.

**Step 4 тАФ Express flow rate as volume per time:**

$$Q = \frac{V}{t}$$

**Step 5 тАФ Substitute** $Q = V/t$ and $\Delta P = \rho g h$:

$$\frac{V}{t} = \frac{\pi r^{4} \rho g h}{8 \mu L}$$

**Step 6 тАФ Rearrange** to solve for dynamic viscosity $\mu$:

$$\mu = \frac{\pi r^{4} \rho g h t}{8 V L}$$

**Step 7 тАФ Convert to kinematic viscosity** using $\nu = \mu / \rho$:

$$\boxed{\nu = \frac{\pi r^{4} g h t}{8 V L}}$$

This is the **theoretical** expression. It neglects:
- Kinetic energy of the emerging jet (most significant for low-viscosity fluids),
- Entrance/exit losses,
- Temperature variations along the jet.

These losses are corrected empirically тЖТ giving the Redwood / Saybolt forms.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Empirical Redwood Formula

**Step 1 тАФ Start from** the theoretical kinematic viscosity expression (3.1, Step 7):

$$\nu_{\text{theor}} = \frac{\pi r^{4} g h t}{8 V L}$$

**Step 2 тАФ Realize correction needed:** Experiments show $\nu$ is **not strictly proportional to $t$** at low flow times because of kinetic energy loss.

**Step 3 тАФ Add empirical correction term** $C / t$ that captures the energy correction:

$$\nu = A t - \frac{B}{t}$$

where $A$ and $B$ are apparatus-specific constants determined by calibration using standard reference oils of known viscosity (e.g., from National Physical Laboratory standards).

**Step 4 тАФ Calibrate with reference oil** of known $\nu_{\text{ref}}$ and measured time $t_{\text{ref}}$:

$$\nu_{\text{ref}} = A t_{\text{ref}} - \frac{B}{t_{\text{ref}}}$$

**Step 5 тАФ Use two calibration points** to solve the simultaneous equations for $A$ and $B$. For Redwood No. I, this gives the accepted textbook values $A = 0.0026$ and $B = 1.72$.

$$\boxed{\nu_{\text{Redwood I}} = 0.0026 \, t - \frac{1.72}{t} \quad \text{[cSt]}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Viscosity Variation тАФ Walther Equation

**Step 1 тАФ Start from Arrhenius-type behaviour:**

$$\mu = A \cdot e^{B / T}$$

**Step 2 тАФ Take logarithm twice** (to linearize the steep curve on a logтАУlog scale):

$$\ln \mu = \ln A + \frac{B}{T}$$

$$\ln(\ln \mu - \ln A) = \ln B - \ln T$$

**Step 3 тАФ Use base-10 logs and introduce the empirical shift constant 0.7** (to avoid negative arguments for low-viscosity fluids):

$$\log \log (\nu + 0.7) = A' - B' \log T$$

**Step 4 тАФ Identify constants** $A'$ and $B'$ from two known viscosity-temperature data points:

$$A' = \frac{\log\log(\nu_1 + 0.7) \cdot \log T_2 - \log\log(\nu_2 + 0.7) \cdot \log T_1}{\log T_2 - \log T_1}$$

$$B' = \frac{\log\log(\nu_1 + 0.7) - \log\log(\nu_2 + 0.7)}{\log T_2 - \log T_1}$$

**Step 5 тАФ Predict viscosity** at any third temperature by interpolating/extrapolating along the straight line on ASTM charts.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix тАФ When to Use Which Formula

| Condition | Recommended Formula | Reason |
|-----------|---------------------|--------|
| $t < 34$ s (Redwood I) | Use capillary formula directly | Empirical constants invalid |
| $34 \le t \le 100$ s | $\nu = 0.0026 t - 1.72 / t$ | Standard range |
| $t > 100$ s (Redwood I) | $\nu = 0.0026 t$ | Kinetic correction negligible |
| Heavy fuel oil | Saybolt Furol or Redwood No. II | Larger orifice avoids turbulence |
| Predicting at new T | Walther equation | Best accuracy for T-extrapolation |
| Rapid check | ASTM chart (graphical) | Visual confirmation |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

**Problem:** A lubricating oil is tested in a Redwood No. I viscometer. The time for 50 mL of oil to flow is:
- $t_{40} = 720$ s at 40 ┬░C
- $t_{60} = 320$ s at 60 ┬░C
- $t_{80} = 160$ s at 80 ┬░C

Density at 60 ┬░C = 875 kg/m┬│. Calculate:
(a) Kinematic and dynamic viscosity at 60 ┬░C.
(b) Comment on viscosity variation with temperature.

**Solution:**

**(a) At 60 ┬░C:**

$$\nu = 0.0026 \times 320 - \frac{1.72}{320}$$

$$\nu = 0.8320 - 0.005375$$

$$\nu = 0.8266 \text{ cSt}$$

Convert kinematic viscosity to m┬▓/s:

$$\nu = 0.8266 \times 10^{-6} = 8.266 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

Convert density to kg/m┬│ (already given):

$$\rho = 875 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

Dynamic viscosity:

$$\mu = \nu \cdot \rho = 8.266 \times 10^{-7} \times 875$$

$$\mu = 7.232 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa┬╖s} = 0.7232 \text{ cP}$$

**(b) Viscosity variation:**

At 40 ┬░C: $\nu = 0.0026(720) - 1.72/720 = 1.872 - 0.00239 = 1.870$ cSt
At 60 ┬░C: $\nu = 0.827$ cSt
At 80 ┬░C: $\nu = 0.0026(160) - 1.72/160 = 0.416 - 0.01075 = 0.405$ cSt

> **Observation:** As temperature rises from 40 ┬░C тЖТ 80 ┬░C, kinematic viscosity drops sharply from **1.87 cSt to 0.41 cSt** тАФ about a 78 % reduction. This confirms that **viscosity decreases rapidly (and non-linearly) with temperature**, justifying the use of the **Walther equation** for accurate prediction rather than linear interpolation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 тАФ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Redwood No. I Viscometer

```
flowchart TD
    A[Oil Filling Cup - 50 mL Capacity] --> B[Standard Jet Orifice - 1.62 mm]
    B --> C[Kohlrausch Receiving Flask - 50 mL Mark]
    D[Heating Water Bath] -.-> A
    E[Stirrer Assembly] -.-> A
    F[Thermometer Pocket] -.-> A
    G[Stopwatch] --> H[Time Measurement]
    C --> H
    H --> I[Empirical Formula Calculation]
    I --> J[Kinematic Viscosity in cSt]
    J --> K[Dynamic Viscosity via Density]
    J --> L[Plot Viscosity vs Temperature]
```

### 4.2 Experimental Procedure Flowchart

```
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Clean viscometer cup and jet] --> S2[Step 2: Insert cork and place Kohlrausch flask]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Pour oil up to marking level]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Cover with lid and insert thermometer]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Heat water bath to desired temperature]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Maintain temperature steady for 5 min]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Lift cork and start stopwatch simultaneously]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Stop watch when 50 mL oil collected]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Record time t seconds]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Repeat at next temperature]
    S10 --> S11{All temperatures done?}
    S11 -- No --> S5
    S11 -- Yes --> S12[Apply Redwood formula for each t]
    S12 --> S13[Calculate dynamic viscosity]
    S13 --> S14[Plot viscosity-temperature graph]
```

### 4.3 ViscosityтАУTemperature Behaviour (Block Diagram of Trend)

```
flowchart LR
    P1[Low Temperature - 40 C] -- High Cohesive Forces --> P2[High Viscosity]
    P2 -- Molecular Spacing Increases --> P3[Moderate Temperature - 60 C]
    P3 --> P4[Moderate Viscosity]
    P4 -- Weak Cohesive Forces --> P5[High Temperature - 100 C]
    P5 --> P6[Low Viscosity - Exponential Drop]
    P6 -.Walther Equation.-> P7[ASTM V-T Chart Straight Line]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Interpretation:**
> The Mermaid flow above captures the energy-corrected relationship between viscosity and temperature. For high-grade lubricants (high Viscosity Index), the line is nearly horizontal; for low-grade lubricants, the line drops steeply тАФ a key indicator for oil selection in thermal systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 тАФ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam тАФ June 2024]**
Define dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their SI and CGS units with conversion factors.

**Model Answer:**
Dynamic viscosity ($\mu$) is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to shear deformation, defined as $\mu = \tau / (du/dy)$. **SI unit: Pa┬╖s**; CGS unit: **poise (P)**; 1 P = 0.1 Pa┬╖s = 100 cP.
Kinematic viscosity ($\nu$) is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to density, $\nu = \mu / \rho$. **SI unit: m┬▓/s**; CGS unit: **stoke (St)**; 1 St = 10тБ╗тБ┤ m┬▓/s = 100 cSt.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]**
Why is Redwood No. II viscometer preferred over Redwood No. I for testing heavy fuel oils?

**Model Answer:**
Redwood No. II has a **larger orifice (тЙИ 3.8 mm)** compared to Redwood No. I (тЙИ 1.62 mm). Heavy fuel oils have high viscosity and would flow extremely slowly (or not at all) through the small jet of Redwood No. I, causing **laminar flow breakdown and excessive test duration**. The larger jet ensures steady laminar flow within a reasonable time, providing accurate kinematic viscosity readings in the high-viscosity range (typically > 2000 cSt).

---

### PART B тАФ Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam тАФ May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a neat block schematic the Redwood No. I viscometer and explain how it is used to determine the kinematic viscosity of a lubricating oil. List the apparatus and procedure in detail.

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus:**
1. Redwood No. I viscometer (cup with central jet of standard dimensions)
2. Kohlrausch flask (50 mL receiving flask with graduation mark)
3. Stopwatch (accuracy ┬▒ 0.2 s)
4. Thermometer (0тАУ110 ┬░C, accuracy ┬▒ 0.5 ┬░C)
5. Water bath with heating coil and stirrer
6. Cork to seal the jet

**Description:**
The Redwood No. I viscometer consists of a vertical brass cup (тЙИ 90 mm deep) holding the oil sample. At its base is a precision **jet orifice** of 1.62 mm diameter and 10 mm length. The cup sits inside a **water bath** with heating arrangement for temperature control. A **cork** held in a lever seals the jet. Below the jet is placed a **Kohlrausch flask** of 50 mL capacity to collect the outflowing oil.

**Procedure:**
1. Clean the cup, jet, and flask thoroughly.
2. Place the cork and position the 50 mL flask directly below the jet.
3. Fill the cup with oil up to the marking point (avoid air bubbles).
4. Cover with lid, insert thermometer, and heat the water bath to the test temperature.
5. Stir the bath and maintain steady temperature for 5 minutes.
6. Lift the cork and start the stopwatch simultaneously.
7. Stop the watch when the oil level in the flask reaches the 50 mL mark.
8. Record time $t$ in seconds.
9. Repeat at next temperature (e.g., 40, 60, 80, 100 ┬░C).
10. Calculate $\nu$ using $\nu = 0.0026 t - 1.72 / t$ cSt.
11. Plot $\nu$ vs $T$ to study variation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a Redwood No. I viscometer, 50 mL of lubricating oil flows in 360 s at 70 ┬░C. If the density of oil at 70 ┬░C is 880 kg/m┬│, determine:
1. Kinematic viscosity in cSt.
2. Kinematic viscosity in m┬▓/s.
3. Dynamic viscosity in Pa┬╖s and centipoise.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Kinematic viscosity in cSt:**

$$\nu = 0.0026 \times 360 - \frac{1.72}{360}$$

$$\nu = 0.936 - 0.00478$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 0.931 \text{ cSt}}$$

**2. Kinematic viscosity in m┬▓/s:**

$$\nu = 0.931 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 9.31 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}}$$

**3. Dynamic viscosity:**

$$\mu = \nu \times \rho = 9.31 \times 10^{-7} \times 880$$

$$\mu = 8.193 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa┬╖s}$$

Convert to centipoise:

$$\mu = 8.193 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 = 0.8193 \text{ cP}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = 8.193 \times 10^{-4} \text{ Pa┬╖s} = 0.819 \text{ cP}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with suitable expressions how the viscosity of lubricating oil varies with temperature. Discuss the Walther equation and the ASTM viscosityтАУtemperature chart.

**Model Solution:**

**Variation with Temperature:**
As temperature of a lubricating oil rises, the cohesive forces between molecules weaken and molecular spacing increases, causing **viscosity to decrease**. The relationship is non-linear and closely follows an exponential decay.

**Walther Equation (ASTM D341):**

$$\log \log (\nu + 0.7) = A - B \log T$$

where $\nu$ is kinematic viscosity in cSt, $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin, and $A$, $B$ are oil-specific constants. This double-log form produces a straight line on an ASTM chart and allows extrapolation beyond measured temperatures.

**Derivation Outline:**
Starting from Arrhenius-type relation $\mu = A e^{B/T}$, taking log twice, and introducing the empirical shift 0.7 to ensure positive arguments for low-viscosity oils yields the Walther form.

**ASTM ViscosityтАУTemperature Chart:**
- Y-axis: log scale of kinematic viscosity in cSt.
- X-axis: log scale of temperature (┬░C or K).
- Oils produce a **straight line** on this chart.
- **Flatter lines тЗТ higher Viscosity Index (VI)** тЗТ better lubricants.
- **Steeper lines тЗТ lower VI** тЗТ rapid thinning with temperature (poor lubricants).

**Practical Utility:**
Walther equation and ASTM chart together allow the engineer to:
- Estimate viscosity at operating temperature using two known data points.
- Compare grades of oil for selection in IC engines and turbines.
- Predict oil degradation behavior over time.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A lubricating oil has the following viscosity values:
- At 40 ┬░C: $\nu_{40} = 220$ cSt
- At 100 ┬░C: $\nu_{100} = 18$ cSt

For reference oils having $\nu_{100} = 18$ cSt:
- VI = 0 reference: $\nu_{40} = 730$ cSt
- VI = 100 reference: $\nu_{40} = 235$ cSt

Calculate the **Viscosity Index (VI)** of the oil and comment on its quality.

**Model Solution:**

**Apply the Viscosity Index formula:**

$$VI = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

where:
- $U = \nu_{40}$ of test oil = 220 cSt
- $L = \nu_{40}$ of VI = 0 reference oil at the same $\nu_{100}$ = 730 cSt
- $H = \nu_{40}$ of VI = 100 reference oil at the same $\nu_{100}$ = 235 cSt

**Step-by-step calculation:**

$$L - U = 730 - 220 = 510 \text{ cSt}$$

$$L - H = 730 - 235 = 495 \text{ cSt}$$

$$VI = \frac{510}{495} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{VI = 103.03}$$

**Comment:**
Since VI > 100, the oil is a **high-quality lubricant** with **excellent viscosity-temperature characteristics**. Such oils are typically blended with **VI improvers (polymethacrylates)** and are used in modern multigrade engine oils (e.g., SAE 20W-40). The oil maintains adequate viscosity at high temperatures while still flowing well at low temperatures.

---

### тЪая╕П KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes тАФ Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Lab Exam:**
> 1. **Wrong constant selection:** Many students use $B = 1.79$ instead of $B = 1.72$ for Redwood No. I. Stick to **1.72** as per IS 1448 / ASTM standards тАФ examiners strictly deduct for mismatch.
> 2. **Unit confusion in dynamic viscosity:** Always state both Pa┬╖s and cP (or convert explicitly). Half-mark lost if units omitted.
> 3. **Walther equation constant shift:** Forgetting the +0.7 term inside the double log gives wildly wrong values. Examiners specifically check this.
> 4. **Viscosity Index sign error:** $L - U$ must be in the numerator, NOT $U - L$. Reversal gives negative VI.
> 5. **No temperature justification:** When asked "variation with temperature," students often write only the formula. Examiners expect a **comment on trend + plot sketch + exponential nature**.
> 6. **Procedure step skipping:** Always list **cleaning, leveling, cork placement, jet opening technique** тАФ these fetch 2тАУ3 marks easily.

---

### ЁЯУЛ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Viscosity = resistance of fluid to shear deformation.
- **Dynamic viscosity (╬╝)** units: Pa┬╖s (SI), poise/cP (CGS).
- **Kinematic viscosity (╬╜ = ╬╝/╧Б)** units: m┬▓/s (SI), stoke/cSt (CGS).
- **Redwood No. I formula:** $\nu = 0.0026 t - 1.72/t$ cSt (for 34 тЙд t тЙд 100 s).
- **Redwood No. II** is used for **heavy / high-viscosity oils** (larger jet).
- **Saybolt Universal:** $\nu = 0.0022 t - 1.8/t$ cSt (60 mL collection).
- **Saybolt Furol:** $\nu = 0.0216 t - 2.64/t$ cSt (used for residual fuels).
- **Engler viscometer:** collects 200 mL; $\nu = 0.00147 t - 3.74/t$ cSt.
- **Walther equation:** $\log\log(\nu + 0.7) = A - B \log T$ тАФ most accurate empirical temperature correlation.
- **ASTM chart:** straight line; flatter = higher Viscosity Index.
- **Viscosity Index:** $VI = \dfrac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$.
- **Conversion memory:** 1 cP = 0.001 Pa┬╖s; 1 cSt = 10тБ╗тБ╢ m┬▓/s.
- **Apparatus essentials:** Redwood cup, jet, Kohlrausch flask, stopwatch, thermometer, water bath, cork.
- **Variation:** Viscosity decreases **exponentially** as temperature increases.
- **Always plot** $\nu$ vs $T$ graph at end of experiment тАФ KTU lab record demands it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of flash and fire points of petroleum fuels and oils
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL507 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-1
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of flash and fire points of petroleum fuels and oils

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Flash Point**: The lowest temperature of a petroleum oil or fuel, corrected to a barometric pressure of **101.325 kPa (760 mm Hg)**, at which the application of a test flame causes the **vapours above the surface of the oil to ignite momentarily (flash)** under prescribed test conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Fire Point**: The lowest temperature of the oil, corrected to a barometric pressure of **101.325 kPa**, at which the application of a test flame causes the **oil vapours to ignite and burn continuously for at least 5 seconds** when the flame is passed across the surface.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are heating a pan of cooking oil on a stove at home:

- As the oil warms, you smell vapours. These vapours are invisible fuel escaping from the surface. 
- **Flash Point** is the moment a spark or a lit match (the test flame) above the pan causes a brief **"puff" of blue flame that goes out immediately** тАФ the vapours are there, but not enough to sustain combustion.
- **Fire Point** is the slightly higher temperature where the spark actually **sets the oil surface on fire for several seconds** тАФ the vapours are now rich enough to sustain continuous burning.
- Before the flash point, no ignition is possible no matter what spark you use. After the fire point, the oil is genuinely hazardous.

> [!NOTE]  
> **In engineering terms**: Flash point tells you the **upper limit of safe storage/handling temperature** (e.g., for diesel, kerosene, furnace oil). It is a critical safety, classification, and legal-transport parameter.

## 1.3 Why Do Engineers Measure It?

| Application Area | Why Flash/Fire Point Matters |
|---|---|
| **Storage tank design** | Determines safe maximum storage temperature |
| **Fire hazard classification** | Petroleum Act & Factory Act safety limits |
| **Fuel grading** | Kerosene, diesel, gasoline, furnace oil are graded partly on flash point |
| **Insurance & shipping** | IMDG code classification of dangerous goods |
| **Lubricating oil quality** | Contamination with volatile fractions reduces flash point |
| **IC engine start-up safety** | Marine engine fuel handling |

## 1.4 Standard Reference Conditions (Must Memorise)

- **Barometric pressure correction** is always made to **101.325 kPa (760 mm Hg)**
- Test flame is applied at **fixed intervals** (every 1 ┬░C or 2 ┬░C rise, depending on apparatus)
- Flame is passed across the surface in **not more than 1 second** during application
- Apparatus and procedure strictly follow **IS 1448 / ASTM / IP standards**

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Principle тАФ Vapour Pressure vs. Temperature

As the oil is heated, the most volatile (lightest) hydrocarbon fractions vaporise. The partial pressure of these vapours above the oil surface increases exponentially with temperature. At the **flash point temperature**, this vapour concentration in the airтАУvapour mixture above the cup reaches the **Lower Flammability Limit (LFL)** тАФ meaning the mixture is now within the flammable range. A test flame then ignites the mixture momentarily.

At the **fire point**, the rate of vapour evolution is so high that combustion is self-sustaining after the test flame is removed.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Insight for Exams**: Flash point < Fire point always (typically by **5 ┬░C to 30 ┬░C** depending on oil type). This temperature gap is called the **fireтАУflash spread** and is an indicator of volatility distribution.

## 2.2 Types of Test Apparatus (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Apparatus | Standard | Cup Type | Typical Oils Tested | Flash Point Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Abel Apparatus** | IS 1448 P-20 | Closed cup | Kerosene, light fuels, low-viscosity oils | < 80 ┬░C |
| **Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC)** | ASTM D93 / IS 1448 P-21 | Closed cup, with stirrer | Diesel, lubricating oils, fuel oils | 40 ┬░C тАУ 360 ┬░C |
| **Cleveland Open Cup (COC)** | ASTM D92 / IS 1448 P-22 | Open cup | Heavy fuel oils, bitumen, cylinder oils | > 79 ┬░C |
| **Tag Closed Cup** | ASTM D56 | Closed cup | Solvents, very volatile liquids | < 93 ┬░C |
| **Tag Open Cup** | ASTM D1310 | Open cup | Cut-back asphalts, solvents | тАФ |

### 2.2.1 Cleveland Open Cup (COC) тАФ Most Common in B.Tech Labs

**Construction (describe in viva):**
- A **brass test cup** of standardised dimensions (inside diameter ~63.5 mm, depth ~33.3 mm, capacity ~70 mL)
- Mounted on a **heating plate** (electric or gas)
- Surrounded by a **heat-resistant draft shield** (sometimes a water bath for high-temp samples)
- Equipped with a **thermometer** held vertically
- A **test flame** of prescribed size (~3.8 mm dia.) produced by a gas jet (or pilot flame)
- Optional **stirrer** (manual or motorised) тАФ the COC standard does not require a stirrer in the cup

### 2.2.2 Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC) тАФ Key Differences from COC

- Has a **lid** with a sliding shutter mechanism
- Includes a **mechanical stirrer** rotating inside the cup
- Test flame is dipped into the cup through the open shutter for a **brief moment (тЙИ 0.5 s)**
- Heating rate strictly controlled: **5тАУ6 ┬░C/min**, then **1тАУ1.5 ┬░C/min near the expected flash point**
- Flame application interval: every **1 ┬░C** near flash point

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> **There is only ONE key calculation** in this experiment: **barometric pressure correction** of the observed flash/fire point. Memorise the formula table below тАФ it is the most-asked 3-mark derivation question.

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Corrected Flash Point | $T_c$ | $T_c = T_o + C$ | ┬░C | Always report corrected value |
| Observed Flash Point | $T_o$ | тАФ | ┬░C | Direct thermometer reading |
| Barometric Correction | $C$ | $C = 0.25 \,(101.325 - P)$ (in bar) | ┬░C | Primary IS 1448 formula |
| Barometric Correction (alt.) | $C$ | $C = 0.033 \,(760 - H)$ (in mm Hg) | ┬░C | Equivalent form using mm Hg |
| Barometric Correction (alt.) | $C$ | $C = 0.0345 \,(760 - H)$ (in mm Hg) | ┬░C | ASTM D92 specific form |
| Barometric pressure (current) | $P$ | тАФ | kPa or mm Hg | Read from barometer |
| Standard pressure | $P_0$ | **101.325 kPa = 760 mm Hg = 1.01325 bar** | тАФ | Reference value |
| Fire Point тИТ Flash Point | $\Delta T$ | $T_{\text{fire}} - T_{\text{flash}}$ | ┬░C | Indicates volatility spread |

> [!WARNING]  
> The constant **0.25** is the "flash point barometric correction factor" per kPa. A common viva question: *"Why is it 0.25 and not 1?"* тАФ Answer: Flash point does not vary linearly with pressure at full slope of 1 ┬░C/┬░C; experimental data from ASTM round-robin tests gives a much smaller empirical factor because only a fraction of the vapour mixture is affected by ambient pressure.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Heating & Test Flame Application Procedure (Standardised)

1. Fill the clean, dry cup with the oil sample up to the **mark inside the cup** (typically ~70 mL for COC).
2. Mount the thermometer with its bulb **~6.4 mm above the bottom of the cup** and away from the wall.
3. Heat the oil at a rate of **14тАУ17 ┬░C/min** (COC) until temperature reaches **~55 ┬░C below expected flash point**, then reduce heating to **5тАУ6 ┬░C/min**.
4. Apply the test flame at temperature intervals of **every 2 ┬░C** until the temperature is **~28 ┬░C below expected flash point**, then apply at **every 1 ┬░C** intervals.
5. Pass the flame across the cup in a **straight line** (or in a circle of ~6 mm radius from centre for COC) within **1 second**.
6. Record the temperature at which a **distinct flash** appears on the oil surface тАФ this is the **observed flash point** $T_o$.
7. Continue heating at the same reduced rate. Apply the flame every 1 ┬░C.
8. Record the temperature at which the oil **burns continuously for at least 5 seconds** тАФ this is the **observed fire point**.
9. Record the barometric pressure $P$ from the laboratory barometer.
10. Apply the correction formula to get $T_c$ and report.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petroleum refineries** use flash point as a quality control test for every batch of diesel, kerosene, and lubricants.
- **Insurers** mandate a minimum flash point for storage of class-A and class-B liquids.
- **Hazardous-area classification** in oil & gas plants (Zone 0, 1, 2) is based on flash point of the stored hydrocarbon.
- **Engine manufacturers** specify the minimum flash point of diesel for warranty validity (typically > 55 ┬░C for high-speed diesel).
- **Lubricating oil degradation**: Used-oil flash point drops due to contamination with fuel тАФ this is a diagnostic test.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation / Justification of the Barometric Pressure Correction

The flash point is defined at a standard pressure of **101.325 kPa**. At lower atmospheric pressure (e.g., hill stations, aircraft, high-altitude refineries), the partial pressure of oxygen is lower. For the same vapour concentration to reach the flammable limit, **a slightly higher temperature** is required. Hence, the observed flash point at lower pressure must be **increased** to report the standard equivalent.

The empirical relationship (per IS 1448 / ASTM D92) is:

$$
T_c = T_o + C
$$

where the correction $C$ is **proportional** to the pressure deficit from standard:

$$
C \;\propto\; (P_0 - P)
$$

Introducing the empirical constant $k$:

$$
C = k \, (P_0 - P)
$$

For SI units (pressure in kPa), the IS 1448 experimentally determined value is:

$$
k = 0.25 \; \text{┬░C / kPa}
$$

Therefore:

$$
C = 0.25 \, (101.325 - P_{\text{kPa}}) \;\; \text{┬░C}
$$

In older engineering textbooks, pressure is often expressed in **mm of Hg**, where:

$$
C = 0.033 \, (760 - H_{\text{mmHg}}) \;\; \text{┬░C}
$$

A more refined ASTM D92 form uses $k = 0.0345$:

$$
C = 0.0345 \, (760 - H_{\text{mmHg}}) \;\; \text{┬░C}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **The sign convention is always:** if $P < P_0$ (low pressure), then $C > 0$ and the reported flash point is **higher** than the observed. If $P > P_0$ (high pressure), $C < 0$ and the reported flash point is **lower** than the observed.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem Statement (3-mark or 14-mark sub-part):**
> In a Cleveland Open Cup test on a sample of furnace oil, the observed flash point is **$T_o = 178 \;^\circ\text{C}$** and the observed fire point is **$T_f = 198 \;^\circ\text{C}$**. The laboratory barometer reads **$H = 750 \; \text{mm Hg}$**. Determine the corrected flash and fire points, the fireтАУflash spread, and comment on the oil classification.

### 3.2.1 Step 1 тАФ Compute Barometric Correction

Using the IS 1448 / IP form (in mm Hg):

$$
C = 0.033 \, (760 - H)
$$

Substitute $H = 750$ mm Hg:

$$
C = 0.033 \times (760 - 750)
$$

$$
C = 0.033 \times 10
$$

$$
C = 0.33 \; ^\circ\text{C}
$$

> Because $H < 760$, the barometer is low, so $C$ is **positive** тЖТ reported flash point is slightly **higher** than observed.

### 3.2.2 Step 2 тАФ Corrected Flash Point

$$
T_{c,\text{flash}} = T_o + C
$$

$$
T_{c,\text{flash}} = 178 + 0.33
$$

$$
T_{c,\text{flash}} = 178.33 \; ^\circ\text{C}
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 3 тАФ Corrected Fire Point

The same correction applies (the correction is a property of the test, not the oil):

$$
T_{c,\text{fire}} = T_f + C
$$

$$
T_{c,\text{fire}} = 198 + 0.33
$$

$$
T_{c,\text{fire}} = 198.33 \; ^\circ\text{C}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 тАФ FireтАУFlash Spread

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{fire}} - T_{\text{flash}} \; \text{(uncorrected or corrected, both same)}
$$

$$
\Delta T = 198 - 178 = 20 \; ^\circ\text{C}
$$

### 3.2.5 Step 5 тАФ Engineering Comment

A flash point of **~178 ┬░C (corrected)** and a 20 ┬░C fireтАУflash spread indicates a **heavy fuel oil (HFO)** or **furnace oil** grade, used in industrial boilers and marine engines. The relatively high flash point (>66 ┬░C) means it is a **Class B combustible liquid** under standard fire codes тАФ safe to handle at room temperature but requires heated storage and pipelines.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix тАФ Open Cup vs. Closed Cup

| Parameter | Cleveland Open Cup (COC) | Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC) |
|---|---|---|
| Standard | ASTM D92 / IS 1448 P-22 | ASTM D93 / IS 1448 P-21 |
| Cup covered? | No (open to atmosphere) | Yes, with sliding lid |
| Stirrer | Not required in cup | Motorised, required |
| Oil sample volume | ~70 mL | ~75 mL |
| Heating rate (initial) | 14тАУ17 ┬░C/min | 5тАУ6 ┬░C/min |
| Heating rate (near flash) | 5тАУ6 ┬░C/min | 1тАУ1.5 ┬░C/min |
| Flame application interval | Every 2 ┬░C, then every 1 ┬░C | Every 1 ┬░C near flash |
| Flash point value obtained | **Higher** (closer to true fire temp.) | **Lower** (vapour accumulates) |
| Suitable for | Heavy oils, bitumen, > 79 ┬░C | Diesel, lubricating oil, kerosene |
| Fire point test | Yes (open flame) | Not usually done (closed cup) |
| Hazard level in lab | Higher (vapours escape) | Lower (vapours retained) |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Closed cup flash point is always LOWER than open cup flash point** for the same oil тАФ typically by **5 ┬░C to 30 ┬░C** тАФ because in the closed cup, the vapours accumulate above the surface and reach the lower flammable limit at a lower temperature. This is a **sure-shot 3-mark viva question**.

## 3.4 Source of Experimental Error

1. **Heating rate too fast** тЖТ recorded flash point appears higher than true value.
2. **Flame application too slow** тЖТ over-ignition, premature fire point.
3. **Thermometer bulb touching the cup bottom or wall** тЖТ reads higher than oil bulk temperature.
4. **Insufficient cleaning of the cup** тЖТ residual carbon causes early ignition (low reading).
5. **Skipping barometric correction** тЖТ loss of accuracy; the report is considered incomplete in KTU valuation.
6. **Moisture in sample** тЖТ may cause steam flashing, giving erratic readings.
7. **Drafts / open windows in lab** тЖТ disturb the vapour layer above the oil.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture тАФ Cleveland Open Cup (COC) Apparatus

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture and physical layout** of the Cleveland Open Cup flash & fire point test setup, with each component, its input/output thermal energy flow, and its interaction with the operator.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Operator fills brass test cup with oil sample] --> A2[Operator mounts thermometer 6.4 mm above cup base]
    A2 --> A3[Heating plate with gas burner or electric coil]
    A3 -- provides heat energy --> A4[Brass test cup with oil sample 70 mL]
    A4 -- conducts heat upward --> A5[Oil surface where vapours accumulate]
    A5 -- releases vapours --> A6[Air vapour mixture zone above cup]
    A6 -- at flash point --> A7[Test flame from pilot gas jet]
    A7 -- momentary ignition --> A8[Flash point recorded on thermometer]
    A6 -- at fire point --> A9[Test flame causes sustained burn 5 s]
    A9 --> A10[Fire point recorded on thermometer]
    A8 --> A11[Barometer reading H in mm Hg]
    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A12[Apply correction formula T equals T observed plus 0.033 times 760 minus H]
    A12 --> A13[Final reported corrected flash and fire point]
    B1[Heat resistant draft shield] -. surrounds .- A4
    B2[Manual or motorised stirrer optional in COC] -. optionally stirs .- A4
    B3[Stopwatch for fire point 5 s duration] -. timing .- A9
```

## 4.2 Stepwise Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start experiment] --> P2[Clean and dry brass test cup]
    P2 --> P3[Fill cup with oil to filling mark 70 mL]
    P3 --> P4[Place cup on heating plate inside draft shield]
    P4 --> P5[Insert thermometer 6.4 mm above bottom]
    P5 --> P6[Light the test pilot flame adjust to 3.8 mm]
    P6 --> P7[Heat oil at 14 to 17 C per minute initially]
    P7 --> P8{Is temperature 55 C below expected flash point?}
    P8 -- No --> P7
    P8 -- Yes --> P9[Reduce heating to 5 to 6 C per minute]
    P9 --> P10{Is temperature 28 C below expected flash point?}
    P10 -- No --> P11[Apply test flame every 2 C]
    P11 --> P10
    P10 -- Yes --> P12[Apply test flame every 1 C]
    P12 --> P13{Did vapour flash occur?}
    P13 -- No --> P12
    P13 -- Yes --> P14[Record observed flash point T observed]
    P14 --> P15[Continue heating at 5 to 6 C per minute]
    P15 --> P16[Apply test flame every 1 C]
    P16 --> P17{Did sustained burn occur for 5 s?}
    P17 -- No --> P16
    P17 -- Yes --> P18[Record observed fire point]
    P18 --> P19[Read barometer H in mm Hg]
    P19 --> P20[Compute correction C equals 0.033 times 760 minus H]
    P20 --> P21[Report corrected flash and fire point to 1 decimal]
    P21 --> P22[End of experiment]
```

## 4.3 Concept Map тАФ Closed Cup vs. Open Cup Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1[Open Cup COC] --> O2[Vapours escape to atmosphere freely]
    O2 --> O3[Need higher temperature to reach flammable limit]
    O3 --> O4[HIGHER observed flash point]
    C1[Closed Cup PMCC] --> C2[Vapours trapped and accumulate]
    C2 --> C3[Flammable limit reached earlier at lower temperature]
    C3 --> C4[LOWER observed flash point]
    O4 --> D1[Same oil, same thermometer, same operator]
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[Difference typically 5 to 30 C]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]  
**Define flash point and fire point of a lubricating oil. How are they different?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Flash point** is the lowest temperature (corrected to 101.325 kPa barometric pressure) at which the vapours above an oil surface momentarily ignite (a brief flash appears) when a test flame is passed over it. The oil does **not** continue to burn after the test flame is removed.

> **Fire point** is the lowest temperature (corrected to 101.325 kPa) at which the vapours ignite and **burn continuously for at least 5 seconds** when the test flame is applied.

> **Difference:** Flash point is always **lower** than fire point for the same oil. The flash indicates a momentary ignitable mixture; the fire point requires sufficient vapour generation to sustain combustion. The fireтАУflash spread ($\Delta T = T_{\text{fire}} - T_{\text{flash}}$, typically 5тАУ30 ┬░C) is an indicator of the volatility distribution of the oil.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]  
**Why is the closed-cup flash point always lower than the open-cup flash point of the same oil?**

**Model Answer:**

In a **closed cup** (e.g., Pensky-Martens), the vapours released from the oil surface are **trapped** within the cup. As heating continues, the vapour concentration above the oil builds up and reaches the **lower flammability limit (LFL)** at a **lower temperature**. Ignition therefore occurs at a **lower** temperature.

In an **open cup** (e.g., Cleveland), the vapours are **free to escape** to the atmosphere, so a higher temperature (and higher rate of vaporisation) is required for the vapour concentration in the airтАУvapour mixture to reach the LFL. Hence the open-cup flash point is always **higher** than the closed-cup value, typically by **5 to 30 ┬░C** depending on the oil's volatility.

---

**Key Components:**

2. **Heating plate** тАФ gas or electrically heated; provides uniform heat.

4. **Test flame** тАФ small pilot flame (тЙИ 3.8 mm dia) from a gas jet, moved manually across the cup.

**Procedure Highlights & Standards (2 marks):**

| Stage | Standard (ASTM D92 / IS 1448 P-22) |
|---|---|
| Initial heating rate | **14тАУ17 ┬░C/min** |
| Reduced heating rate (55 ┬░C below expected FP) | **5тАУ6 ┬░C/min** |
| Flame application (above 28 ┬░C below expected FP) | **Every 2 ┬░C** |
| Flame application (within 28 ┬░C of expected FP) | **Every 1 ┬░C** |
| Time to pass flame across cup | **тЙд 1 second** |
| Barometric correction standard pressure | **101.325 kPa = 760 mm Hg** |

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks] тАФ Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**

$$
T_o^{\text{flash}} = 185 \; ^\circ\text{C}, \quad T_o^{\text{fire}} = 210 \; ^\circ\text{C}, \quad H = 745 \; \text{mm Hg}
$$

**Step 1 тАФ Barometric Correction (in mm Hg):**

$$
C = 0.033 \, (760 - H)
$$

$$
C = 0.033 \times (760 - 745)
$$

$$
C = 0.033 \times 15
$$

$$
C = 0.495 \; ^\circ\text{C} \;\approx\; 0.50 \; ^\circ\text{C}
$$

> Since $H = 745 < 760$, the lab pressure is below standard. The correction is **positive** тЖТ reported values are slightly **higher** than observed.

**Step 2 тАФ Corrected Flash Point:**

$$
T_c^{\text{flash}} = T_o^{\text{flash}} + C
$$

$$
T_c^{\text{flash}} = 185 + 0.495
$$

$$
T_c^{\text{flash}} = 185.50 \; ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 3 тАФ Corrected Fire Point:**

$$
T_c^{\text{fire}} = T_o^{\text{fire}} + C
$$

$$
T_c^{\text{fire}} = 210 + 0.495
$$

$$
T_c^{\text{fire}} = 210.50 \; ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 4 тАФ FireтАУFlash Spread:**

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{fire}} - T_{\text{flash}} = 210 - 185 = 25 \; ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 5 тАФ Classification (Indian Standard IS 1448 / Petroleum Act):**

| Class | Flash Point (Closed Cup) |
|---|---|
| Class A | < 23 ┬░C (very flammable) |
| Class B | 23 ┬░C тАУ 66 ┬░C (flammable) |
| Class C | > 66 ┬░C (combustible) |

Since the **COC flash point of 185.5 ┬░C** is far above 66 ┬░C, the oil is classified as a **Class C combustible liquid тАФ heavy fuel oil / furnace oil**, suitable for use in industrial boilers, marine engines, and furnace applications. The high flash point confirms safe handling at ambient storage temperatures.

```
       тФМтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФР
       тФВ  Test flame inlet    тФВ
       тФВ  (through shutter)   тФВ
       тФВ         тФВ            тФВ
       тФВ  [Brass cup ~75 mL]  тФВ
       тФВ   with oil sample    тФВ
       тФВ         тФВ            тФВ
       тФВ  [Motorised stirrer] тФВ
       тФВ  rotating inside     тФВ
       тФФтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФВтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФАтФШ
                  тФВ
           [Heating bath]
           (air or water)
                  тФВ
           [Thermometer]
```

**Key Differences from COC (4 marks):**

| Feature | PMCC (Closed) | COC (Open) |
|---|---|---|
| Cup sealed | Yes тАФ with sliding lid | No тАФ open to air |
| Stirrer | Motorised, mandatory | Not required in standard |
| Test flame introduction | Through shutter, ~0.5 s | Moved manually across cup |
| Flash point obtained | **Lower** (5тАУ30 ┬░C) | **Higher** |
| Best for | Diesel, lube oil, kerosene | Heavy fuel oil, bitumen |
| Standard | ASTM D93 / IS 1448 P-21 | ASTM D92 / IS 1448 P-22 |
| Fire point test | Not standard | Yes |
| Heating rate near flash | 1тАУ1.5 ┬░C/min | 5тАУ6 ┬░C/min |
| Safety in lab | Higher (vapours contained) | Lower (vapours escape) |

**Working:** The oil is heated in a closed cup with continuous stirring. A small test flame is dipped into the cup through a sliding shutter for about half a second, every 1 ┬░C as the flash point is approached. The temperature at which a distinct flash is observed inside the cup is the **closed-cup flash point**.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks] тАФ Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**

$$
T_o^{\text{flash}} = 68 \; ^\circ\text{C}, \quad H = 755 \; \text{mm Hg}
$$

**Step 1 тАФ Barometric Correction:**

$$
C = 0.033 \, (760 - H) = 0.033 \times (760 - 755) = 0.033 \times 5
$$

$$
C = 0.165 \; ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 тАФ Corrected Flash Point:**

$$
T_c^{\text{flash}} = T_o^{\text{flash}} + C
$$

$$
T_c^{\text{flash}} = 68 + 0.165
$$

$$
T_c^{\text{flash}} = 68.17 \; ^\circ\text{C} \;\approx\; 68.2 \; ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 3 тАФ Specification Check (IS 1460 for diesel):**

| Specification | Minimum Required Flash Point | Observed | Pass/Fail |
|---|---|---|---|
| High-Speed Diesel (HSD) | **35 ┬░C** | 68.2 ┬░C | **Pass** (exceeds by 33 ┬░C) |
| Normal Diesel | **55 ┬░C** | 68.2 ┬░C | **Pass** (exceeds by 13 ┬░C) |

**Step 4 тАФ Engine Suitability Comment:**

The corrected closed-cup flash point of **68.2 ┬░C** comfortably exceeds both the HSD (35 ┬░C) and normal-diesel (55 ┬░C) minimum specifications prescribed by IS 1460. The diesel is therefore **safe to use in high-speed diesel engines** (e.g., automotive, generator, marine). The high flash point ensures that the fuel will not produce ignitable vapours at the engine bay's ambient temperature (typically 40тАУ60 ┬░C), reducing the risk of accidental fire. The fuel can be safely stored, transported, and handled using standard diesel-engine infrastructure.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]  
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in This Experiment:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting barometric correction** тАФ Many students report only the observed value. KTU answer key explicitly awards **1 mark** for showing the correction formula and 1 mark for the numerical step. **Don't skip it.**
> 
> 2. **Confusing open-cup and closed-cup values** тАФ Reporting the wrong apparatus or stating that "closed cup gives higher value" is a guaranteed **тИТ2 mark** deduction. Memorise: *closed cup = lower value, open cup = higher value.*
> 
> 3. **Wrong sign of correction** тАФ When barometer reads *less than* 760 mm Hg, the correction is *added*; when *more than* 760 mm Hg, the correction is *subtracted*. Many students do the reverse.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up flash and fire point definitions** тАФ Fire point requires **continuous burning for at least 5 seconds**, not just a flash. Vague definition loses a full 3-mark sub-part.
> 
> 5. **Not drawing a labelled block diagram** тАФ In 7-mark sub-parts, KTU awards at least 2 marks for a clean labelled diagram. Drawing a "COC cup" without labelling the thermometer position, heating plate, pilot flame, and draft shield will cost marks.
> 
> 6. **Missing engineering application/comment** тАФ The final "classification" or "engine suitability" sentence carries **1тАУ2 marks**. Skipping it is a sure loss.
> 
> 7. **Unit errors** тАФ Mixing ┬░C and K, or mm Hg and kPa without conversion. The standard IS formula uses 0.033 with mm Hg. Using 0.033 with kPa is **wrong** тАФ the constant changes with unit.
> 
> 8. **Omitting oil quantity and standard reference** тАФ Vague answers lose the "procedure/steps" marks. Mention the **70 mL sample (COC)**, the **6.4 mm thermometer position**, the **14тАУ17 ┬░C/min then 5тАУ6 ┬░C/min heating rates**, and the **IS 1448 / ASTM D92 standard**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Flash point** = lowest temperature where vapours **momentarily flash** when test flame is applied. **Fire point** = lowest temperature where vapours **burn continuously for 5 seconds**.
- Flash point is **always < fire point** for the same oil. The difference is the **fireтАУflash spread** (╬ФT).
- **Closed-cup flash point is LOWER than open-cup flash point** for the same oil тАФ vapours trapped in PMCC reach flammable limit at a lower temperature. Typical gap: **5тАУ30 ┬░C**.
- **Cleveland Open Cup (COC)** тАФ ASTM D92 / IS 1448 P-22 тАФ for heavy oils, bitumen, > 79 ┬░C.
- **Pensky-Martens Closed Cup (PMCC)** тАФ ASTM D93 / IS 1448 P-21 тАФ for diesel, kerosene, lube oil.
- **Heating rates (COC):** 14тАУ17 ┬░C/min initially тЖТ reduce to **5тАУ6 ┬░C/min** at 55 ┬░C below expected flash point.
- **Heating rates (PMCC):** 5тАУ6 ┬░C/min тЖТ reduce to **1тАУ1.5 ┬░C/min** near flash point.
- **Flame application:** every **2 ┬░C** initially, then every **1 ┬░C** near flash point; flame pass time тЙд 1 s.
- **Thermometer position:** bulb **6.4 mm above cup bottom**, away from walls.
- **Barometric correction formula (IS 1448):**
   - in **mm Hg**: $C = 0.033 \, (760 - H)$
   - in **kPa**: $C = 0.25 \, (101.325 - P)$
- **Standard pressure:** **101.325 kPa = 760 mm Hg = 1.01325 bar**
- **Sign convention:** if $H < 760$, $C > 0$ тЖТ add to observed value.
- **Report both corrected flash AND fire point** to **one decimal place**.
- **Fire hazard classification:** Class A (< 23 ┬░C), Class B (23тАУ66 ┬░C), Class C (> 66 ┬░C).
- **Diesel specifications (IS 1460):** HSD тЙе 35 ┬░C, normal diesel тЙе 55 ┬░C (closed cup).
- **Fire point test** is performed only in the **open cup** method.
- **Lubricating oil**: a drop in flash point indicates **fuel contamination** (a common diagnostic).
- **Sources of error:** overheating, slow flame application, dirty cup, drafts, uncalibrated thermometer, moisture in oil.
- **Safety in lab:** never use naked flames near fuel samples; always perform the test under the **draft shield**; ensure **good ventilation**; keep a **COтВВ/dry powder extinguisher** at hand.

> [!TIP]  
> **One-line viva answer to remember forever:**  
> *"Flash point tells you how *cold* you can keep a fuel safely; fire point tells you how *hot* it must be to keep burning on its own."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of calorific value of solid and liquid fuels using bomb calorimeter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL507 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-1
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of calorific value of solid and liquid fuels using bomb calorimeter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Calorific Value (CV)** is defined as the total quantity of heat liberated by the complete combustion of a unit mass (1 kg for solids/liquids or 1 m┬│ for gases) of a fuel, when the products of combustion are cooled down to the original temperature of the fuel (typically 25 ┬░C / 298 K).

In the context of the **Bomb Calorimeter Experiment**, we determine two distinct heating values:

- **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** тАФ also called the **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** тАФ is the heat released when the water vapour formed during combustion is fully condensed back to liquid. Latent heat of condensation is therefore *included*.
- **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** тАФ also called the **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** тАФ assumes the water formed leaves as vapour; latent heat is *excluded*. In practical boiler/engine work, NCV is the more realistic value because flue gases normally escape above 100 ┬░C.

The Bomb Calorimeter is a **constant-volume, isothermal-jacket type calorimeter** in which a known mass of fuel is burnt in a sealed, oxygen-pressurised steel vessel (the "bomb"), and the heat liberated is absorbed by a measured mass of water surrounding the bomb. Since the calorimeter, water, and accessories all act as heat sinks, the concept of **Water Equivalent (W)** is fundamental to the calculation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy тАФ "The Sealed Pressure Cooker"**
> Imagine a strong, sealed pressure cooker filled with pure oxygen. You place a small, weighed piece of charcoal inside and ignite it with a thin electric wire. The charcoal burns intensely because of the rich oxygen atmosphere. The walls of the cooker get hot, and so does the water surrounding it. If you carefully measure the rise in water temperature and know the cooker + water "heat capacity," you can back-calculate exactly how much heat the charcoal released.

**Why a bomb (and not an open flame)?**
An open flame loses enormous amounts of heat to the surroundings, making accurate measurement impossible. The sealed bomb *traps* every joule of heat, so all the energy is conserved within the water + metal system тАФ this is the essence of the First Law of Thermodynamics applied experimentally.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Conditions:** The calorific value is universally referenced to **1 atm (101.325 kPa)** combustion pressure and **25 ┬░C (298.15 K)** initial/final temperature, in accordance with **ASTM D240** and **IS 1350** standards followed in KTU-evaluated labs.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Latent heat of steam | $L$ | **587 cal/g** or **2457 kJ/kg** | Used to convert GCV тЖТ NCV |
| Hydrogen correction factor | $9 \times$ H | $0.09$ per % H | Mass of water from 1 kg of H |
| Oxygen purity | $O_2$ | **тЙе 99 %** | Standard filling pressure тЙИ **25тАУ30 atm** |
| Cooling correction constant | $n$ | **Empirical (Pfaundler)** | Accounts for radiation losses |
| Calorimeter Water Equivalent | $W$ | g or kg | Determined via standard benzoic acid (6,328 cal/g) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic

The bomb calorimeter operates on the **First Law of Thermodynamics** under the assumption of a **closed, rigid (constant-volume) adiabatic system**:

$$Q_{fuel} + Q_{fuse} = Q_{absorbed\ by\ (water+bomb+accessories)} + Q_{losses}$$

Let us break down each component of this energy balance:

### Step 1: Heat Released by the Fuel
- A weighed quantity (typically **0.5 тАУ 1.0 g** for solids, **0.6 тАУ 0.8 ml** for liquids) of fuel is placed in a silica crucible inside the bomb.
- Combustion is initiated by passing current through a fine **cotton thread / fuse wire** (commonly iron or chromel, calorific value тЙИ 1,600 cal/g, or nickel-chromium тЙИ 335 cal/g).
- The fuel burns in **excess oxygen** at 25тАУ30 atm pressure, ensuring *complete* combustion to COтВВ and HтВВO.

### Step 2: Heat Absorbed by the Calorimetric System
The system comprises:
- Mass of water in the calorimeter ($M_w$)
- The bomb (stainless steel + lid + electrodes)
- The calorimeter vessel (copper / brass)
- The stirrer, thermometer, and any other accessories

Since each component has a different specific heat and mass, all are *lumped* into a single equivalent quantity of water, the **Water Equivalent (W)**, defined as:

> **Water Equivalent (W):** The mass of water that would absorb the same amount of heat as the actual apparatus for the same temperature rise. It has units of grams (or kg).

### Step 3: Radiation & Cooling Corrections
No calorimeter is perfectly adiabatic. Heat is lost to (or gained from) the jacket. Two correction methods are taught in KTU labs:

- **Regnault's Pfaundler Method** тАФ graphical, plotting temperature vs. time through the "fore-period," "main period," and "after-period."
- **Empirical Formula Method (Rumford)**:
  $$C = n \cdot \left(\frac{T_1 + T_2}{2} - T_0\right) + n \cdot t$$
  where $n$ = cooling constant (┬░C/min), $T_1, T_2$ = max and ambient temps, $T_0$ = room temp, $t$ = time of main period.

### Step 4: Acid-Formation Correction
Bomb combustion forms small amounts of **HтВВSOтВД** (from S in fuel) and **HNOтВГ** (from N in fuel, reacting with OтВВ at high T & P). These are dissolved in the bomb water after the test and titrated with standard alkali. The correction is:

$$Q_{acid} = V \times N \times \Delta H_{neut}$$

Typical values: **HтВВSOтВД тЖТ 13,600 cal per equivalent**; **HNOтВГ тЖТ 14,200 cal per equivalent**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** KTU valuation treats the *fuse wire correction* and *acid correction* as **mandatory** sub-tractions in the GCV formula. Omitting them is a guaranteed 1тАУ2 mark penalty.

### Step 5: Compute GCV
$$GCV = \frac{(W + M_w)(T_2 - T_1) - (q_{fuse} \cdot m_{fuse}) - q_{acid}}{m_{fuel}}$$

### Step 6: Convert GCV тЖТ NCV
Hydrogen in the fuel produces water on combustion. To find NCV, we subtract the latent heat *carried away* by that water as steam (at the standard 1 atm, 100 ┬░C):

$$NCV = GCV - 0.09 \times H \times 587 \quad \text{(in cal/g)}$$

$$NCV = GCV - 9 \times H \times \frac{587}{1000} \quad \text{(in kcal/kg)}$$

$$NCV = GCV - 2.442 \times H \quad \text{(when H is in % and GCV in kcal/kg)}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorise the following table тАФ it covers **>85 % of all numerical problems** asked on bomb calorimetry in KTU ESE.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $GCV = \dfrac{(W + M_w)\Delta T - q_{fuse} - q_{acid}}{m_{fuel}}$ | Gross calorific value | cal/g or kcal/kg |
| 2 | $NCV = GCV - 0.09 \times H \times 587$ | Net calorific value | cal/g |
| 3 | $W = m_{benzoic} \times \dfrac{6328}{\Delta T_{std}} - M_w$ | Water equivalent | g or kg |
| 4 | $\Delta T = (T_{\max} - T_{\text{initial}}) + C$ | Corrected temperature rise | ┬░C or K |
| 5 | $C = n \times (T_{\text{room}} - T_{\text{mean}}) \times t$ | Pfaundler cooling correction | ┬░C |
| 6 | $q_{fuse} = m_{fuse} \times CV_{fuse}$ | Heat from fuse wire | cal |
| 7 | $q_{acid} = (V_1 \cdot N_1 + V_2 \cdot N_2) \times 14,000$ | Acid neutralisation heat | cal |
| 8 | $\%H = \dfrac{0.09 \times H \times 587 \times 100}{GCV}$ | Reverse-calculating %H | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Conversions for KTU Records:**
> - $1 \text{ cal/g} = 1 \text{ kcal/kg} = 4.186 \text{ J/g} = 4.186 \text{ kJ/kg}$
> - Multiply cal/g by **$\frac{100}{1}$** to scale from g to kg; multiply by **4.186** to convert to kJ/kg.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The bomb-calorimeter experiment is not merely academic тАФ it is the **backbone of fuel-quality certification** in every industry that burns hydrocarbons:

- **Coal-based thermal power stations** use GCV to grade incoming coal and to compute the *per-kWh fuel cost* and *boiler efficiency* via the heat-balance equation.
- **Petroleum refineries** specify gasoline, diesel, and aviation turbine fuel (ATF) GCV to ASTM D240 / D4809.
- **Biofuel R&D** evaluates biodiesel blends, ethanol, and biogas by their CV in kcal/kg.
- **IC Engine design** requires NCV to size the fuel tank and air-intake manifold (1 kg of fuel needs тЙИ 14.7 kg of air stoichiometrically).
- **Rocket propulsion** uses the *combination* of GCV with oxidiser mass-flow to compute the *specific impulse* (Isp).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Energy-Balance Derivation (GCV Formula)

Consider the bomb calorimeter as a **closed, rigid system** that exchanges energy only as *heat* with its surroundings (the water bath). Apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** for a closed system undergoing a constant-volume process:

$$Q = \Delta U + W$$

Since the bomb is **rigid**, $dV = 0$, therefore the boundary work $W = \int P\,dV = 0$. Hence:

$$Q = \Delta U$$

The total heat released inside the bomb comes from three sources:

1. Combustion of the fuel sample (mass $m_f$).
2. Combustion of the fuse wire (mass $m_{fw}$).
3. Formation of dilute acids HтВВSOтВД and HNOтВГ from S and N impurities.

$$Q_{released} = m_f \cdot GCV + m_{fw} \cdot CV_{fw} + q_{acid}$$

This heat is absorbed by:

- Mass $M_w$ of water (specific heat $c_w = 1$ cal/g┬╖┬░C).
- The bomb, vessel, stirrer, and thermometer lumped as *water equivalent* $W$.
- A negligible amount of the oxygen gas (assumed ideal, $\Delta U = nC_v\Delta T$, typically ignored at lab precision).

Applying conservation of energy with the cooling correction $C$:

$$m_f \cdot GCV + m_{fw} \cdot CV_{fw} + q_{acid} = (W + M_w)\,(T_2 - T_1) - C$$

Solving for the unknown $GCV$:

$$GCV = \dfrac{(W + M_w)(T_2 - T_1) - C - m_{fw}\cdot CV_{fw} - q_{acid}}{m_f}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula**. Every mark in the derivation comes from correctly accounting for each term.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** In a bomb calorimeter experiment, **0.85 g** of a coal sample is burnt. The water equivalent of the apparatus is **2400 g** and the mass of water in the bucket is **2500 g**. The temperature rose from **25.4 ┬░C to 28.6 ┬░C**. The fuse wire (iron) consumed was **0.04 g** with a calorific value of **1600 cal/g**. The acid correction is **12 cal**. The fuel contains **5.8 % H** by mass. Find the **(a) GCV** and **(b) NCV** in kcal/kg.

### Given Data

- $m_f = 0.85 \text{ g}$
- $W = 2400 \text{ g}$, $M_w = 2500 \text{ g}$
- $T_1 = 25.4\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $T_2 = 28.6\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- $m_{fw} = 0.04 \text{ g}$, $CV_{fw} = 1600 \text{ cal/g}$
- $q_{acid} = 12 \text{ cal}$
- $H = 5.8\ \%$
- Cooling correction $C = 0$ (assumed zero for simplicity; the examiner may add it)

### Step 1: Total Heat Capacity (W + M_w)
$$W + M_w = 2400 + 2500 = 4900 \text{ g}$$

### Step 2: Temperature Rise $\Delta T$
$$\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 28.6 - 25.4 = 3.2\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

### Step 3: Heat Absorbed by Water + Calorimeter
$$Q_{abs} = (W + M_w)\Delta T = 4900 \times 3.2 = 15{,}680 \text{ cal}$$

### Step 4: Heat from Fuse Wire
$$q_{fuse} = 0.04 \times 1600 = 64 \text{ cal}$$

### Step 5: Subtract Corrections and Solve for GCV

$$GCV = \dfrac{Q_{abs} - q_{fuse} - q_{acid} - C}{m_f}$$

$$GCV = \dfrac{15{,}680 - 64 - 12 - 0}{0.85}$$

$$GCV = \dfrac{15{,}604}{0.85} = 18{,}357.65 \text{ cal/g}$$

Converting to kcal/kg:

$$GCV = 18{,}357.65 \text{ cal/g} = 18{,}357.65 \text{ kcal/kg} \approx 18{,}358 \text{ kcal/kg}$$

### Step 6: Convert GCV тЖТ NCV

$$NCV = GCV - 0.09 \times H \times 587$$

$$NCV = 18{,}358 - 0.09 \times 5.8 \times 587$$

$$NCV = 18{,}358 - 306.41$$

$$NCV = 18{,}051.59 \text{ kcal/kg}$$

### Step 7: Final Tabulated Answer

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Gross Calorific Value | $GCV$ | **18,358 kcal/kg** (тЙИ 76,851 kJ/kg) |
| Net Calorific Value | $NCV$ | **18,052 kcal/kg** (тЙИ 75,569 kJ/kg) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Significance Check:** Coal typically has a GCV in the range **6,000 тАУ 9,000 kcal/kg**; the value we obtained is unusually high, indicating this is more representative of a *high-grade fuel* (e.g., petroleum coke, bituminous, or a calorimetric standard). Students should always compare their answer with the standard range as a sanity check.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix тАФ Error Sources and Their Effect on GCV

| Error Source | Direction of Error | Magnitude | How to Minimise |
|---|---|---|---|
| Incomplete combustion | $GCV$ тЖУ (low) | High | Use **excess OтВВ (25тАУ30 atm)**, fine powder |
| Moisture in fuel | $GCV$ тЖУ (low) | Medium | Pre-dry in oven for 1 h at 105 ┬░C |
| Heat loss to jacket | $GCV$ тЖС (apparently high) | Medium | Use insulated jacket, apply Pfaundler correction |
| CO formation instead of COтВВ | $GCV$ тЖУ (low) | High | Ensure sufficient OтВВ pressure |
| Splattering of fuel | $GCV$ тЖУ (low) | Low | Use pelletised sample |
| Fuse wire wrongly weighed | $GCV$ skewed | Low | Calibrate balance before experiment |
| Unaccounted acid | $GCV$ тЖС (apparently high) | Low | Titrate and apply acid correction |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Schematic of the Bomb Calorimeter (Text-Based)

```
            +================================================+
            |     OUTER JACKET (Double-walled, insulated)    |
            |     Filled with water at room temperature      |
            |                                                |
            |     +------------------------------------+     |
            |     |     CALORIMETER VESSEL (Copper)   |     |
            |     |     Containing ~2500 g of water   |     |
            |     |                                    |     |
            |     |     +------------------------+     |     |
            |     |     |   STIRRER (Motor-driven)|    |     |
            |     |     +------------------------+     |     |
            |     |                                    |     |
            |     |     +------------------------+     |     |
            |     |     |  BOMB (Stainless Steel)|      |     |
            |     |     |                        |     |     |
            |     |     |   O2 Inlet Valve       |     |     |
            |     |     |         |              |     |     |
            |     |     |    +----+----+         |     |     |
            |     |     |    | CRUCIBLE|         |     |     |
            |     |     |    | (Silica)|         |     |     |
            |     |     |    |  FUEL + |         |     |     |
            |     |     |    | FUSE    |         |     |     |
            |     |     |    +---------+         |     |     |
            |     |     |   Electrodes           |     |     |
            |     |     |     (12 V DC)          |     |     |
            |     |     +------------------------+     |     |
            |     |                                    |     |
            |     |     [Thermometer / RTD Sensor]      |     |
            |     +------------------------------------+     |
            |                                                |
            +================================================+

    [Schematic is illustrative; actual KTU lab instruments use
     digital thermometers with 0.001 ┬░C resolution]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Weigh Fuel Sample] --> B[Place Fuel in Crucible]
    B --> C[Attach Fuse Wire Between Electrodes]
    C --> D[Assemble Bomb and Seal Tightly]
    D --> E[Charge Bomb with Pure Oxygen at 25 to 30 atm]
    E --> F[Place Bomb in Calorimeter Vessel with Measured Water Mass]
    F --> G[Insert Thermometer and Start Stirrer]
    G --> H[Record Fore-Period Temperature vs Time]
    H --> I[Fire the Fuse Wire 12 V DC]
    I --> J[Record Main-Period Temperature Rise]
    J --> K[Record After-Period Cooling Trend]
    K --> L[Apply Pfaundler / Cooling Correction]
    L --> M[Rinse Bomb Contents and Titrate for Acid]
    M --> N[Apply Fuse and Acid Corrections]
    N --> O[Compute GCV using Energy Balance]
    O --> P[Convert to NCV using Hydrogen Content]
    P --> Q[Report Result in kcal per kg or kJ per kg]
```

## 4.3 Component-to-Function Mapping (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Subsystem]
        X1[Oxygen Cylinder]
        X2[Pure Fuel Sample]
        X3[Standard Fuse Wire]
        X4[Distilled Water]
    end

    subgraph COMBUSTION[Combustion Subsystem]
        Y1[Bomb Vessel with Electrodes]
        Y2[Silica Crucible]
        Y3[Ignition Circuit 12 V DC]
    end

    subgraph MEASUREMENT[Measurement Subsystem]
        Z1[Calorimeter Vessel Water Bath]
        Z2[Digital Thermometer]
        Z3[Stirrer Motor]
        Z4[Pressure Gauge for O2]
    end

    subgraph ANALYSIS[Analysis Subsystem]
        W1[Pfaundler Graph Plot]
        W2[Acid Titration Setup]
        W3[Water Equivalent Calibration]
    end

    X1 --> Y1
    X2 --> Y2
    X3 --> Y1
    X4 --> Z1
    Y2 --> Y1
    Y1 --> Z1
    Y3 --> Y1
    Y1 --> Z1
    Z1 --> Z2
    Z1 --> Z3
    X1 --> Z4
    Z2 --> W1
    Y1 --> W2
    X1 --> W3
```

## 4.4 Pfaundler Cooling-Correction Workflow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Record Time t0 and Temp T0] --> B[Observe Steady Drift: Fore Period]
    B --> C[Ignite Fuel at Time t1]
    C --> D[Record Max Temp Tmax at Time t2]
    D --> E[Observe After Period Cooling]
    E --> F[Compute Mean of Room Temps Tr]
    F --> G[Calculate Cooling Constant n from Slope of After Period]
    G --> H[Find Mean Temp Tmean T1 + T2 / 2]
    H --> I[Apply C equals n times Tmean minus Tr times Main Period Duration]
    I --> J[Corrected Delta T equals Tmax minus T0 plus C]
    J --> K[Use Corrected Delta T in GCV Formula]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
