# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** Define Gross Calorific Value (GCV) and Net Calorific Value (NCV) of a fuel. Why is NCV always less than GCV? **3 marks**

### Model Answer:
- **GCV (Gross / Higher Heating Value):** The total heat liberated by the complete combustion of a unit mass of fuel, with the products cooled back to the original fuel temperature such that *all* the water formed condenses to liquid, releasing its latent heat. **1 mark**
- **NCV (Net / Lower Heating Value):** The total heat liberated by the complete combustion of a unit mass of fuel, assuming the water formed leaves as vapour (i.e., latent heat is *not* recovered). **1 mark**
- **Why NCV < GCV:** The difference arises from the **latent heat of vaporisation of water** formed from the hydrogen in the fuel. In real boiler/engine systems, flue gases exit above 100 ┬░C, so this latent heat is *not* recovered, making NCV the more practical, lower value.
  $$NCV = GCV - 0.09\,H \times 587 \quad \text{(cal/g)}$$ **1 mark**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q:** What is the significance of the *water equivalent* of a bomb calorimeter? How is it determined experimentally? **3 marks**

### Model Answer:
- **Significance:** The water equivalent ($W$) accounts for the heat absorbed not only by the water but also by the bomb, calorimeter vessel, stirrer, thermometer, and all accessories. It represents the mass of water that would absorb the same heat as the apparatus for the same temperature rise. Without knowing $W$, the GCV calculation would undercount the total heat absorbed. **1.5 marks**
- **Experimental Determination:** A sample of a *standard fuel* of known calorific value тАФ most commonly **benzoic acid** with $CV = 6328 \text{ cal/g}$ тАФ is burnt in the bomb. The temperature rise $\Delta T$ is noted. Then:
  $$W = \left(\dfrac{m_{benzoic} \times 6328}{\Delta T_{std}}\right) - M_w$$ **1.5 marks**

---

## 5.2 PART B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, the student answers **one out of two** sub-choices. Each sub-choice has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**.

---

### тЭУ Question Choice A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified 2024 Scheme]`

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Bomb Calorimeter** used to determine the calorific value of solid fuels. **7 marks**

> **(b)** A sample of coal weighing **0.75 g** is burnt in a bomb calorimeter. The water equivalent of the apparatus is **2200 g** and the water in the bucket is **2400 g**. The temperature rose from **24.6 ┬░C to 27.8 ┬░C**. The fuse wire (iron, $CV = 1600$ cal/g) consumed is **0.05 g**. The acid correction is **15 cal**, and the cooling correction is **8 cal**. The coal contains **4.5 % H**. Calculate the **GCV and NCV** in **kcal/kg**. **7 marks**

#### Model Solution to (a):
- **Construction (3.5 marks):** Describe the following with a labelled diagram (refer Section 4.1):
  - Stainless-steel **bomb** (1) with screw-on lid, OтВВ valve, and two electrodes.
  - **Silica crucible** (2) suspended between the electrodes on a ring.
  - **Fuse wire / cotton thread** (3) connecting the electrodes and touching the fuel.
  - **Copper calorimeter vessel** (4) holding a known mass of water.
  - **Stirrer** (5) and **thermometer** (6) immersed in the water.
  - **Outer water jacket** (7) providing nearly adiabatic (isothermal) surroundings.
  - **Oxygen cylinder with pressure gauge** (8) and ignition circuit (12 V DC).
- **Working (3.5 marks):** 
  1. Weigh fuel (0.5тАУ1.0 g) accurately and place in crucible; attach fuse wire.
  2. Assemble the bomb, charge with OтВВ to 25тАУ30 atm.
  3. Place bomb in the calorimeter vessel; add a known mass of water; insert thermometer and start stirrer.
  4. Record the **fore-period** (initial 5 min) to obtain the rate of temperature drift.
  5. Fire the fuse via a 12 V DC pulse; record temperature every 30 s through the **main period** and **after period**.
  6. Determine $\Delta T$ with Pfaundler / cooling correction; rinse bomb and titrate for acid.
  7. Apply the energy balance formula (Section 3.1) to obtain GCV; subtract the water-from-hydrogen term to get NCV.

#### Model Solution to (b):
**Given:**
- $m_f = 0.75$ g, $W = 2200$ g, $M_w = 2400$ g
- $T_1 = 24.6$ ┬░C, $T_2 = 27.8$ ┬░C, $\Delta T_{raw} = 3.2$ ┬░C
- $m_{fw} = 0.05$ g, $CV_{fw} = 1600$ cal/g
- $q_{acid} = 15$ cal, $C = 8$ cal, $H = 4.5$ %

**Step 1:** Total heat capacity
$$W + M_w = 2200 + 2400 = 4600 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2:** Corrected temperature rise
$$\Delta T = 3.2 + 0.008 = 3.208\ ^\circ\text{C}$$
*(For a 7-mark question, full Pfaundler graph is not required; simply add $C$ to $\Delta T$ as the examiner expects the data reduction form.)*

**Step 3:** Heat absorbed
$$Q_{abs} = 4600 \times 3.208 = 14{,}756.8 \text{ cal}$$

**Step 4:** Heat from fuse wire
$$q_{fuse} = 0.05 \times 1600 = 80 \text{ cal}$$

**Step 5:** GCV Calculation
$$GCV = \dfrac{Q_{abs} - q_{fuse} - q_{acid} - C}{m_f}$$

$$GCV = \dfrac{14{,}756.8 - 80 - 15 - 8}{0.75}$$

$$GCV = \dfrac{14{,}653.8}{0.75} = 19{,}538.4 \text{ cal/g}$$

$$GCV = 19{,}538.4 \text{ kcal/kg} \approx 19{,}538 \text{ kcal/kg}$$

**Step 6:** NCV Calculation
$$NCV = GCV - 0.09 \times H \times 587$$

$$NCV = 19{,}538.4 - 0.09 \times 4.5 \times 587$$

$$NCV = 19{,}538.4 - 237.735 = 19{,}300.66 \text{ kcal/kg}$$

$$NCV \approx 19{,}300.66 \text{ kcal/kg}$$

**Final Results:**
- **GCV тЙИ 19,538 kcal/kg** (тЙИ 81,778 kJ/kg)
- **NCV тЙИ 19,300.66 kcal/kg** (тЙИ 80,773 kJ/kg)

---

### тЭУ Question Choice B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified 2024 Scheme]`

> **(a)** Explain the **Pfaundler cooling correction** with the help of a labelled timeтАУtemperature graph. Why is this correction necessary in a bomb calorimeter experiment? **7 marks**

> **(b)** A liquid fuel sample of mass **0.62 g** is tested in a bomb calorimeter. The water equivalent is **2100 g** and water in the bucket is **2600 g**. The temperature rose from **26.2 ┬░C to 29.4 ┬░C** with a cooling correction of **5 cal**. The fuse wire (NiтАУCr, $CV = 335$ cal/g) used was **0.03 g** and acid correction is **10 cal**. The fuel contains **12 % H** by mass. Determine (i) the GCV in kcal/kg and (ii) the NCV in kJ/kg. **7 marks**

#### Model Solution to (a):
**Pfaundler Cooling Correction (3.5 marks):**
- The bomb calorimeter cannot be made perfectly adiabatic; heat is exchanged with the outer water jacket, which is maintained at *room temperature* ($T_r$).
- A timeтАУtemperature graph has three distinct regions:
  - **Fore Period** (AB): The initial drift before ignition. Slope gives $n_1$ (rate of temperature change, ┬░C/min).
  - **Main Period** (BC): Rapid temperature rise due to combustion, taking time $t$ minutes.
  - **After Period** (CD): Final cooling drift. Slope gives $n_2$.
- The Pfaundler formula corrects the *observed* $\Delta T$ for heat lost during the main period:
  $$C = n \times (T_{mean} - T_r) \times t$$
  where $n$ is the mean cooling constant, $T_{mean}$ is the average of initial and final temperatures, and $t$ is the duration of the main period in minutes.
- The **corrected temperature rise** used in the GCV formula is:
  $$\Delta T_{corrected} = (T_2 - T_1) + C$$
  where $T_1$ is the temperature at ignition, $T_2$ is the maximum temperature reached.

**Why the Correction is Necessary (3.5 marks):**
- Without this correction, the calculated GCV would be *erroneously high* (apparent heat content > actual), because some of the heat is leaked to the jacket during the rapid temperature-rise phase.
- The Pfaundler correction mathematically *adds back* the lost heat so that the energy balance is satisfied, ensuring the computed GCV matches the *true* heat released by the fuel. KTU examiners expect this correction to be **explicitly stated and applied** in numerical problems.

#### Model Solution to (b):
**Given:**
- $m_f = 0.62$ g, $W = 2100$ g, $M_w = 2600$ g
- $T_1 = 26.2$ ┬░C, $T_2 = 29.4$ ┬░C, $C = 5$ cal
- $m_{fw} = 0.03$ g, $CV_{fw} = 335$ cal/g
- $q_{acid} = 10$ cal, $H = 12$ %

**Step 1:** Total heat capacity
$$W + M_w = 2100 + 2600 = 4700 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2:** Corrected temperature rise
$$\Delta T = (29.4 - 26.2) + \dfrac{5}{4700} = 3.2 + 0.00106 \approx 3.201\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 3:** Heat absorbed
$$Q_{abs} = 4700 \times 3.201 = 15{,}044.7 \text{ cal}$$

**Step 4:** Heat from fuse wire
$$q_{fuse} = 0.03 \times 335 = 10.05 \text{ cal}$$

**Step 5:** GCV in cal/g
$$GCV = \dfrac{15{,}044.7 - 10.05 - 10 - 5}{0.62} = \dfrac{15{,}019.65}{0.62} = 24{,}225.24 \text{ cal/g}$$

**Step 6:** Convert to kcal/kg
$$GCV = 24{,}225.24 \text{ kcal/kg}$$

**Step 7:** Convert to kJ/kg (for reporting)
$$GCV = 24{,}225.24 \times 4.186 = 101{,}406.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 8:** NCV in cal/g
$$NCV = 24{,}225.24 - 0.09 \times 12 \times 587$$

$$NCV = 24{,}225.24 - 633.96 = 23{,}591.28 \text{ cal/g}$$

**Step 9:** Convert NCV to kJ/kg
$$NCV = 23{,}591.28 \times 4.186 = 98{,}754.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Final Results:**
- **GCV тЙИ 24,225 kcal/kg** тЙИ **101,407 kJ/kg**
- **NCV тЙИ 23,591 kcal/kg** тЙИ **98,754 kJ/kg**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls тАФ Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cooling correction ($C$)** тАФ examiner deducts 1 full mark.
> 2. **Wrong unit conversion** between cal/g and kcal/kg (1 cal/g = 1 kcal/kg; many students apply an erroneous factor of 1000).
> 3. **Not subtracting the acid correction** тАФ students who skip the titration step lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Using $H$ in decimal form (0.12) instead of percentage (12)** in the NCV formula тАФ silently gives the wrong answer.
> 5. **Mixing up GCV and NCV** in the final statement; examiners cross-check the relation $GCV > NCV$.
> 6. **Omitting the labelled diagram** in 7-mark construction questions тАФ costs 2тАУ3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Bomb Calorimetry (KTU ESE-ready):**

- **Aim:** Determine the calorific value (GCV and NCV) of a solid/liquid fuel under standard conditions.

- **Apparatus:** Bomb, crucible, electrodes, fuse wire, calorimeter vessel, water bath, stirrer, thermometer, oxygen cylinder, ignition circuit.

- **Working Principle:** Combustion in pure OтВВ (тЙИ 25тАУ30 atm) тЖТ heat absorbed by water + apparatus тЖТ temperature rise measured тЖТ energy balance yields GCV.

- **Master Formula:**
  $$GCV = \dfrac{(W + M_w)\,\Delta T_{\text{corrected}} - q_{fuse} - q_{acid} - C}{m_{fuel}}$$

- **Water Equivalent (W):** Determined using **benzoic acid** ($CV = 6328$ cal/g); $W = \dfrac{m_{ba} \times 6328}{\Delta T} - M_w$.

- **GCV тЖТ NCV Conversion:**
  $$NCV = GCV - 0.09 \times H \times 587 \quad \text{(cal/g)}$$
  $$NCV = GCV - 2.442 \times H \quad \text{(kcal/kg, H in \%)}$$

- **Cooling Correction (Pfaundler):** $C = n\,(T_{mean} - T_r)\,t$ тАФ always add to $\Delta T$.

- **Acid Correction:** HтВВSOтВД тЖТ 13,600 cal/eq; HNOтВГ тЖТ 14,200 cal/eq; determined by titration with standard NaOH/NaтВВCOтВГ.

- **Standard Mass of Sample:** 0.5тАУ1.0 g (solid) or 0.5тАУ0.8 mL (liquid).

- **Typical Result Range:** Coal: 6,000тАУ9,000 kcal/kg; Petrol/Diesel: 10,500тАУ11,500 kcal/kg; Natural gas: ~8,500тАУ9,500 kcal/Nm┬│.

- **Unit Conversions (must be fluent):**
  - $1 \text{ cal/g} = 1 \text{ kcal/kg} = 4.186 \text{ kJ/kg}$
  - $1 \text{ Btu/lb} = 2.326 \text{ kJ/kg}$

- **Sources of Error:** Incomplete combustion, heat losses, fuse wire / acid mis-correction, moisture in sample, CO formation.

- **Engineering Relevance:** Coal grading, fuel-pricing in thermal power plants, IC-engine fuel-tank sizing, rocket Isp calculations, biofuel certification.

- **Common Confusions:**
  - Bomb Calorimeter gives **GCV** (not NCV directly).
  - GCV is for **constant volume**; not constant pressure (which is the standard enthalpy of combustion).
  - Higher hydrogen content тЖТ larger GCVтАУNCV gap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Mantra for the KTU Lab Exam:** Always include тАФ (1) labelled diagram, (2) formula with all 4 corrections, (3) final GCV and NCV in both **cal/g and kcal/kg**, and (4) a brief significance statement comparing your value with standard fuel ranges. This guarantees full marks on the experiment record and viva.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of calorific value of gaseous fuels using gas calorimeter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMEL507 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-1 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Determination of calorific value of gaseous fuels using gas calorimeter # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Calorific Value (CV)** of a fuel is defined as the **total amount of heat liberated** by the **complete combustion of a unit mass (or unit volume, in the case of gaseous fuels) of the fuel**, when the products of combustion are cooled back to the **initial temperature of the fuel and air**, and **all the water vapour formed during combustion is condensed** (for Gross/Superior CV) or remains as vapour (for Net/Inferior CV). For **gaseous fuels**, the calorific value is expressed in **kJ/m┬│ (or kJ/Nm┬│)** since the mass of a gas varies drastically with temperature and pressure, making a **volume basis** the standard industrial practice. A **Gas Calorimeter** is a precision instrument designed to experimentally measure this heat release by burning a known volume of gas and transferring the liberated heat to a metered stream of water. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The JunkerтАЩs Continuous-Flow Gas Calorimeter is the **de-facto standard** prescribed in the KTU Thermal Engineering Lab-I syllabus because of its high accuracy, continuous operation, and excellent water-gas counter-flow heat exchange. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ The "Tea-Cooler" Intuition Imagine you are boiling water for tea using a **LPG stove**. The stove releases chemical energy locked inside the gas. Now imagine a strict laboratory scientist watching you: - The scientist wants to know **exactly how much energy is in the gas**. - They cannot use a thermometer in the flame (the flame is 1,800┬░C тАФ dangerous and impractical). - Instead, they wrap the flame inside a sealed box with **copper pipes carrying cold water** flowing through it. - The flame heats the copper pipes, which heat the water. - By measuring: **(a) how much water flowed through, (b) how much hotter the water got, and (c) how much gas was burned**, the scientist can back-calculate the energy released. This is precisely the **JunkerтАЩs Gas Calorimeter** in essence тАФ a beautifully engineered тАЬheat exchanger that counts calories.тАЭ The gas burns inside, water flows around in counter-flow, and the energy balance yields the calorific value. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Constants Used in Gas Calorimetry:** > - **Specific heat of water, $C_p$ = 4.186 kJ/(kg┬╖K)** (or 4.18 kJ/kg┬╖K for engineering approximations) > - **Density of water at standard conditions, $\rho_w$ = 1000 kg/m┬│** > - **Standard atmospheric pressure, $P_{atm}$ = 101.325 kPa = 760 mm Hg = 1.01325 bar** > - **Standard reference temperature, $T_{ref}$ = 288.15 K (15┬░C)** for gas volume correction > - **Latent heat of vaporisation of water at 25┬░C, $h_{fg}$ тЙИ 2442 kJ/kg** > - **Universal Gas Constant, $R_u$ = 8.314 kJ/(kmol┬╖K)** ## 1.4 Classification of Calorific Values | Type | Symbol | Definition | Water Vapour State | |------|--------|------------|---------------------| | **Gross / Higher CV** | **GCV / HCV** | Heat released with water condensed | Liquid | | **Net / Lower CV** | **NCV / LCV** | Heat released with water as vapour | Vapour | > [!NOTE] > **GCV > NCV** always. The difference is the latent heat of the water formed during combustion: > $$NCV = GCV - 9 \cdot H_2 \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \rho_v / 1000$$ > (a more rigorous form is shown in Section 2). ## 1.5 Types of Gas Calorimeters (Overview) 1. **JunkerтАЩs Continuous-Flow Gas Calorimeter** тАФ *Most accurate, KTU-prescribed.* 2. **BoyтАЩs Gas Calorimeter** тАФ *Older, less accurate, uses latent heat principle.* 3. **Rosenhain Calorimeter** тАФ *Uses a recording mechanism for continuous CV monitoring of town gas.* 4. **Fairweather Recording Calorimeter** тАФ *Industrial flow type for gas works.* > [!NOTE] > Among all, **JunkerтАЩs calorimeter is the laboratory workhorse** and the only one that will appear in KTU viva/practical examinations for PCMEL507. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle тАФ The Science Behind JunkerтАЩs Calorimeter The JunkerтАЩs calorimeter is a **steady-state, continuous-flow, counter-flow heat exchanger** designed so that the only major heat loss is via the **flue gases** (which leave at room temperature if operated correctly). ### Step-by-Step Operational Logic: 1. **Gas Inlet & Metering:** Gaseous fuel is fed through a **wet gas meter** (measures volume at line conditions) into the apparatus. 2. **Combustion:** The gas burns in a **Bunsen-type burner** with stoichiometric air, producing a hot flame. 3. **Heat Transfer Stage:** The hot combustion products traverse a **series of concentric copper tubes** (heat exchanger coils). Cold water flows over these tubes in the **opposite direction (counter-flow)**. 4. **Counter-flow Advantage:** Maximum mean temperature difference is achieved тЖТ high heat recovery (>95%). 5. **Energy Balance at Steady State:** Heat released by gas = Heat absorbed by water + Heat lost in flue gases. For an ideally designed calorimeter, flue gas exit temperature тЙИ water inlet temperature, so losses become negligible. 6. **Condensation Trap:** A **drip trap** at the flue exit collects any condensed water vapour. The JunkerтАЩs calorimeter is designed to be **slightly overcooled** so that ALL water vapour condenses тАФ hence the value measured is **GCV (Gross Calorific Value)**. ## 2.2 Constructional Details > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Components to Remember for KTU Viva:** > - **Outer Casing:** Cylindrical vessel, double-walled, gas-tight. > - **Bunsen Burner:** At the bottom; provides complete combustion with excess air. > - **Copper Helical Coils:** Concentric arrangement maximises surface area for heat transfer. > - **Inlet & Outlet Water Thermometers:** Precision thermometers (┬▒0.1┬░C accuracy). > - **Inlet & Outlet Water Manometers:** Used to measure water flow rate using a calibrated orifice/meter. > - **Condensate Drip Trap:** Collects condensed water vapour from flue. > - **Flue Gas Outlet:** Side wall тАФ exit gas should be near room temperature. > - **Gas Flow Control Valve:** Adjusts gas flow rate for steady-state operation. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | **Heat absorbed by water** | $Q_w = m_w \cdot C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/min or kJ/s | $T_2$ = outlet, $T_1$ = inlet | | 2 | **Gross Calorific Value (as measured)** | $GCV = \dfrac{m_w \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T}{V_g}$ | kJ/m┬│ | Primary result | | 3 | **Corrected Gas Volume (SATP)** | $V_c = V_g \cdot \dfrac{(P_{atm} - P_{w,vapour})}{(P_{atm} - P_{s,vapour})} \cdot \dfrac{273}{273 + T_g}$ | m┬│ | $P_{s,vapour}$ = saturation pressure at gas meter temp | | 4 | **Net Calorific Value** | $NCV = GCV - \dfrac{m_{vapour} \cdot h_{fg}}{V_c}$ | kJ/m┬│ | $m_{vapour}$ = mass of water formed per m┬│ of gas | | 5 | **Water Mass Flow Rate** | $m_w = \rho_w \cdot Q_w$ | kg/min | $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m┬│ | | 6 | **Flue gas loss correction (Boy's)** | $L_{fg} = \dfrac{m_{fg} \cdot C_{p,fg} \cdot (T_{fg} - T_{ref})}{V_g}$ | kJ/m┬│ | If not operating ideally | | 7 | **Radiation Loss** | $Q_{rad} = K \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{surf}$ | kJ | Empirical; often ignored if well-insulated | | 8 | **Density of gas (ideal gas law)** | $\rho_g = \dfrac{P \cdot M}{R_u \cdot T}$ | kg/m┬│ | For mass-based NCV calculations | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL LaTeX Pitfall Avoidance:** In the above table, all absolute-value or magnitude expressions have been written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\Delta(\cdot)$ тАФ **never using a raw vertical pipe `|`** to avoid markdown-table parsing failures. ## 2.4 Step-by-Step Procedure (For Practical Record / Lab Manual) 1. **Inspect** the calorimeter тАФ ensure no soot deposits, all thermometers calibrated, drip trap empty. 2. **Light the burner** and allow 5тАУ10 minutes for steady-state. 3. **Set water flow** to a steady rate (typically 2тАУ6 L/min). 4. **Wait** for steady-state: $T_1$ and $T_2$ become constant; gas meter reading is steady. 5. **Start the stopwatch** and simultaneously record: - Initial gas meter reading - $T_1$ (water inlet, ┬░C) - $T_2$ (water outlet, ┬░C) - Water manometer reading (for flow rate) - Gas temperature near meter - Barometric pressure 6. **Run** for exactly 2тАУ5 minutes. 7. **Stop** and record final gas meter reading, condensate volume (if measured). 8. **Repeat** the experiment 2тАУ3 times for accuracy. 9. **Apply gas volume correction** for temperature, pressure, and humidity. 10. **Calculate GCV** using the formula; convert to **NCV** if required. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why This Experiment Matters in Industry:** > - **Billing of natural gas & LPG:** Commercial gas tariffs are based on GCV (kJ/m┬│ or BTU/SCF). > - **Boiler efficiency calculations:** Calorific value is the denominator тАФ even 1% error in CV cascades into 5% error in efficiency. > - **Combustion system design:** Stoichiometric air, flame temperature, and emissions all depend on CV. > - **Gas turbine & IC engine fuel blending:** Hydrogen-enriched fuels have very different NCV/GCV ratios. > - **Energy auditing & ISO 50001 compliance:** Mandatory for industrial facilities. ## 2.6 Sources of Error & Precautions | Source of Error | Effect | Mitigation | |-----------------|--------|------------| | Incomplete combustion | Lowers apparent GCV | Maintain blue flame; avoid yellow tip | | Flue gas not cooled to room temp | Heat loss тЖТ under-estimate GCV | Adjust water flow; check $T_{fg}$ | | Heat loss to surroundings | Under-estimate GCV | Insulate calorimeter; short runs | | Soot deposition on coils | Reduces heat transfer | Clean coils before experiment | | Gas meter calibration drift | Volume error | Periodic calibration | | Reading $T_1$ and $T_2$ at wrong points | Random error | Use calibrated thermometers, read at eye level | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Fundamental Energy Balance тАФ The Master Equation > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite:** The examiner loves asking students to *derive* the calorific value formula. Memorise this derivation тАФ it appears in 90% of viva questions. At **steady state**, the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a control volume enclosing the calorimeter gives: $$ Q_{fuel} = Q_{water} + Q_{losses} $$ Where: - $Q_{fuel}$ = Total heat released by burning $V_g$ m┬│ of gas = $V_g \cdot CV$ - $Q_{water}$ = Heat absorbed by the cooling water = $m_w \cdot C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$ - $Q_{losses}$ = Heat lost via flue gases, radiation, and unburnt fuel For an **ideally designed JunkerтАЩs calorimeter** operating near room temperature, $Q_{losses} \rightarrow 0$. Therefore: $$ V_g \cdot CV = m_w \cdot C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1) $$ Rearranging for the calorific value: $$ CV = \frac{m_w \cdot C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)}{V_g} \quad \text{[kJ/m}^3\text{]} $$ > This is the **single most important equation** in the entire experiment. It is the formula the lab record-book is graded on. ## 3.2 Derivation of Gas Volume Correction The wet gas meter reads gas volume at **line conditions** ($P_g$, $T_g$, saturated with water vapour). To compare with the **standard reference condition** ($P_{ref} = 760$ mm Hg, $T_{ref} = 288.15$ K = 15┬░C, dry gas), we use the **Combined Gas Law** with humidity correction. ### Step 1: Dry gas partial pressure at meter The gas meter measures the gas saturated with water vapour. The actual pressure of **dry gas** is: $$ P_{dry} = P_{atm} - P_{s,vapour} $$ Where $P_{s,vapour}$ is the saturation vapour pressure at the gas meter temperature $T_g$. ### Step 2: Apply Ideal Gas Law For the dry gas, using $PV = nRT$ at constant moles: $$ \frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2} $$ Where state 1 = meter conditions, state 2 = SATP (standard ambient temperature & pressure). ### Step 3: Solve for $V_{SATP}$ $$ V_{SATP} = V_g \cdot \frac{P_{dry}}{P_{ref}} \cdot \frac{T_{ref}}{T_g} $$ Substituting $P_{dry} = P_{atm} - P_{s,vapour}$: $$ V_{SATP} = V_g \cdot \frac{(P_{atm} - P_{s,vapour})}{(P_{ref})} \cdot \frac{273}{(273 + T_g)} $$ Where $T_g$ is in ┬░C and $P_{atm}$ and $P_{s,vapour}$ are in the same units as $P_{ref}$. ### Step 4: Corrected Calorific Value $$ \boxed{GCV_{corrected} = \frac{m_w \cdot C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)}{V_{SATP}}} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation of NCV from GCV > [!IMPORTANT] > **Concept:** The difference between GCV and NCV lies in the **physical state of the water formed during combustion**. For a fuel containing $H_2$ (hydrogen) and water vapour already present: For **1 m┬│ of gas** at SATP: - Mass of water formed from HтВВ combustion = $9 \cdot H_2 \cdot \rho_{vapour}$ (where $H_2$ is the volume fraction) - Latent heat not recovered when water stays as vapour = $m_{vapour} \cdot h_{fg}$ Therefore: $$ NCV = GCV - (9 \cdot H_2) \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \rho_{vapour} $$ In a more general, **elemental-mass based** form (assuming gas of molecular weight $M$): $$ NCV = GCV - \frac{h_{fg} \cdot \text{(mass of H}_2\text{O formed per m}^3\text{)}}{1} $$ For a **typical natural gas** with $\sim 90\%$ CHтВД by volume: - 1 m┬│ of CHтВД тЖТ 2 m┬│ of HтВВO (when condensed at SATP) - Mass of water тЙИ 1.61 kg - Latent heat loss тЙИ 1.61 ├Ч 2442 тЙИ 3931 kJ/m┬│ - So **NCV тЙИ GCV тИТ 3930 kJ/m┬│** for pure methane. ## 3.4 Full Worked Numerical Example тАФ KTU Style > [!NOTE] > **Sample Problem (Model Paper Standard):** > In a JunkerтАЩs gas calorimeter test, the following observations were recorded: > - Mass of water flowing through the calorimeter: $m_w$ = **4.2 kg/min** > - Water inlet temperature: $T_1$ = **22.5┬░C** > - Water outlet temperature: $T_2$ = **36.8┬░C** > - Gas consumed in 3 minutes: $V_g$ = **0.135 m┬│** (at meter conditions) > - Gas temperature at meter: $T_g$ = **28┬░C** > - Barometric pressure: $P_{atm}$ = **755 mm Hg** > - Saturation vapour pressure at 28┬░C: $P_{s,vapour}$ = **28.35 mm Hg** > > **Calculate:** (a) The Gross Calorific Value (GCV) as measured, (b) The corrected GCV at SATP, and (c) Net Calorific Value (NCV) assuming fuel is pure methane (CHтВД). ### Part (a): GCV as measured **Step 1: Calculate temperature rise** $$ \Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 36.8 - 22.5 = 14.3 \text{ ┬░C (= K, since difference)} $$ **Step 2: Calculate heat absorbed by water per minute** $$ Q_w = m_w \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 4.2 \times 4.18 \times 14.3 $$ $$ Q_w = 251.05 \text{ kJ/min} $$ **Step 3: Calculate gas consumption per minute** $$ \dot{V}_g = \frac{V_g}{\text{time}} = \frac{0.135}{3} = 0.045 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} $$ **Step 4: Calculate GCV (as measured)** $$ GCV_{measured} = \frac{Q_w}{\dot{V}_g} = \frac{251.05}{0.045} = 5578.9 \text{ kJ/m}^3 $$ ### Part (b): Corrected GCV at SATP **Step 5: Apply gas volume correction** $$ V_{SATP} = V_g \cdot \frac{(P_{atm} - P_{s,vapour})}{P_{ref}} \cdot \frac{273}{(273 + T_g)} $$ $$ V_{SATP} = 0.135 \times \frac{(755 - 28.35)}{760} \times \frac{273}{(273 + 28)} $$ $$ V_{SATP} = 0.135 \times \frac{726.65}{760} \times \frac{273}{301} $$ $$ V_{SATP} = 0.135 \times 0.9561 \times 0.9070 = 0.1171 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 6: Corrected gas flow rate** $$ \dot{V}_{SATP} = \frac{0.1171}{3} = 0.03903 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} $$ **Step 7: Corrected GCV** $$ GCV_{SATP} = \frac{251.05}{0.03903} = 6432.5 \text{ kJ/m}^3 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** The corrected GCV is **higher** than the as-measured GCV because the gas meter was reading a larger volume at higher temperature and lower pressure тАФ i.e., the **same energy is spread over a larger measured volume**, so dividing by it gives a lower number. The correction "concentrates" the gas back to standard conditions. ### Part (c): NCV for pure methane **Step 8: Combustion reaction** $$ CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O $$ **Step 9: Mass of water produced per m┬│ of methane** 1 mole of CHтВД (22.4 L at STP) produces 2 moles of HтВВO (36 g). So per m┬│ = 1000 L: $$ n_{CH_4} = \frac{1000}{22.4} = 44.64 \text{ mol} $$ $$ m_{H_2O} = 44.64 \times 2 \times 18 = 1607.1 \text{ g} = 1.607 \text{ kg per m}^3 $$ **Step 10: Latent heat loss** $$ Q_{latent} = m_{H_2O} \cdot h_{fg} = 1.607 \times 2442 = 3924.3 \text{ kJ/m}^3 $$ **Step 11: NCV** $$ NCV = GCV_{SATP} - Q_{latent} = 6432.5 - 3924.3 = 2508.2 \text{ kJ/m}^3 $$ ### Result Summary Table | Quantity | Value | Unit | |----------|-------|------| | GCV (as measured) | 5578.9 | kJ/m┬│ | | GCV (corrected to SATP) | 6432.5 | kJ/m┬│ | | NCV (pure methane assumption) | 2508.2 | kJ/m┬│ | ## 3.5 Comparison Table: Typical GCV Values of Gaseous Fuels | Gaseous Fuel | GCV (kJ/m┬│) | NCV (kJ/m┬│) | Density (kg/m┬│) | Main Component | |--------------|-------------|-------------|------------------|----------------| | Natural Gas | 38,000 тАУ 40,000 | 34,000 тАУ 36,000 | 0.7 тАУ 0.85 | CHтВД (~90%) | | LPG (vapour) | 92,000 тАУ 95,000 | 87,000 тАУ 88,000 | 1.8 тАУ 2.0 | CтВГHтВИ/CтВДHтВБтВА | | Producer Gas | 5,000 тАУ 6,000 | 4,800 тАУ 5,800 | 1.0 тАУ 1.2 | CO + NтВВ | | Water Gas | 10,000 тАУ 12,000 | 9,500 тАУ 11,500 | 0.7 тАУ 0.8 | CO + HтВВ | | Coal Gas (Town Gas) | 18,000 тАУ 20,000 | 16,500 тАУ 18,500 | 0.55 тАУ 0.65 | HтВВ + CHтВД | | Biogas | 20,000 тАУ 25,000 | 18,000 тАУ 22,000 | 1.0 тАУ 1.2 | CHтВД + COтВВ | | Hydrogen | 12,750 | 10,800 | 0.09 | HтВВ | > [!NOTE] > The result of the worked example (GCV тЙИ 6432 kJ/m┬│) suggests a **producer gas** or **low-grade fuel gas** тАФ not natural gas. The student must always **interpret** their result against this table in the viva. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram тАФ JunkerтАЩs Gas Calorimeter Functional Architecture > The following block diagram maps the **functional flow of mass, energy, and information** through the calorimeter.
