Why is GCV greater than NCV?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **GCV (Gross/Superior Calorific Value):** The total amount of heat liberated by the **complete combustion of 1 m┬│ of gaseous fuel** at STP, with combustion products cooled to the **initial temperature** of fuel and air, and with **all water vapour condensed** to liquid. > > **NCV (Net/Inferior Calorific Value):** The total amount of heat liberated by the complete combustion of 1 m┬│ of gaseous fuel at STP, with combustion products cooled to the initial temperature, but with **water vapour allowed to remain as vapour** (not condensed). > > **Reason GCV > NCV:** During combustion, hydrogen in the fuel produces water vapour. To condense this water vapour back to liquid, **latent heat of vaporisation (тЙИ 2442 kJ/kg at 25┬░C)** must be released. In NCV, this latent heat is *not* recovered because the water remains as vapour and is lost with the flue gases. Therefore, NCV = GCV тИТ (latent heat of water formed per m┬│ of gas). *(Mark distribution: 1 mark for each definition + 1 mark for the comparison reason.)* --- ### Question A2: **[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2022]** **With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of JunkerтАЩs gas calorimeter.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Principle:** JunkerтАЩs gas calorimeter works on the principle of **steady-state heat transfer** in a counter-flow heat exchanger, where heat released by the combustion of a known volume of gaseous fuel is absorbed by a known mass flow rate of cooling water, and the calorific value is calculated from an energy balance. > > **Construction (1 mark):** It consists of a cylindrical outer casing containing concentric **copper helical coils**. A **Bunsen burner** is fitted at the bottom, and a **condensate drip trap** at the side flue outlet. **Inlet/outlet thermometers** measure water temperatures, and a **wet gas meter** measures gas volume. > > **Working (1 mark):** The gas burns in the Bunsen burner, hot combustion gases pass upward through the copper coils, transferring heat to cooling water flowing downward (counter-flow). At steady state, the water temperature rise ($\Delta T = T_2 - T_1$) is constant. The calorific value is calculated as: > $$GCV = \frac{m_w \cdot C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)}{V_g}$$ > Since the calorimeter is designed to over-cool slightly, all water vapour condenses тЖТ **measured value is GCV**. --- ## 5.2 PART B тАФ Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulation, students answer ONE full 14-mark question per module. Below are **TWO independent alternatives** (Question A and Question B) as internal-choice options. --- ### тнР Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023 Style]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **Gross Calorific Value of a gaseous fuel** using the energy balance of a JunkerтАЩs gas calorimeter. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a JunkerтАЩs gas calorimeter test, the following observations were made: **(7 marks)** - Mass of cooling water: $m_w$ = **3.6 kg/min** - Inlet water temperature: $T_1$ = **20┬░C** - Outlet water temperature: $T_2$ = **34┬░C** - Volume of gas burnt in 4 minutes: $V_g$ = **0.18 m┬│** at meter conditions - Gas meter temperature: $T_g$ = **30┬░C** - Barometric pressure: $P_{atm}$ = **752 mm Hg** - Saturation vapour pressure at 30┬░C: $P_{s,vapour}$ = **31.82 mm Hg** Calculate: **(i)** GCV as measured, **(ii)** GCV corrected to SATP, and **(iii)** NCV assuming fuel is pure methane. --- #### Model Solution to Question A: **Part (a) тАФ Derivation (7 marks):** **Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. Combustion is **complete** тАФ all carbon тЖТ COтВВ, all hydrogen тЖТ HтВВO. 2. **Steady-state** operation тАФ temperatures are constant with time. 3. **Heat losses** to surroundings and via flue gases are **negligible** (justified by the counter-flow design which cools flue gases to room temperature). 4. No condensation of water vapour occurs in the gas meter line (saturated condition only at meter). 5. Cooling water properties (density, $C_p$) are constant over the temperature range. **Energy Balance (3 marks):** Consider a control volume enclosing the calorimeter at steady state. **Heat released by gas** = $V_g \cdot GCV$ (kJ) **Heat absorbed by water** = $m_w \cdot C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)$ (kJ) By **First Law of Thermodynamics** at steady state (no accumulation, no work transfer): $$ Q_{in} = Q_{out} $$ $$ V_g \cdot GCV = m_w \cdot C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1) + Q_{losses} $$ Since the JunkerтАЩs calorimeter is well-designed and the flue gas exits near room temperature, $Q_{losses} \approx 0$. Therefore: $$ \boxed{GCV = \frac{m_w \cdot C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)}{V_g}} $$ **Discussion of correction (2 marks):** The gas meter reads gas volume at line conditions ($T_g$, $P_{atm}$, saturated). To convert to **SATP** (Standard Ambient: 15┬░C, 760 mm Hg, dry), the gas volume correction formula is applied: $$ V_{SATP} = V_g \cdot \frac{(P_{atm} - P_{s,vapour})}{P_{ref}} \cdot \frac{273}{(273 + T_g)} $$ The corrected GCV is then: $$ GCV_{SATP} = \frac{m_w \cdot C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)}{V_{SATP}} $$ **Part (b) тАФ Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) GCV as measured (2 marks):** $$ \Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 34 - 20 = 14 \text{ K} $$ $$ Q_w = m_w \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 3.6 \times 4.18 \times 14 = 210.67 \text{ kJ/min} $$ $$ \dot{V}_g = \frac{0.18}{4} = 0.045 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} $$ $$ GCV_{measured} = \frac{210.67}{0.045} = 4681.6 \text{ kJ/m}^3 $$ **(ii) Corrected GCV at SATP (3 marks):** $$ V_{SATP} = 0.18 \times \frac{(752 - 31.82)}{760} \times \frac{273}{(273 + 30)} $$ $$ V_{SATP} = 0.18 \times \frac{720.18}{760} \times \frac{273}{303} $$ $$ V_{SATP} = 0.18 \times 0.9476 \times 0.9010 = 0.1536 \text{ m}^3 $$ $$ \dot{V}_{SATP} = \frac{0.1536}{4} = 0.0384 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} $$ $$ GCV_{SATP} = \frac{210.67}{0.0384} = 5486.2 \text{ kJ/m}^3 $$ **(iii) NCV for pure methane (2 marks):** For CHтВД + 2OтВВ тЖТ COтВВ + 2HтВВO: 1 m┬│ of CHтВД at SATP тЖТ 2 m┬│ of HтВВO (vapour) at SATP. Mass of water = $\dfrac{2 \times 18}{22.4 \times 10^{-3}} \times 10^{-3} = 1.607$ kg/m┬│ (using STP molar volume). Wait тАФ at SATP, molar volume is тЙИ 23.69 L/mol. Let me recompute: $$ n_{CH_4} = \frac{1 \text{ m}^3}{23.69 \text{ L/mol}} = 42.21 \text{ mol} $$ $$ m_{H_2O} = 42.21 \times 2 \times 18 = 1519.6 \text{ g} \approx 1.52 \text{ kg/m}^3 $$ Using $h_{fg}$ at 25┬░C = 2442 kJ/kg: $$ Q_{latent} = 1.52 \times 2442 = 3711.8 \text{ kJ/m}^3 $$ $$ NCV = GCV_{SATP} - Q_{latent} = 5486.2 - 3711.8 = 1774.4 \text{ kJ/m}^3 $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU ExaminerтАЩs Valuation Pitfall тАФ Part (b):** > - **Most common mistake (тАУ1 mark):** Forgetting to convert the **3-minute total volume** to **per-minute volume** before dividing. Always work in **consistent time basis** (per minute or per second). > - **тАУ1 mark:** Mixing up $P_{ref}$ (760 mm Hg) with $P_{atm}$ тАФ students often write $\frac{P_{atm}}{P_{atm}}$ and get correction = 1, which is wrong. The numerator must use the **dry-gas partial pressure**, not total barometric pressure. > - **тАУ1 mark:** Using $\Delta T$ in ┬░C and treating it as numerically different from K. In a **temperature *difference***, ┬░C and K are interchangeable, but you must write one explicitly. > - **тАУ0.5 mark:** Omitting the units in the final answer. --- ### тнР Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024 Style]** **(a)** What are the **different types of gas calorimeters** used in practice? Compare JunkerтАЩs and BoyтАЩs calorimeters on the basis of principle, accuracy, and application. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A **producer gas** sample is tested in a JunkerтАЩs calorimeter. The following data is recorded: **(7 marks)** - Mass of water: $m_w$ = **5.0 kg/min** - $T_1$ = **18┬░C**, $T_2$ = **30┬░C** - Gas flow rate at meter: $\dot{V}_g$ = **0.06 m┬│/min** - Gas temperature: $T_g$ = **27┬░C** - Barometric pressure: $P_{atm}$ = **758 mm Hg** - $P_{s,vapour}$ at 27┬░C = **26.74 mm Hg** - Producer gas composition by volume: **CO = 30%, HтВВ = 12%, CHтВД = 4%, NтВВ = 54%** Calculate: **(i)** GCV of the producer gas (corrected to SATP), and **(ii)** theoretical NCV using the standard latent-heat method. --- #### Model Solution to Question B: **Part (a) тАФ Types & Comparison (7 marks):** **Types of Gas Calorimeters (3 marks):** 1. **JunkerтАЩs Continuous-Flow Calorimeter** тАФ most accurate, laboratory standard. 2. **BoyтАЩs Latent-Heat Calorimeter** тАФ older, uses latent heat of condensed water. 3. **Rosenhain Calorimeter** тАФ flow-type, for continuous monitoring of town gas. 4. **Fairweather Calorimeter** тАФ industrial recording calorimeter. 5. **ThomasтАЩs Recording Calorimeter** тАФ modern automatic flow calorimeter. **Comparison Table (4 marks):** | Feature | JunkerтАЩs Calorimeter | BoyтАЩs Calorimeter | |---------|----------------------|--------------------| | **Principle** | Heat absorbed by counter-flow cooling water (sensible heat) | Heat absorbed by water formed during combustion (latent heat) | | **Operation** | Continuous-flow, steady-state | Batch-type, requires weighing condensate | | **Heat Transfer** | Sensible heat of water (high $C_p$) | Latent heat of vaporisation of water | | **Accuracy** | High (┬▒1%) | Moderate (┬▒3-5%) | | **Time per test** | 5тАУ10 minutes | 30тАУ60 minutes | | **Standard use** | Labs, research, ISO testing | Older industrial, town gas works | | **Cooling required** | External water supply | Built-in water jacket | | **Measured value** | GCV (condensed) | GCV (via latent heat) | **Part (b) тАФ Numerical (7 marks):** **(i) GCV corrected to SATP (4 marks):** $$ \Delta T = 30 - 18 = 12 \text{ K} $$ $$ Q_w = 5.0 \times 4.18 \times 12 = 250.8 \text{ kJ/min} $$ $$ V_{SATP} = 0.06 \times \frac{(758 - 26.74)}{760} \times \frac{273}{(273 + 27)} $$ $$ V_{SATP} = 0.06 \times \frac{731.26}{760} \times \frac{273}{300} $$ $$ V_{SATP} = 0.06 \times 0.9622 \times 0.9100 = 0.05254 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} $$ $$ GCV_{SATP} = \frac{250.8}{0.05254} = 4773.5 \text{ kJ/m}^3 $$ **(ii) Theoretical NCV via latent heat (3 marks):** Combustion reactions for each combustible component per m┬│ of producer gas: - CO + ┬╜OтВВ тЖТ COтВВ: 0.30 m┬│ CO produces 0 m┬│ HтВВO. - HтВВ + ┬╜OтВВ тЖТ HтВВO: 0.12 m┬│ HтВВ produces 0.12 m┬│ HтВВO. - CHтВД + 2OтВВ тЖТ COтВВ + 2HтВВO: 0.04 m┬│ CHтВД produces 0.08 m┬│ HтВВO. Total water vapour formed per m┬│ of producer gas: $$ V_{H_2O} = 0.12 + 0.08 = 0.20 \text{ m}^3 $$ Moles of water = $\dfrac{0.20}{23.69 \times 10^{-3}} = 8.44$ mol per m┬│ of gas Mass of water = $8.44 \times 18 = 151.9$ g = 0.1519 kg/m┬│ Latent heat not recovered: $$ Q_{latent} = 0.1519 \times 2442 = 370.9 \text{ kJ/m}^3 $$ NCV: $$ NCV = GCV_{SATP} - Q_{latent} = 4773.5 - 370.9 = 4402.6 \text{ kJ/m}^3 $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU ExaminerтАЩs Valuation Pitfall тАФ Question B:** > - **тАУ1.5 marks (Part a):** Students often describe only **JunkerтАЩs** and forget to compare with at least one other calorimeter. KTU demands **comparison** тАФ never a standalone description. > - **тАУ1 mark (Part b):** Using the **wrong molar volume** (22.4 L for STP vs 23.69 L for SATP). KTU accepts either if used consistently, but be explicit which you are using. > - **тАУ0.5 marks:** Forgetting to **sum all sources of water** (from HтВВ and CHтВД, not just one). Always list each combustion reaction. > - **тАУ0.5 marks:** Reporting the final GCV in **kcal/m┬│** instead of **kJ/m┬│**. KTU follows SI units strictly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist тАФ Must Memorise for KTU Exam Day** ### ЁЯФС Core Formula to Memorise - $GCV = \dfrac{m_w \cdot C_p \cdot (T_2 - T_1)}{V_g}$ тАФ the **single most important equation** in this experiment. - $V_{SATP} = V_g \cdot \dfrac{(P_{atm} - P_{s,vapour})}{760} \cdot \dfrac{273}{(273 + T_g)}$ - $NCV = GCV - m_{H_2O} \cdot h_{fg}$ ### ЁЯФС Key Constants - $C_p$ of water = **4.18 kJ/(kg┬╖K)** - $h_{fg}$ at 25┬░C = **2442 kJ/kg** - Standard pressure = **760 mm Hg = 101.325 kPa** - Standard temperature = **15┬░C (288.15 K)** for SATP - Molar volume at SATP = **23.69 L/mol** ### ЁЯФС Key Facts - **JunkerтАЩs calorimeter** measures **GCV** (not NCV) because all water vapour is condensed in the heat exchanger. - **Counter-flow** heat exchange gives the highest LMTD тЖТ minimum heat loss. - **Wet gas meter** measures **saturated** gas volume тЖТ correction needed for dry gas at SATP. - **NCV < GCV** by the latent heat of water formed during combustion. - Higher **molecular weight** of gas тЖТ lower GCV per m┬│ (e.g., LPG > Natural Gas > Producer Gas). ### ЁЯФС Common Viva Questions (Asked in 90% of KTU Practical Exams) 1. *"Why is the heat exchanger made of copper?"* тАФ High thermal conductivity, easy to form into coils, resists corrosion. 2. *"Why counter-flow instead of parallel-flow?"* тАФ Counter-flow gives higher LMTD, hence more accurate heat recovery. 3. *"What does the condensate drip trap collect?"* тАФ Water formed from HтВВ combustion (and any moisture from inlet air). 4. *"Why measure gas temperature at the meter, not at the burner?"* тАФ The gas volume at the meter is the quantity divided by GCV; we need the volume at the point of measurement. 5. *"What is the difference between wet and dry gas meters?"* тАФ Wet meter saturates gas with water (correction needed); dry meter measures gas without humidity (no correction). 6. *"Name two industrial applications of gas calorimetry."* тАФ Gas billing, boiler efficiency testing, gas turbine fuel quality control. ### ЁЯФС Numerical Problem Traps - **Always** convert total volume to per-minute (or per-second) before dividing. - **Always** apply gas volume correction for **temperature, pressure, AND humidity** (three corrections combined). - **Always** state units in the final answer (kJ/m┬│ is KTU's preferred unit). - **Always** perform **2тАУ3 trials** and take the **average** (this is a practical exam norm). ### ЁЯФС Quick Reference: Typical GCV Values - Natural gas: ~38,000 kJ/m┬│ - LPG vapour: ~93,000 kJ/m┬│ - Producer gas: ~5,500 kJ/m┬│ тЖР **matches typical JunkerтАЩs lab result** - Biogas: ~22,000 kJ/m┬│ - Hydrogen: ~12,750 kJ/m┬│ > [!NOTE] > **End of Topic Recap.** You are now fully equipped to tackle any KTU 2024 Scheme question on the determination of calorific value of gaseous fuels using a gas calorimeter. Best of luck for your exam! ЁЯОУ

topics.title: Calibration of thermocouples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL507 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-1
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of thermocouples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermocouple Calibration** is the experimental procedure of establishing the precise functional relationship between the **thermoelectric EMF (in mV or ╬╝V)** generated by a thermocouple and the corresponding **temperature (in ┬░C or K)** of its measuring (hot) junction, with the reference (cold) junction maintained at a fixed known temperature (conventionally 0 ┬░C using an ice bath).

A **thermocouple** is a temperature-measuring transducer consisting of two dissimilar thermoelectric conductors joined at one end (the *hot junction* or *measuring junction*) with the other two ends maintained at a lower known temperature (the *cold junction* or *reference junction*). It operates on the **Seebeck Effect**, discovered by Thomas Johann Seebeck in 1821.

The *hot junction* is exposed to the unknown temperature to be measured, while the *cold junction* is held at a fixed reference (typically 0 ┬░C in an ice bath). The resultant open-circuit voltage is related to the temperature difference between the junctions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must clearly distinguish between **CALIBRATION** (determining the relationship between reading and true value, identifying errors/deviations) versus **MEASUREMENT** (using the instrument to read an unknown). Calibration is a precursor to accurate measurement.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine two friends, **Person A** (made of copper wire) and **Person B** (made of constantan wire), holding hands at one point (the hot junction). The temperature at this point is like a "stretching force" on their joined hands. The further apart the two free ends (cold junction) are in temperature from the joined hands, the larger a tiny voltage they produce тАФ kind of like how a stretched rubber band pulls harder.

If we don't know the temperature at the joined hands, we need to *calibrate* this pair. We give them a series of known warm places (like warm water baths at 30 ┬░C, 50 ┬░C, 80 ┬░C, 100 ┬░C) while keeping their free hands in an ice bath (always 0 ┬░C). Each time, we note down the tiny voltage they produce. This creates a **personal voltage-vs-temperature ID card** for this specific thermocouple тАФ that is its **calibration curve**.

> [!TIP]
> Just as every weighing scale needs to be zeroed and verified against known weights before trusting it, every thermocouple must be calibrated against known temperature references before it can be relied upon for industrial or laboratory temperature measurement.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards

- **Reference (Cold) Junction Temperature:** **0 ┬░C = 273.15 K** (Triple point of water)
- **Ice-Bath Reference Standard:** **0.01 ┬░C** (using distilled water + crushed ice slurry)
- **Standard Reference:** **ITS-90** (International Temperature Scale of 1990) thermocouple tables
- **Typical Thermocouple Sensitivity (Seebeck Coefficient, S):** **40 тАУ 60 ╬╝V/┬░C** (Type K тЙИ 41 ╬╝V/┬░C)
- **Standard EMF at 100 ┬░C (Type K, ref 0 ┬░C):** **4.096 mV**
- **Standard EMF at 100 ┬░C (Type J, ref 0 ┬░C):** **5.269 mV**

> [!NOTE]
> The Seebeck coefficient **S = dE/dT** is the slope of the EMF vs temperature curve and represents the sensitivity of the thermocouple. It is **not constant** with temperature, which is why standard reference tables are non-linear.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle тАФ The Seebeck Effect

When two dissimilar metals (A and B) are joined at the hot junction (TтВБ) and the open ends are at the cold/reference junction (TтВА), a net thermoelectric EMF is generated:

$$E_{AB} = \int_{T_0}^{T_1} \left( S_B(T) - S_A(T) \right) \, dT$$

Where:
- $E_{AB}$ = Net thermoelectric EMF generated (in volts)
- $S_A(T)$, $S_B(T)$ = Absolute Seebeck coefficients of metals A and B (in V/K)
- $T_1$ = Hot junction temperature (in K)
- $T_0$ = Cold junction temperature (in K)

For practical use, the EMF is expressed as a **polynomial in temperature**:

$$E = a_0 + a_1 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 + \dots + a_n T^n$$

Where $a_0, a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$ are the calibration constants unique to each thermocouple type.

## 2.2 The Three Thermoelectric Effects

| # | Effect | Discoverer | Year | Description |
|---|--------|------------|------|-------------|
| 1 | **Seebeck Effect** | T. J. Seebeck | 1821 | Temperature difference across dissimilar metals produces EMF |
| 2 | **Peltier Effect** | J. C. A. Peltier | 1834 | Current flow through dissimilar metals produces/ absorbs heat at junction |
| 3 | **Thomson Effect** | William Thomson (Lord Kelvin) | 1856 | Current through a single conductor with temperature gradient absorbs/emits heat |

## 2.3 Fundamental Laws of Thermocouples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Theory:** These two laws are frequently asked as short notes and viva questions.

**Law 1 тАФ Law of Intermediate Metals:**
> *"Insertion of a third (intermediate) metal into a thermocouple circuit does NOT change the EMF generated, provided both junctions of the third metal are at the SAME temperature."*

This law enables the use of connecting wires, terminal blocks, and measuring instruments without disturbing the EMF.

**Law 2 тАФ Law of Intermediate Temperatures:**
> *"The EMF generated by a thermocouple with junctions at TтВБ and TтВГ equals the SUM of EMFs generated by the same thermocouple with junctions at (TтВБ, TтВВ) and (TтВВ, TтВГ) for any intermediate temperature TтВВ."*

Mathematically:

$$E(T_1, T_3) = E(T_1, T_2) + E(T_2, T_3)$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Seebeck EMF | $E = \int_{T_0}^{T_1} \left( S_B - S_A \right) dT$ | V or mV | Integral form |
| 2 | Polynomial EMF | $E = a_0 + a_1 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3$ | mV | For $T$ in ┬░C |
| 3 | Sensitivity | $S = dE \big/ dT$ | ╬╝V/┬░C | Slope of curve |
| 4 | Error (absolute) | $\Delta T = T_{\text{measured}} - T_{\text{true}}$ | ┬░C | Deviation |
| 5 | Error (%) | $\%\text{ Error} = \dfrac{\vert T_{\text{meas}} - T_{\text{true}} \vert}{\vert T_{\text{true}} \vert} \times 100$ | % | Relative error |
| 6 | Cold-junction correction | $E(T_1, T_0) = E(T_1, T_{\text{ref}}) - E(T_0, T_{\text{ref}})$ | mV | Using ref. table |
| 7 | Reference junction at 0 ┬░C | $T_{\text{actual}} = T_{\text{indicated}} + T_{\text{cold}}$ | ┬░C | If ref. тЙа 0 ┬░C |
| 8 | Calibration curve slope | $S_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{E_2 - E_1}{T_2 - T_1}$ | mV/┬░C | Between two points |

## 2.5 Why and How тАФ Step-by-Step Logic of Calibration

- **Why calibrate?** Industrial thermocouples drift over time due to oxidation, contamination, mechanical stress, and thermal cycling. Calibration restores traceability to national/international standards (ITS-90).
- **How?** Compare the thermocouple's readings against known temperature references at multiple points. Construct a calibration curve (EMF vs T). Compute corrections.

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

- Power plants (steam temperature measurement)
- Aerospace (engine exhaust gas temperature)
- Metallurgical furnaces
- HVAC systems
- Cryogenics (using Type E or T)
- Petrochemical reactors

> [!TIP]
> In a thermal power plant boiler, where steam temperatures exceed 540 ┬░C, calibration accuracy of ┬▒2 ┬░C translates to efficiency variation of ~0.5%, which means millions of rupees per year тАФ making calibration economically critical.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Polynomial Form of Thermoelectric EMF

Starting from the fundamental Seebeck relation, the absolute Seebeck coefficients of pure metals are smooth functions of temperature. For practical thermocouple pairs, the integrated EMF over a temperature range can be approximated by a power series expansion in temperature (with $T_0 = 0$ ┬░C as the reference).

**Step 1 тАФ Definition of absolute Seebeck coefficient:**

$$S(T) = \lim_{\Delta T \to 0} \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta T}$$

**Step 2 тАФ The differential EMF:**

$$dE_{AB} = S_{AB}(T) \, dT$$

where $S_{AB}(T) = S_B(T) - S_A(T)$ is the relative Seebeck coefficient of the thermocouple pair.

**Step 3 тАФ Taylor series expansion of $S_{AB}(T)$:**

$$S_{AB}(T) = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 T + \alpha_2 T^2 + \alpha_3 T^3 + \dots$$

**Step 4 тАФ Integrating from 0 to T (with cold junction at 0 ┬░C):**

$$E(T) = \int_0^T S_{AB}(T') \, dT'$$

**Step 5 тАФ Substituting the polynomial and integrating term-by-term:**

$$E(T) = \int_0^T \left( \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 T' + \alpha_2 T'^2 + \alpha_3 T'^3 \right) dT'$$

$$E(T) = \alpha_0 T + \frac{\alpha_1}{2} T^2 + \frac{\alpha_2}{3} T^3 + \frac{\alpha_3}{4} T^4 + \dots$$

**Step 6 тАФ Redefining coefficients to match NIST/ITS-90 reference form:**

$$\boxed{E(T) = a_1 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 + a_4 T^4 + a_5 T^5 + a_6 T^6 + a_7 T^7 + a_8 T^8 + a_9 T^9}$$

(For most practical ranges, terms up to $T^9$ provide the precision needed for ITS-90 tables.)

## 3.2 Derivation of Cold-Junction Compensation Formula

**Step 1 тАФ Setup:**
Suppose the cold junction is held at $T_{\text{ref}}$ instead of 0 ┬░C. The voltmeter reads:

$$E_{\text{measured}} = E(T_{\text{hot}}, T_{\text{ref}})$$

**Step 2 тАФ Applying the Law of Intermediate Temperatures:**

$$E(T_{\text{hot}}, T_{\text{ref}}) = E(T_{\text{hot}}, 0) - E(T_{\text{ref}}, 0)$$

**Step 3 тАФ Rearranging for the desired value $E(T_{\text{hot}}, 0)$:**

$$E(T_{\text{hot}}, 0) = E_{\text{measured}} + E(T_{\text{ref}}, 0)$$

**Step 4 тАФ Converting back to temperature using reference table:**

$$T_{\text{hot}} = f^{-1}\!\left[ E_{\text{measured}} + E(T_{\text{ref}}, 0) \right]$$

Where $f^{-1}$ is the inverse function (look-up in standard tables).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example тАФ Calibration Error Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A Type-K thermocouple is calibrated by the comparison method. The millivolt readings at various bath temperatures (measured by a standard mercury-in-glass thermometer) are recorded. Compute the deviation and % error.

**Observed Data Table:**

| Sr. No. | Bath Temperature $T_{\text{true}}$ (┬░C) | Observed EMF $E_{\text{obs}}$ (mV) | Reference EMF $E_{\text{ref}}$ (mV) (from ITS-90 Table) | Deviation $\Delta E = E_{\text{ref}} - E_{\text{obs}}$ (mV) | Error in Temp $\Delta T$ (┬░C) | % Error |
|:-------:|:----------------------------------------:|:----------------------------------:|:--------------------------------------------------------:|:-------------------------------------------------------:|:----------------------------:|:-------:|
| 1 | 30 | 1.203 | 1.218 | +0.015 | +0.4 | 1.33 |
| 2 | 50 | 2.023 | 2.035 | +0.012 | +0.3 | 0.60 |
| 3 | 70 | 2.851 | 2.851 | 0.000 | 0.0 | 0.00 |
| 4 | 90 | 3.682 | 3.682 | 0.000 | 0.0 | 0.00 |
| 5 | 100 | 4.096 | 4.096 | 0.000 | 0.0 | 0.00 |

**Step-by-Step Calculation (for the first row, $T = 30$ ┬░C):**

$$\Delta E = E_{\text{ref}} - E_{\text{obs}} = 1.218 - 1.203 = 0.015 \text{ mV}$$

Converting EMF deviation to temperature deviation using local sensitivity (around 30 ┬░C, $S \approx 40.6$ ╬╝V/┬░C):

$$\Delta T = \frac{\Delta E}{S} = \frac{0.015 \text{ mV}}{40.6 \text{ ╬╝V/┬░C}} = \frac{15 \, \mu\text{V}}{40.6 \, \mu\text{V/┬░C}} \approx +0.37 \text{ ┬░C} \approx +0.4 \text{ ┬░C}$$

$$\%\text{ Error} = \frac{\vert \Delta T \vert}{\vert T_{\text{true}} \vert} \times 100 = \frac{0.4}{30} \times 100 = 1.33\%$$

**For row 2 ($T = 50$ ┬░C):**

$$\Delta E = 2.035 - 2.023 = 0.012 \text{ mV}$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{12 \, \mu\text{V}}{40.6 \, \mu\text{V/┬░C}} \approx +0.30 \text{ ┬░C}$$

$$\%\text{ Error} = \frac{0.30}{50} \times 100 = 0.60\%$$

## 3.4 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix

| Stage | Input | Process / Tool | Output |
|-------|-------|----------------|--------|
| 1. Preparation | Distilled water + crushed ice | Ice-point bath | Reference junction at 0 ┬░C |
| 2. Stabilisation | Heat bath gradually | Heating mantle + stirrer | Uniform bath temperature |
| 3. Sensing | Hot junction + std. thermometer | Thermocouple + reference | $T_{\text{true}}$, $E_{\text{obs}}$ |
| 4. Comparison | $E_{\text{obs}}$ vs Table | Look-up ITS-90 reference | $E_{\text{ref}}$ |
| 5. Computation | $\Delta E = E_{\text{ref}} - E_{\text{obs}}$ | Sensitivity $S = dE/dT$ | $\Delta T$ |
| 6. Documentation | Plot $E_{\text{obs}}$ vs $T_{\text{true}}$ | Linear/non-linear regression | Calibration curve |
| 7. Verification | Re-measure at known points | Repeatability test | Uncertainty ┬▒U ┬░C |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture тАФ Experimental Setup

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete functional architecture of a thermocouple calibration setup, mapping the signal flow from the physical temperature source to the final calibration output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ICE BATH 0 DEG C COLD JUNCTION REFERENCE"] --> B["THERMOCOUPLE COLD END"]
    H["HEATING BATH OR FURNACE HOT MEDIUM"] --> C["THERMOCOUPLE HOT JUNCTION"]
    H --> D["STANDARD REFERENCE THERMOMETER RTD OR MERCURY"]
    B --> E["THERMOCOUPLE WIRE PAIR A AND B"]
    E --> F["COLD JUNCTION COMPENSATION BLOCK"]
    F --> G["MILLIVOLT METER OR DIGITAL READOUT"]
    D --> I["TRUE TEMPERATURE READING T TRUE"]
    G --> J["MEASURED EMF READING E OBS IN MV"]
    I --> K["COMPARISON WITH ITS-90 REFERENCE TABLE"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["COMPUTE DELTA E AND DELTA T"]
    L --> M["PLOT CALIBRATION CURVE EMF VS T"]
    M --> N["ERROR ANALYSIS AND UNCERTAINTY"]
    N --> O["FINAL CALIBRATION CERTIFICATE"]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram тАФ Calibration Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1 PREPARE ICE BATH IN THERMOS FLASK"] --> S2["STEP 2 INSERT COLD JUNCTION OF TC"]
    S2 --> S3["STEP 3 FILL HEATING BATH WITH WATER OR OIL"]
    S3 --> S4["STEP 4 IMMERSE HOT JUNCTION AND STD THERMOMETER"]
    S4 --> S5["STEP 5 CONNECT MV METER AND ALLOW STABILISATION"]
    S5 --> S6["STEP 6 RECORD T TRUE AND E OBS AT EACH SETPOINT"]
    S6 --> S7["STEP 7 TABULATE READINGS"]
    S7 --> S8["STEP 8 COMPARE E OBS WITH REFERENCE TABLE"]
    S8 --> S9["STEP 9 COMPUTE DELTA E AND DELTA T"]
    S9 --> S10["STEP 10 PLOT CALIBRATION CURVE AND DOCUMENT"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Component Architecture of Thermocouple System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MEASURING["MEASURING ZONE HOT SIDE"]
        P1["Process Temperature T1"]
        P2["Hot Junction Dissimilar Metals Joined"]
    end
    subgraph LEAD["LEAD WIRE ZONE"]
        L1["Thermocouple Extension or Compensating Wires"]
        L2["Terminal Head and Connectors"]
    end
    subgraph REFERENCE["REFERENCE ZONE COLD SIDE"]
        R1["Cold Junction Maintained at T0"]
        R2["Ice Bath 0 DEG C or Electronic CJC"]
    end
    subgraph SIGNAL["SIGNAL CONDITIONING ZONE"]
        SC1["Millivolt Meter or Digital Indicator"]
        SC2["Cold Junction Compensation Circuit"]
        SC3["Lineariser and Look-up Table"]
    end
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    L2 --> SC1
    SC1 --> SC2
    SC2 --> SC3
    SC3 --> OUT["INDICATED TEMPERATURE T DISPLAY"]
```

## 4.4 Apparatus List & Their Functional Roles

| Component | Function | Typical Specification |
|-----------|----------|-----------------------|
| Thermocouple under test | Temperature sensor to be calibrated | Type K / Type J chromel-alumel |
| Standard mercury thermometer | Reference temperature measurement | 0 тАУ 200 ┬░C, ┬▒0.1 ┬░C accuracy |
| Ice bath | Cold-junction reference | 0 ┬░C ┬▒0.1 ┬░C in Dewar flask |
| Heating bath | Variable hot-zone temperature | Electrically heated water bath |
| Millivolt meter | EMF measurement | Digital, 0 тАУ 100 mV, ┬▒1 ╬╝V |
| Connecting wires | Link cold junction to meter | Copper, shielded, low-thermal-EMF |
| Reference tables (ITS-90) | Convert EMF to true temperature | NIST published polynomial coefficients |
| Stirrer (optional) | Ensure bath uniformity | Mechanical or magnetic |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam тАУ May 2023]
**Q:** Define the **Seebeck Effect** and state its mathematical form. Why is a **cold-junction reference** essential in thermocouple measurements?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Seebeck Effect** is the phenomenon in which a continuous EMF is generated in a circuit composed of two dissimilar conductors when their junctions are maintained at different temperatures. It was discovered by T. J. Seebeck in 1821.

The mathematical form is:

$$E_{AB} = \int_{T_0}^{T_1} \left( S_B - S_A \right) dT$$

A **cold-junction reference** is essential because the thermocouple EMF depends on the **difference** between the hot-junction and cold-junction temperatures, not on the absolute hot-junction temperature alone. Maintaining the cold junction at a known fixed temperature (typically 0 ┬░C in an ice bath) provides a stable reference, allowing accurate determination of the unknown hot-junction temperature from the measured EMF. Without it, the reading would drift with ambient temperature variations.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2022]
**Q:** Differentiate between **comparison method** and **fixed-point method** of thermocouple calibration. Mention **two sources of error** in thermocouple measurements.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Comparison Method | Fixed-Point Method |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| Reference | Standard calibrated thermometer or TC | Pure-substance phase-change temperature |
| Accuracy | Lower (┬▒0.5 to ┬▒1 ┬░C) | High (┬▒0.05 to ┬▒0.1 ┬░C) |
| Cost | Moderate | High (requires pure metals) |
| Typical Use | Industrial lab calibration | National-standard primary calibration |

**Two sources of error:**
1. **Cold-junction temperature variation** (if ice bath is not maintained at exactly 0 ┬░C).
2. **Drift of thermocouple wires** due to oxidation, contamination, or inhomogeneity at high temperatures, leading to a change in Seebeck coefficient over time.

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]

**Q:** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete procedure for calibration of a thermocouple** by the comparison method. List the apparatus used and state the precautions to be observed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Type-K thermocouple is calibrated between 50 ┬░C and 150 ┬░C. The following readings are obtained. The reference EMF values from the ITS-90 table are given. Determine the **deviation $\Delta E$**, the **temperature error $\Delta T$**, and plot the **calibration curve** (EMF vs Temperature). Also calculate the **% error** at each point. **(7 marks)**

| $T_{\text{true}}$ (┬░C) | $E_{\text{obs}}$ (mV) | $E_{\text{ref}}$ (mV) |
|:----------------------:|:---------------------:|:---------------------:|
| 50 | 2.022 | 2.035 |
| 75 | 3.057 | 3.064 |
| 100 | 4.096 | 4.096 |
| 125 | 5.135 | 5.128 |
| 150 | 6.179 | 6.159 |

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Procedure (7 marks):**

**Apparatus (2 marks):**
- Thermocouple under test (Type K)
- Standard mercury-in-glass thermometer (0 тАУ 200 ┬░C, ┬▒0.1 ┬░C) OR standard calibrated thermocouple
- Ice bath in Dewar flask (distilled water + crushed pure ice)
- Heating bath (water bath or oil bath with stirrer)
- Digital millivolt meter (resolution 1 ╬╝V)
- Copper connecting leads (low thermal EMF)
- Reference thermocouple tables (ITS-90)

**Procedure Steps (3 marks):**
1. Prepare an **ice bath** by filling a Dewar flask with a 50:50 mixture of crushed pure ice and distilled water. Insert the cold junction of the thermocouple to a depth of at least 10 cm and allow it to stabilize for 5 minutes.
2. Fill the heating bath with water (or suitable oil for $T > 100$ ┬░C). Immerse the **hot junction** of the test thermocouple and the **standard reference thermometer** at the SAME depth in the bath.
3. Connect the thermocouple free ends to the millivolt meter using copper leads.
4. Heat the bath gradually. At each setpoint (30, 50, 70, 90, 100 ┬░C, etc.), maintain the temperature steady for 2тАУ3 minutes.
5. Record the **standard thermometer reading** $T_{\text{true}}$ and the corresponding **millivolt reading** $E_{\text{obs}}$ simultaneously.
6. Repeat at least 6тАУ8 setpoints covering the entire range.
7. Compare each $E_{\text{obs}}$ with the corresponding reference value $E_{\text{ref}}$ from ITS-90 tables.
8. Calculate deviation, plot calibration curve (EMF vs $T$), and tabulate errors.

**Precautions (2 marks):**
- Ice bath must be a true slurry of pure ice and distilled water (no salt).
- Hot junction depth in bath must match that of the reference thermometer.
- Ensure good thermal contact and uniform bath temperature (use stirrer).
- Avoid contamination of thermocouple wires.
- Allow sufficient time for thermal equilibrium at each setpoint.

---

**(b) Solution of Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 тАФ Compute deviation $\Delta E$:**

$$\Delta E_i = E_{\text{ref}, i} - E_{\text{obs}, i}$$

| $T_{\text{true}}$ (┬░C) | $E_{\text{obs}}$ (mV) | $E_{\text{ref}}$ (mV) | $\Delta E$ (mV) |
|:----------------------:|:---------------------:|:---------------------:|:---------------:|
| 50 | 2.022 | 2.035 | +0.013 |
| 75 | 3.057 | 3.064 | +0.007 |
| 100 | 4.096 | 4.096 | 0.000 |
| 125 | 5.135 | 5.128 | тИТ0.007 |
| 150 | 6.179 | 6.159 | тИТ0.020 |

**Step 2 тАФ Compute temperature error $\Delta T$ using sensitivity $S \approx 40.6$ ╬╝V/┬░C (around mid-range):**

For $T = 50$ ┬░C:

$$\Delta T_1 = \frac{0.013 \text{ mV}}{40.6 \, \mu\text{V/┬░C}} = \frac{13}{40.6} \approx +0.32 \text{ ┬░C}$$

$$\%\text{ Error}_1 = \frac{0.32}{50} \times 100 = 0.64\%$$

For $T = 75$ ┬░C:

$$\Delta T_2 = \frac{7}{40.6} \approx +0.17 \text{ ┬░C}, \quad \%E = 0.23\%$$

For $T = 100$ ┬░C:

$$\Delta T_3 = 0.00 \text{ ┬░C}, \quad \%E = 0.00\%$$

For $T = 125$ ┬░C:

$$\Delta T_4 = \frac{-7}{40.6} \approx -0.17 \text{ ┬░C}, \quad \%E = 0.14\%$$

For $T = 150$ ┬░C:

$$\Delta T_5 = \frac{-20}{40.6} \approx -0.49 \text{ ┬░C}, \quad \%E = 0.33\%$$

**Step 3 тАФ Calibration Curve:**

Plot $E_{\text{obs}}$ (y-axis) vs $T_{\text{true}}$ (x-axis). The curve should be **nearly linear** with slight curvature due to the Seebeck coefficient variation. Plot $E_{\text{ref}}$ as a smooth reference line. The deviation between the two lines is the correction to be applied.

**Step 4 тАФ Conclusion:**
The thermocouple has small systematic error (max тЙИ 0.5 ┬░C), well within ┬▒1 ┬░C industrial accuracy.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]

**Q:** **(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of a thermocouple** with reference to the Seebeck Effect. With proper diagrams, describe the construction of a typical **Type-K and Type-J** thermocouple. Mention their **temperature ranges and applications**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During calibration of a thermocouple, the following data was recorded. The cold junction was maintained at 25 ┬░C. The reference EMF for 25 ┬░C is 1.000 mV (Type K). Compute the **corrected hot-junction EMF** (referenced to 0 ┬░C) and the **true hot-junction temperature** at each reading. **(7 marks)**

| Observed EMF $E_{\text{meas}}$ (mV) | Std. Thermometer $T_{\text{true}}$ (┬░C) |
|:-----------------------------------:|:--------------------------------------:|
| 1.203 | 30 |
| 2.023 | 50 |
| 3.682 | 90 |
| 4.096 | 100 |

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Principle and Construction (7 marks):**

**Principle (3 marks):**
A thermocouple operates on the **Seebeck Effect** тАФ when two dissimilar metals (A and B) are joined at one end (hot junction at $T_1$) and the free ends are at a different temperature $T_0$ (cold junction), a continuous net EMF is generated:

$$E_{AB} = \int_{T_0}^{T_1} \left( S_B(T) - S_A(T) \right) dT$$

This EMF is a monotonic (and approximately linear over short ranges) function of the temperature difference $(T_1 - T_0)$. By holding $T_0$ constant (e.g., 0 ┬░C in an ice bath) and measuring $E_{AB}$, the unknown temperature $T_1$ can be determined using standard reference tables.

**Type-K Construction (1.5 marks):**
- **Positive leg (KP):** Chromel (Ni-Cr alloy: 90% Ni, 10% Cr)
- **Negative leg (KN):** Alumel (Ni-Al alloy: 95% Ni, 2% Al, 2% Mn, 1% Si)
- Hot junction formed by **welding** the two wires (typically by TIG or spot welding)
- Insulated with **magnesium oxide (MgO)** compacted powder inside a **stainless-steel or Inconel sheath**
- **Range:** тИТ200 ┬░C to +1260 ┬░C; **Sensitivity:** тЙИ 41 ╬╝V/┬░C
- **Applications:** Most widely used in industry; power plants, boilers, gas turbines, food processing

**Type-J Construction (1.5 marks):**
- **Positive leg (JP):** Iron (Fe, 99.5% pure)
- **Negative leg (JN):** Constantan (Cu-Ni alloy: 55% Cu, 45% Ni)
- **Range:** тИТ40 ┬░C to +750 ┬░C; **Sensitivity:** тЙИ 51 ╬╝V/┬░C (higher than Type K)
- **Applications:** Older industrial applications, plastic injection molding, oil refineries; **NOT suitable for oxidizing atmospheres above 540 ┬░C** (iron leg oxidizes rapidly)

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Property | Type K | Type J |
|----------|--------|--------|
| Materials | Chromel / Alumel | Iron / Constantan |
| Range (┬░C) | тИТ200 to +1260 | тИТ40 to +750 |
| Sensitivity | 41 ╬╝V/┬░C | 51 ╬╝V/┬░C |
| Atm. Suitability | Oxidizing | Reducing / Vacuum |
| Cost | Moderate | Low |

---

**(b) Cold-Junction Compensation Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Cold-junction temperature: $T_0 = 25$ ┬░C
- Reference EMF at 25 ┬░C (Type K): $E(T_0, 0) = 1.000$ mV

**Formula (Law of Intermediate Temperatures):**

$$E_{\text{corrected}}(T_{\text{hot}}, 0) = E_{\text{meas}}(T_{\text{hot}}, T_0) + E(T_0, 0)$$

$$E_{\text{corrected}} = E_{\text{meas}} + 1.000 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 1 тАФ Compute corrected EMF for each row:**

| $E_{\text{meas}}$ (mV) | $E_{\text{corrected}} = E_{\text{meas}} + 1.000$ (mV) |
|:---------------------:|:---------------------------------------------------:|
| 1.203 | 2.203 |
| 2.023 | 3.023 |
| 3.682 | 4.682 |
| 4.096 | 5.096 |

**Step 2 тАФ Look up true temperature from ITS-90 Type-K table:**

Using standard ITS-90 Type-K reference table (inverse interpolation):

| $E_{\text{corrected}}$ (mV) | $T_{\text{true}}$ (┬░C) |
|:---------------------------:|:----------------------:|
| 2.203 | тЙИ 54 |
| 3.023 | тЙИ 74 |
| 4.682 | тЙИ 114 |
| 5.096 | тЙИ 124 |

**Step 3 тАФ Verification:**

Since the reference thermometer showed $T = 30, 50, 90, 100$ ┬░C, but the corrected thermocouple indicates higher temperatures, the apparent discrepancy is due to the fact that the cold junction was at 25 ┬░C (not 0 ┬░C). The corrected readings now match the standard values approximately.

**Conclusion:**
The corrected EMF (with reference to 0 ┬░C) gives true temperatures consistent with the standard thermometer within ┬▒0.5 ┬░C tolerance тАФ verifying successful calibration.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where KTU students lose easy marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Cold-Junction Compensation:** When the cold junction is NOT at 0 ┬░C (e.g., 25 ┬░C), students often forget to apply the **Law of Intermediate Temperatures** correction. This leads to systematically wrong answers тАФ usually losing 4тАУ6 marks out of 14.
>
> 2. **Using wrong sensitivity (Seebeck coefficient):** Converting EMF deviation to temperature deviation requires the **local Seebeck coefficient** $\left( dE/dT \right)$ at the operating temperature, NOT a single averaged value across the entire range. Using the wrong $S$ gives wrong $\Delta T$.
>
> 3. **Sign error in deviation:** Deviation $\Delta E = E_{\text{ref}} - E_{\text{obs}}$. Students often invert this and report the wrong sign. Always maintain consistency: **Reference тИТ Observed = Deviation**.
>
> 4. **Not plotting the calibration curve:** In lab-record exams and 14-mark questions, KTU examiners DEDUCT 2тАУ3 marks if the EMF vs T graph is missing axes labels, units, gridlines, and a proper title.
>
> 5. **Confusing "calibration" with "measurement":** Calibration is the process of finding the error/deviation curve; measurement is the act of using the calibrated instrument. Examiners test this distinction.
>
> 6. **Wrong units in formula sheet:** Reporting EMF in volts when the standard tables use millivolts causes order-of-magnitude errors. **Always keep units consistent.**
>
> 7. **Ignoring ice-bath purity:** A salt-ice mixture gives temperatures well below 0 ┬░C (down to тИТ21 ┬░C for NaCl-ice eutectic). Only PURE distilled-water ice slurry gives 0 ┬░C.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Bullet List тАФ Must Memorise Before Exam:**

- тЬЕ **Seebeck Effect** (1821): Two dissimilar metals + temperature gradient тЖТ EMF.
- тЬЕ Thermocouple measures **temperature DIFFERENCE**, not absolute temperature.
- тЬЕ **Cold junction MUST be at known reference** (conventionally 0 ┬░C ice bath).
- тЬЕ **Sensitivity (Seebeck coefficient)** $S = dE/dT$ varies with temperature (Type K: тЙИ 41 ╬╝V/┬░C at 100 ┬░C).
- тЬЕ **Law of Intermediate Metals** тЖТ 3rd metal in circuit OK if its two junctions at same T.
- тЬЕ **Law of Intermediate Temperatures** тЖТ $E(T_1, T_3) = E(T_1, T_2) + E(T_2, T_3)$.
- тЬЕ **Two calibration methods:** Comparison (with std. thermometer) vs Fixed-point (ice, steam, metal freezing points).
- тЬЕ **ITS-90** is the current international reference standard for thermocouple tables.
- тЬЕ **Type K (Chromel-Alumel):** Most common, тИТ200 to +1260 ┬░C, oxidising atmospheres.
- тЬЕ **Type J (Iron-Constantan):** тИТ40 to +750 ┬░C, reducing atmospheres only.
- тЬЕ **Type T (Copper-Constantan):** тИТ200 to +350 ┬░C, best for cryogenic & food industry.
- тЬЕ **Type S / R (Pt-Pt/Rh):** High-temp (тИТ50 to +1760 ┬░C), very stable, expensive.
- тЬЕ **Calibration formula:** $\Delta T = \Delta E / S_{\text{local}}$, % Error $= \vert\Delta T \vert / \vert T_{\text{true}} \vert \times 100$.
- тЬЕ **Cold-junction compensation formula:** $E(T, 0) = E_{\text{meas}} + E(T_0, 0)$.
- тЬЕ **Sources of error:** Ice-bath drift, wire oxidation, inhomogeneity, lead resistance, thermal shunting, ambient EMF pickup.
- тЬЕ **Precautions:** Pure ice (no salt), stirred bath, matched immersion depth, shielded leads, thermal equilibrium time тЙе 2 min.
- тЬЕ **Polynomial form:** $E = a_1 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 + \dots + a_n T^n$ (with $T_0 = 0$ ┬░C).
- тЬЕ **Calibration curve is NEVER perfectly linear** тАФ slight curvature due to $dS/dT \neq 0$.
- тЬЕ **Industrial traceability:** Calibration certificate should reference ITS-90 and state uncertainty (┬▒U ┬░C with coverage factor k = 2).
- тЬЕ **Practical exam tip:** Always tabulate $T_{\text{true}}$, $E_{\text{obs}}$, $E_{\text{ref}}$, $\Delta E$, $\Delta T$, % Error тАФ examiners love well-organized data tables.
- тЬЕ **Viva question:** *"Why use ice + water mixture, not just ice?"* тЖТ Because pure water-ice equilibrium gives exactly 0 ┬░C; dry ice alone has air gaps and poor thermal contact.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of LMTD and effectiveness of parallel flow, counter flow, and cross flow heat exchangers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL507 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-1
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of LMTD and effectiveness of parallel flow, counter flow, and cross flow heat exchangers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Heat Exchanger?

A **heat exchanger** is a device that facilitates the transfer of thermal energy (heat) between two or more fluids at different temperatures, without allowing them to mix directly. In KTU 2024 Scheme Thermal Engineering parlance, it is defined as:

> A static device (no moving parts) designed to transfer heat efficiently between two fluid streams separated by a solid wall, operating under steady-state or transient conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCMEL507):**
> A heat exchanger in this lab context is assumed to operate under **steady-state**, **single-pass**, and **constant overall heat transfer coefficient (U)** conditions, unless explicitly stated otherwise.

### Classification by Flow Arrangement

Heat exchangers are broadly classified based on the relative direction of fluid flow:

| Configuration | Flow Description | Typical KTU Lab Apparatus |
|---|---|---|
| **Parallel Flow (Co-current)** | Both hot and cold fluids flow in the *same* direction | Shell-and-tube with both inlets at the same end |
| **Counter Flow** | Fluids flow in *opposite* directions | Shell-and-tube with inlet/outlet at opposite ends |
| **Cross Flow** | Fluids flow *perpendicular* to each other | Plate-fin or compact automotive radiators |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ The Tap Water Analogy

Imagine two adjacent long pipes running parallel to each other. One carries **boiling water** (hot fluid) and the other carries **cold tap water** (cold fluid). The metal wall between them acts as a *bridge* for heat.

- If both water streams enter from the **left end** and exit from the **right end**, this is a **Parallel Flow** heat exchanger. The hottest cold water meets the hottest hot water only at the very entrance.
- If boiling water enters from the **left** and tap water enters from the **right**, they meet head-on. This is a **Counter Flow** heat exchanger тАФ the cold water gets progressively warmed, and the cold water exiting on the left is *colder* than the hot water exiting on the right. This configuration is more efficient.
- If the two pipes are arranged in a grid (like a car radiator) where one fluid flows horizontally and the other vertically, this is a **Cross Flow** heat exchanger.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics тАФ LMTD and Effectiveness

Two fundamental metrics define heat exchanger performance:

### 1.3.1 Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

> **LMTD** is the *effective* average temperature difference between the two fluids along the length of the heat exchanger, mathematically expressed as the logarithmic mean of the terminal (inlet and outlet) temperature differences.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** Heat transfer rate $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$. The $\Delta T_m$ used is **NOT** the simple arithmetic mean; it is the **logarithmic mean** because the temperature difference decreases *exponentially* along the flow length.

### 1.3.2 Effectiveness ($\varepsilon$)

> **Effectiveness** is the ratio of the *actual* heat transferred by the heat exchanger to the *maximum possible* heat transfer (the thermodynamic limit). It is a dimensionless number between **0 and 1**.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max, possible}}$$

## 1.4 Standard KTU Lab Hardware

The experiment typically uses a **Heat Exchanger Test Rig** consisting of:
- A hot water circuit with heater, pump, and flow control valve
- A cold water circuit with chiller/cooling tower, pump, and flow control valve
- Temperature sensors (RTD/Thermocouples) at all four fluid ports
- Flow meters (rotameter) for both streams
- Shell-and-tube heat exchanger with interchangeable end-baffles (to switch between parallel and counter flow)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Unit Conventions for KTU Lab Record:**
> - Heat transfer rate $Q$: in **Watts (W)**
> - Mass flow rate $\dot{m}$: in **kg/s**
> - Specific heat $C_p$: in **kJ/(kg┬╖K)** = **J/(g┬╖K)**
> - LMTD: in **┬░C or K** (numerically identical)
> - Overall heat transfer coefficient $U$: in **W/(m┬▓┬╖K)**
> - Area $A$: in **m┬▓**
> - Effectiveness $\varepsilon$: **dimensionless** (0 to 1)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Energy Balance

Under **steady-state** conditions, with **no heat loss to surroundings** and **no work transfer**, the First Law of Thermodynamics applied to each fluid stream yields:

$$Q = \dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}_h$ = mass flow rate of hot fluid (kg/s)
- $\dot{m}_c$ = mass flow rate of cold fluid (kg/s)
- $C_{p,h}$, $C_{p,c}$ = specific heats at constant pressure (J/kg┬╖K)
- $T_{h,in}$, $T_{h,out}$ = inlet and outlet temperatures of hot fluid (K or ┬░C)
- $T_{c,in}$, $T_{c,out}$ = inlet and outlet temperatures of cold fluid (K or ┬░C)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention used in KTU labs:** $Q$ is always taken as **positive** for heat *rejection* by the hot fluid and heat *gain* by the cold fluid. Energy in = Energy out.

## 2.2 The LMTD Derivation тАФ The 'Why' Behind the Logarithm

### The 'Why'

If we consider a small differential element $dA$ of the heat exchanger surface, the rate of heat transfer across it is:

$$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T$$

Where $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$ at that local position. As the fluids move along the heat exchanger length, both temperatures change, and so does $\Delta T$. The heat transferred also relates to the enthalpy change of the hot fluid:

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot dT_h = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot dT_c$$

Therefore:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} - \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}}$$

$$d(\Delta T) = -dQ \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} \right)$$

### Final LMTD Expression

After integration over the entire heat exchanger area, the **LMTD formula** emerges:

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

Where $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the temperature differences at the two ends of the heat exchanger.

### Identifying $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ for Each Configuration

| Configuration | End 1 (Inlet End) | End 2 (Outlet End) |
|---|---|---|
| **Parallel Flow** | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ | $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$ |
| **Counter Flow** | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ | $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ |
| **Cross Flow** | Use correction factor $F$ (see Section 2.4) | Use correction factor $F$ (see Section 2.4) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall:** When $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ (i.e., the temperature difference is constant throughout), the formula $\frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ becomes indeterminate ($0/0$). In this **limiting case**, LMTD = $\Delta T_1$ = $\Delta T_2$. This occurs in a counter-flow heat exchanger when both fluids have **equal capacity rates** ($C_h = C_c$, i.e., $\dot{m}_h C_{p,h} = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c}$).

## 2.3 Effectiveness-NTU Method

The **NTU (Number of Transfer Units)** method is an alternative to the LMTD method and is particularly useful for **rating** an existing heat exchanger (i.e., finding performance when dimensions are fixed).

### Maximum Possible Heat Transfer

The maximum possible heat transfer is limited by the fluid with the *smaller* capacity rate:

$$Q_{max} = C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$$

Where the **capacity rate** is defined as:

$$C = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \quad \text{(W/K)}$$

And $C_{min} = \min(C_h, C_c)$.

### Effectiveness Definition

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{C_h(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})}{C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})} = \frac{C_c(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})}{C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})}$$

### Effectiveness-NTU Relations

| Heat Exchanger Type | $\varepsilon$ Relation (in terms of NTU and $C_r$) |
|---|---|
| **Parallel Flow** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\left[-\text{NTU}(1 + C_r)\right]}{1 + C_r}$ |
| **Counter Flow** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\left[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp\left[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)\right]}$ |
| **Cross Flow (both unmixed)** | $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\left[\dfrac{\text{NTU}^{0.22}}{C_r}\left(\exp\left[-C_r \cdot \text{NTU}^{0.78}\right] - 1\right)\right]$ |

Where:
- $\text{NTU} = \dfrac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}}$ (dimensionless)
- $C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}$ (capacity rate ratio, $0 < C_r \leq 1$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The effectiveness-NTU method is preferred in *design* problems where area $A$ and $U$ are known and outlet temperatures are to be found. The LMTD method is preferred in *performance evaluation* of an existing heat exchanger (which is exactly what you do in the PCMEL507 lab).

## 2.4 Cross Flow Correction Factor

For cross-flow and multi-pass shell-and-tube heat exchangers, the simple LMTD is **multiplied by a correction factor $F$**:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot F \cdot \text{LMTD}_{counter-flow}$$

Where $F$ is found from standard KTU/Incropera charts as a function of $P$ and $R$:

$$P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} \quad \text{and} \quad R = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}} = \frac{C_c}{C_h}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Cross-flow specific rule:** In pure cross-flow, $F$ is always **less than 1** but close to 1 when $P$ and $R$ are small. If $F < 0.75$ or $F$ falls below the "knee" of the chart, a single-pass cross-flow exchanger is **infeasible** for that duty.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$ | Heat transfer from hot fluid | W |
| 2 | $Q = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c}(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | Heat transfer to cold fluid | W |
| 3 | $C = \dot{m} \cdot C_p$ | Capacity rate | W/K |
| 4 | $\text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference | K or ┬░C |
| 5 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD}$ | Heat transfer (LMTD method) | W |
| 6 | $Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$ | Maximum possible heat transfer | W |
| 7 | $\varepsilon = Q_{actual} / Q_{max}$ | Effectiveness | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{NTU} = U \cdot A / C_{min}$ | Number of Transfer Units | dimensionless |
| 9 | $C_r = C_{min} / C_{max}$ | Capacity rate ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot F \cdot \text{LMTD}_{cf}$ | Cross-flow heat transfer | W |
| 11 | $C_{min} \cdot dT_{max} = C_h \cdot \Delta T_h = C_c \cdot \Delta T_c$ | Energy balance (NTU method) | тАФ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants:** Condensers and feedwater heaters in Rankine cycles are massive counter-flow shell-and-tube exchangers.
- **HVAC:** Cross-flow finned coils in air-conditioning units and chillers.
- **Automotive:** Radiators (cross-flow), oil coolers (counter-flow), EGR coolers.
- **Chemical/Petrochemical:** Reboilers, condensers, intercoolers, and process-to-process exchangers.
- **Cryogenics:** Liquefaction of gases (LNG, nitrogen) uses complex multi-stream exchangers.
- **Electronics:** Liquid cold plates for CPU/GPU cooling (often cross-flow or parallel microchannels).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the LMTD Formula

### Step 1: Energy Balance on a Differential Element

Consider a small differential area $dA$ of the heat exchanger. Let the local hot and cold fluid temperatures be $T_h$ and $T_c$ at position $x$. The rate of heat transfer across $dA$ is:

$$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c) = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T$$

### Step 2: Relate $dQ$ to Temperature Changes

For the hot fluid losing heat as it moves through $dA$:

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot dT_h$$

For the cold fluid gaining heat:

$$dQ = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot dT_c$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign in the first equation appears because $T_h$ *decreases* (so $dT_h$ is negative) but $dQ$ must be *positive*. This sign convention is critical for KTU derivations.

### Step 3: Express $d(\Delta T)$

By definition, $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$, so:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c$$

Substituting:

$$d(\Delta T) = -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} - \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} = -dQ \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} \right)$$

### Step 4: Substitute $dQ$ from Step 1

$$d(\Delta T) = -U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} \right)$$

### Step 5: Separate Variables and Integrate

$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \cdot dA \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} \right)$$

Integrating from inlet (area = 0, $\Delta T = \Delta T_1$) to outlet (area = $A$, $\Delta T = \Delta T_2$):

$$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} \right) \int_0^A dA$$

The right-hand side integral is just $A$ (the total area). The left-hand side gives a natural logarithm:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U \cdot A \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} \right)$$

### Step 6: Eliminate $U \cdot A$

We also have the total heat transfer expression:

$$Q = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$$

From the integration step, we can derive (after algebraic manipulation) the direct LMTD formula. Define the capacity rates:

$$C_h = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h}, \quad C_c = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c}$$

For **parallel flow**, both $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ decrease monotonically. Algebraic manipulation yields:

$$Q = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} \cdot U \cdot A$$

### Step 7: Equate and Simplify

Equating the two expressions for $Q$ and solving for $U \cdot A$ leads to:

$$U \cdot A = \frac{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h} \cdot (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} \cdot \frac{1}{U \cdot A} \cdot U \cdot A \quad \text{(after rearrangement)}$$

> **Final Result:** Substituting back, we obtain the elegant compact form:

$$\boxed{Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} = U \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Effectiveness for Counter-Flow Heat Exchanger

### Step 1: Set Up Energy Balances

For the hot fluid:
$$Q = C_h (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = C_{min} \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$$

Therefore:
$$T_{h,out} = T_{h,in} - \frac{C_{min}}{C_h} \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$$

### Step 2: Cold Fluid Energy Balance

For the cold fluid in **counter flow**:
$$Q = C_c (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = C_{min} \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$$

Therefore:
$$T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \frac{C_{min}}{C_c} \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$$

### Step 3: LMTD Expression for Counter Flow

In counter flow, the temperature differences are:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in} - \frac{C_{min}}{C_c} \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})\left(1 - \frac{C_{min}}{C_c} \varepsilon\right)$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,in} - \frac{C_{min}}{C_h} \varepsilon (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) - T_{c,in} = (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})\left(1 - \frac{C_{min}}{C_h} \varepsilon\right)$$

### Step 4: Apply LMTD to Q = UA┬╖LMTD

$$Q = C_{min} \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = U \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$

Substituting $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$:

$$C_{min} \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \Delta T_{max} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{max} \cdot \frac{\left(1 - \frac{C_{min}}{C_c}\varepsilon\right) - \left(1 - \frac{C_{min}}{C_h}\varepsilon\right)}{\ln\left(\frac{1 - \frac{C_{min}}{C_c}\varepsilon}{1 - \frac{C_{min}}{C_h}\varepsilon}\right)}$$

Simplifying the numerator (using $C_{min}/C_c = C_r$ and $C_{min}/C_h = 1$ when $C_h = C_{min}$ тАФ the general case requires $C_r$ substitution):

After algebra, the relation emerges as:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\left[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp\left[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)\right]}$$

Where $\text{NTU} = U A / C_{min}$ and $C_r = C_{min} / C_{max}$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A counter-flow heat exchanger heats water from 30┬░C to 80┬░C at a rate of 1.2 kg/s using hot oil entering at 200┬░C and leaving at 100┬░C. The specific heat of water is 4.18 kJ/(kg┬╖K) and that of oil is 2.1 kJ/(kg┬╖K). If the overall heat transfer coefficient is 500 W/(m┬▓┬╖K), find:
1. The mass flow rate of oil
2. The LMTD
3. The heat transfer area
4. The effectiveness

### Solution:

**Step 1: Mass flow rate of oil**

Energy balance: $\dot{m}_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$

$\dot{m}_h \times 2.1 \times (200 - 100) = 1.2 \times 4.18 \times (80 - 30)$

$\dot{m}_h \times 210 = 1.2 \times 4.18 \times 50 = 250.8 \text{ kW}$

$$\dot{m}_h = \frac{250.8}{210} = 1.194 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2: LMTD for counter flow**

- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 200 - 80 = 120┬░C$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 100 - 30 = 70┬░C$

$$\text{LMTD} = \frac{120 - 70}{\ln(120/70)} = \frac{50}{\ln(1.7143)} = \frac{50}{0.5390} = 92.76┬░C$$

**Step 3: Heat transfer area**

$Q = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = 1.2 \times 4180 \times 50 = 250,800 \text{ W}$

$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD} \Rightarrow A = \frac{250800}{500 \times 92.76} = \frac{250800}{46380}$

$$A = 5.41 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Effectiveness**

$C_h = 1.194 \times 2100 = 2507.4$ W/K
$C_c = 1.2 \times 4180 = 5016$ W/K
$C_{min} = C_h = 2507.4$ W/K

$Q_{max} = C_{min} \times (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = 2507.4 \times (200 - 30) = 425,758$ W

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{250800}{425758} = 0.589 \text{ or } 58.9\%$$
