# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]
**Define Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD). Why is the logarithmic mean preferred over the arithmetic mean in heat exchanger analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** LMTD is the logarithmic mean of the terminal temperature differences between the hot and cold fluids at the two ends of the heat exchanger, given by:

$$\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

**Why logarithmic (3 key points):**
1. **Exponential temperature profile:** Along the heat exchanger length, both fluid temperatures change exponentially (not linearly) because heat transfer is proportional to the local $\Delta T$. Hence $dT/dx \propto (T_h - T_c)$, which integrates to a logarithmic function.
2. **Arithmetic mean overestimates $\Delta T$:** For unequal terminal temperature differences, the arithmetic mean $(\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2)/2$ is *always* greater than the true logarithmic mean. Using it would over-predict $Q$.
3. **Limit case consistency:** When $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ (constant temperature difference, e.g., condensing or boiling), LMTD = $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ (matches arithmetic mean). When $\Delta T_2 \to 0$ (pinch point), LMTD $\to 0$, but arithmetic mean $\to \Delta T_1/2$ (non-physical).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for justification of logarithmic nature.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]
**Define heat exchanger effectiveness. Compare the effectiveness of counter-flow and parallel-flow heat exchangers for the same capacity rate ratio $C_r$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Effectiveness $\varepsilon$ is the ratio of the actual heat transfer $Q_{actual}$ to the maximum possible heat transfer $Q_{max}$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})}$$

**Comparison (key points):**

| Parameter | Parallel Flow | Counter Flow |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum achievable $\varepsilon$ | $< 0.5$ (as NTU $\to \infty$) | $\to 1$ (as NTU $\to \infty$) |
| Thermodynamic limit | Capped because $T_{c,out} < T_{h,out}$ always | $T_{c,out}$ can approach $T_{h,in}$ |
| Practical $\varepsilon$ range | 0.3 to 0.45 | 0.5 to 0.85 |
| Preferred for | Low-temperature heat recovery | High-efficiency heat transfer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The key reason counter-flow is superior:** In counter flow, the cold fluid leaving the exchanger has been in contact with the *hottest part of the hot fluid*, allowing it to reach a temperature higher than the cold-side outlet of a parallel-flow unit. This breaks the *temperature cross* barrier.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]

**A shell-and-tube heat exchanger is used to condense steam at 0.2 MPa (saturation temperature 120.23┬░C) by cooling water. The cooling water enters at 30┬░C and leaves at 80┬░C at a flow rate of 5 kg/s ($C_p$ = 4.18 kJ/kg┬╖K). The overall heat transfer coefficient is 1500 W/(m┬▓┬╖K). The exchanger is arranged in a single-pass counter-flow configuration.**

**(a)** Calculate the LMTD and the heat transfer area. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the effectiveness and the NTU. If the same exchanger is reconfigured as a parallel-flow unit, calculate the new outlet temperature of the cooling water. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution: LMTD and Area (7 marks)

**Step 1: Calculate heat transfer rate (1 mark)**

$$Q = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = 5 \times 4180 \times (80 - 30) = 1,045,000 \text{ W} = 1045 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Identify temperatures (1 mark)**

For condensing steam, $T_{h,in} = T_{h,out} = 120.23┬░C$ (isothermal hot fluid).

$T_{c,in} = 30┬░C$, $T_{c,out} = 80┬░C$

**Step 3: Compute terminal temperature differences for counter flow (1 mark)**

- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 120.23 - 80 = 40.23┬░C$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 120.23 - 30 = 90.23┬░C$

**Step 4: Apply LMTD formula (1 mark)**

$$\text{LMTD} = \frac{90.23 - 40.23}{\ln(90.23 / 40.23)} = \frac{50}{\ln(2.2427)} = \frac{50}{0.8075} = 61.92┬░C$$

**Step 5: Calculate area (1 mark)**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \text{LMTD}} = \frac{1,045,000}{1500 \times 61.92} = \frac{1,045,000}{92,880} = 11.25 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 6: Verification and result (2 marks)**

- Unit check: W / [W/(m┬▓┬╖K) ├Ч K] = m┬▓ тЬУ
- Result: $A = 11.25 \text{ m}^2$ and LMTD = $61.92┬░C$

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution: Effectiveness, NTU, and Parallel-Flow Reconfiguration (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute capacity rates (1 mark)**

- $C_h$ for steam = effectively infinite (condensing isothermal fluid has $\Delta T_h = 0$, so $C_h \to \infty$).
- $C_c = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} = 5 \times 4180 = 20,900$ W/K
- Therefore $C_{min} = C_c = 20,900$ W/K and $C_{max} = \infty$, hence $C_r = 0$.

**Step 2: Effectiveness (1 mark)**

$Q_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = 20,900 \times (120.23 - 30) = 20,900 \times 90.23 = 1,885,807$ W

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{1,045,000}{1,885,807} = 0.554$$

**Step 3: NTU (1 mark)**

For $C_r = 0$ (one fluid with infinite capacity), the counter-flow $\varepsilon$-NTU relation simplifies to $\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-\text{NTU}}$:

$$\text{NTU} = -\ln(1 - \varepsilon) = -\ln(1 - 0.554) = -\ln(0.446) = 0.807$$

**Step 4: Parallel-flow outlet temperature (3 marks)**

For parallel flow with $C_r = 0$ (one fluid isothermal), the $\varepsilon$-NTU relation is the *same*:

$\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-\text{NTU}}$, so the same $\varepsilon$ is obtained for the *same NTU*.

Since $U$ and $A$ are the same, NTU is the same, so $\varepsilon$ is the same. Hence $Q_{actual}$ is the same:

$Q = 1,045,000$ W (unchanged)

For parallel flow: $T_{c,out,new} = T_{c,in} + Q/C_c = 30 + 1,045,000/20,900 = 30 + 50 = 80┬░C$

> **Wait** тАФ this is the same result! Why?

**Physical explanation (1 mark):** When the hot fluid is **isothermal** (condensing or boiling), both parallel and counter flow give the **same effectiveness** and hence the **same outlet temperature**, because the local $\Delta T$ history does not affect the *terminal* temperatures тАФ only the *area* required changes. Since area is fixed, $Q$ remains the same. This is a classic KTU trap question.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]

**In a cross-flow heat exchanger with one fluid mixed and the other unmixed, air is to be heated from 15┬░C to 40┬░C using hot flue gas entering at 250┬░C and leaving at 120┬░C. The mass flow rates are $\dot{m}_{air} = 2.0$ kg/s ($C_{p,air} = 1.005$ kJ/kg┬╖K) and $\dot{m}_{gas} = 1.5$ kg/s ($C_{p,gas} = 1.15$ kJ/kg┬╖K). The overall heat transfer coefficient times area product is $U \cdot A = 1200$ W/K.**

**(a)** Calculate the LMTD (assuming counter-flow equivalence for the $F$-factor method) and the actual heat transfer rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the effectiveness of the heat exchanger. If the same exchanger is converted to counter flow, what would be the new effectiveness? Use $C_r = 0.576$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution: LMTD and Heat Transfer (7 marks)

**Step 1: Heat transfer rate from energy balance (2 marks)**

- $C_{air} = 2.0 \times 1005 = 2010$ W/K (cold fluid)
- $C_{gas} = 1.5 \times 1150 = 1725$ W/K (hot fluid)
- $C_{min} = 1725$ W/K (gas), $C_{max} = 2010$ W/K (air), $C_r = 1725/2010 = 0.858$

$Q = C_{air}(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = 2010 \times (40 - 15) = 50,250$ W

**Step 2: LMTD for counter-flow reference (2 marks)**

- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 250 - 40 = 210┬░C$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 120 - 15 = 105┬░C$

$$\text{LMTD}_{cf} = \frac{210 - 105}{\ln(210/105)} = \frac{105}{\ln 2} = \frac{105}{0.6931} = 151.5┬░C$$

**Step 3: F-factor and actual Q (3 marks)**

Compute $P$ and $R$:

$$P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} = \frac{40 - 15}{250 - 15} = \frac{25}{235} = 0.1064$$

$$R = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = C_r = 0.858$$

From the standard cross-flow (one mixed, one unmixed) correction-factor chart: $F \approx 0.97$

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot F \cdot \text{LMTD}_{cf} = 1200 \times 0.97 \times 151.5 = 176,346 \text{ W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Inconsistency note:** $Q = 50,250$ W from the cold-side energy balance, but $Q = 176,346$ W from the LMTD calculation. This means the given $U \cdot A = 1200$ W/K is inconsistent with the stated outlet temperatures тАФ students should report the *given energy balance result* ($50.25$ kW) and note the inconsistency. In a real lab, the LMTD value would be used to back-calculate $U$.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution: Effectiveness and Counter-Flow Comparison (7 marks)

**Step 1: Effectiveness from energy balance (2 marks)**

$Q_{max} = C_{min} \times (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}) = 1725 \times (250 - 15) = 405,375$ W

$$\varepsilon_{cross} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{50,250}{405,375} = 0.124$$

**Step 2: NTU (1 mark)**

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}} = \frac{1200}{1725} = 0.696$$

**Step 3: Counter-flow effectiveness (3 marks)**

Using $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)]}$ with NTU = 0.696 and $C_r$ = 0.576 (as given in problem):

Note: the problem gives $C_r = 0.576$, but our calculation gave $C_r = 0.858$. Following the *given* $C_r$:

$1 - C_r = 0.424$
$\text{NTU}(1 - C_r) = 0.696 \times 0.424 = 0.295$
$e^{-0.295} = 0.7444$

$$\varepsilon_{counter} = \frac{1 - 0.7444}{1 - 0.576 \times 0.7444} = \frac{0.2556}{1 - 0.4288} = \frac{0.2556}{0.5712} = 0.4475$$

**Step 4: Comparison and conclusion (1 mark)**

Counter-flow effectiveness (0.4475) is much higher than cross-flow (0.124) for the same NTU, confirming the **thermodynamic superiority** of counter flow.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in PCMEL507 viva and exam:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors** (3тАУ4 marks loss): Mixing up $T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}$ with $T_{h,out} - T_{h,in}$. The hot fluid *loses* heat, so the change is *negative*; using absolute values works only if consistent.
> 2. **Wrong LMTD endpoints for counter-flow** (4тАУ5 marks loss): Using $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ (parallel convention) instead of $T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$. This is the #1 error in KTU heat-exchanger papers.
> 3. **Forgetting $F$ factor for cross flow** (2тАУ3 marks loss): Applying pure LMTD formula to cross flow without correction factor $F$ gives inflated $Q$ values.
> 4. **Mixing LMTD and NTU methods inconsistently** (3 marks loss): Computing $\varepsilon$ from one method and $A$ from another without consistency check.
> 5. **Unit inconsistencies** (2 marks loss): Using $C_p$ in kJ/(kg┬╖K) and $\dot{m}$ in kg/s but forgetting to convert: $1 \text{ kJ} = 1000 \text{ J}$.
> 6. **Neglecting heat loss to surroundings** (1тАУ2 marks loss): In the lab, the *measured* $Q$ from the energy balance and the *computed* $Q$ from LMTD will not match exactly because of heat loss. The percentage deviation should be reported (typically 5тАУ15%).
> 7. **Forgetting to identify $C_{min}$ and $C_{max}$** (2 marks loss): Effectiveness-NTU formulas depend on which fluid has minimum capacity rate.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary for PCMEL507 тАУ Experiment 2:**

- **Heat Exchanger:** Static device, no moving parts, transfers heat between two fluids separated by a solid wall.

- **Three configurations to master:** Parallel (co-current), Counter, Cross flow.

- **LMTD formula (Universal):**
$$\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

- **LMTD endpoint rule of thumb:**
  - **Parallel flow:** Both $\Delta T$'s are at the *same end* (inlet-inlet and outlet-outlet).
  - **Counter flow:** $\Delta T$'s are at *opposite ends* (inlet-outlet and outlet-inlet).
  - **Cross flow:** Use counter-flow LMTD and multiply by correction factor $F < 1$.

- **Limiting case:** If $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, LMTD = $\Delta T_1$ (no logarithm needed; this happens in counter flow with $C_h = C_c$ or in condensing/boiling).

- **Effectiveness:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})}$$

- **NTU:** $\text{NTU} = \dfrac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}}$; capacity rate ratio $C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}$.

- **Key inequalities:** $0 < C_r \leq 1$ and $0 < \varepsilon < 1$ always.

- **Counter flow is most efficient** for any $C_r > 0$; cross flow is intermediate; parallel flow is least efficient.

- **Cap on parallel-flow effectiveness:** As NTU $\to \infty$, $\varepsilon_{parallel} \to \dfrac{1}{1 + C_r}$, **never reaching 1** unless $C_r = 1$.

- **Counter flow cap:** As NTU $\to \infty$, $\varepsilon_{counter} \to 1$ (regardless of $C_r$), *except* when $C_r = 1$ and NTU $\to \infty$ giving $\varepsilon = \text{NTU}/(1 + \text{NTU}) \to 1$.

- **Hot fluid isothermal (boiling/condensing):** $C_h = \infty$, $C_r = 0$, $\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-\text{NTU}}$, and parallel/counter/cross give **same effectiveness**.

- **Lab procedure must include:** Steady-state wait (10тАУ15 min), at least 5 readings at varied flow rates, plot of $\varepsilon$ vs NTU.

- **Practical $U$ values to remember:**
  - Gas-to-gas: 10тАУ50 W/(m┬▓┬╖K)
  - Liquid-to-liquid: 500тАУ3000 W/(m┬▓┬╖K)
  - Liquid-to-boiling/condensing: 1000тАУ5000 W/(m┬▓┬╖K)
  - Gas-to-boiling (e.g., boiler): 25тАУ250 W/(m┬▓┬╖K)

- **Energy balance (always check):** $Q_{hot} = Q_{cold}$ within ┬▒15% (heat loss); deviation > 15% indicates measurement error.

- **Cross-flow $F$ factor:** Read from $P$ vs $R$ chart; $F$ is dimensionless, $0 < F \leq 1$.

- **Quick sanity check:** If $T_{c,out} > T_{h,out}$ in your answer, the configuration MUST be counter flow (this is the *temperature cross* phenomenon).

- **KTU Viva Favorites (must answer correctly):**
  1. Why is LMTD a logarithm? тЖТ Exponential temperature profile.
  2. What is NTU? тЖТ Dimensionless number of transfer units = $UA/C_{min}$.
  3. Why counter flow best? тЖТ Cold outlet can exceed hot outlet temperature; better $\Delta T$ maintained.
  4. What is pinch point? тЖТ Location where $\Delta T$ is minimum; for parallel flow, pinch always occurs inside; for counter flow, pinch can be at either end.
  5. Effect of $C_r$ on effectiveness? тЖТ Higher $C_r$ (closer fluid capacities) means more uniform temperature change and higher possible effectiveness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance studies on a shell and tube heat exchanger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL507 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-1
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance studies on a shell and tube heat exchanger

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)** is a class of recuperative heat transfer equipment in which one fluid flows through a bundle of enclosed tubes while the second fluid flows over the tubes (inside the shell), transferring heat across the tube walls through forced convection and conduction. In KTU 2024 Scheme Thermal Engineering Lab-I (PCMEL507), Module Experiment 2 specifically deals with the **performance evaluation** of a STHE in terms of its **overall heat transfer coefficient (U)**, **heat duty (Q)**, **LMTD (Log Mean Temperature Difference)**, and **effectiveness (╬╡)** under steady-state operating conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A shell and tube heat exchanger is the most widely used industrial heat exchanger consisting of a cylindrical pressure vessel (shell) containing a parallel bundle of tubes. One fluid flows through the tubes (tube-side fluid) and the other flows outside the tubes but inside the shell (shell-side fluid). Heat is transferred between the two fluids through the tube walls.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two crowded corridors inside a building:
- **Corridor A (Tube Side):** Hot students (hot fluid) walk rapidly through small parallel tunnels carrying their warmth.
- **Corridor B (Shell Side):** Cool students (cold fluid) walk in the opposite direction along the outer area.
- **The Tunnel Wall (Tube Wall):** Acts like a thin barrier where heat from the hot side seeps into the cool side.

The hot students lose heat (cool down) and the cool students gain heat (warm up) **without ever mixing**. The "performance" of this corridor system depends on:
1. How fast the students walk (flow rate)
2. How thin/wide the tunnel wall is (tube material & thickness)
3. The temperature gap between the two sides
4. The direction they walk (parallel/counter-flow)

This is precisely the heat exchanger тАФ **a device that transfers heat between two fluids separated by a solid wall, without mixing them.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics in STHE:**
> - **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient, U:** Range **100 тАУ 10,000 W/m┬▓┬╖K** (water-to-water exchangers typically 1000тАУ5000 W/m┬▓┬╖K)
> - **LMTD (Log Mean Temperature Difference):** Used in $Q = U \cdot A_s \cdot \text{LMTD}$
> - **Effectiveness, ╬╡:** Ratio of actual heat transfer to maximum possible heat transfer, range **0 тЙд ╬╡ тЙд 1**
> - **NTU (Number of Transfer Units):** Dimensionless parameter used with the effectiveness-NTU method
> - **Fouling Factor (R_f):** Accounts for deposits on tube surfaces; reduces U over time

## 1.4 Types of Flow Configurations

| Flow Type | Description | Application |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Parallel (Co-current) Flow** | Both fluids enter at the same end, flow in same direction | Limited use; lower effectiveness |
| **Counter-current Flow** | Fluids enter at opposite ends, flow in opposite directions | Most common; highest effectiveness |
| **Cross Flow** | Fluids flow perpendicular to each other | Air-cooled condensers, radiators |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles тАФ Step-by-Step Logic

The performance evaluation of a STHE follows a structured analytical flow:

1. **Establish Steady-State:** Ensure inlet/outlet temperatures are constant over time (typically 15тАУ20 min of constant readings).
2. **Measure Mass Flow Rates:** Use rotameters, orifice meters, or weighing tanks. Convert volumetric flow (L/min) to mass flow (kg/s) using density.
3. **Record Temperatures:** Measure hot fluid inlet ($T_{h,i}$), hot fluid outlet ($T_{h,o}$), cold fluid inlet ($T_{c,i}$), cold fluid outlet ($T_{c,o}$).
4. **Compute Heat Duty (Q):** Apply energy balance: heat lost by hot fluid = heat gained by cold fluid.
5. **Compute LMTD:** Use the appropriate temperature difference formula based on flow configuration.
6. **Compute Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U):** Solve $Q = U \cdot A_s \cdot \text{LMTD}$ for U.
7. **Compute Effectiveness (╬╡):** Compare actual heat transfer to the thermodynamic maximum.
8. **Compute NTU:** A dimensionless design parameter.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Equation | Description |
|---------|----------|-------------|
| Heat Duty (Hot Fluid Side) | $Q = \dot{m}_h \cdot c_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o})$ | Energy released by hot fluid |
| Heat Duty (Cold Fluid Side) | $Q = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$ | Energy gained by cold fluid |
| Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = \frac{\rho \cdot V}{t}$ | kg/s |
| LMTD (General) | $\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log mean temp difference |
| Counter-flow LMTD | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$, $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$ | Both ╬ФT positive |
| Parallel-flow LMTD | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}$, $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,o}$ | Both ╬ФT positive |
| Overall U | $U = \frac{Q}{A_s \cdot \text{LMTD}}$ | W/m┬▓┬╖K |
| Overall Resistance | $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{r_o}{k \cdot r_i} + \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$ | Sum of resistances |
| Effectiveness | $\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}{T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}}$ (when $C_{min} = C_c$) | Dimensionless |
| NTU | $\text{NTU} = \frac{U \cdot A_s}{C_{min}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Heat Capacity Rate | $C = \dot{m} \cdot c_p$ | W/K |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** In real heat exchangers, the **LMTD is corrected by a correction factor F** (when flow is neither purely parallel nor counter). $Q = U \cdot A_s \cdot F \cdot \text{LMTD}_{CF}$, where F < 1 accounts for the deviation from true counter-flow. In KTU lab experiments, STHEs are usually operated in true counter-flow, so F тЙИ 1.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Plants:** Condensers and feedwater heaters in steam power cycles.
- **Petrochemical Industry:** Crude oil heaters, refinery heat recovery.
- **HVAC Systems:** Chilled water systems, cooling coils.
- **Chemical Processing:** Reactor feed preheating, product coolers.
- **Food & Pharma:** Pasteurizers, sterilizers (sanitary STHEs).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of LMTD (Log Mean Temperature Difference)

### Step 1: Start with Differential Heat Transfer

For a differential area $dA$ of the heat exchanger, the rate of heat transfer is proportional to the local temperature difference $\Delta T$ between the two fluids:

$$
dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T
$$

### Step 2: Express Heat Transfer in Terms of Fluid Temperatures

For the hot fluid, the heat lost as it cools from $T_h$ to $(T_h - dT_h)$ is:

$$
dQ = -\dot{m}_h \cdot c_{p,h} \cdot dT_h
$$

For the cold fluid, the heat gained as it warms from $T_c$ to $(T_c + dT_c)$ is:

$$
dQ = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot dT_c
$$

### Step 3: Combine Both Expressions

Adding these gives the change in temperature difference along the exchanger. For a counter-flow exchanger:

$$
d(\Delta T) = dT_h + dT_c
$$

$$
d(\Delta T) = -dQ \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} \right) = -dQ \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right)
$$

### Step 4: Substitute $dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T$ and Rearrange

$$
\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right) dA
$$

### Step 5: Integrate from Inlet to Outlet

For counter-flow, integrate from $A = 0$ to $A = A_s$ and from $\Delta T_1$ to $\Delta T_2$:

$$
\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right) \int_0^{A_s} dA
$$

$$
\ln \left( \frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -U \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right) A_s
$$

### Step 6: Total Heat Transfer from Energy Balance

$$
Q = C_h (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = C_c (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})
$$

Solving for the constant $U \cdot A_s \cdot \left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$ and substituting, we get:

$$
Q = U \cdot A_s \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

### Step 7: Final LMTD Equation

$$
\boxed{\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Effectiveness (╬╡)

The **maximum possible heat transfer** is achieved when the fluid with smaller heat capacity rate undergoes the maximum possible temperature change, i.e., equals the inlet temperature difference:

$$
Q_{max} = C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})
$$

Therefore, effectiveness is:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{C_h (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o})}{C_{min} (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})} = \frac{C_c (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})}{C_{min} (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix тАФ Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem (Typical KTU Lab Calculation Style):**
>
> **Given:**
> - Hot water: $T_{h,i} = 70┬░C$, $T_{h,o} = 55┬░C$, $\dot{V}_h = 10 \text{ L/min}$
> - Cold water: $T_{c,i} = 25┬░C$, $T_{c,o} = 40┬░C$, $\dot{V}_c = 12 \text{ L/min}$
> - Heat transfer area: $A_s = 0.5 \text{ m}^2$
> - Take $c_p = 4.18 \text{ kJ/kg┬╖K}$ and $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for both fluids.
> - Flow configuration: Counter-flow

### Step 1: Convert Volumetric Flow to Mass Flow

$$
\dot{m}_h = \frac{\rho \cdot \dot{V}_h}{60} = \frac{1000 \times 10 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 0.1667 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_c = \frac{1000 \times 12 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 0.2000 \text{ kg/s}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Heat Duty (Q) тАФ Both Sides

Hot side:
$$
Q_h = \dot{m}_h \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = 0.1667 \times 4180 \times (70 - 55) = 10,451 \text{ W}
$$

Cold side:
$$
Q_c = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}) = 0.2000 \times 4180 \times (40 - 25) = 12,540 \text{ W}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Experimental Reality:** In a real lab, $Q_h$ and $Q_c$ will differ by 5тАУ15% due to heat losses to surroundings. The average $Q = (Q_h + Q_c)/2$ is used for U calculation.

$$
Q_{avg} = \frac{10451 + 12540}{2} = 11,495.5 \text{ W} \approx 11.5 \text{ kW}
$$

### Step 3: Compute LMTD (Counter-Flow)

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 70 - 40 = 30┬░C
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 55 - 25 = 30┬░C
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Case:** When $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, the LMTD equals the temperature difference itself (LMTD = 30┬░C). In general, when both ╬ФT are equal, the logarithmic mean equals the arithmetic mean.

### Step 4: Compute Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)

$$
U = \frac{Q_{avg}}{A_s \cdot \text{LMTD}} = \frac{11495.5}{0.5 \times 30} = 766.4 \text{ W/m}^2\text{┬╖K}
$$

### Step 5: Compute Effectiveness (╬╡)

Heat capacity rates:
$$
C_h = \dot{m}_h \cdot c_p = 0.1667 \times 4180 = 696.7 \text{ W/K}
$$

$$
C_c = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_p = 0.2000 \times 4180 = 836.0 \text{ W/K}
$$

$C_{min} = 696.7$ W/K (hot side is the limiting fluid).

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{C_c (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})}{C_{min} (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})} = \frac{836 \times 15}{696.7 \times 45} = 0.40 \text{ or } 40\%
$$

### Step 6: Compute NTU

$$
\text{NTU} = \frac{U \cdot A_s}{C_{min}} = \frac{766.4 \times 0.5}{696.7} = 0.55
$$

## 3.4 Summary Table of Computed Results

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|-----------|-------|------|
| $Q_{hot}$ | 10,451 | W |
| $Q_{cold}$ | 12,540 | W |
| $Q_{avg}$ | 11,495.5 | W |
| LMTD | 30 | ┬░C |
| **U** | **766.4** | **W/m┬▓┬╖K** |
| Effectiveness (╬╡) | 0.40 | тАУ |
| NTU | 0.55 | тАУ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of STHE Performance Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Hot water tank with heater] --> B[Hot water pump]
    B --> C[Flow control valve Hot side]
    C --> D[Hot fluid inlet rotameter]
    D --> E[Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger]
    E --> F[Hot fluid outlet temperature T_h,o]
    E --> G[Cold fluid outlet temperature T_c,o]
    E --> H[Cold fluid inlet temperature T_c,i]
    E --> I[Cold fluid inlet rotameter]
    I --> J[Flow control valve Cold side]
    J --> K[Cold water pump]
    K --> L[Cold water storage tank]
    E --> M[Measure all four temperatures]
    M --> N[Compute mass flow rates]
    N --> O[Compute heat duty Q]
    O --> P[Compute LMTD]
    P --> Q[Compute Overall U]
    Q --> R[Compute Effectiveness]
    R --> S[Compute NTU]
    S --> T[Plot Q vs flow rate]
    S --> U[Plot U vs flow rate]
    S --> V[Generate Performance Report]
```

## 4.2 Heat Exchanger Internal Cross-Section Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot fluid inlet] --> B[Tube bundle inside shell]
    B --> C[Hot fluid outlet]
    D[Cold fluid inlet] --> E[Shell side - baffles]
    E --> F[Cold fluid outlet]
    G[Baffles direct shell flow] --> E
    H[Tubes carry hot fluid] --> B
    I[Heat transfers through tube wall] --> B
```

## 4.3 Energy Balance Schematic (Counter-Flow STHE)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot fluid INLET T_h,i] --> B[Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger]
    B --> C[Hot fluid OUTLET T_h,o]
    D[Cold fluid INLET T_c,i] --> E[Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger]
    E --> F[Cold fluid OUTLET T_c,o]
    G[Heat Q transfers from hot to cold] --> B
    H[Q = m_h c_p T_h,i minus T_h,o] --> A
    I[Q = m_c c_p T_c,o minus T_c,i] --> D
```

## 4.4 LMTD Calculation Logic Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Record four temperatures] --> B{Identify flow type}
    B -->|Counter flow| C[Delta T1 = T_h,i minus T_c,o]
    B -->|Parallel flow| D[Delta T1 = T_h,i minus T_c,i]
    C --> E[Delta T2 = T_h,o minus T_c,i]
    D --> F[Delta T2 = T_h,o minus T_c,o]
    E --> G{Both Delta T positive?}
    F --> G
    G -->|Yes| H[Apply LMTD = Delta T1 minus Delta T2 over ln Delta T1 by Delta T2]
    G -->|No| I[Apply LMTD correction factor F]
    H --> J[Compute U = Q by A_s times LMTD]
    I --> J
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define LMTD. Why is it preferred over arithmetic mean temperature difference in heat exchanger analysis?**

**Model Answer:**
LMTD stands for **Log Mean Temperature Difference**. It is defined as:

$$
\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

It is preferred over arithmetic mean temperature difference (AMTD) because:
1. It represents the **true average temperature driving force** along the length of the heat exchanger, accounting for the nonlinear (logarithmic) variation of ╬ФT with area.
2. AMTD is accurate only when $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$ (within ~40%); LMTD is universally valid.
3. LMTD arises directly from integration of the differential heat transfer equation $dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T$, making it the rigorous design quantity.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is the effectiveness of a heat exchanger? Write its formula.**

**Model Answer:**
Effectiveness (╬╡) is defined as the **ratio of actual heat transfer rate to the maximum possible heat transfer rate** in a heat exchanger:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}}
$$

Where:

$$
Q_{max} = C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})
$$

The value of ╬╡ ranges from **0 to 1**. A higher value indicates better heat exchanger performance.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**In a shell and tube heat exchanger experiment, the following readings were recorded:**

| Parameter | Hot Fluid (Water) | Cold Fluid (Water) |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------|
| Inlet temperature | $T_{h,i} = 80┬░C$ | $T_{c,i} = 30┬░C$ |
| Outlet temperature | $T_{h,o} = 60┬░C$ | $T_{c,o} = 50┬░C$ |
| Volumetric flow rate | 15 L/min | 20 L/min |
| Specific heat | 4.18 kJ/kg┬╖K | 4.18 kJ/kg┬╖K |
| Density | 1000 kg/m┬│ | 1000 kg/m┬│ |

**Heat transfer area = 0.4 m┬▓. Flow configuration: Counter-flow.**

**(a)** Calculate the heat transfer rate (Q) and overall heat transfer coefficient (U).  
**(b)** Determine the effectiveness and NTU of the heat exchanger. Comment on whether the performance is satisfactory.

#### Solution (a): Heat Transfer Rate and U

**Step 1:** Mass flow rates:

$$
\dot{m}_h = \frac{1000 \times 15 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 0.2500 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_c = \frac{1000 \times 20 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 0.3333 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Heat duties:

$$
Q_h = 0.2500 \times 4180 \times (80 - 60) = 20,900 \text{ W}
$$

$$
Q_c = 0.3333 \times 4180 \times (50 - 30) = 27,866 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3:** Average heat duty (to account for heat losses):

$$
Q_{avg} = \frac{20900 + 27866}{2} = 24,383 \text{ W} \approx 24.38 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4:** LMTD (counter-flow):

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 80 - 50 = 30┬░C
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 60 - 30 = 30┬░C
$$

Since both ╬ФT are equal, LMTD = 30┬░C.

**Step 5:** Overall U:

$$
U = \frac{Q_{avg}}{A_s \cdot \text{LMTD}} = \frac{24383}{0.4 \times 30} = 2031.9 \text{ W/m}^2\text{┬╖K}
$$

#### Solution (b): Effectiveness and NTU

**Step 1:** Heat capacity rates:

$$
C_h = 0.2500 \times 4180 = 1045 \text{ W/K}
$$

$$
C_c = 0.3333 \times 4180 = 1393.3 \text{ W/K}
$$

$C_{min} = 1045$ W/K (hot side), $C_{max} = 1393.3$ W/K, $C^* = C_{min}/C_{max} = 0.75$.

**Step 2:** Effectiveness:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{C_h (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o})}{C_{min} (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})} = \frac{1045 \times 20}{1045 \times 50} = 0.40 \text{ or } 40\%
$$

**Step 3:** NTU:

$$
\text{NTU} = \frac{U \cdot A_s}{C_{min}} = \frac{2031.9 \times 0.4}{1045} = 0.778
$$

**Comment:** An effectiveness of 40% with NTU = 0.78 is typical for a small laboratory STHE. For industrial STHEs, effectiveness is usually 50тАУ80%. Performance can be improved by:
- Increasing heat transfer area
- Increasing turbulence (higher Reynolds number)
- Using enhanced surfaces (finned tubes)

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**A counter-flow shell and tube heat exchanger is used to cool oil using water. The following data are given:**

- Hot oil: $T_{h,i} = 150┬░C$, $T_{h,o} = 100┬░C$, $\dot{m}_h = 1.0$ kg/s, $c_{p,h} = 2.1$ kJ/kg┬╖K
- Cold water: $T_{c,i} = 30┬░C$, $T_{c,o} = 80┬░C$, $\dot{m}_c = 1.5$ kg/s, $c_{p,c} = 4.18$ kJ/kg┬╖K
- Heat transfer area: $A_s = 5$ m┬▓

**(a)** Compute the heat transfer rate, LMTD, and overall heat transfer coefficient.  
**(b)** If the hot water flow rate is increased to 2.5 kg/s while keeping all inlet temperatures same, find the new outlet temperature of water and comment on the change in effectiveness.

#### Solution (a): Q, LMTD, and U

**Step 1:** Heat duty:

$$
Q_h = 1.0 \times 2100 \times (150 - 100) = 105,000 \text{ W} = 105 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
Q_c = 1.5 \times 4180 \times (80 - 30) = 313,500 \text{ W} = 313.5 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2:** Average Q:

$$
Q_{avg} = \frac{105000 + 313500}{2} = 209,250 \text{ W}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** The large difference between $Q_h$ and $Q_c$ (105 kW vs 313.5 kW) suggests a data inconsistency in the problem. In real practice, $Q_h \approx Q_c$. The student should highlight this discrepancy in the answer.

**Step 3:** LMTD (counter-flow):

$$
\Delta T_1 = 150 - 80 = 70┬░C, \quad \Delta T_2 = 100 - 30 = 70┬░C
$$

LMTD = 70┬░C.

**Step 4:** U:

$$
U = \frac{209250}{5 \times 70} = 597.86 \text{ W/m}^2\text{┬╖K}
$$

#### Solution (b): New Outlet Temperature

Assuming U remains approximately constant (typical first approximation in KTU problems), and with the new cold water flow rate $\dot{m}_c^{new} = 2.5$ kg/s:

**Step 1:** New heat capacity rate (cold side):

$$
C_c^{new} = 2.5 \times 4180 = 10,450 \text{ W/K}
$$

**Step 2:** Heat capacity rate (hot side, unchanged):

$$
C_h = 1.0 \times 2100 = 2100 \text{ W/K}
$$

$C_{min} = 2100$ W/K (hot side now limits).

**Step 3:** Energy balance тАФ using $Q = C_h (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}^{new})$:

For the new scenario, with higher cold flow, the cold water outlet temperature will **decrease** because more fluid is heated by the same amount of energy. Assume hot oil outlet temperature remains around 100┬░C (a reasonable engineering assumption for first analysis):

$$
Q_{new} = C_h (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}^{new}) = 2100 \times (150 - 100) = 105,000 \text{ W}
$$

$$
T_{c,o}^{new} = T_{c,i} + \frac{Q_{new}}{C_c^{new}} = 30 + \frac{105000}{10450} = 30 + 10.05 = 40.05┬░C
$$

**Step 4:** New effectiveness:

$$
\varepsilon_{new} = \frac{T_{c,o}^{new} - T_{c,i}}{T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}} = \frac{40.05 - 30}{150 - 30} = \frac{10.05}{120} = 0.084 = 8.4\%
$$

**Comment:** Increasing the cold water flow rate from 1.5 to 2.5 kg/s **decreases the effectiveness** from a higher value to ~8.4%. This is because more fluid needs to be heated by the same amount of energy, resulting in a smaller temperature rise. However, the **total heat duty** can also change тАФ careful energy balance is essential.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert volumetric flow to mass flow** is the #1 mistake (loses 2 marks).
> 2. **Mixing up ╬ФTтВБ and ╬ФTтВВ for parallel vs counter-flow** is a common 1-mark error.
> 3. **Not using average Q** for U calculation тАФ both sides should be averaged.
> 4. **Ignoring the heat loss assumption** in the energy balance (3-mark penalty).
> 5. **Forgetting to identify the limiting fluid (Cmin)** in effectiveness calculation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**
>
> 1. **STHE definition:** Two fluids separated by a tube wall; tube-side and shell-side.
> 2. **Counter-flow** gives the highest LMTD and effectiveness тАФ preferred in design.
> 3. **LMTD equation** is universally applicable: $\text{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$.
> 4. **Special case:** If $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, LMTD = ╬ФT (logarithmic mean equals arithmetic mean).
> 5. **Heat balance:** $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ on both hot and cold sides. Always average them.
> 6. **Overall U equation:** $U = Q / (A_s \cdot \text{LMTD})$; units W/m┬▓┬╖K.
> 7. **Effectiveness** ranges 0 to 1; $C_{min} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p$ of the limiting fluid.
> 8. **NTU** = $U \cdot A_s / C_{min}$ тАФ dimensionless design parameter.
> 9. **Resistance network:** $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{r_o}{k \cdot r_i} + \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$
> 10. **Correction factor F:** For multi-pass or cross-flow exchangers, $Q = U \cdot A_s \cdot F \cdot \text{LMTD}_{CF}$.
> 11. **Water-water STHE** typically has U = 1000тАУ3000 W/m┬▓┬╖K.
> 12. **Experimental errors** arise from heat loss to surroundings, instrument calibration, and unsteady-state conditions.
> 13. **Use SI units consistently** (W for Q, kg/s for m, m┬▓ for A, K or ┬░C for T).
> 14. **For plotting:** Q vs hot water flow rate shows linear increase; U vs flow rate typically increases logarithmically.
> 15. **For KTU lab record:** Always include aim, apparatus, procedure, observations (with units), calculations, graphs, result, and sources of error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Development of heat transfer correlation for heat exchangers/condenser using modified Wilson plot method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL507 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-1
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Development of heat transfer correlation for heat exchangers/condenser using modified Wilson plot method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Modified Wilson Plot Method** is an indirect, semi-empirical graphical-experimental technique used in thermal engineering to **develop a heat transfer correlation** (typically of the form $h_o = C \cdot v_g^n$) for the **shell-side (condensing-vapor side)** of a shell-and-tube heat exchanger or condenser. The method works by experimentally measuring the **overall heat transfer coefficient ($U$)** at several operating conditions, mathematically subtracting the **tube-side convective resistance ($1/h_i$)** тАФ computed from a standard forced-convection correlation тАФ and then graphically separating the remaining **shell-side convective resistance ($1/h_o$)** from the **fouling resistance ($R_f$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> The modification over the classical *EE Wilson (1915)* plot is the explicit inclusion of the **vapor/gas mass-velocity ($v_g$)** on the shell side as an independent variable. In the original Wilson plot, the shell-side coefficient was assumed constant, which is invalid when the condensing vapor velocity is high or when non-condensable gases are present.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a multi-layered windowpane: an inner glass sheet, an air gap, an outer glass sheet, and a thin film of dirt on the outside. You can easily measure the *total* heat loss through the entire window, but you want to know how much resistance is offered by just the dirt film, so you can decide when to clean it.

- **The "window"** = heat exchanger
- **The "total heat loss"** = measured $1/U$
- **The "glass sheets"** = known (calculable) tube-side and shell-side convective resistances
- **The "dirt film"** = unknown fouling resistance $R_f$

The trick: blow air (vary vapor velocity) on the outside. The convective loss on the outside *changes*, but the dirt's resistance *does not*. By plotting cleverly and extrapolating to "infinite wind speed" (where convective resistance $\to 0$), the intercept on the y-axis reveals the dirt's resistance alone. This is the essence of the Wilson-plot philosophy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constants / metrics used in this lab:**
> - $h_{fg}$ of water at 100 ┬░C = **2257 kJ/kg**
> - $c_{p,w} \approx$ **4.18 kJ/(kg┬╖K)**
> - $D_i$ (tube inner diameter), $D_o$ (tube outer diameter) тАФ measured
> - $A_o = \pi D_o L N_t$ тАФ heat-transfer area based on **outside diameter**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation тАФ Why It Works

For any heat exchanger, the total thermal resistance between the two bulk fluids is the sum of *individual* resistances in series:

$$\frac{1}{U_o A_o} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{R_{f,i}}{A_i} + \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 \pi k L N_t} + \frac{R_{f,o}}{A_o} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o}$$

For clean, thin-walled tubes with negligible wall resistance and lumped inside/outside fouling ($R_f$ тАФ referred to the outside area), this simplifies to the working equation:

$$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{D_o}{D_i \, h_i} + \frac{1}{h_o} + R_f \quad \text{...(Eq. 1)}$$

The **modified Wilson plot** assumes a power-law form for the shell-side coefficient:

$$h_o = C \cdot v_g^n \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{C \cdot v_g^n} \quad \text{...(Eq. 2)}$$

where $v_g$ is the mass velocity (kg/m┬▓┬╖s) of the vapor/non-condensable gas on the shell side, and $C, n$ are the empirical constants we wish to determine.

Substituting Eq. (2) into Eq. (1):

$$\boxed{\underbrace{\left(\frac{1}{U_o} - \frac{D_o}{D_i h_i}\right)}_{Y\text{-axis}} = \underbrace{\frac{1}{C}}_{{\text{slope}}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1}{v_g^n}}_{X\text{-axis}} + \underbrace{R_f}_{\text{intercept}}}$$

This is a **straight line** in a plot of $Y$ vs. $1/v_g^n$. Linear regression gives us:

- **Intercept (at $1/v_g^n = 0$)** $\Rightarrow R_f$ (fouling resistance, m┬▓┬╖K/W)
- **Slope** $\Rightarrow 1/C \Rightarrow C$, and hence the correlation $h_o = C \cdot v_g^n$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "modified" term refers to two key changes from the classical Wilson plot:
> 1. The y-axis uses $\left(\frac{1}{U} - \frac{D_o}{D_i h_i}\right)$ instead of just $1/U$, isolating the *shell-side* resistances.
> 2. The x-axis uses $1/v_g^n$ instead of $1/v^{0.8}$ (tube velocity), because the *shell-side* coefficient (not the tube-side) is the unknown being correlated.

### 2.2 Operational Logic тАФ Step by Step

1. **Steady-state operation** at $N$ different cooling-water flow rates ($m_w$) and corresponding steam/gas flow rates ($m_g$).
2. **Measure** inlet and outlet temperatures of water ($T_{c,i}, T_{c,o}$) and of the steam-side ($\approx T_{sat}$).
3. **Energy balance** on water side: $Q = \dot{m}_w c_{p,w} (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$.
4. **LMTD** calculation: $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{(T_{sat} - T_{c,i}) - (T_{sat} - T_{c,o})}{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{T_{sat} - T_{c,i}}{T_{sat} - T_{c,o}}\right]}$.
5. **Experimental $U_o$** from: $U_o = \dfrac{Q}{A_o \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}$.
6. **Calculate $h_i$** from DittusтАУBoelter (heating): $Nu_i = 0.023 \, Re_i^{0.8} Pr_i^{0.4}$.
7. **Compute $Y_i = \dfrac{1}{U_o} - \dfrac{D_o}{D_i h_i}$** for each run.
8. **Compute $v_g = \dot{m}_g / A_{shell}$** for each run; assume an initial $n$ (often $n \approx 0.5$ for condensation; iterate if required).
9. **Plot** $Y$ vs. $1/v_g^n$ on a linear scale; draw best-fit line.
10. **Read off** $R_f$ (intercept) and $1/C$ (slope); refine $n$ by minimizing residual error.
11. **Final correlation:** $h_o = C \cdot v_g^n$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m}_w c_{p,w} (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$ | Heat duty from water side | W or kW |
| 2 | $Q = \dot{m}_s h_{fg}$ | Heat duty from steam side (cross-check) | W or kW |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference | K or ┬░C |
| 4 | $U_o = \dfrac{Q}{A_o \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}$ | Overall HTC (outside basis) | W/(m┬▓┬╖K) |
| 5 | $A_o = \pi D_o L N_t$ | Heat-transfer area (outside) | m┬▓ |
| 6 | $Re_i = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}_w}{\pi D_i \mu_w N_t}$ | Reynolds number inside tubes | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Pr_i = \dfrac{\mu_w c_{p,w}}{k_w}$ | Prandtl number of water | dimensionless |
| 8 | $Nu_i = 0.023 \, Re_i^{0.8} Pr_i^{0.4}$ | DittusтАУBoelter (heating) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $h_i = \dfrac{Nu_i \cdot k_w}{D_i}$ | Tube-side HTC | W/(m┬▓┬╖K) |
| 10 | $1/U_o = (D_o/D_i)(1/h_i) + 1/h_o + R_f$ | Resistance in series | m┬▓┬╖K/W |
| 11 | $h_o = C \cdot v_g^n$ | **Target correlation** | W/(m┬▓┬╖K) |
| 12 | $v_g = \dot{m}_g / A_{cs,shell}$ | Vapor/gas mass velocity | kg/(m┬▓┬╖s) |
| 13 | $\dfrac{1}{U_o} - \dfrac{D_o}{D_i h_i} = \dfrac{1}{C \cdot v_g^n} + R_f$ | **Wilson-plot linear form** | m┬▓┬╖K/W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The modified Wilson plot is the *de-facto* industry tool for:
> - **Rating existing shell-and-tube condensers** (e.g., HVAC, refrigeration, power-plant condensers) when manufacturer data is unavailable.
> - **Performance monitoring** of condensers over time to track fouling build-up ($R_f$ increasing тЗТ cleaning due).
> - **Validating CFD / simulation predictions** of condensation HTCs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of the Modified Wilson-Plot Linear Form

We start from the **resistance-in-series** model (lumped-fouling, negligible wall):

$$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{D_o}{D_i} \cdot \frac{1}{h_i} + R_f \quad \text{...(D.1)}$$

Rearranging to isolate shell-side and fouling resistances on the RHS:

$$\frac{1}{U_o} - \frac{D_o}{D_i \, h_i} = \frac{1}{h_o} + R_f \quad \text{...(D.2)}$$

Assume the **shell-side HTC** follows a power-law in vapor mass velocity $v_g$:

$$h_o = C \cdot v_g^{\,n} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{C} \cdot v_g^{-n} \quad \text{...(D.3)}$$

Substitute (D.3) into (D.2):

$$\frac{1}{U_o} - \frac{D_o}{D_i \, h_i} \;=\; \frac{1}{C} \cdot v_g^{-n} \;+\; R_f \quad \text{...(D.4)}$$

Define the LHS as the **Wilson ordinate** $Y$ and the RHS first term's coefficient as the **Wilson abscissa** $X$:

$$Y = \underbrace{\left(\frac{1}{C}\right)}_{\text{slope}} \cdot \underbrace{\left(\frac{1}{v_g^{\,n}}\right)}_{X} + \underbrace{R_f}_{\text{intercept}} \quad \text{...(D.5)}$$

This is the equation of a straight line. The exponent $n$ is **not known a priori** for condensation, so a **trial-and-error / regression** is performed:

- Try $n = 0.4,\ 0.5,\ 0.6,\ 0.7$тАж
- For each $n$, compute $X_i = 1/v_{g,i}^{\,n}$, perform linear regression of $Y$ on $X$.
- The "$n$" giving the **highest $R^2$** (closest to 1) is the correct exponent.

Finally:

$$\boxed{h_o = C \cdot v_g^{\,n} \quad \text{with} \quad R_f \text{ read from intercept}}$$

### 3.2 Illustrative Worked Example

**Statement:** A horizontal shell-and-tube steam condenser has the following specifications and steady-state data. Develop the shell-side correlation using the modified Wilson plot.

**Tube-side:** cooling water, $c_{p,w} = 4.18$ kJ/(kg┬╖K), $T_{c,i} = 25\ ^\circ$C in all trials.
**Shell-side:** saturated steam at $T_{sat} = 100\ ^\circ$C, $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg.
**Geometry:** $D_i = 0.020$ m, $D_o = 0.025$ m, $L = 2$ m, $N_t = 10$ tubes (single pass), shell cross-sectional area $A_{sh} = 0.008$ m┬▓.

| Trial | $\dot{m}_w$ (kg/s) | $T_{c,o}$ (┬░C) | $\dot{m}_s$ (kg/h) |
|:-----:|:------------------:|:--------------:|:------------------:|
| 1 | 0.20 | 45.0 | 5.00 |
| 2 | 0.30 | 41.5 | 5.00 |
| 3 | 0.45 | 38.0 | 5.00 |
| 4 | 0.60 | 35.5 | 5.00 |
| 5 | 0.80 | 33.5 | 5.00 |

**Step 1 тАФ Heat duty** $Q = \dot{m}_w c_{p,w} (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$:

| Trial | $Q$ (kW) |
|:-----:|:--------:|
| 1 | $0.20 \times 4.18 \times 20.0 = 16.72$ |
| 2 | $0.30 \times 4.18 \times 16.5 = 20.69$ |
| 3 | $0.45 \times 4.18 \times 13.0 = 24.45$ |
| 4 | $0.60 \times 4.18 \times 10.5 = 26.33$ |
| 5 | $0.80 \times 4.18 \times 8.5 = 28.42$ |

**Step 2 тАФ Heat-transfer area** $A_o = \pi D_o L N_t = \pi \times 0.025 \times 2 \times 10 = 1.571$ m┬▓.

**Step 3 тАФ LMTD** (condenser, shell-side at constant $T_{sat}$):

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{(T_{sat}-T_{c,i}) - (T_{sat}-T_{c,o})}{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{T_{sat}-T_{c,i}}{T_{sat}-T_{c,o}}\right]} = \frac{75 - (T_{sat}-T_{c,o})}{\ln\!\left[75/(T_{sat}-T_{c,o})\right]}$$

| Trial | $\Delta T_2 = 100-T_{c,o}$ (┬░C) | $\Delta T_{lm}$ (┬░C) |
|:-----:|:-------------------------------:|:--------------------:|
| 1 | 55.0 | $\dfrac{20}{\ln(75/55)} = 64.47$ |
| 2 | 58.5 | $\dfrac{16.5}{\ln(75/58.5)} = 66.40$ |
| 3 | 62.0 | $\dfrac{13}{\ln(75/62)} = 68.31$ |
| 4 | 64.5 | $\dfrac{10.5}{\ln(75/64.5)} = 69.63$ |
| 5 | 66.5 | $\dfrac{8.5}{\ln(75/66.5)} = 70.77$ |

**Step 4 тАФ Experimental $U_o$** $U_o = Q / (A_o \cdot \Delta T_{lm})$:

| Trial | $U_o$ (W/m┬▓┬╖K) |
|:-----:|:--------------:|
| 1 | $16720 / (1.571 \times 64.47) = 165.1$ |
| 2 | $20690 / (1.571 \times 66.40) = 198.4$ |
| 3 | $24450 / (1.571 \times 68.31) = 227.9$ |
| 4 | $26330 / (1.571 \times 69.63) = 240.7$ |
| 5 | $28420 / (1.571 \times 70.77) = 255.6$ |

**Step 5 тАФ Tube-side $h_i$** using DittusтАУBoelter, evaluated at mean bulk temperature $T_m = (T_{c,i}+T_{c,o})/2$ with water properties interpolated from standard tables (showing Trial 1 in detail; others follow identically):

For Trial 1, $T_m = (25+45)/2 = 35\ ^\circ$C, so $\mu_w \approx 7.20 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa┬╖s, $k_w \approx 0.625$ W/(m┬╖K), $Pr \approx 4.83$.

$$Re_i = \frac{4 \dot{m}_w}{\pi D_i \mu_w N_t} = \frac{4 \times 0.20}{\pi \times 0.020 \times 7.20\times 10^{-4} \times 10} = 1768$$

$$Nu_i = 0.023 \times (1768)^{0.8} \times (4.83)^{0.4} \approx 0.023 \times 295.2 \times 1.892 \approx 12.85$$

$$h_i = \frac{Nu_i \cdot k_w}{D_i} = \frac{12.85 \times 0.625}{0.020} = 401.6 \ \text{W/(m┬▓┬╖K)}$$

Repeating for all trials and tabulating the corrected term $(D_o/D_i)(1/h_i)$:

| Trial | $h_i$ (W/m┬▓┬╖K) | $\frac{D_o}{D_i}\cdot\frac{1}{h_i}$ (m┬▓┬╖K/W) |
|:-----:|:--------------:|:-------------------------------------------:|
| 1 | 401.6 | $1.25/401.6 = 3.11 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 2 | 568.4 | $2.20 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 3 | 802.5 | $1.56 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 4 | 1020.9 | $1.22 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 5 | 1300.1 | $9.61 \times 10^{-4}$ |

**Step 6 тАФ Wilson ordinate** $Y = 1/U_o - (D_o/D_i)(1/h_i)$:

| Trial | $1/U_o$ | $Y$ (m┬▓┬╖K/W) |
|:-----:|:-------:|:------------:|
| 1 | $6.06 \times 10^{-3}$ | $2.95 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 2 | $5.04 \times 10^{-3}$ | $2.84 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 3 | $4.39 \times 10^{-3}$ | $2.83 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 4 | $4.15 \times 10^{-3}$ | $2.93 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 5 | $3.91 \times 10^{-3}$ | $2.95 \times 10^{-3}$ |

**Step 7 тАФ Vapor mass velocity** $v_g = \dot{m}_s / (3600 \cdot A_{sh}) = 5/(3600 \times 0.008) = 0.174$ kg/(m┬▓┬╖s) тАФ *constant* in this example.

Because $v_g$ is held constant, the abscissa $X = 1/v_g^n$ is the same for all points and the plot degenerates to a horizontal scatter band. To make a meaningful Wilson plot, **$v_g$ must be varied** between trials (e.g., by bleeding different steam flow rates into the shell, or by adding non-condensable gas at controlled rates). For the present illustration, let us **re-define Trials 1тАУ5 with $v_g$ varying from 0.10 to 0.50 kg/(m┬▓┬╖s)**, and assume the linear regression on $Y$ vs. $1/v_g^{0.5}$ yields:

$$Y = 0.0042 \, \left(\frac{1}{v_g^{0.5}}\right) + 1.85 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 8 тАФ Read off parameters:**

- **Intercept** $R_f = 1.85 \times 10^{-3}$ m┬▓┬╖K/W
- **Slope** $1/C = 0.0042$ $\Rightarrow C = 238.1$ W/(m┬▓┬╖K) per (kg/m┬▓┬╖s)$^n$

**Step 9 тАФ Final correlation:**

$$\boxed{\,h_o = 238.1 \, v_g^{\,0.5} \quad [\text{W/(m┬▓┬╖K)}], \quad R_f = 1.85 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m┬▓┬╖K/W}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** For $v_g = 0.2$ kg/(m┬▓┬╖s), the correlation gives $h_o \approx 238.1 \times 0.447 = 106.4$ W/(m┬▓┬╖K), which is of the right order for **dropwise/film condensation** of low-pressure steam in a horizontal shell-and-tube unit (typical range 50тАУ500 W/m┬▓┬╖K).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix тАФ Sources of Error & Mitigation

| Source of Error | Effect on $U_o$ | Mitigation |
|-----------------|-----------------|------------|
| Inlet/outlet thermocouple drift | Wrong $Q$, wrong $\Delta T_{lm}$ | Calibrate before each lab; use RTD ┬▒0.1 ┬░C |
| Non-uniform flow distribution among tubes | Local $Re_i$ deviates from mean | Use calibrated orifice + adequate straight pipe length |
| Sub-cooling of condensate (steam not saturated) | Overestimates $Q$ on steam side | Use steam-side $Q = \dot{m}_s h_{fg}$ only; cross-check |
| Heat loss to surroundings | Underestimates $U_o$ | Insulate shell; apply heat-loss correction (~3тАУ5 %) |
| Non-condensable gas accumulation | Suppresses $h_o$ | Continuously vent shell; record vent rate |
| Property drift with $T_m$ | Wrong $h_i$ from DittusтАУBoelter | Evaluate $\mu, k, Pr$ at *local* $T_m$ for each run |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the Modified Wilson-Plot Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Start Condenser at Steady State] --> B[Step2: Fix Cooling Water Flow mw]
    B --> C[Step3: Set Shell-Side Vapor Mass Velocity vg]
    C --> D[Step4: Record Tc,i and Tc,o and Steam Mass Flow]
    D --> E[Step5: Calculate Heat Duty Q = mw cp dT]
    E --> F[Step6: Calculate LMTD]
    F --> G[Step7: Calculate Overall HTC Uo = Q / Ao LMTD]
    G --> H[Step8: Calculate Tube-Side hi via Dittus Boelter]
    H --> I[Step9: Form Wilson Ordinate Y = 1/Uo - Do over Di hi]
    I --> J[Step10: Form Abscissa X = 1 over vg to the power n]
    J --> K[Step11: Plot Y versus X on Linear Graph]
    K --> L[Step12: Perform Linear Regression]
    L --> M[Step13: Extract Intercept Rf and Slope 1 over C]
    M --> N[Step14: Refine Exponent n for Maximum R2]
    N --> O[Step15: Final Correlation ho = C vg power n]
    O --> P[End of Procedure]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of Resistance Network and Wilson-Plot Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph R1[Tube-Side Resistance]
        T1[hi convective] --> T2[Do by Di scaling]
    end
    subgraph R2[Shell-Side Resistance]
        S1[ho convective = C vg to n] --> S2[Interpolable from slope]
    end
    subgraph R3[Fouling Resistance]
        F1[Rf constant] --> F2[Read as intercept]
    end
    R1 --> SUM[Sum 1 by Uo]
    R2 --> SUM
    R3 --> SUM
    SUM --> RESULT[Wilson's linearised plot: Y = 1 over C X + Rf]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Lab Rig

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BOILER[Steam Generator with Pressure Control]
        B1[Heater] --> B2[Saturated Steam Line]
    end
    B2 --> VLV1[Control Valve V1]
    VLV1 --> SHELL[Shell Side of Condenser at Tsat]
    SHELL --> VENT[Vent for Non Condensables]
    SHELL --> COND[Condensate Drain with Level Control]
    subgraph TUBE[Tube Side Coolant Circuit]
        TANK[Water Storage Tank] --> PUMP[Centrifugal Pump]
        PUMP --> FM[Orifice Flow Meter]
        FM --> TUBEIN[Tube Inlet Manifold]
        TUBEIN --> TUBEOUT[Tube Outlet Manifold]
        TUBEOUT --> TANK
    end
    T1[Tc,i RTD] -. Temperature .-> TUBEIN
    T2[Tc,o RTD] -. Temperature .-> TUBEOUT
    T3[Tsat RTD] -. Temperature .-> SHELL
    T1 --> DAQ[Data Acquisition System]
    T2 --> DAQ
    T3 --> DAQ
    FM --> DAQ
    DAQ --> PC[PC for Wilson Plot Computation]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Wilson-Plot Sketch (Linearized Form)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AXES[Plot Axes]
        XAX[Abscissa X equals 1 over vg to the n] --> ORIG[Origin 0 0]
        ORIG --> YAX[Ordinate Y equals 1 by Uo minus Do over Di hi]
    end
    AXES --> LINE[Straight Best Fit Line]
    LINE --> INT[Intercept on Y axis equals Rf Fouling Resistance]
    LINE --> SLP[Slope equals 1 over C]
    SLP --> CORR[Correlation ho equals C vg to the n]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A тАФ Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

**Q.A1.** **[KTU University Exam тАФ May 2024 Model]**
*State the fundamental equation of the modified Wilson plot and identify the physical meaning of its slope and intercept.*

**Model Answer:**
The fundamental equation is

$$\frac{1}{U_o} - \frac{D_o}{D_i h_i} = \frac{1}{C} \cdot \frac{1}{v_g^{\,n}} + R_f$$

- **Slope** = $1/C$: yields the proportionality constant $C$ in the shell-side correlation $h_o = C\,v_g^{\,n}$.
- **Intercept** (when $1/v_g^{\,n} = 0$) = $R_f$: the lumped fouling resistance (m┬▓┬╖K/W) of the heat-exchanger surfaces.

---

**Q.A2.** **[KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023 Model]**
*Why is the classical Wilson plot unsuitable for a condenser with non-condensable gases, and how does the modified plot overcome this limitation?*

**Model Answer:**
The classical Wilson plot assumes the shell-side HTC $h_o$ to be **constant** and independent of the shell-side fluid velocity, which fails when the shell-side coefficient varies with the vapor/non-condensable-gas mass velocity. The **modified** Wilson plot:

1. Replaces the abscissa $1/v^{0.8}$ (tube-side velocity) with $1/v_g^{\,n}$ (shell-side vapor mass velocity).
2. Explicitly correlates $h_o = C\,v_g^{\,n}$ through the slope.
3. Thus captures the dependence of condensation HTC on the prevailing vapor/gas flow rate, which is essential when non-condensables are present.

---

### 5.2 Part B тАФ Descriptive / Numerical (14 marks each)

> **Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following Q1 or Q2.**

#### Q1 (a) + (b) тАФ *Experiment + Numerical*

**[KTU University Exam тАФ June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat line diagram, describe the **experimental setup** and **step-by-step procedure** to develop a heat-transfer correlation for a shell-and-tube condenser using the **modified Wilson plot method**. Clearly state the measurements to be recorded and the data-reduction equations. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus:** Saturated-steam shell-and-tube condenser, boiler/steam line with pressure control, cooling-water circulating pump, calibrated orifice/rotameter, RTD/thermocouples at $T_{c,i}, T_{c,o}, T_{sat}$, condensate collection tank, vent for non-condensables, data-acquisition system.

**Procedure:**
1. Start the cooling-water pump; set the desired $\dot{m}_w$ using the control valve.
2. Admit steam to the shell; set the desired vapor mass velocity $v_g$.
3. Open the vent to remove non-condensables.
4. Wait for **steady state** (typically 10тАУ15 min after each change).
5. Record: $T_{c,i}, T_{c,o}, T_{sat}, \dot{m}_w, \dot{m}_s$, shell pressure.
6. Repeat for **at least 5** different combinations of $\dot{m}_w$ and $v_g$.
7. Reduce the data:
   - $Q = \dot{m}_w c_{p,w}(T_{c,o}-T_{c,i})$
   - $\Delta T_{lm}$ from LMTD formula
   - $U_o = Q/(A_o\,\Delta T_{lm})$
   - $h_i$ from DittusтАУBoelter
   - $Y = 1/U_o - (D_o/D_i)(1/h_i)$
   - $X = 1/v_g^{\,n}$ (try $n = 0.5$ initially)
8. Plot $Y$ vs $X$; perform linear regression; refine $n$ for best $R^2$.
9. Report $h_o = C\,v_g^{\,n}$ and $R_f$.

---

**(b)** In a shell-and-tube condenser test, the following readings are obtained at steady state. Tube inner diameter $D_i = 0.020$ m, outer diameter $D_o = 0.025$ m, length $L = 2$ m, number of tubes $N_t = 10$. Steam condenses at 100 ┬░C ($h_{fg}=2257$ kJ/kg); cooling water enters at 25 ┬░C in all trials. Mean water temperature properties ($\mu_w = 7.2\times 10^{-4}$ Pa┬╖s, $k_w = 0.625$ W/(m┬╖K), $Pr = 4.83$) may be used. Calculate the shell-side correlation $h_o = C\,v_g^{\,n}$ and the fouling factor $R_f$. (7 marks)

| Trial | $\dot{m}_w$ (kg/s) | $T_{c,o}$ (┬░C) | $\dot{m}_s$ (kg/h) |
|:-----:|:------------------:|:--------------:|:------------------:|
| 1 | 0.30 | 50.0 | 4.0 |
| 2 | 0.50 | 44.0 | 6.0 |
| 3 | 0.70 | 40.0 | 7.5 |
| 4 | 0.90 | 37.0 | 9.0 |
| 5 | 1.10 | 35.0 | 10.0 |

Shell cross-section $A_{sh} = 0.010$ m┬▓.

**Model Solution (compact):**

**Step 1:** $A_o = \pi \times 0.025 \times 2 \times 10 = 1.571$ m┬▓.

**Step 2:** $Q = \dot{m}_w \times 4.18 \times (T_{c,o}-25)$:

| Trial | $Q$ (kW) | $\Delta T_{lm}$ (┬░C) | $U_o$ (W/m┬▓┬╖K) |
|:-----:|:--------:|:--------------------:|:--------------:|
| 1 | 31.35 | 60.97 | 327.3 |
| 2 | 39.71 | 64.93 | 389.3 |
| 3 | 43.89 | 66.93 | 417.5 |
| 4 | 45.14 | 68.10 | 421.9 |
| 5 | 45.98 | 68.84 | 425.3 |

**Step 3:** $h_i$ from DittusтАУBoelter (mean $T_m \approx 37.5$ ┬░C, properties as given):

| Trial | $Re_i$ | $Nu_i$ | $h_i$ (W/m┬▓┬╖K) |
|:-----:|:------:|:------:|:--------------:|
| 1 | 2652 | 17.6 | 549.4 |
| 2 | 4420 | 26.5 | 828.6 |
| 3 | 6188 | 34.6 | 1081.0 |
| 4 | 7956 | 42.2 | 1317.5 |
| 5 | 9724 | 49.4 | 1543.6 |

**Step 4:** $v_g = \dot{m}_s / (3600 \times 0.010)$:

| Trial | $v_g$ (kg/m┬▓┬╖s) | $1/v_g$ |
|:-----:|:---------------:|:-------:|
| 1 | 0.111 | 9.00 |
| 2 | 0.167 | 6.00 |
| 3 | 0.208 | 4.80 |
| 4 | 0.250 | 4.00 |
| 5 | 0.278 | 3.60 |

**Step 5:** Form $Y$:

| Trial | $1/U_o$ (├Ч10тБ╗┬│) | $(D_o/D_i)(1/h_i)$ (├Ч10тБ╗┬│) | $Y$ (├Ч10тБ╗┬│) |
|:-----:|:-----------------:|:--------------------------:|:-----------:|
| 1 | 3.06 | 2.27 | 0.79 |
| 2 | 2.57 | 1.51 | 1.06 |
| 3 | 2.40 | 1.16 | 1.24 |
| 4 | 2.37 | 0.95 | 1.42 |
| 5 | 2.35 | 0.81 | 1.54 |

**Step 6:** Linear regression of $Y$ on $X = 1/v_g$:

$$\boxed{Y = 0.165 \, X - 0.0007, \quad R^2 = 0.99}$$

**Step 7:** From regression:
- Slope $1/C = 0.165$ $\Rightarrow C = 6.06$ W/(m┬▓┬╖K) per (kg/m┬▓┬╖s)
- Intercept $R_f = -0.0007$ m┬▓┬╖K/W тЙИ **0** (clean exchanger)

**Final correlation (with $n = 1$):**

$$\boxed{h_o = 6.06 \, v_g \quad [\text{W/(m┬▓┬╖K)}]}$$

---

#### Q2 (a) + (b) тАФ *Theory + Numerical*

**[KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Starting from the fundamental resistance-in-series equation for a shell-and-tube heat exchanger, **derive the linearised form** of the modified Wilson plot. State clearly the assumptions made and explain the physical significance of the slope and intercept. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:** *(See Section 3.1 derivation D.1 тЖТ D.5 above.)*

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, constant-property operation.
2. Tube-wall thermal resistance is negligible.
3. Fouling is lumped into a single resistance $R_f$ referred to the outside area.
4. DittusтАУBoelter (or similar) accurately predicts the tube-side $h_i$.
5. The shell-side HTC obeys a power-law in $v_g$: $h_o = C\,v_g^{\,n}$.
6. Heat loss to surroundings is negligible (or corrected for).

**Significance:**
- **Slope** = $1/C$: a steeper slope means *weaker* dependence on vapor velocity (smaller $C$, larger $1/C$).
- **Intercept** = $R_f$: directly gives the fouling factor; positive intercept = fouled exchanger, zero = clean, negative = data scatter (physically impossible, so $R_f \geq 0$ is enforced).

---

**(b)** The overall heat transfer coefficient of a shell-and-tube condenser is found to be **500 W/(m┬▓┬╖K)** when the cooling-water mass velocity inside the tubes is **900 kg/(m┬▓┬╖s)**. From a modified Wilson plot of the same unit, the slope is **0.0048 m┬▓┬╖K/W per (kg/m┬▓┬╖s)тБ░┬╖тБ╡** and the intercept is **$8.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m┬▓┬╖K/W**. If the shell-side vapor mass velocity is **0.25 kg/(m┬▓┬╖s)** and the tube-side $h_i$ is **2500 W/(m┬▓┬╖K)** with $D_o/D_i = 1.25$, calculate:
(i) the shell-side HTC $h_o$,
(ii) the fouling factor $R_f$, and
(iii) comment on the cleanliness of the exchanger. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Shell-side HTC** $h_o = C\,v_g^{\,n}$ with $1/C = 0.0048 \Rightarrow C = 208.3$, $n = 0.5$:

$$h_o = 208.3 \times (0.25)^{0.5} = 208.3 \times 0.5 = 104.2 \ \text{W/(m┬▓┬╖K)}$$

**(ii) Fouling factor** (directly from intercept):

$$R_f = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m┬▓┬╖K/W}$$

**(iii) Check overall** $U_o$:

$$\frac{1}{U_o,\text{predicted}} = \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{D_o}{D_i}\frac{1}{h_i} + R_f = \frac{1}{104.2} + \frac{1.25}{2500} + 0.0008$$

$$= 9.596 \times 10^{-3} + 5.0 \times 10^{-4} + 0.800 \times 10^{-3} = 10.896 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m┬▓┬╖K/W}$$

$$U_o,\text{predicted} = 91.8 \ \text{W/(m┬▓┬╖K)}$$

The **measured** $U_o = 500$ W/(m┬▓┬╖K) is much higher than the **Wilson-predicted** 91.8 W/(m┬▓┬╖K). This inconsistency indicates either: (a) the correlation $h_o = C\,v_g^{0.5}$ was determined for a *different* operating regime, or (b) the operating data point lies **outside** the correlation range.

**(iii) Cleanliness:** A fouling factor of $8.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m┬▓┬╖K/W is **small** (typical "moderately clean" condensers in power plants have $R_f \sim 1.0\text{тАУ}3.0 \times 10^{-4}$ m┬▓┬╖K/W). Hence the unit is essentially **clean**, and the discrepancy must be traced to a wrong assumption or data set.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units mismatch in $Q$:** Many students mix kW and W; remember $1\,\text{kW} = 10^3\,\text{W}$ and use the same unit in $U = Q/(A\,\Delta T_{lm})$.
> 2. **Area confusion:** $A_o = \pi D_o L N_t$, $A_i = \pi D_i L N_t$. Use **the same basis** for $h_i$ and $h_o$ throughout (outside basis is convention).
> 3. **Sign of $R_f$:** Physically $R_f \geq 0$. If your regression intercept is negative, do **not** report it; refit forcing intercept $\geq 0$ or re-check your $h_i$ calculation.
> 4. **Forgetting the $D_o/D_i$ ratio:** The tube-side term in Eq. (1) carries a ratio тАФ skipping it leads to a 20тАУ30 % error.
> 5. **Steady state:** The single most-common experimental error; wait for $T_{c,o}$ to stabilise within $\pm 0.2$ ┬░C for 5 min before recording.
> 6. **Trial $n$:** Do not lock in $n=0.5$; try 0.4, 0.5, 0.6 and pick the $n$ with the best $R^2$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **modified Wilson plot** separates shell-side convective resistance from fouling resistance by exploiting the dependence of $h_o$ on vapor mass velocity.
- **Working equation:** $\dfrac{1}{U_o} - \dfrac{D_o}{D_i h_i} = \dfrac{1}{C} \cdot \dfrac{1}{v_g^{\,n}} + R_f$
- **Y-axis (ordinate):** measured overall resistance minus calculated tube-side resistance тЗТ shell-side + fouling.
- **X-axis (abscissa):** $1/v_g^{\,n}$ where $v_g = \dot{m}_s / A_{shell}$.
- **Intercept** $\Rightarrow R_f$ (fouling factor, m┬▓┬╖K/W).
- **Slope** $\Rightarrow 1/C \Rightarrow C$ in $h_o = C\,v_g^{\,n}$ (the target correlation).
- **Always** evaluate water properties at the *mean bulk* temperature $T_m = (T_{c,i}+T_{c,o})/2$.
- **LMTD** for a condenser: $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{(T_{sat}-T_{c,i}) - (T_{sat}-T_{c,o})}{\ln[(T_{sat}-T_{c,i})/(T_{sat}-T_{c,o})]}$
- **Heat-duty cross-check:** $Q_w = \dot{m}_w c_p (T_{c,o}-T_{c,i})$ should equal $Q_s = \dot{m}_s h_{fg}$ within $\pm 5\%$.
- **Exponent $n$** is not fixed; iterate for best $R^2$ (often $0.4 \le n \le 0.7$ for condensation; $0.5$ is a fair first guess).
- **Fouling is always $\ge 0$**; negative intercept тЗТ data scatter or wrong $h_i$ correlation.
- **DittusтАУBoelter** (heating): $Nu = 0.023\,Re^{0.8}\,Pr^{0.4}$; for cooling use exponent 0.3 on $Pr$.
- **Significance:** This is the **industry-standard** method for rating in-service condensers and tracking fouling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line memory hook for KTU viva:** *"Y minus tube = slope ├Ч 1 over vapor^n + fouling"* тАФ write it once, recall it forever.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of heat transfer coefficients in free convection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL507 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-1
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of heat transfer coefficients in free convection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Free (or Natural) Convection** is the mode of heat transfer between a solid surface and a fluid (liquid or gas) in which the fluid motion is generated *entirely* by the density differences arising from temperature gradients within the fluid itself тАФ **without any external agency** like a fan, pump, or blower. The bulk fluid movement is driven by the **buoyancy force** caused by the gravitational field acting on these density variations.

The **convective heat transfer coefficient** in free convection, denoted $h_c$ (W/m┬▓K), is defined by **Newton's Law of Cooling** as:

$$Q = h_c \, A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

where $T_s$ is the mean surface temperature of the body, $T_\infty$ is the free-stream (ambient) fluid temperature far from the surface, $A_s$ is the effective heat transfer area, and $Q$ is the rate of heat transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The lab experiment aims at the **experimental determination of $h_c$** for standard geometries (vertical plate, horizontal plate, or horizontal cylinder) by supplying a known electrical heat input and measuring the steady-state surface and ambient temperatures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hot pan kept on the kitchen counter without the gas flame on (i.e., the stove is off but the pan is still hot). The air just above the pan becomes warm and **rises up** like a hot-air balloon, while the cooler, denser air from the surroundings rushes in from the sides to take its place. This continuous circulation of warm air upwards and cool air sideways тАФ caused solely by *gravity acting on the warm (lighter) air* тАФ is **free convection**.

- If you switched on the chimney/exhaust fan, the same heat transfer would happen, but driven by the fan. That would be **forced convection**.
- The driving force here is the **buoyancy force** тАФ invisible, but always present wherever a fluid is heated or cooled in a gravitational field.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold-Highlighted)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value/Units |
|---|---|---|
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | **9.81 m/s┬▓** |
| $\beta$ | Volumetric thermal expansion coefficient (ideal gas) | $\beta = 1/T$ (**KтБ╗┬╣**) |
| $\nu$ | Kinematic viscosity of air | $\sim \mathbf{1.5 \times 10^{-5} \; m^2/s}$ at 300 K |
| $k_{air}$ | Thermal conductivity of air | $\sim \mathbf{0.026 \; W/m\cdot K}$ at 300 K |
| $Pr$ | Prandtl number of air | $\sim \mathbf{0.71}$ (nearly constant) |
| $C_p$ | Specific heat of air | $\sim \mathbf{1005 \; J/kg\cdot K}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Free convection heat transfer coefficients are **much smaller** than forced convection тАФ typically in the range **$h_c \approx 2 - 25 \; W/m^2K$** for air at atmospheric pressure, while forced convection may reach **$h_c \approx 25 - 250 \; W/m^2K$**.

## 1.4 Why This Experiment Matters (Engineering Context)

Free convection governs heat loss from many real engineering systems:

- **Transformer windings and electrical switchgear** dissipating heat to ambient air.
- **Building walls, windows, and roofs** losing heat to the environment.
- **Electronic equipment chassis** cooling passively.
- **Solar flat-plate collectors** losing heat to surroundings.
- **Reactor vessels, hot pipelines, and overhead tanks** in process industries.

An accurate value of $h_c$ is essential for thermal design, material selection, and safety analysis of all such equipment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

### Step A тАФ Why Does Free Convection Occur?
When a surface at temperature $T_s$ is in contact with a fluid at $T_\infty$ (with $T_s > T_\infty$), the fluid layers adjacent to the surface get heated. These heated layers experience a **reduction in density**, which under gravity creates an **upward buoyancy force** per unit volume:

$$\text{Buoyancy force per unit volume} = \rho \, g \, \beta \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

This force sets the fluid in motion, forming a **thermal boundary layer** and a **velocity boundary layer** along the surface.

### Step B тАФ The Boundary Layer Concept
- A **thermal boundary layer** of thickness $\delta_t$ develops, where temperature drops from $T_s$ (at wall) to $T_\infty$ (at edge).
- A **velocity boundary layer** of thickness $\delta$ develops in tandem, where velocity rises from zero (at wall, no-slip condition) to ambient (free-stream).
- For free convection: $\delta$ and $\delta_t$ are typically **comparable in magnitude** (different from forced convection where $\delta \gg \delta_t$).

### Step C тАФ Governing Dimensionless Groups
Dimensional analysis yields four key non-dimensional groups for free convection:

1. **Nusselt Number** тАФ ratio of convective to conductive thermal resistance at the surface:
$$Nu_L = \frac{h_c L}{k_f}$$
where $L$ is the characteristic length and $k_f$ is the fluid thermal conductivity evaluated at the **film temperature** $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$.

2. **Prandtl Number** тАФ ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity:
$$Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{\mu \, C_p}{k_f}$$
For air, $Pr \approx 0.71$.

3. **Grashof Number** тАФ ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces:
$$Gr_L = \frac{g \, \beta \, (T_s - T_\infty) \, L^3}{\nu^2}$$

4. **Rayleigh Number** тАФ product of Grashof and Prandtl:
$$Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr = \frac{g \, \beta \, (T_s - T_\infty) \, L^3 \, Pr}{\nu^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** $Nu$ is what we want; $Ra$ (or $Gr$) is what we measure. Empirical correlations express $Nu = f(Ra, Pr, \text{geometry})$.

### Step D тАФ Flow Regimes
- **Laminar regime:** $Ra_L < 10^9$ тАФ smooth, ordered flow.
- **Transition regime:** $10^9 \le Ra_L \le 10^{13}$ тАФ intermittent turbulence.
- **Turbulent regime:** $Ra_L > 10^{13}$ тАФ fully chaotic flow.

### Step E тАФ Newton's Law of Cooling & the Lab Equation
In the experiment, an electrical heater supplies known power $Q = V \cdot I$ to a test plate/cylinder. At steady state, this heat is lost from the surface by:
1. **Free convection** to surrounding air ($Q_c$)
2. **Radiation** to surroundings ($Q_r$)

If radiation is neglected (or absorbed into the experimental correlation by reporting an "apparent" $h$):

$$Q_{in} = V \cdot I = h_c \, A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

Hence, the experimental heat transfer coefficient is:

$$\boxed{h_{exp} = \frac{V \cdot I}{A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty)}}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = V I$ | Electrical heat input (steady state) | W |
| 2 | $Q = h_c A_s (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Newton's law of cooling | W |
| 3 | $h_{exp} = \dfrac{V I}{A_s \theta}$ | Experimental $h_c$ (where $\theta = T_s - T_\infty$) | W/m┬▓K |
| 4 | $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$ | Film temperature for property evaluation | K |
| 5 | $\beta = 1/T_f$ | Volumetric expansion coefficient (ideal gas) | KтБ╗┬╣ |
| 6 | $Nu_L = h_c L / k_f$ | Nusselt number | тАУ |
| 7 | $Pr = \mu C_p / k_f$ | Prandtl number | тАУ |
| 8 | $Gr_L = g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L^3 / \nu^2$ | Grashof number | тАУ |
| 9 | $Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr$ | Rayleigh number | тАУ |
| 10 | $Nu_{vert} = \{0.825 + 0.387 Ra_L^{1/6}/[1+(0.492/Pr)^{9/16}]^{8/27}\}^2$ | Churchill-Chu correlation (vertical plate) | тАУ |
| 11 | $Nu_{hor-cyl} = \{0.60 + 0.387 Ra_D^{1/6}/[1+(0.559/Pr)^{9/16}]^{8/27}\}^2$ | Churchill-Chu correlation (horizontal cylinder) | тАУ |
| 12 | $Bi = h_c L_c / k_s$ | Biot number (for lumped check) | тАУ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** When evaluating fluid properties (e.g., $k_f$, $\nu$, $Pr$), use the **film temperature** $T_f$, NOT $T_s$ or $T_\infty$. This is a frequent board-exam mark-loser.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Thermal design** of transformer radiators, busbars, and overhead lines where fans are infeasible.
- **Sizing of heat sinks** for electronic components in sealed enclosures.
- **Passive cooling** of nuclear spent-fuel pools and molten-salt reactors.
- **HVAC load calculations** for rooms with hot equipment.
- **Solar thermal collector efficiency** predictions.
- **Fire-safety engineering** тАФ predicting plume temperatures during compartment fires.
