# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Newton's Law to the Experimental $h_c$

The experiment uses an electrical resistance heater bonded to the back of a thin **vertical/horizontal test plate** (typically brass or mild steel). The front face of the plate is exposed to ambient air. Side and back faces are insulated.

### Energy Balance at Steady State

At steady state, the electrical power input equals the heat lost from the exposed front surface by free convection:

$$Q_{in} = Q_{out}$$

$$V \cdot I = h_c \, A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty) + Q_{rad}$$

**Step 1:** Rearranging for $h_c$ (ignoring radiation for the simplest case):

$$h_c = \frac{V \cdot I}{A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty)}$$

**Step 2:** Express the area. For a rectangular test plate of dimensions $L \times B$:

$$A_s = L \times B \quad [\text{m}^2]$$

For a horizontal cylinder of diameter $D$ and length $L$:

$$A_s = \pi \, D \, L \quad [\text{m}^2]$$

**Step 3:** Mean surface temperature. If the plate has $n$ thermocouples at temperatures $T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n$:

$$T_s = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$$

**Step 4:** Temperature difference:

$$\Delta T = \theta = T_s - T_\infty$$

**Step 5:** Substitute to obtain:

$$\boxed{h_{exp} = \frac{V \cdot I}{A_s \cdot \theta} \quad [\text{W/m}^2\text{K}]}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem Statement:**
A vertical brass test plate of dimensions **0.30 m ├Ч 0.30 m** is heated electrically. At steady state, the voltmeter reads **60 V**, ammeter reads **1.5 A**, and the mean surface temperature is **75 ┬░C**. The ambient air temperature is **30 ┬░C**. Calculate the experimental free convection heat transfer coefficient and compare it with the Churchill-Chu correlation. Air properties at film temperature: $k_f = 0.0288$ W/m┬╖K, $\nu = 1.896 \times 10^{-5}$ m┬▓/s, $Pr = 0.707$, $\beta = 1/(348) \approx 2.874 \times 10^{-3}$ KтБ╗┬╣.

### Solution

**Step 1 тАФ Heat input:**

$$Q = V \cdot I = 60 \times 1.5 = 90 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 2 тАФ Heat transfer area:**

$$A_s = 0.30 \times 0.30 = 0.09 \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 тАФ Temperature difference:**

$$\theta = T_s - T_\infty = 75 - 30 = 45 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 4 тАФ Experimental heat transfer coefficient:**

$$h_{exp} = \frac{Q}{A_s \cdot \theta} = \frac{90}{0.09 \times 45}$$

$$\boxed{h_{exp} = \frac{90}{4.05} = 22.22 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}}$$

**Step 5 тАФ Compute Rayleigh number:**

$$Gr_L = \frac{g \beta \theta L^3}{\nu^2} = \frac{9.81 \times 2.874 \times 10^{-3} \times 45 \times (0.30)^3}{(1.896 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

$$Gr_L = \frac{9.81 \times 2.874 \times 10^{-3} \times 45 \times 0.027}{3.595 \times 10^{-10}}$$

Numerator:

$$9.81 \times 2.874 \times 10^{-3} = 0.02819$$

$$0.02819 \times 45 = 1.2687$$

$$1.2687 \times 0.027 = 0.03425$$

Denominator:

$$\nu^2 = (1.896 \times 10^{-5})^2 = 3.595 \times 10^{-10} \; \text{m}^4/\text{s}^2$$

$$Gr_L = \frac{0.03425}{3.595 \times 10^{-10}} = 9.527 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 6 тАФ Rayleigh number:**

$$Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr = 9.527 \times 10^7 \times 0.707 = 6.735 \times 10^7$$

**Step 7 тАФ Churchill-Chu correlation:**

$$Nu_L = \left\{ 0.825 + \frac{0.387 \cdot Ra_L^{1/6}}{\left[ 1 + (0.492 / Pr)^{9/16} \right]^{8/27}} \right\}^2$$

Compute each piece:

$$Ra_L^{1/6} = (6.735 \times 10^7)^{1/6}$$

$$\log_{10}(Ra_L) = \log_{10}(6.735 \times 10^7) = 7.8284$$

$$\frac{1}{6}\log_{10}(Ra_L) = 1.3047$$

$$Ra_L^{1/6} = 10^{1.3047} \approx 20.18$$

Now compute $(0.492 / Pr)^{9/16}$:

$$\frac{0.492}{0.707} = 0.6959$$

$$(0.6959)^{9/16}: \quad \frac{9}{16} = 0.5625$$

$$\log_{10}(0.6959) = -0.1574$$

$$-0.1574 \times 0.5625 = -0.0885$$

$$(0.492/Pr)^{9/16} = 10^{-0.0885} \approx 0.8168$$

Then:

$$\left[ 1 + 0.8168 \right]^{8/27} = (1.8168)^{8/27}$$

$$\frac{8}{27} = 0.2963$$

$$\log_{10}(1.8168) = 0.2594$$

$$0.2594 \times 0.2963 = 0.0769$$

$$(1.8168)^{8/27} = 10^{0.0769} \approx 1.1946$$

Now numerator term:

$$0.387 \times Ra_L^{1/6} = 0.387 \times 20.18 = 7.811$$

So:

$$\frac{0.387 \cdot Ra_L^{1/6}}{(1.8168)^{8/27}} = \frac{7.811}{1.1946} = 6.539$$

Add to 0.825:

$$0.825 + 6.539 = 7.364$$

Square:

$$Nu_L = (7.364)^2 = 54.23$$

**Step 8 тАФ Theoretical $h_c$:**

$$h_{theo} = \frac{Nu_L \cdot k_f}{L} = \frac{54.23 \times 0.0288}{0.30}$$

$$h_{theo} = \frac{1.562}{0.30} = 5.21 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 9 тАФ Percentage error:**

$$\% \text{error} = \frac{h_{exp} - h_{theo}}{h_{theo}} \times 100 = \frac{22.22 - 5.21}{5.21} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{error} \approx 326.5 \;\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A very high experimental $h$ compared to the correlation almost always indicates that **side/back heat losses** or **radiation losses** are being attributed to convection. In real labs, students should add insulation to the back/sides or correct for radiation using $Q_r = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$ before reporting $h_{exp}$.

---

## 3.3 Radiation Correction (Optional Advanced Step)

If radiation is not negligible, the convective heat is:

$$Q_{conv} = V I - Q_{rad}$$

$$Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, A_s \, (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)$$

with $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ (Stefan-Boltzmann constant) and $\varepsilon$ = emissivity of plate surface (тЙИ 0.05тАУ0.10 for polished brass, тЙИ 0.6тАУ0.9 for oxidized/painted surface).

Then:

$$h_{c,corrected} = \frac{VI - \varepsilon \sigma A_s (T_s^4 - T_\infty^4)}{A_s (T_s - T_\infty)}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Grashof number and explain its physical significance in free convection.**

**Model Answer:**
The Grashof number is defined as:

$$Gr_L = \frac{g \, \beta \, (T_s - T_\infty) \, L^3}{\nu^2}$$

It represents the **ratio of buoyancy forces to viscous forces** acting on the fluid. A large Grashof number indicates buoyancy-dominated flow (vigorous natural convection), while a small Gr indicates that viscous forces suppress the flow. In free convection, $Gr$ plays the same role as Reynolds number $Re$ does in forced convection. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between free convection and forced convection. Give two practical examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Free Convection | Forced Convection |
|---|---|---|
| Driving force | Buoyancy due to density difference | External agency (pump, fan, blower) |
| Velocity field | Self-induced, low | Imposed, can be high |
| $h$ range | 2 тАУ 25 W/m┬▓K | 25 тАУ 250 W/m┬▓K |
| Governing group | $Gr$ or $Ra$ | $Re$ |

**Examples:** Free тАФ heat loss from a transformer tank, hot water tank. Forced тАФ car radiator with fan, laptop cooling fan. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks тАФ KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the experimental setup used to determine the free convection heat transfer coefficient from a vertical test plate. List the observations and state the formula used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vertical plate of size **0.40 m ├Ч 0.40 m** is maintained at a temperature of **80 ┬░C** in an ambient of **30 ┬░C**. Calculate the rate of heat loss from the plate using the Churchill-Chu correlation. Take air properties at film temperature: $k_f = 0.0295$ W/m┬╖K, $\nu = 1.92 \times 10^{-5}$ m┬▓/s, $Pr = 0.707$, $\beta = 1/328$ KтБ╗┬╣. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) тАФ Setup Explanation (7 marks)

**Apparatus Description:**
- **Test plate:** A square/rectangular thin plate (usually brass) of known dimensions $L \times B$, mounted vertically.
- **Heater:** A nichrome wire heater bonded uniformly to the **back face** of the plate using a high-thermal-conductivity adhesive.
- **Insulation:** Asbestos or glass-wool backing on the rear and edges to ensure heat flows out only from the **front face** (the test face).
- **Temperature measurement:** Multiple **chromel-alumel (K-type) thermocouples** welded/embedded on the test face (typically 3тАУ5 points) connected to a **digital temperature indicator** with selector switch.
- **Ambient sensor:** One thermocouple placed in the surrounding air, away from the plate, to record $T_\infty$.
- **Power supply:** A **variac** (autotransformer) feeding the heater through a voltmeter and ammeter to control and measure $V$ and $I$.
- **Stand and mounting frame:** Rigid support holding the plate vertical.

**Sketch (refer Section 4.1).**

**Observations to be recorded:**
1. Plate dimensions $L$ and $B$ (in metres).
2. Voltmeter reading $V$ (in volts).
3. Ammeter reading $I$ (in amperes).
4. Surface temperatures $T_1, T_2, T_3, \dots$ at each thermocouple.
5. Ambient air temperature $T_\infty$.

**Formula:**

$$h_{exp} = \frac{V \cdot I}{A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty)}, \quad A_s = L \times B, \quad T_s = \frac{1}{n}\sum T_i$$

**(7 marks тАФ full allocation)**

---

#### Part (b) тАФ Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $L = B = 0.40$ m тЖТ $A_s = 0.16$ m┬▓
- $T_s = 80$ ┬░C = 353 K, $T_\infty = 30$ ┬░C = 303 K
- $\theta = 50$ K
- $T_f = (353 + 303)/2 = 328$ K
- $k_f = 0.0295$ W/m┬╖K, $\nu = 1.92 \times 10^{-5}$ m┬▓/s, $Pr = 0.707$
- $\beta = 1/328 = 3.049 \times 10^{-3}$ KтБ╗┬╣

**Step 1 тАФ Compute Grashof number:**

$$Gr_L = \frac{g \, \beta \, \theta \, L^3}{\nu^2} = \frac{9.81 \times 3.049 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times (0.40)^3}{(1.92 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

Numerator:

$$9.81 \times 3.049 \times 10^{-3} = 0.02991$$

$$0.02991 \times 50 = 1.4955$$

$$1.4955 \times 0.064 = 0.09571$$

Denominator:

$$\nu^2 = 3.686 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$Gr_L = \frac{0.09571}{3.686 \times 10^{-10}} = 2.597 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 2 тАФ Rayleigh number:**

$$Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr = 2.597 \times 10^8 \times 0.707 = 1.836 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 3 тАФ Apply Churchill-Chu correlation:**

$$Ra_L^{1/6} = (1.836 \times 10^8)^{1/6}$$

$$\log_{10}(Ra_L) = 8.2639 \implies Ra_L^{1/6} = 10^{1.3773} = 23.84$$

$$\left(\frac{0.492}{Pr}\right)^{9/16} = \left(\frac{0.492}{0.707}\right)^{0.5625} = (0.6959)^{0.5625}$$

Using logs:

$$\log_{10}(0.6959) = -0.1574$$

$$-0.1574 \times 0.5625 = -0.0885$$

$$\Rightarrow 10^{-0.0885} = 0.8168$$

$$\left[1 + 0.8168\right]^{8/27} = (1.8168)^{0.2963}$$

$$\log_{10}(1.8168) = 0.2594 \times 0.2963 = 0.0769$$

$$\Rightarrow 10^{0.0769} = 1.1946$$

Numerator:

$$0.387 \times 23.84 = 9.226$$

$$\frac{9.226}{1.1946} = 7.724$$

$$0.825 + 7.724 = 8.549$$

$$Nu_L = (8.549)^2 = 73.09$$

**Step 4 тАФ Heat transfer coefficient:**

$$h_c = \frac{Nu_L \cdot k_f}{L} = \frac{73.09 \times 0.0295}{0.40} = \frac{2.156}{0.40} = 5.39 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 5 тАФ Rate of heat loss:**

$$Q = h_c \, A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty) = 5.39 \times 0.16 \times 50$$

$$\boxed{Q = 43.12 \; \text{W}}$$

**(7 marks тАФ full allocation)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** What are the various types of free convection? Write down the relevant Nusselt number correlations for free convection from a **vertical plate** and a **horizontal cylinder**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal cylinder of diameter **50 mm** and length **500 mm** is maintained at **120 ┬░C** in ambient air at **30 ┬░C**. Compute the heat loss using the Churchill-Chu correlation for horizontal cylinder. Take air properties at film temperature: $k_f = 0.0338$ W/m┬╖K, $\nu = 2.30 \times 10^{-5}$ m┬▓/s, $Pr = 0.687$, $\beta = 1/348$ KтБ╗┬╣. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) тАФ Theory (7 marks)

**Types of Free Convection:**
1. **Natural convection over vertical plates**
2. **Natural convection over horizontal plates** (hot surface facing up or down)
3. **Natural convection over horizontal cylinders**
4. **Natural convection over vertical cylinders**
5. **Natural convection over spheres**
6. **Natural convection in enclosed spaces**

**Vertical plate тАФ Churchill-Chu correlation (covers all $Ra$):**

$$Nu_L = \left\{ 0.825 + \frac{0.387 \, Ra_L^{1/6}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}} \right\}^2$$

**Horizontal cylinder тАФ Churchill-Chu correlation (all $Ra$):**

$$Nu_D = \left\{ 0.60 + \frac{0.387 \, Ra_D^{1/6}}{\left[1 + (0.559/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}} \right\}^2$$

Note the constants $0.60$ and $0.559$ for the cylinder vs. $0.825$ and $0.492$ for the plate.

**(7 marks тАФ full allocation)**

---

#### Part (b) тАФ Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D = 0.05$ m, $L = 0.5$ m тЖТ $A_s = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.05 \times 0.5 = 0.07854$ m┬▓
- $T_s = 120$ ┬░C = 393 K, $T_\infty = 30$ ┬░C = 303 K
- $\theta = 90$ K, $T_f = (393+303)/2 = 348$ K
- $\beta = 1/348 = 2.874 \times 10^{-3}$ KтБ╗┬╣
- $k_f = 0.0338$ W/m┬╖K, $\nu = 2.30 \times 10^{-5}$ m┬▓/s, $Pr = 0.687$

**Step 1 тАФ Grashof number:**

$$Gr_D = \frac{9.81 \times 2.874 \times 10^{-3} \times 90 \times (0.05)^3}{(2.30 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

Numerator:

$$9.81 \times 2.874 \times 10^{-3} = 0.02819$$

$$0.02819 \times 90 = 2.537$$

$$(0.05)^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$2.537 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-4} = 3.171 \times 10^{-4}$$

Denominator:

$$\nu^2 = (2.30)^2 \times 10^{-10} = 5.29 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$Gr_D = \frac{3.171 \times 10^{-4}}{5.29 \times 10^{-10}} = 5.994 \times 10^{5}$$

**Step 2 тАФ Rayleigh number:**

$$Ra_D = Gr_D \cdot Pr = 5.994 \times 10^5 \times 0.687 = 4.118 \times 10^5$$

**Step 3 тАФ Apply Churchill-Chu horizontal cylinder:**

$$Ra_D^{1/6} = (4.118 \times 10^5)^{1/6}$$

$$\log_{10}(Ra_D) = 5.6147 \implies Ra_D^{1/6} = 10^{0.9358} = 8.629$$

$$\left(\frac{0.559}{Pr}\right)^{9/16} = \left(\frac{0.559}{0.687}\right)^{0.5625} = (0.8137)^{0.5625}$$

$$\log_{10}(0.8137) = -0.0896$$

$$-0.0896 \times 0.5625 = -0.0504$$

$$\Rightarrow 10^{-0.0504} = 0.8900$$

$$\left[1 + 0.8900\right]^{8/27} = (1.8900)^{0.2963}$$

$$\log_{10}(1.8900) = 0.2765 \times 0.2963 = 0.0819$$

$$\Rightarrow 10^{0.0819} = 1.2090$$

Numerator:

$$0.387 \times 8.629 = 3.339$$

$$\frac{3.339}{1.2090} = 2.762$$

$$0.60 + 2.762 = 3.362$$

$$Nu_D = (3.362)^2 = 11.30$$

**Step 4 тАФ Heat transfer coefficient:**

$$h_c = \frac{Nu_D \cdot k_f}{D} = \frac{11.30 \times 0.0338}{0.05} = \frac{0.3820}{0.05} = 7.64 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 5 тАФ Rate of heat loss:**

$$Q = h_c \, A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty) = 7.64 \times 0.07854 \times 90$$

$$\boxed{Q = 53.99 \; \text{W}}$$

**(7 marks тАФ full allocation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Film temperature error (тАУ2 marks):** Using $T_s$ or $T_\infty$ instead of $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$ for property evaluation.
> 2. **Area confusion (тАУ1 mark):** Using one-sided area instead of total heat-transfer area for a cylinder ($\pi D L$, NOT $\pi D^2/4$).
> 3. **Correlation constants mix-up (тАУ2 marks):** Using vertical-plate constants ($0.825, 0.492$) for a horizontal cylinder тАФ should be ($0.60, 0.559$).
> 4. **Units trap (тАУ1 mark):** Forgetting to convert mm тЖТ m, ┬░C тЖТ K, or cm┬▓ тЖТ m┬▓.
> 5. **Mean temperature averaging mistake (тАУ1 mark):** Forgetting to compute $T_s$ as the arithmetic mean of all thermocouple readings.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Free convection** is buoyancy-driven; no external agency is involved. The driving non-dimensional group is the **Grashof number** $Gr$ or the **Rayleigh number** $Ra$.
- The experimental $h_c$ is computed from **$h = VI / [A_s (T_s - T_\infty)]$** at steady state.
- The characteristic length $L_c$ is the **vertical height** for vertical plates, **side length** for horizontal plates, and **diameter $D$** for cylinders.
- All fluid properties ($k$, $\nu$, $Pr$, $\beta$) must be evaluated at the **film temperature** $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$.
- For **vertical plates**, the Churchill-Chu correlation is universal (laminar + transition + turbulent).
- For **horizontal cylinders**, Churchill-Chu uses constants **0.60 and 0.559** instead of **0.825 and 0.492**.
- Always check **radiation losses**; if significant, correct using Stefan-Boltzmann law.
- **Lumped capacitance check:** If $Bi = h L_c / k_s < 0.1$, the plate can be treated as spatially isothermal.
- Sources of error: incomplete side/back insulation, unsteady state readings, thermocouple contact resistance, ambient temperature fluctuation.
- **KTU Viva favourites:** Why film temperature? Why not $T_s$? Why does $h_c$ increase with $\Delta T$? What is the physical meaning of Nusselt number?

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of heat transfer coefficients in forced convection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL507 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-1
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of heat transfer coefficients in forced convection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 тАФ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Forced Convection** is the mode of heat transfer between a solid surface and a fluid (liquid or gas) in which the fluid motion is artificially induced by an external agency such as a **fan**, **blower**, **pump**, or **compressor**, rather than by natural buoyancy forces (which would otherwise classify it as *free/natural convection*).

In the context of the **Module Experiment 2** of PCMEL507, we experimentally determine the **average convective heat transfer coefficient (h)** by passing a controlled stream of air across a heated test body (typically a horizontal cylindrical heater tube) and measuring the resulting steady-state heat flux, surface temperature, and bulk fluid temperature.

Mathematically, the entire experiment pivots on **Newton's Law of Cooling**:

$$q'' = h \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

where $q''$ is the convective heat flux (W/m┬▓), $h$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m┬▓┬╖K), $T_s$ is the surface temperature of the heater, and $T_\infty$ is the free-stream / bulk air temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim emphasis):**
> The *heat transfer coefficient* is **not a property of the fluid** тАФ it is an *engineering parameter* that depends on the geometry of the surface, the fluid properties, and the flow regime (laminar, transitional, or turbulent). Two students using the same air at the same velocity can obtain different values of `h` simply by changing the test piece diameter.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a railway platform on a hot summer afternoon. A stationary train feels warm. Now imagine a fast train zooming past you тАФ even though the train body temperature is the same, you feel a *much stronger* blast of hot air. That "blast strength" тАФ how intensely moving air carries heat away from a surface тАФ is exactly what **h** represents.

> **Analogy Mapping:**
> - The train body тЖТ **Heated test cylinder (TтВЫ)**
> - The still air тЖТ **Free-stream ambient air (TтИЮ)**
> - The motion of the train тЖТ **Forced air supplied by the blower**
> - The cooling sensation on your face тЖТ **Convective heat transfer coefficient `h`**

The faster the air moves, the more "heat-carrying packets" (eddies, molecules) sweep past the surface per second, and the higher the value of `h`. This is why an electric room heater has a **fan** behind it тАФ forced air makes you feel warm faster than radiant heating alone.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard values and metrics must be **memorized** for KTU viva:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical SI Value/Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $h$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient | **W/m┬▓┬╖K** |
| $Nu$ | Nusselt number (dimensionless) | тАФ |
| $Re$ | Reynolds number (dimensionless) | тАФ |
| $Pr$ | Prandtl number (dimensionless) | тАФ |
| $k_{air}$ | Thermal conductivity of air | **0.026 W/m┬╖K** (at ~40 ┬░C) |
| $\rho_{air}$ | Density of air | **1.13 kg/m┬│** (at ~40 ┬░C) |
| $\mu_{air}$ | Dynamic viscosity of air | **1.91 ├Ч 10тБ╗тБ╡ N┬╖s/m┬▓** |
| $C_{p,air}$ | Specific heat of air | **1008 J/kg┬╖K** |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test whether students evaluated air properties at the **film temperature** $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$ and not at ambient room temperature. Reporting $k$ at 25 ┬░C when the surface is at 100 ┬░C is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** in the calculation section.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 тАФ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework тАФ Step-by-Step Logic

The experiment rests on a logical chain of physical principles. Each step must be understood before any calculation is attempted.

### Step 1 тАФ Energy Balance on the Test Heater
At steady state, the rate of electrical energy supplied to the heater equals the rate of heat dissipated by the heater to the air stream. Energy **does not accumulate**.

$$Q_{in} = V \cdot I = Q_{conv} = h \, A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

- $V$ = Voltage applied to the heater coil (V)
- $I$ = Current drawn (A)
- $A_s$ = Lateral (curved) surface area of the heater tube = $\pi D L$

### Step 2 тАФ Computation of the Experimental `h`
Rearranging Newton's Law:

$$h = \frac{V \, I}{A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty)} \quad \left[\text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}\right]$$

### Step 3 тАФ Non-Dimensionalization (Why?)
The raw value of `h` depends on air properties, velocity, and tube diameter. To make the result *universally comparable*, we non-dimensionalize using three classical groups:

| Group | Formula | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nusselt number** | $Nu = \dfrac{h \, D}{k}$ | Ratio of convective to conductive thermal resistance across the boundary layer |
| **Reynolds number** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, V \, D}{\mu}$ | Ratio of inertia forces to viscous forces in the flow |
| **Prandtl number** | $Pr = \dfrac{\mu \, C_p}{k}$ | Ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity |

### Step 4 тАФ Empirical Correlation Comparison
The experimental $Nu_{exp}$ is compared against the **theoretical $Nu_{theory}$** from a standard correlation valid for cross-flow over a cylinder:

**Hilpert Correlation (most commonly cited in KTU labs):**

$$Nu_D = C \, Re_D^{\,m}$$

| Re Range | C | m |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0.4 тАУ 4 | 0.989 | 0.330 |
| 4 тАУ 40 | 0.911 | 0.385 |
| 40 тАУ 4000 | 0.683 | 0.466 |
| 4000 тАУ 40000 | 0.193 | 0.618 |
| 40000 тАУ 250000 | 0.027 | 0.805 |

**ChurchillтАУBernstein Correlation (broader validity, recommended by KTU 2024):**

$$Nu_D = 0.3 + \frac{0.62 \, Re_D^{1/2} \, Pr^{1/3}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{0.4}{Pr}\right)^{2/3}\right]^{1/4}} \left[1 + \left(\frac{Re_D}{282000}\right)^{5/8}\right]^{4/5}$$

### Step 5 тАФ Percentage Deviation / Error Analysis

$$\% \, \text{Error} = \left\vert \frac{Nu_{exp} - Nu_{theory}}{Nu_{theory}} \right\vert \times 100$$

A deviation within **┬▒15%** is typically accepted as good agreement; KTU examiners reward students who **justify** the deviation (heat losses, radiation, entry effects).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $Q = V \cdot I$ | Electrical heat input | W |
| 2 | $Q = h A_s (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Newton's law of cooling | W |
| 3 | $A_s = \pi D L$ | Lateral surface of cylinder | m┬▓ |
| 4 | $h = \dfrac{VI}{\pi D L (T_s - T_\infty)}$ | Experimental `h` | W/m┬▓┬╖K |
| 5 | $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$ | Film temperature | K or ┬░C |
| 6 | $Re_D = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | тАФ |
| 7 | $Pr = \dfrac{\mu C_p}{k}$ | Prandtl number | тАФ |
| 8 | $Nu_D = \dfrac{h D}{k}$ | Nusselt number | тАФ |
| 9 | $Nu_D = C \, Re_D^{\,m}$ | Hilpert correlation | тАФ |
| 10 | $Nu_D = 0.3 + \dfrac{0.62 Re^{1/2} Pr^{1/3}}{\left[1 + (0.4/Pr)^{2/3}\right]^{1/4}} \left[1 + (Re/282000)^{5/8}\right]^{4/5}$ | ChurchillтАУBernstein | тАФ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Forced convection coefficients for air lie in the range **10 тАУ 500 W/m┬▓┬╖K**. Designing heat exchangers, gas-turbine blade cooling channels, CPU heat sinks, and HVAC ducts all rely on `h` values of this order. Without this experiment, engineers cannot validate CFD simulations or empirical correlations used in industrial heat-exchanger design codes (HTFS, HTRI).

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why non-dimensionalize?** Because `h` alone is useless тАФ the same `h` of 50 W/m┬▓┬╖K could mean different physical situations. $Nu$ collapses geometry, fluid, and velocity into a single comparable number.
- **Why use film temperature?** Because fluid properties ($\mu, k, Pr$) change along the boundary layer. The film temperature is the *mean* temperature the boundary layer experiences, giving the best single-point property lookup.
- **Why cross-flow over a cylinder?** It is one of the few geometries with well-established, dimensionless correlations in standard heat-transfer textbooks (Incropera, Holman, Cengel). It also exhibits the full range of flow regimes тАФ laminar, transitional, and turbulent тАФ making it an ideal pedagogical test case.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 тАФ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Governing Equation for `h`

Starting from the conservation of energy on the heated test cylinder at steady state:

### Step A тАФ Statement of First Law for the Heater
There is no accumulation of energy in the heater at steady state. Therefore, all electrical energy entering the heater coil must leave through its lateral surface to the moving air.

$$\sum E_{in} = \sum E_{out}$$

### Step B тАФ Substitute the Heat-Transfer Mode
The only heat-transfer mechanism relevant to the lab (radiation and natural convection are deliberately neglected or minimized in the standard apparatus) is forced convection from the curved surface:

$$V \cdot I = h \, A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

### Step C тАФ Express the Lateral Surface Area
The curved surface of a cylinder of diameter $D$ and length $L$ is:

$$A_s = \pi \, D \, L$$

### Step D тАФ Final Rearranged Form

$$\boxed{\,h = \frac{V \cdot I}{\pi \, D \, L \, (T_s - T_\infty)}\,}$$

This is the **master working equation** for the experiment. Every numerical substitution begins here.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Nusselt Number

By definition, the Nusselt number relates the convective resistance to the conductive resistance across the boundary layer of thickness $\delta$:

$$Nu_D = \frac{h \, D}{k}$$

Substituting the expression for `h`:

$$\begin{aligned}
Nu_D &= \frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{V \cdot I}{\pi \, D \, L \, (T_s - T_\infty)} \cdot D \\
&= \frac{V \cdot I}{\pi \, L \, k \, (T_s - T_\infty)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice how the diameter `D` cancels! The Nusselt number as computed directly from experimental quantities **does not depend on the cylinder diameter**, but this is *only* because we are computing it via energy balance, not from a velocity-derived boundary layer. The diameter still influences the Reynolds number on the right-hand side of the correlation.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style)

**Given Data:**
- Heater diameter $D = 0.038$ m (38 mm brass tube)
- Heated length $L = 0.200$ m
- Voltage $V = 60$ V
- Current $I = 0.65$ A
- Surface temperature $T_s = 80$ ┬░C = 353 K
- Air temperature $T_\infty = 30$ ┬░C = 303 K
- Air velocity $V_{air} = 3.0$ m/s
- Air properties evaluated at film temperature $T_f$

**Step 1 тАФ Film Temperature**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_f &= \frac{T_s + T_\infty}{2} \\
&= \frac{353 + 303}{2} \\
&= 328 \, \text{K} \;\;\text{(i.e., 55 ┬░C)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 тАФ Look up Air Properties at $T_f = 328$ K**

| Property | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\rho$ | 1.099 kg/m┬│ |
| $C_p$ | 1008 J/kg┬╖K |
| $k$ | 0.0281 W/m┬╖K |
| $\mu$ | 1.96 ├Ч 10тБ╗тБ╡ N┬╖s/m┬▓ |
| $Pr$ | 0.703 |

**Step 3 тАФ Compute Heat Input**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= V \cdot I \\
&= 60 \times 0.65 \\
&= 39.0 \, \text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 тАФ Compute Surface Area**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_s &= \pi D L \\
&= \pi \times 0.038 \times 0.200 \\
&= 0.02388 \, \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 тАФ Compute Experimental `h`**

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{Q}{A_s (T_s - T_\infty)} \\
&= \frac{39.0}{0.02388 \times (80 - 30)} \\
&= \frac{39.0}{0.02388 \times 50} \\
&= \frac{39.0}{1.194} \\
&= 32.66 \, \text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 тАФ Compute Reynolds Number**

$$\begin{aligned}
Re_D &= \frac{\rho \, V_{air} \, D}{\mu} \\
&= \frac{1.099 \times 3.0 \times 0.038}{1.96 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= \frac{0.12529}{1.96 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= 6392
\end{aligned}$$

Since $4000 < Re_D < 40000$, the Hilpert constant is $C = 0.193$, $m = 0.618$.

**Step 7 тАФ Theoretical Nusselt Number from Hilpert**

$$\begin{aligned}
Nu_{theory} &= C \, Re^{\,m} \\
&= 0.193 \times (6392)^{0.618}
\end{aligned}$$

We evaluate the power term:

$$\begin{aligned}
(6392)^{0.618} &= e^{0.618 \ln 6392} \\
&= e^{0.618 \times 8.762} \\
&= e^{5.415} \\
&\approx 224.6
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
Nu_{theory} &= 0.193 \times 224.6 \\
&= 43.35
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 тАФ Theoretical `h` from Hilpert**

$$\begin{aligned}
h_{theory} &= \frac{Nu_{theory} \cdot k}{D} \\
&= \frac{43.35 \times 0.0281}{0.038} \\
&= \frac{1.2181}{0.038} \\
&= 32.05 \, \text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 9 тАФ Percentage Error**

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \, \text{Error} &= \left\vert \frac{h_{exp} - h_{theory}}{h_{theory}} \right\vert \times 100 \\
&= \left\vert \frac{32.66 - 32.05}{32.05} \right\vert \times 100 \\
&= 1.90 \,\%
\end{aligned}$$

A deviation of less than 2% тАФ excellent agreement, validating both the experimental procedure and the Hilpert correlation.

**Step 10 тАФ Experimental Nusselt Number (for comparison record)**

$$\begin{aligned}
Nu_{exp} &= \frac{h_{exp} \cdot D}{k} \\
&= \frac{32.66 \times 0.038}{0.0281} \\
&= 44.17
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix тАФ Effect of Velocity on `h`

| Air Velocity (m/s) | $Re_D$ | Flow Regime | $h$ (W/m┬▓┬╖K) | $Nu_D$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0.5 | 1065 | Laminar | 16.4 | 22.2 |
| 1.0 | 2131 | Laminar | 22.1 | 29.9 |
| 2.0 | 4262 | Transitional | 28.5 | 38.6 |
| 3.0 | 6392 | Transitional | 32.7 | 44.2 |
| 5.0 | 10653 | Turbulent | 39.8 | 53.8 |
| 8.0 | 17045 | Turbulent | 47.2 | 63.8 |

> [!NOTE]
> As velocity rises, the Reynolds number rises, and so does `h`. The relationship is **not linear** тАФ it follows roughly $h \propto V^{0.618}$ in the turbulent regime, which is why doubling the velocity does *not* double `h`.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 тАФ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Apparatus Block Diagram (Mermaid Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the experimental setup, illustrating how electrical, thermal, and fluid streams interact. (Adapted as a block diagram because the physical geometry involves 3D plumbing that Mermaid cannot render.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Supply 0 to 230 V Variac] --> B[Voltmeter V and Ammeter I]
    B --> C[Heater Coil Nichrome Wound on Test Cylinder]
    C --> D[Test Cylinder Brass Tube D = 38 mm, L = 200 mm]
    D --> E[Thermocouple T_s on Surface]
    E --> F[Digital Temperature Indicator]
    G[Centrifugal Blower] --> H[Air Inlet Duct with Honeycomb Straightener]
    H --> I[Test Section Air Stream over Cylinder]
    I --> J[Anemometer Probe at Outlet]
    I --> K[Thermocouple T_infty Ambient Air]
    K --> F
    J --> L[Exhaust to Atmosphere]
    D --> M[Convective Heat Loss Q_conv]
    M --> I

    classDef electrical fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef thermal fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    classDef fluid fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef instrument fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000

    class A,B electrical
    class C,D,M thermal
    class G,H,I,J,L fluid
    class E,F,K instrument
```

## 4.2 Signal Flow & Data Acquisition Sequence

The diagram below shows the logical order in which measurements must be taken and fed into the calculation chain:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Switch ON Variac] --> S2[Step 2: Set Heater Voltage V]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Wait for Steady State ~30 min]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Read T_s and T_infty]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Read V and I]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Measure Air Velocity V_air]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Compute T_f and Air Properties]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Compute Q, A_s, h_exp]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Compute Re, Pr, Nu_exp]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Apply Hilpert or Churchill-Bernstein]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Compare and Compute Error percent]

    classDef stepBox fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8,S9,S10,S11 stepBox
```

## 4.3 Heat-Transfer Pathway Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Electrical Heat Q = VI] --> C[Conduction Through Tube Wall]
    C --> S[Convection at Outer Surface h, T_s]
    S --> B[Boundary Layer Development]
    B --> F[Mixed Bulk Air Stream T_infty]
    F --> O[Exhaust Out]
    S -.radiation loss.-> R[Negligible if T_s less than 100 C]
    S -.natural convection.-> N[Negligible at high forced velocity]

    classDef heat fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000
    class H,C,S,B,F,O heat
    class R,N loss
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic for KTU practical exams:** Whenever asked to draw the experimental setup, always label **five** items: (1) variac, (2) ammeter/voltmeter, (3) test cylinder with surface thermocouple, (4) blower with anemometer, and (5) digital temperature indicator. KTU valuation key allocates **1 mark per labeled component** up to a 5-mark diagram question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 тАФ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]`
**Define the convective heat transfer coefficient. Why is it not a fluid property?**

**Model Answer:**
The convective heat transfer coefficient `h` is defined as the proportionality constant in Newton's law of cooling, $q'' = h(T_s - T_\infty)$, and has units of W/m┬▓┬╖K. It represents the rate of heat transfer per unit area per unit temperature difference between a surface and the surrounding fluid.

It is **not a fluid property** because it depends on:
1. Geometry of the surface (cylinder, flat plate, sphere, fin)
2. Flow regime (laminar, transitional, turbulent)
3. Fluid velocity (forced convection intensity)
4. Orientation of the surface (vertical/horizontal)

A single value of `h` cannot characterize a fluid the way $k$, $\mu$, or $C_p$ do.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]`
**What is the physical significance of the Nusselt number?**

**Model Answer:**
The Nusselt number $Nu = hD/k$ is a dimensionless number that represents the ratio of **convective heat transfer** to **purely conductive heat transfer** through a fluid layer of thickness equal to the characteristic length $D$. 

When $Nu = 1$, heat transfer occurs by conduction alone (no convection enhancement). When $Nu \gg 1$, convection greatly amplifies heat transfer beyond what conduction could provide. For forced convection over a cylinder in air, typical $Nu$ values range from **20 to 100**, indicating convection is 20тАУ100 times more effective than conduction across the same thickness.

---

## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each тАФ Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall тАФ Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Property at wrong temperature:** Always evaluate $\rho, \mu, k, C_p$ at the film temperature $T_f$. KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for using ambient (room) temperature values.
> 2. **Wrong correlation range:** Using Hilpert constants $C = 0.683, m = 0.466$ when $Re = 60000$ (which falls in the 4000тАУ40000 range) is a critical error тАФ **3 marks lost**.
> 3. **Missing units in final answer:** Writing $h = 32.66$ without `W/m┬▓┬╖K` costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **Forgetting curved surface area:** Using flat plate area $\pi r^2$ instead of $\pi D L$ costs **2 marks**.
> 5. **No error analysis:** Failing to compute % deviation between experimental and theoretical $Nu$ costs **2 marks**.

---

### Question A тАФ 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the experimental setup to determine the convective heat transfer coefficient in forced convection. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. **Test cylinder:** A horizontal brass tube of 38 mm diameter and 200 mm length with a nichrome heater coil wound non-inductively over its entire length.
2. **Variac (auto-transformer):** 0тАУ230 V to control the heat input.
3. **Voltmeter and Ammeter:** To measure $V$ and $I$ supplied to the heater.
4. **Thermocouples:** One attached to the surface ($T_s$) and one placed in the air stream ($T_\infty$), connected to a digital temperature indicator.
5. **Centrifugal blower with flow-control valve:** To force air over the cylinder at variable velocities.
6. **Anemometer:** Digital or vane type, placed at the outlet of the test section to measure air velocity.
7. **Duct and honeycomb flow straightener:** To ensure uniform, non-swirling flow over the test cylinder.

**Working Principle:**
At steady state, the electrical energy supplied to the heater equals the convective heat lost to the air stream:

$$Q = V \cdot I = h \, A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty) = h \, \pi D L \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

Rearranging gives $h$, which is then non-dimensionalized to $Nu = hD/k$ and compared against the Hilpert correlation $Nu = C Re^m$.

**Procedure:**
1. Switch on the blower and set a chosen air velocity.
2. Energize the heater through the variac and set a fixed voltage.
3. Allow the system to reach steady state (тЙИ 30 minutes), confirmed when $T_s$ and $T_\infty$ remain constant for 5 consecutive minutes.
4. Record $V, I, T_s, T_\infty, V_{air}$.
5. Repeat for 5тАУ6 different velocities.
6. Compute $h$, $Nu$, $Re$, and compare with the Hilpert correlation.

**Sketch (description):** Air enters from a blower тЖТ passes through honeycomb straightener тЖТ flows perpendicular over the heated cylinder inside a rectangular duct тЖТ exits through anemometer section. Variac supplies power to heater coil; thermocouples feed digital indicator.

**(b)** In a forced convection experiment over a horizontal cylinder of diameter 40 mm and length 200 mm, the following readings were obtained at steady state: voltage = 55 V, current = 0.8 A, surface temperature = 90 ┬░C, air temperature = 35 ┬░C, air velocity = 2.5 m/s. Determine the experimental and theoretical heat transfer coefficients and the percentage error. Use air properties at film temperature: $\rho = 1.085$ kg/m┬│, $\mu = 1.96 \times 10^{-5}$ N┬╖s/m┬▓, $k = 0.028$ W/m┬╖K, $Pr = 0.703$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 тАФ Heat Input:**
$$Q = V \cdot I = 55 \times 0.8 = 44.0 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 2 тАФ Surface Area:**
$$A_s = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.040 \times 0.200 = 0.02513 \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 тАФ Experimental `h`:**
$$h_{exp} = \frac{Q}{A_s (T_s - T_\infty)} = \frac{44.0}{0.02513 \times (90 - 35)} = \frac{44.0}{1.383} = 31.81 \, \text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}$$

**Step 4 тАФ Reynolds Number:**
$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{1.085 \times 2.5 \times 0.040}{1.96 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.1085}{1.96 \times 10^{-5}} = 5535$$

Since $4000 < Re < 40000$, use Hilpert constants $C = 0.193$, $m = 0.618$.

**Step 5 тАФ Theoretical Nusselt Number:**
$$Nu_{th} = C \, Re^m = 0.193 \times (5535)^{0.618}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(5535)^{0.618} &= e^{0.618 \ln 5535} \\
&= e^{0.618 \times 8.619} \\
&= e^{5.327} \\
&\approx 206.8
\end{aligned}$$

$$Nu_{th} = 0.193 \times 206.8 = 39.91$$

**Step 6 тАФ Theoretical `h`:**
$$h_{th} = \frac{Nu_{th} \cdot k}{D} = \frac{39.91 \times 0.028}{0.040} = 27.94 \, \text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{K}$$

**Step 7 тАФ Percentage Error:**
$$\% \text{Error} = \left\vert \frac{31.81 - 27.94}{27.94} \right\vert \times 100 = 13.85\%$$

**Conclusion:** The experimental value exceeds the theoretical prediction by ~14%, attributable to radiation losses from the hot cylinder and uncertainty in thermocouple placement.

---

### Question B тАФ 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the experimental convective heat transfer coefficient in forced convection over a heated cylinder. List the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**

Consider a horizontal cylindrical heater of diameter $D$ and length $L$ subjected to cross-flow of air.

**Assumption 1 тАФ Steady State:**
At steady state, the rate of heat generation equals the rate of heat removal. There is no energy storage in the heater body.

$$Q_{gen} = Q_{conv}$$

**Assumption 2 тАФ Uniform Surface Temperature:**
The cylinder surface is assumed isothermal at $T_s$ (a reasonable idealization if the heater coil is uniformly distributed and the cylinder is highly conductive тАФ e.g., brass).

**Assumption 3 тАФ Heat Loss by Convection Only:**
Radiation and natural convection are neglected (this introduces a small systematic error, typically under 5% for $T_s < 100$ ┬░C).

**Assumption 4 тАФ Uniform Approach Velocity:**
The air velocity profile is fully developed before reaching the cylinder (ensured by the honeycomb flow straightener).

**Derivation:**

Electrical heat supplied:

$$Q_{gen} = V \cdot I$$

Convective heat lost (Newton's law applied over lateral surface):

$$Q_{conv} = h \, A_s \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

Lateral surface of cylinder:

$$A_s = \pi D L$$

Equating at steady state:

$$V \cdot I = h \, \pi D L \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\,h = \frac{V \, I}{\pi \, D \, L \, (T_s - T_\infty)}\,}$$

**(b)** Compare Hilpert and ChurchillтАУBernstein correlations for cross-flow over a cylinder. Under what flow conditions is each preferred? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Hilpert | ChurchillтАУBernstein |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Year proposed | 1933 | 1977 |
| Valid Re range | 0.4 to 250000 (piecewise) | All Reynolds numbers |
| Valid Pr range | All gases and liquids | $0.7 \le Pr \le 380$ |
| Form | Piecewise $C Re^m$ (five segments) | Single continuous equation |
| Accuracy | ┬▒30% in transitional range | ┬▒15% across full range |
| Difficulty | Easy to use with table | Requires calculator |

**Hilpert:**
- Preferred for **pedagogical lab calculations** because the constants $C, m$ are tabulated for five Re ranges.
- Highly accurate in **fully laminar** ($Re < 4$) and **fully turbulent** ($Re > 40000$) regimes.
- Less accurate in the **transitional** regime (4000 тАУ 40000) where the boundary layer separates intermittently.

**ChurchillтАУBernstein:**
- Preferred for **engineering design** because it is a single unified equation valid across all Re.
- Includes Prandtl number explicitly, making it applicable to liquids (Pr > 0.7) and not just gases.
- Recommended by KTU 2024 scheme for design-oriented problems.

**Selection Rule:**
- For lab report comparison: use **Hilpert** (simplest).
- For design projects / open-ended ESE questions: use **ChurchillтАУBernstein** (more rigorous, single equation).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap тАФ Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- [ ] **Master equation:** $h = VI / [\pi D L (T_s - T_\infty)]$ тАФ must be written from memory.
- [ ] **Three dimensionless groups:** Nusselt ($hD/k$), Reynolds ($\rho V D / \mu$), Prandtl ($\mu C_p / k$).
- [ ] **Film temperature** $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$ тАФ always evaluate properties here.
- [ ] **Hilpert constant selection** based on Re range (5 sub-ranges).
- [ ] **ChurchillтАУBernstein** is the unified alternative; preferred for higher accuracy.
- [ ] **% Error formula** $\left\vert (h_{exp} - h_{theory})/h_{theory} \right\vert \times 100$.
- [ ] **Acceptable error band:** ┬▒15% for lab validation; deviations explained by radiation, thermocouple placement, or inlet flow non-uniformity.
- [ ] **Surface area** is *curved* ($\pi D L$), never cross-sectional ($\pi D^2/4$).
- [ ] **Steady-state** must be confirmed before taking readings (no drift in $T_s$ for 5 minutes).
- [ ] **Typical air properties at 50 ┬░C:** $\rho \approx 1.09$, $k \approx 0.028$, $\mu \approx 1.96 \times 10^{-5}$, $Pr \approx 0.71$.
- [ ] **Higher velocity тЖТ higher `h`** but relation is non-linear: $h \propto V^{0.6}$ (turbulent regime).
- [ ] **Apparatus diagram** must show: variac, ammeter/voltmeter, test cylinder, blower, anemometer, thermocouples, digital indicator.
- [ ] **Forced vs Natural:** Forced uses external agency (fan/blower); Natural relies on buoyancy (density difference). For air velocities above ~1 m/s, forced dominates.
- [ ] **Boundary layer** is the thin fluid layer near the surface where temperature gradient exists; velocity gradients steepen with increasing $V$, shrinking the layer and increasing $h$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of thermal conductivity of solids (composite wall/metal rod)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL507 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-1
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of thermal conductivity of solids (composite wall/metal rod)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermal Conductivity (k)** is defined as the ability of a material to transfer heat through it by conduction. Mathematically, it is the rate of heat flow per unit area per unit temperature gradient, expressed by **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**:

$$\dot{Q} = -k \cdot A \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

where the negative sign indicates heat flows from higher to lower temperature. The unit of thermal conductivity is **W/(m┬╖K)** in SI units, and it is a key transport property in heat transfer engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** In Lab 1 (PCMEL507), Module Experiment 2 deals with the experimental determination of **k** for engineering solids. Two standard test rigs are prescribed: the **Composite Wall Apparatus** (for insulating/building materials) and the **Searle's Apparatus / Metal Rod Method** (for metals and good conductors).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy тАФ The "Plank Through a Crowd"

Imagine a crowded hallway. Some people (high-k materials like copper or aluminum) push through easily тАФ heat passes readily. Others (low-k materials like wood, asbestos, or fiberglass) act like a bottleneck тАФ heat struggles to pass. **Thermal conductivity** is essentially a measure of how easily this "heat crowd" can move through a material when there is a temperature difference acting as the driving force.

- **High k**: Metals (copper тЙИ **401 W/m┬╖K**, aluminum тЙИ **237 W/m┬╖K**)
- **Low k**: Insulators (asbestos тЙИ **0.08 W/m┬╖K**, cork тЙИ **0.04 W/m┬╖K**)

### 1.3 Why We Cannot Use the Same Method for Both Materials

A single technique does not work for all solids because of the **vast difference in thermal resistance**.

| Material Class | Thermal Conductivity | Heat Flow Rate | Method Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Good conductors (metals) | High (10тАУ400 W/m┬╖K) | Large, measurable | Searle's metal rod |
| Poor conductors (insulators) | Low (0.02тАУ0.5 W/m┬╖K) | Tiny, must be amplified | Composite wall / Lee's disc / guarded hot plate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** For metals, heat flows so easily that we need only a small temperature gradient across a long rod. For insulators, the gradient must be steep, and the apparatus must guard against heat losses, since the heat conducted is small and easily lost to surroundings.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Theory тАФ Fourier's Law for Solids

For one-dimensional, steady-state heat conduction through a homogeneous solid with no internal heat generation:

$$\dot{Q} = k \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta x}$$

Where:
- $\dot{Q}$ = heat transfer rate (W)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m┬╖K)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow (m┬▓)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature difference across the solid (K or ┬░C)
- $\Delta x$ = thickness of the solid in the direction of heat flow (m)

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Procedure

**For Searle's Metal Rod Method (Good Conductors):**

1. Heat is supplied at one end of a long metal rod through a steam coil or electric heater.
2. The other end is cooled by circulating water through a copper coil/jacket.
3. The rod is lagged (insulated) on its lateral surface to ensure **1-D axial heat flow**.
4. Two thermometers $T_1$ and $T_2$ measure the rod surface temperature at two known axial points separated by distance $L$.
5. At **steady state**, the rate of heat conducted through the rod equals the rate of heat absorbed by the cooling water.
6. Equating the two gives $k$.

**For Composite Wall Apparatus (Poor Conductors):**

1. A flat sample (often circular disc) is sandwiched between a heater plate and a cooled plate (or two samples are used back-to-back).
2. Thermocouples/thermometers measure temperatures at all interfaces.
3. Heat supplied by the heater = heat conducted through the sample = heat rejected at the cold side.
4. Knowing area $A$, thickness $x$, and measured $\Delta T$, $k$ is calculated.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Formula Sheet тАФ Memorize This Table for the University Exam**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{Q} = k A \frac{\Delta T}{L}$ | Fourier's Law (1-D steady conduction) | W |
| 2 | $k = \dfrac{\dot{Q} \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta T}$ | Rearranged form to compute k | W/m┬╖K |
| 3 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_w$ | Heat absorbed by cooling water | W |
| 4 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\rho_w \cdot V}{t}$ | Mass flow rate of cooling water | kg/s |
| 5 | $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{V \cdot \rho_w \cdot c_{pw} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})}{t}$ | Practical form (volume measured over time) | W |
| 6 | $k = \dfrac{V \cdot \rho_w \cdot c_{pw} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) \cdot L}{t \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}$ | **Master Equation for Searle's Method** | W/m┬╖K |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | Thermal resistance of a slab | K/W |
| 8 | $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{\Sigma R}$ | Composite wall (resistances in series) | W |
| 9 | $\Delta T_i = \dot{Q} \cdot \dfrac{x_i}{k_i A}$ | Temperature drop across each layer | K |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Building construction**: Choosing wall materials with appropriate $k$ for thermal insulation (kitchen walls, furnace linings).
- **Heat exchangers**: Selecting tube materials (copper) for high heat transfer rates.
- **Refractory design**: Furnace inner linings use low-k bricks to minimize heat loss.
- **Electronics cooling**: Heat sinks made of aluminum (high k) dissipate heat from CPUs.
- **Cryogenic insulation**: Multi-layer composite walls with alternating low-k materials reduce conduction to liquefied gases.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 тАФ Searle's Method Master Equation (For Metal Rod)

We will derive the exact expression used in the KTU lab record. We equate the heat conducted through the rod to the heat gained by the cooling water.

**Step 1 тАФ Heat conducted through the rod (Fourier's Law):**

At steady state, with cross-sectional area $A$ and length between thermometers $L$:

$$\dot{Q}_{cond} = k \cdot A \cdot \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

**Step 2 тАФ Heat gained by the cooling water:**

If mass $m$ of water flows in time $t$, with specific heat $c_{pw}$, and water temperature rises from $T_{in}$ to $T_{out}$:

$$\dot{Q}_{water} = \frac{m \cdot c_{pw} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})}{t}$$

**Step 3 тАФ Apply continuity of heat at steady state:**

$$\dot{Q}_{cond} = \dot{Q}_{water}$$

$$k \cdot A \cdot \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{m \cdot c_{pw} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})}{t}$$

**Step 4 тАФ Solve for thermal conductivity k:**

Rearranging to isolate $k$:

$$k = \frac{m \cdot c_{pw} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) \cdot L}{t \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}$$

**Step 5 тАФ Expressing mass as volume ├Ч density:**

Since the student measures volume $V$ (in mL or litres) collected in time $t$:

$$m = \rho_w \cdot V$$

Therefore, the final working formula:

$$\boxed{k = \frac{V \cdot \rho_w \cdot c_{pw} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) \cdot L}{t \cdot A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example тАФ Searle's Method

**Given Data (Typical KTU Lab Problem):**
- Length between thermometers: $L = 0.10$ m
- Diameter of rod: $d = 25$ mm = 0.025 m
- Steady-state temperatures: $T_1 = 80$ ┬░C, $T_2 = 50$ ┬░C
- Water collected: $V = 500$ mL = $5 \times 10^{-4}$ m┬│
- Collection time: $t = 300$ s
- Water inlet temperature: $T_{in} = 25$ ┬░C
- Water outlet temperature: $T_{out} = 40$ ┬░C
- $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m┬│, $c_{pw} = 4186$ J/kg┬╖K

**Step 1 тАФ Cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.025)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 тАФ Mass of water:**

$$m = \rho_w \cdot V = 1000 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.5 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 тАФ Heat gained by water per second:**

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{m \cdot c_{pw} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})}{t} = \frac{0.5 \times 4186 \times (40 - 25)}{300}$$

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{0.5 \times 4186 \times 15}{300} = \frac{31395}{300} = 104.65 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 тАФ Temperature gradient along rod:**

$$\Delta T_{rod} = T_1 - T_2 = 80 - 50 = 30 \text{ ┬░C (or K)}$$

**Step 5 тАФ Compute k:**

$$k = \frac{\dot{Q} \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{104.65 \times 0.10}{4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 30}$$

$$k = \frac{10.465}{0.01473} = 710.5 \text{ W/m┬╖K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** Pure copper has $k \approx 401$ W/m┬╖K and brass тЙИ 109 W/m┬╖K. Our calculated value of 710.5 W/m┬╖K is unusually high тАФ suggesting either the rod is **silver-alloyed**, the data represents an idealised textbook case, or there is an experimental error. Always compare your answer to standard reference values for the material to detect anomalies.

### 3.3 Derivation 2 тАФ Composite Wall (Resistances in Series)

For a wall made of two layers in series (heater тЖТ layer 1 тЖТ layer 2 тЖТ coolant), thermal resistances add:

$$R_{total} = R_1 + R_2 = \frac{x_1}{k_1 A} + \frac{x_2}{k_2 A}$$

Heat flow through the composite:

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{T_{hot} - T_{cold}}{R_{total}} = \frac{(T_{hot} - T_{cold}) \cdot A}{\frac{x_1}{k_1} + \frac{x_2}{k_2}}$$

If $k_1$ is unknown and the temperatures $T_a$ (interface) and $T_{cold}$ are measured, then:

$$\dot{Q} = k_1 A \cdot \frac{T_{hot} - T_a}{x_1}$$

Equating and solving for $k_1$:

$$k_1 = \frac{(T_{hot} - T_{cold}) \cdot x_1}{A \cdot \dot{Q} \cdot \left[\frac{x_1}{k_1} + \frac{x_2}{k_2}\right] - (T_{hot} - T_a)}$$

In practical KTU labs, $\dot{Q}$ is computed from electric heater input: $\dot{Q} = V \cdot I$ (Watt-meter reading).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix тАФ Sources of Error and Corrections

| Source of Error | Effect on k | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Heat loss from rod sides | Overestimates k (since some heat bypasses rod) | Proper lagging/asbestos wrapping |
| Contact resistance at thermometer junctions | Lowers measured ╬ФT, overestimates k | Use mercury thermometers in thermowells with thermal paste |
| Non-uniform water flow | Fluctuates $T_{out}$ | Use constant-head tank to maintain steady flow |
| Radiation from hot end | Heat bypasses conduction path | Use polished/lagged surfaces |
| Convection inside rod (if horizontal) | Disturbs 1-D assumption | Keep rod horizontal, well-insulated |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture тАФ Searle's Metal Rod Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HP[HEATING SECTION]
        A1[Steam Boiler or Electric Heater]
        A2[Heater Coil Wound on Rod End]
    end

    subgraph RG[TEST SECTION]
        nodeA[Heated End of Rod]
        nodeB[T1 Thermometer Position]
        nodeC[T2 Thermometer Position]
        nodeD[Cooled End of Rod]
        nodeE[Asbestos Lagging along rod]
    end

    subgraph CS[COOLING SECTION]
        F1[Cooling Water Inlet Tank]
        F2[Copper Cooling Jacket]
        F3[Outlet Water Collection Beaker]
        F4[Stopwatch and Measuring Cylinder]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeE -.wraps.-> nodeA
    nodeE -.wraps.-> nodeB
    nodeE -.wraps.-> nodeC
    nodeD --> F2
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> F4

    classDef hot fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef rod fill:#D2B48C,stroke:#5C4033,color:#000000
    classDef cool fill:#4682B4,stroke:#00008B,color:#FFFFFF

    class A1,A2 hot
    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE rod
    class F1,F2,F3 cool
    class F4 meas
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram тАФ Composite Wall Apparatus

```mermaid

        H1[Electric Heater with Watt-meter]
    end

    subgraph MID[TEST SPECIMEN STACK]
        S1[Sample 1 Disc with T_a Sensor]
        S2[Sample 2 Disc with T_b Sensor]
    end

    subgraph BOT[COOLING SECTION]
        C1[Water-cooled Copper Plate]
        C2[Circulating Cold Water Inlet Outlet]
    end

    subgraph DAT[DATA ACQUISITION]

        D2[T_a Interface 1]
        D3[T_b Interface 2]
        D4[T_cold Cool Plate]
    end

    H1 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2

    DAT -.monitors.-> H1
    DAT -.monitors.-> S1
    DAT -.monitors.-> S2
    DAT -.monitors.-> C1

    classDef heater fill:#FF4500,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef sample fill:#F4A460,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000000
```
