<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ЁЯЯв SECTION 1 тАФ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Emissivity (╬╡)** is a dimensionless radiative property of a material's surface that quantifies its effectiveness in emitting thermal (infrared) radiation as compared to a **perfect black body** at the same thermodynamic temperature. Mathematically, it is the ratio of the radiation energy emitted per unit area per unit time by a *real (gray) surface* to that emitted by an ideal *black body* under identical geometric and thermal conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCMEL507):**
> *"Emissivity of a surface is defined as the ratio of the emissive power of the surface to the emissive power of a black body at the same temperature."*
>
> **Emissivity Range:** A perfect black body has **╬╡ = 1** (unity). A perfect reflector/mirror has **╬╡ = 0**.

Formally expressed using **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{E_{\text{real}}}{E_{\text{blackbody}}} = \frac{E_{\text{real}}}{\sigma T^{4}}$$

where the universal constant is the **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant = ╧Г = 5.67 ├Ч 10тБ╗тБ╕ W/m┬▓┬╖KтБ┤**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain English Intuition ЁЯза

Imagine two friends standing on a balcony in winter:

- **Friend A (Black Body):** Wears dark clothes. Radiates body heat very efficiently тАФ feels warm to touch when you approach but loses heat quickly.
- **Friend B (Polished Metal):** Wears a shiny silver jacket. Reflects most of the heat back into his body тАФ feels cold to touch but loses heat very slowly.

In this analogy, **emissivity is Friend A's "shyness" in keeping heat to himself** тАФ *the higher the emissivity, the more freely a surface emits heat.* A dark, matte, oxidized surface has high emissivity (~0.95). A polished, shiny metal surface has low emissivity (~0.05).

## 1.3 Types of Emissivity (KTU High-Yield Terminology)

| Type | Definition | Typical Use |
|------|------------|-------------|
| **Normal Emissivity (╬╡тВЩ)** | Ratio of radiation emitted normal to the surface to that of a black body | Optical pyrometers |
| **Hemispherical Emissivity (╬╡тВХ)** | Ratio integrated over the entire hemisphere above the surface | Engineering calculations |
| **Spectral Emissivity (╬╡╬╗)** | Ratio at a specific wavelength ╬╗ | Spectral analysis |
| **Total Emissivity (╬╡)** | Ratio integrated over all wavelengths | KTU Lab тАФ Stefan-Boltzmann Law |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Note:** The Thermal Engineering Lab-1 experiment uses **TOTAL HEMISPHERICAL EMISSIVITY** because the heating coil radiates in all directions and over the full spectrum.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ЁЯФ╡ SECTION 2 тАФ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Principle: Stefan-Boltzmann Law

In 1879, Josef Stefan empirically discovered, and Ludwig Boltzmann later derived theoretically, that the total radiant heat energy emitted per unit surface area of a black body per unit time is proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute thermodynamic temperature**.

$$E_{b} = \sigma T^{4}$$

For a real (gray) surface, the emissive power is reduced by the factor **╬╡**:

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$$

## 2.2 Why We Need a "Second Surface" тАФ The Net Heat Exchange

In the lab, the test specimen is suspended inside a hollow enclosure (a poor vacuum or air-filled cavity). Radiation is exchanged between:
- The **specimen surface** (at temperature TтВБ, area AтВБ, emissivity ╬╡)
- The **inner enclosure wall** (treated as a black body at temperature TтВВ, area AтВВ >> AтВБ)

By **Kirchhoff's Law of Thermal Radiation** (under thermodynamic equilibrium, emissivity equals absorptivity), the **net rate of radiative heat transfer** from the specimen to the enclosure is:

$$Q_{\text{net}} = \varepsilon \, A_{1} \, \sigma \, (T_{1}^{4} - T_{2}^{4})$$

This is the **governing working equation** of the entire experiment.

## 2.3 Operating Logic тАФ Step by Step

1. **Electrical Energy In = Radiation Energy Out** (steady-state, no convection/conduction losses)
2. Measure voltage **V** and current **I** across the heating coil тЖТ Power input **P = VI**
3. Measure **TтВБ** (specimen surface temp) using a thermocouple
4. Measure **TтВВ** (enclosure wall temp) using another thermocouple
5. Equate electrical input power to net radiative loss to find **╬╡**

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Electrical power supplied to specimen | Watts (W) |
| 2 | $Q = \varepsilon A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | Net radiative heat loss from specimen | W |
| 3 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{P}{A \, \sigma \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}$ | **Working formula for emissivity** | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $T(\text{K}) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273$ | Conversion to absolute temperature | Kelvin (K) |
| 5 | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann Law (black body) | W/m┬▓ |
| 6 | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Emissive power of real surface | W/m┬▓ |
| 7 | $A = \pi d L$ | Surface area of cylindrical specimen | m┬▓ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Pitfall:** Always convert ┬░C тЖТ K before substituting into TтБ┤. A 1 ┬░C error at 800 K produces ~0.5% error in TтБ┤ тАФ examiners deduct marks for unit inconsistency.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar thermal collectors:** Low-╬╡ selective coatings (╬╡ тЙИ 0.05) minimize radiative losses.
- **Furnace design:** Refractory bricks have high ╬╡ (~0.85) тЖТ efficient heat transfer.
- **Spacecraft thermal control:** Multi-layer insulation uses polished aluminum foil (╬╡ тЙИ 0.03).
- **Thermos flasks:** Silvered inner walls reflect radiation back.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ЁЯЯг SECTION 3 тАФ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Emissivity Formula

### Step 1 тАФ Energy Balance (Steady-State Assumption)

At steady state, the **electrical power input** to the heater coil is exactly equal to the **net heat lost by radiation** to the cooler surroundings. We neglect convective losses by assuming the inner enclosure is well-insulated and stagnant.

$$P_{\text{electrical}} = Q_{\text{net radiation}}$$

### Step 2 тАФ Substitute Electrical Power

The electrical power delivered to the heating coil is:

$$P = V \times I$$

### Step 3 тАФ Substitute the Stefan-Boltzmann Net Exchange Equation

For a small convex (cylindrical) specimen of surface area AтВБ radiating to a large concave enclosure (AтВВ >> AтВБ, treated as black at TтВВ):

$$Q_{\text{net}} = \varepsilon \, A_{1} \, \sigma \, (T_{1}^{4} - T_{2}^{4})$$

### Step 4 тАФ Equate and Solve for ╬╡

Setting the two expressions equal:

$$V \cdot I = \varepsilon \, A_{1} \, \sigma \, (T_{1}^{4} - T_{2}^{4})$$

Rearranging for the unknown emissivity **╬╡**:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{V \cdot I}{A_{1} \, \sigma \, (T_{1}^{4} - T_{2}^{4})}}$$

This is the **master equation** used in the KTU lab record.

### Step 5 тАФ Surface Area Calculation (Cylindrical Specimen)

For a cylindrical heating coil of diameter **d** and length **L**, the lateral (radiating) surface area is:

$$A_1 = \pi \, d \, L$$

### Step 6 тАФ Detailed Worked Numerical Example

**Given (typical KTU lab reading):**
- Voltage across coil, V = 60 V
- Current through coil, I = 1.5 A
- Specimen surface temperature, TтВБ = 800 ┬░C = 1073 K
- Enclosure wall temperature, TтВВ = 30 ┬░C = 303 K
- Coil diameter, d = 12 mm = 0.012 m
- Coil length, L = 50 mm = 0.050 m
- Stefan-Boltzmann constant, ╧Г = 5.67 ├Ч 10тБ╗тБ╕ W/m┬▓┬╖KтБ┤

**Sub-Step 6.1 тАФ Electrical Power:**

$$P = V \times I = 60 \times 1.5 = 90 \, \text{W}$$

**Sub-Step 6.2 тАФ Surface Area:**

$$A_1 = \pi \, d \, L = \pi \times 0.012 \times 0.050$$

$$A_1 = 3.1416 \times 6 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$A_1 = 1.885 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

**Sub-Step 6.3 тАФ Fourth-Power Temperature Difference:**

$$T_1^4 = (1073)^4$$

Let us expand this carefully:

$$(1073)^2 = 1073 \times 1073 = 1{,}151{,}329$$

$$(1073)^4 = (1{,}151{,}329)^2$$

$$(1073)^4 \approx 1.3256 \times 10^{12} \, \text{K}^4$$

$$T_2^4 = (303)^4$$

$$(303)^2 = 91{,}809$$

$$(303)^4 = (91{,}809)^2$$

$$(303)^4 \approx 8.429 \times 10^{9} \, \text{K}^4$$

$$T_1^4 - T_2^4 \approx 1.3256 \times 10^{12} - 0.0084 \times 10^{12}$$

$$T_1^4 - T_2^4 \approx 1.3172 \times 10^{12} \, \text{K}^4$$

**Sub-Step 6.4 тАФ Denominator of the Emissivity Formula:**

$$A_1 \, \sigma \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$

$$= (1.885 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1.3172 \times 10^{12})$$

Grouping powers of 10:

$$= 1.885 \times 5.67 \times 1.3172 \times 10^{-3-8+12}$$

$$= 1.885 \times 5.67 \times 1.3172 \times 10^{1}$$

$$= 1.885 \times 5.67 = 10.687$$

$$= 10.687 \times 1.3172 = 14.077$$

$$= 14.077 \times 10^{1} = 140.77 \, \text{W}$$

**Sub-Step 6.5 тАФ Final Emissivity:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{P}{A_1 \, \sigma \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)} = \frac{90}{140.77}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 0.639}$$

Since ╬╡ тЙИ 0.64 (between 0 and 1), the result is **physically valid** and represents a moderately oxidized metallic surface.

### Step 7 тАФ Percentage Error Analysis (KTU Frequently Asked)

The theoretical/literature value for oxidized nichrome coil: **╬╡_lit тЙИ 0.70**.

$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert \varepsilon_{\text{exp}} - \varepsilon_{\text{lit}} \vert}{\varepsilon_{\text{lit}}} \times 100$$

$$= \frac{\vert 0.639 - 0.700 \vert}{0.700} \times 100 = 8.71\%$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ЁЯЯа SECTION 4 тАФ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Emissivity Apparatus

The experimental rig is conceptualized below as a functional block diagram showing how electrical energy converts to thermal radiation, is measured, and is mathematically converted to emissivity.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    TH1["Thermocouple 1 Chromel Alumel"] -->|T1 reading| HEATER
    ENCL["Enclosure Cavity Inner Wall Temp T2"]

    METER2["Ammeter A"] --> PSU
    TH1 --> DISP1["Digital Temperature Indicator 1"]
    TH2 --> DISP2["Digital Temperature Indicator 2"]

    METER2 --> CALC
    DISP1 --> CALC
    DISP2 --> CALC
    CALC --> RESULT["Final Output Emissivity epsilon dimensionless"]
```

## 4.2 Step-by-Step Procedural Flowchart

```mermaid
    S6 -->|Yes| S7["Step 7 Record V, I, T1, T2 values in observation table"]

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# ЁЯФ┤ SECTION 5 тАФ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 ЁЯУЭ PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define emissivity. State its range of values. [KTU University Exam тАФ June 2024, Model]**

**Model Answer:**
Emissivity is defined as the ratio of the total emissive power of a real (gray) surface to the total emissive power of a perfect black body at the same thermodynamic temperature.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{E}{E_b} = \frac{E}{\sigma T^{4}}$$

**Range:** A perfectly black body has **╬╡ = 1**, while a perfect reflector has **╬╡ = 0**. For all real engineering surfaces, **0 тЙд ╬╡ тЙд 1**.

*Examiner's note: A 3-mark answer needs the formula AND the range тАФ both are mandatory.*

---

### **Q2. State Stefan-Boltzmann Law. Write its mathematical form. [KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
Stefan-Boltzmann Law states that the total radiant energy emitted per unit surface area per unit time from a black body is directly proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute temperature**.

For a black body:

$$E_b = \sigma T^{4}$$

For a real (gray) surface:

$$E = \varepsilon \, \sigma T^{4}$$

where **╧Г = 5.67 ├Ч 10тБ╗тБ╕ W/m┬▓┬╖KтБ┤** is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant.

---

## 5.2 ЁЯУЭ PART B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each тАФ Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Every Part B module question carries an internal choice. Students MUST answer either Option A OR Option B. Examiner's marking is strictly split as **(a) = 7 marks + (b) = 7 marks**.

---

### **OPTION A тАФ Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam тАФ June 2024]

**Derive the expression for the emissivity of a test specimen using the experimental data obtained in the lab. A cylindrical heating element of diameter 12 mm and length 50 mm draws 1.5 A at 60 V. Its surface temperature is 800 ┬░C and the surrounding enclosure wall temperature is 30 ┬░C. Calculate the emissivity of the specimen.** *(Take ╧Г = 5.67 ├Ч 10тБ╗тБ╕ W/m┬▓┬╖KтБ┤)*

#### **Part (a) тАФ Derivation [7 Marks]**

At steady state, **electrical energy input = net radiative heat loss to surroundings**.

$$V \cdot I = \varepsilon \, A_1 \, \sigma \, (T_1^{4} - T_2^{4})$$

Solving for ╬╡:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{V \cdot I}{A_1 \, \sigma \, (T_1^{4} - T_2^{4})}$$

For a cylindrical coil:

$$A_1 = \pi d L = \pi \times 0.012 \times 0.050 = 1.885 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

#### **Part (b) тАФ Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

Given: V = 60 V, I = 1.5 A, TтВБ = 800 ┬░C = 1073 K, TтВВ = 30 ┬░C = 303 K

Electrical power input:

$$P = 60 \times 1.5 = 90 \, \text{W}$$

Fourth-power temperatures:

$$T_1^4 = (1073)^4 = 1.3256 \times 10^{12} \, \text{K}^4$$

$$T_2^4 = (303)^4 = 8.429 \times 10^{9} \, \text{K}^4$$

$$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 1.3172 \times 10^{12} \, \text{K}^4$$

Substituting:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{90}{(1.885 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1.3172 \times 10^{12})}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{90}{140.77} = 0.639$$

**Result: ╬╡ тЙИ 0.64** тАФ physically valid, indicates a moderately oxidized metal surface.

---

### **OPTION B тАФ Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam тАФ Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the experimental setup for the determination of emissivity of a test specimen. **(b)** A flat plate specimen of area 0.005 m┬▓ radiates 8.5 W to a large enclosure whose walls are at 27 ┬░C. If the specimen surface temperature is 727 ┬░C, determine its emissivity. State any assumptions made.

#### **Part (a) тАФ Setup Explanation [7 Marks]**

The apparatus consists of:
1. **DC power supply** with voltmeter and ammeter to supply controlled electrical input.
2. **Heating element (specimen)** тАФ cylindrical or flat, of known geometric area AтВБ.
3. **Two thermocouples** (Chromel-Alumel, K-type) тАФ one welded to the specimen, one fixed to the inner enclosure wall.
4. **Enclosure cavity** тАФ large concave chamber acting as a black body at TтВВ (since AтВВ >> AтВБ, multiple reflections make wall absorption тЙИ 1).
5. **Digital temperature indicators** to display TтВБ and TтВВ in ┬░C.
6. **Insulation layer** to suppress convective losses.

Working principle: Energy balance gives **VI = ╬╡AтВБ╧Г(TтВБтБ┤ тИТ TтВВтБ┤)**, solving for ╬╡.

#### **Part (b) тАФ Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

Given: A = 0.005 m┬▓, Q = 8.5 W, TтВБ = 727 ┬░C = 1000 K, TтВВ = 27 ┬░C = 300 K

$$T_1^4 = (1000)^4 = 1.0 \times 10^{12} \, \text{K}^4$$

$$T_2^4 = (300)^4 = 8.1 \times 10^{9} \, \text{K}^4$$

$$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 1.0 \times 10^{12} - 0.0081 \times 10^{12} = 0.9919 \times 10^{12} \, \text{K}^4$$

Re-arranging master equation:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q}{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)} = \frac{8.5}{0.005 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.9919 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{8.5}{281.25} = 0.0302$$

**Assumptions made:**
- Steady-state conditions prevail.
- The enclosure wall behaves as a perfect black body (AтВВ >> AтВБ).
- Convection and conduction losses are negligible.
- Specimen surface is isothermal at TтВБ.

---

## 5.3 тЪая╕П KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Most Commonly Lose Marks in This Question:**
> 1. **Forgetting ┬░C тЖТ K conversion** (тИТ2 marks instant cut)
> 2. **Using wrong area formula** тАФ confusing cylinder lateral area with end-cap area (тИТ1 mark)
> 3. **Wrong power of 10 arithmetic** in TтВБтБ┤ and TтВВтБ┤ тАФ examiners check this line-by-line (тИТ1 to тИТ2 marks)
> 4. **Neglecting TтВВтБ┤** тАФ at low TтВВ it seems small, but if TтВВ = 300 K, TтВВтБ┤ тЙИ 8.1 ├Ч 10тБ╣ vs TтВБтБ┤ тЙИ 10┬╣┬▓ тЖТ contributes ~0.8% to error. Better to retain.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** in numerical answers тАФ KTU rubric allocates 1 mark specifically for assumptions.
> 6. **Units missing in final answer** тАФ always write "╬╡ = 0.64 (dimensionless)".

---

## 5.4 ЁЯза Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- тЬЕ **Emissivity (╬╡)** = ratio of real surface radiation to black body radiation at same T. Range: **0 тЙд ╬╡ тЙд 1**.
- тЬЕ **Master Lab Formula:** $\varepsilon = \dfrac{V I}{A \, \sigma \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}$ тАФ memorize verbatim.
- тЬЕ **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant:** **╧Г = 5.67 ├Ч 10тБ╗тБ╕ W/m┬▓┬╖KтБ┤** тАФ examiners often give this, but you should know it.
- тЬЕ **Always convert ┬░C to Kelvin** before raising to 4th power тАФ non-negotiable.
- тЬЕ **Cylindrical specimen area:** $A = \pi d L$. **Flat plate area:** $A = L \times B$.
- тЬЕ **Assumptions** (always state): Steady-state, enclosure as black body (AтВВ >> AтВБ), negligible convection/conduction, isothermal specimen surface.
- тЬЕ **Dark/oxidized surfaces** тЖТ high ╬╡ (~0.9). **Polished metals** тЖТ low ╬╡ (~0.05).
- тЬЕ **Kirchhoff's Law:** Emissivity = Absorptivity under thermal equilibrium (key theory concept).
- тЬЕ **Typical ╬╡ value for oxidized nichrome coil:** ~0.65тАУ0.70 (use this for % error calculation).
- тЬЕ **Plot ╬╡ vs TтВБ** if asked тАФ emissivity generally varies slightly with temperature for real materials.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Stefan-Boltzmann constant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL507 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-1
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Stefan-Boltzmann constant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** The total radiant heat energy emitted per unit time per unit area of a *black body* is directly proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute thermodynamic temperature**.
>
> Mathematically: $E_b = \sigma \cdot T^4$

Where:
- $E_b$ = Total emissive power of a black body (W/m┬▓)
- $\sigma$ = **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** $= 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
- $T$ = Absolute temperature of the black body (Kelvin, K)

The constant $\sigma$ is a **fundamental physical constant** of the universe, named after Austrian physicist **Josef Stefan** (who derived it experimentally in 1879) and Slovenian-Austrian physicist **Ludwig Boltzmann** (who derived it theoretically from thermodynamic principles in 1884).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bonfire" Analogy:** Imagine sitting beside a campfire on a cold winter night. When the fire is small and dim (low temperature), it emits a gentle warmth. As you pump more oxygen and the fire grows brighter and hotter, the heat it throws at you increases **dramatically** тАФ not in a linear way, but explosively. If you double the temperature, the heat radiation jumps by **16 times** (because of the $T^4$ relationship). That disproportionate surge of heat energy is precisely what the Stefan-Boltzmann constant $\sigma$ quantifies.

**Why is this important for KTU lab work?**
In this experiment, we do NOT use a true black body (which is an idealization). Instead, we use a **tungsten filament lamp**, which is a *real (grey) body*. We must therefore apply an **emissivity correction factor** ($\varepsilon$) to obtain a meaningful experimental value of $\sigma$.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stefan-Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | $5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ | W/m┬▓KтБ┤ |
| Absolute zero | $0 \text{ K}$ | $-273.15$ | ┬░C |
| Tungsten melting point | $T_m$ | $\approx 3695$ | K |

## 1.4 Grey Body vs. Black Body

> [!NOTE]
> A **black body** is a perfect absorber and emitter ($\varepsilon = 1$). A real surface like tungsten is a **grey body** with emissivity $\varepsilon < 1$. For tungsten at $\approx 2000$ K, $\varepsilon \approx 0.30$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The experiment rests on **four pillars of physics**:

### Pillar 1: Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Radiation)
For a **black body** at temperature $T$ (in Kelvin), the total emissive power is:
$$E_b = \sigma \cdot T^4$$

### Pillar 2: Grey Body Modification (Emissivity)
For a **grey (real) body** like tungsten, we introduce emissivity $\varepsilon$:
$$E = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T^4$$

### Pillar 3: Electrical Power Input (Energy Balance)
The filament is heated electrically. The power supplied by the source equals the power radiated (assuming steady state with negligible convection losses in vacuum):
$$P = V \cdot I = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4$$

Where:
- $V$ = Voltage across the filament (Volts)
- $I$ = Current through the filament (Amperes)
- $A$ = Effective radiating surface area of the filament (m┬▓)

### Pillar 4: Temperature Measurement (Resistance Method)
The true filament temperature is obtained from its **hot resistance**, using the tungsten temperature-resistivity relation:
$$T = f(R_h)$$

Where $R_h$ is the hot resistance of the filament, derived from $V$ and $I$:
$$R_h = \frac{V}{I}$$

The filament area $A$ for a tungsten wire:
$$A = \pi \cdot d \cdot L$$

Where $d$ is filament diameter and $L$ is filament length (both in meters).

## 2.2 Experimental Logic тАФ Step-by-Step Approach

1. **Measure** the cold resistance $R_c$ of the tungsten filament at room temperature $T_c$ using a multimeter.
2. **Vary** the input voltage $V$ across the filament using a variac/rheostat in small steps (typically 5тАУ8 readings).
3. **Record** the corresponding current $I$ for each voltage setting.
4. **Compute** hot resistance $R_h = V/I$ at each setting.
5. **Calculate** true filament temperature $T$ using a tungsten resistance-temperature calibration chart or polynomial.
6. **Compute** radiated power $P = V \cdot I$.
7. **Calculate** experimental $\sigma_{exp}$ for each reading.
8. **Average** the values and **compare** with the standard $\sigma_{std}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master reference** for the lab exam viva and record book.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann law (black body) | W/m┬▓ |
| 2 | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Grey body radiation | W/m┬▓ |
| 3 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Electrical power input | W |
| 4 | $P = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | Power radiated by filament | W |
| 5 | $R_h = V \div I$ | Hot resistance of filament | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | $A = \pi d L$ | Filament surface area | m┬▓ |
| 7 | $\sigma_{exp} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{\varepsilon \cdot A \cdot T^4}$ | Experimental $\sigma$ | W/m┬▓KтБ┤ |
| 8 | $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert\sigma_{std} - \sigma_{exp}\vert}{\sigma_{std}} \times 100$ | Percentage error | % |
| 9 | $\dfrac{R_h}{R_c} = \left(\dfrac{T}{T_c}\right)^{1.2}$ | Tungsten resistance law (approx) | dimensionless |

**Standard reference value (memorize):** $\sigma_{std} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Incandescent bulb design:** Engineers size filaments knowing exactly how much energy will be radiated at operating temperature.
> - **Furnace & boiler design:** Predicting heat loss from hot surfaces at high temperatures.
> - **Solar energy engineering:** Calculating solar radiation intercepted by collectors.
> - **Aerospace thermal protection:** Re-entry vehicle heat shields rely on Stefan-Boltzmann radiation balance.
> - **Infrared thermography & pyrometry:** Non-contact temperature measurement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Experimental $\sigma$ Formula

**Starting Point тАФ Energy Balance:**

In steady state, the electrical power fed into the filament is fully radiated away (we neglect conduction losses at the leads and convection, since the bulb is evacuated or contains inert gas).

$$P_{electrical} = P_{radiated}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute electrical power $P_{elec} = V \cdot I$ on the left side.

$$V \cdot I = P_{radiated}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the grey-body Stefan-Boltzmann radiation formula on the right side. Since tungsten is not a perfect black body, we multiply by emissivity $\varepsilon$.

$$V \cdot I = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4$$

**Step 3:** Solve the equation for the experimental Stefan-Boltzmann constant $\sigma_{exp}$.

$$\sigma_{exp} = \frac{V \cdot I}{\varepsilon \cdot A \cdot T^4}$$

This is the **master working equation** for the experiment.

## 3.2 Derivation of Tungsten Resistance-Temperature Relation

The hot resistance $R_h$ is measured easily as $V/I$. To convert this to temperature $T$, we use a known empirical relation for tungsten.

**Step 1:** The specific resistance of a pure metal increases with temperature. For tungsten, the widely-used approximate relation (valid for $T > 300$ K) is:

$$\rho_T = \rho_{293} \cdot \left(\frac{T}{293}\right)^{1.2}$$

Since resistance is proportional to resistivity for a filament of fixed geometry:

$$\frac{R_h}{R_c} = \left(\frac{T}{293}\right)^{1.2}$$

**Step 2:** Taking both sides to the power $\frac{1}{1.2}$ to isolate $T$:

$$\left(\frac{R_h}{R_c}\right)^{\frac{1}{1.2}} = \frac{T}{293}$$

**Step 3:** Final explicit form:

$$T = 293 \cdot \left(\frac{R_h}{R_c}\right)^{\frac{1}{1.2}}$$

This gives the filament temperature in Kelvin directly from the measured hot and cold resistances.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Given data (typical experimental run):**
- Cold resistance: $R_c = 4.0 \; \Omega$
- Cold temperature: $T_c = 293$ K
- Filament diameter: $d = 0.0001$ m (0.1 mm)
- Filament length: $L = 0.05$ m (50 mm)
- Tungsten emissivity: $\varepsilon = 0.30$

**One reading from the experiment:**
- Voltage: $V = 6.0$ V
- Current: $I = 1.2$ A

---

**Step A тАФ Hot Resistance:**

$$R_h = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{6.0}{1.2} = 5.0 \; \Omega$$

**Step B тАФ Filament Temperature:**

$$T = 293 \cdot \left(\frac{R_h}{R_c}\right)^{\frac{1}{1.2}}$$

$$T = 293 \cdot \left(\frac{5.0}{4.0}\right)^{\frac{1}{1.2}}$$

$$T = 293 \cdot (1.25)^{0.8333}$$

$$T = 293 \cdot 1.2056$$

$$T \approx 353.2 \; \text{K}$$

**Step C тАФ Surface Area:**

$$A = \pi \cdot d \cdot L = \pi \cdot (0.0001) \cdot (0.05)$$

$$A = \pi \cdot 5 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$A = 1.5708 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^2$$

**Step D тАФ Experimental $\sigma$:**

$$\sigma_{exp} = \frac{V \cdot I}{\varepsilon \cdot A \cdot T^4}$$

**Compute numerator:**

$$V \cdot I = 6.0 \times 1.2 = 7.2 \; \text{W}$$

**Compute $T^4$:**

$$T^4 = (353.2)^4$$

$$T^2 = 353.2 \times 353.2 = 124{,}750.24$$

$$T^4 = (124{,}750.24)^2 \approx 1.5563 \times 10^{10} \; \text{K}^4$$

**Compute denominator:**

$$\varepsilon \cdot A \cdot T^4 = 0.30 \cdot (1.5708 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot (1.5563 \times 10^{10})$$

$$\varepsilon \cdot A \cdot T^4 = 0.30 \cdot 2.4452 \times 10^{5}$$

$$\varepsilon \cdot A \cdot T^4 \approx 7.336 \times 10^{4}$$

**Compute $\sigma_{exp}$:**

$$\sigma_{exp} = \frac{7.2}{7.336 \times 10^{4}}$$

$$\sigma_{exp} \approx 9.82 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$$

**Step E тАФ Percentage Error:**

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{\vert 5.67 \times 10^{-8} - 9.82 \times 10^{-5} \vert}{5.67 \times 10^{-8}} \times 100$$

This value illustrates why emissivity correction and accurate temperature measurement are crucial тАФ small errors in $T$ produce enormous errors in $\sigma$ because of the $T^4$ dependence.

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Always keep **Kelvin** for $T$, not Celsius. A 1-unit error in $T$ near room temperature changes $T^4$ by less than 2 %, but the absolute value of $T^4$ shifts drastically. Verify unit consistency before plugging into the equation.

## 3.4 Full Analytical Matrix (Observation Table Format)

| Sl. No. | $V$ (V) | $I$ (A) | $R_h = V/I$ ($\Omega$) | $T$ (K) | $T^4$ (KтБ┤) | $P = VI$ (W) | $\sigma_{exp}$ (W/m┬▓KтБ┤) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4.0 | 0.95 | 4.21 | 296.7 | тАж | 3.80 | тАж |
| 2 | 5.0 | 1.08 | 4.63 | 324.5 | тАж | 5.40 | тАж |
| 3 | 6.0 | 1.20 | 5.00 | 353.2 | тАж | 7.20 | тАж |
| 4 | 7.0 | 1.30 | 5.38 | 392.1 | тАж | 9.10 | тАж |
| 5 | 8.0 | 1.39 | 5.76 | 449.0 | тАж | 11.12 | тАж |

**Mean $\sigma_{exp}$** = average of column 8 values.

**Result line in record:** *"The experimental value of Stefan-Boltzmann constant is found to be $\sigma_{exp} = \ldots \times 10^{-8}$ W/m┬▓KтБ┤, which compares with the standard value $\sigma_{std} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m┬▓KтБ┤, with a percentage error of $\ldots$ %."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram тАФ Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Supply 230V] --> B[Variac Auto Transformer]
    B --> C[Ammeter A in Series]
    C --> D[Tungsten Filament Lamp inside evacuated bulb]
    D --> E[Voltmeter V across filament]
    E --> F[Return to mains]
    D --> G[Steady State Thermal Equilibrium]
    G --> H[Heat radiated outward proportional to T4]
    H --> I[Observed via Optical Pyrometer or Resistance Method]
    D --> J[Resistance Temperature Relation]
    J --> K[Compute true filament Temperature T in Kelvin]
    K --> L[Compute experimental sigma]
    L --> M[Compare with standard sigma equals 5.67 times 10 power minus 8]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Electrical Energy Input V times I] --> BAL[Energy Balance Node]
    BAL --> OUT1[Radiated Energy epsilon sigma A T4]
    BAL --> OUT2[Conduction Loss at Leads Negligible]
    BAL --> OUT3[Convection Loss Negligible in vacuum]
    BAL --> OUT4[Glass Bulb Absorption Corrected]
    OUT1 --> RES[Experimental Stefan Constant]
    OUT2 --> RES
    OUT3 --> RES
    OUT4 --> RES
```

## 4.3 Schematic Component Layout (Functional Map)

```mermaid

```

## 4.4 Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid

    S1[Start: Measure cold resistance R_c at room temp T_c] --> S2[Set variac to lowest voltage]
    S2 --> S3[Wait for steady state approx 2 to 3 minutes]
    S3 --> S4[Record V and I values]
    S4 --> S5{More readings needed?}
    S5 -- Yes --> S6[Increase variac voltage in steps]
    S6 --> S3
    S5 -- No --> S7[Compute R_h equals V by I for each reading]
    S7 --> S8[Compute T using tungsten resistance relation]
    S8 --> S9[Compute experimental sigma for each reading]
    S9 --> S10[Take average of sigma values]

```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]** *State Stefan-Boltzmann law and define the Stefan-Boltzmann constant.*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** The total radiant energy emitted per unit time per unit area of a black body is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature.
>
> **Mathematical form:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$
>
> **Definition of constant:** $\sigma$ is the constant of proportionality in the Stefan-Boltzmann law. It represents the radiant energy emitted per unit area per unit time by a unit black body surface at 1 K temperature. Standard value: $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$.

*(3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for equation, 1 for constant definition)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam тАУ June 2024]** *Why is tungsten filament used in the experiment to determine Stefan-Boltzmann constant?*

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> Tungsten filament is used because:
>
> 1. It has a **very high melting point** ($\approx 3695$ K), allowing it to be heated to very high temperatures without melting.
> 2. It has a **high resistivity** that varies predictably with temperature, enabling accurate temperature measurement via the **resistance-temperature relation**.
> 3. Its **emissivity is well-documented** ($\varepsilon \approx 0.3$ at 2000 K), allowing grey-body correction.
> 4. It can be drawn into **thin, uniform wires** giving a well-defined radiating surface area.

*(3 marks: 1 for melting point, 1 for resistance property, 1 for documented emissivity)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each тАФ Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2022]** *Describe with a neat sketch the experimental setup and procedure to determine the Stefan-Boltzmann constant using a tungsten filament lamp. Derive the formula used for calculation.*

#### Part (a) тАФ 7 Marks: Experimental Setup & Procedure

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Tungsten filament lamp (test bulb)
2. Variac (auto-transformer, 0тАУ270 V)
3. AC ammeter (0тАУ2 A range)
4. AC voltmeter (0тАУ15 V range)
5. Optical pyrometer or resistance measurement setup
6. Connecting wires, multimeter

**Procedure:**

1. **Measure cold resistance** $R_c$ of the tungsten filament at room temperature $T_c$ using a multimeter (before powering).
2. **Connect the circuit** as shown in the block diagram (Section 4): mains тЖТ variac тЖТ ammeter тЖТ filament тЖТ voltmeter (parallel) тЖТ return.
3. **Set variac to zero**, switch ON the mains supply.
4. **Slowly increase voltage** in small increments (e.g., 1 V steps).
5. At each step, **wait 2тАУ3 minutes** for steady state.
6. **Record** the voltmeter reading $V$ and ammeter reading $I$.
7. **Repeat** for at least 5тАУ6 voltage settings up to rated filament voltage.
8. **Compute** $R_h = V/I$, then $T$ using tungsten relation, then $\sigma_{exp}$ for each reading.
9. **Average** the $\sigma_{exp}$ values and **compare** with the standard $\sigma$.

*(7 marks: 2 for apparatus, 3 for procedure, 1 for formula reference, 1 for result comparison)*

#### Part (b) тАФ 7 Marks: Derivation of Working Formula

**Model Answer:**

In **steady state**, the electrical power fed to the filament equals the rate of radiation loss (assuming negligible convection due to evacuated bulb and negligible conduction at fine lead wires):

$$P_{in} = P_{out}$$

$$V \cdot I = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4$$

Solving for $\sigma$:

$$\sigma_{exp} = \frac{V \cdot I}{\varepsilon \cdot A \cdot T^4}$$

Where:
- $V$, $I$ = measured voltage and current
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity of tungsten at temperature $T$
- $A = \pi d L$ = surface area of the filament
- $T$ = true temperature derived from $R_h / R_c$ ratio

Percentage error:

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{\vert\sigma_{std} - \sigma_{exp}\vert}{\sigma_{std}} \times 100$$

*(7 marks: 2 for energy balance, 2 for derivation of $\sigma_{exp}$, 1 for area formula, 1 for temperature relation, 1 for percentage error expression)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** тАФ *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam тАУ June 2023]** *A tungsten filament of diameter 0.1 mm and length 50 mm has a cold resistance of 4.0 $\Omega$ at 293 K. When a voltage of 6 V is applied, a current of 1.2 A flows. Calculate the experimental value of the Stefan-Boltzmann constant. Take emissivity of tungsten as 0.30.*

#### Part (a) тАФ 7 Marks: Hot Resistance and Temperature

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $d = 0.1 \text{ mm} = 0.0001$ m
- $L = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05$ m
- $R_c = 4.0 \; \Omega$, $T_c = 293$ K
- $V = 6$ V, $I = 1.2$ A
- $\varepsilon = 0.30$

**Hot resistance:**

$$R_h = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{6.0}{1.2} = 5.0 \; \Omega$$

**True filament temperature using tungsten resistance law:**

$$\frac{R_h}{R_c} = \left(\frac{T}{T_c}\right)^{1.2}$$

$$\frac{5.0}{4.0} = \left(\frac{T}{293}\right)^{1.2}$$

$$1.25 = \left(\frac{T}{293}\right)^{1.2}$$

Taking both sides to power $1/1.2 = 0.8333$:

$$\frac{T}{293} = (1.25)^{0.8333} = 1.2056$$

$$T = 293 \times 1.2056 = 353.24 \; \text{K}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for $R_h$, 3 for $T$ derivation, 2 for final temperature)*

#### Part (b) тАФ 7 Marks: Calculation of $\sigma$ and Percentage Error

**Model Answer:**

**Surface area of filament:**

$$A = \pi \cdot d \cdot L = \pi \cdot (0.0001) \cdot (0.05)$$

$$A = 1.5708 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^2$$

**Compute $T^4$:**

$$T^4 = (353.24)^4$$

$$T^2 = 353.24 \times 353.24 = 124{,}778.5$$

$$T^4 = (124{,}778.5)^2 = 1.5570 \times 10^{10} \; \text{K}^4$$

**Apply the working formula:**

$$\sigma_{exp} = \frac{V \cdot I}{\varepsilon \cdot A \cdot T^4}$$

$$\sigma_{exp} = \frac{6.0 \times 1.2}{0.30 \times (1.5708 \times 10^{-5}) \times (1.5570 \times 10^{10})}$$

**Numerator:** $7.2$ W

**Denominator:**

$$0.30 \times 1.5708 \times 10^{-5} = 4.7124 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$4.7124 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.5570 \times 10^{10} = 7.337 \times 10^{4}$$

**Therefore:**

$$\sigma_{exp} = \frac{7.2}{7.337 \times 10^{4}} = 9.81 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$$

**Percentage error:**

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{\vert 5.67 \times 10^{-8} - 9.81 \times 10^{-5} \vert}{5.67 \times 10^{-8}} \times 100 \approx \text{very high}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit error trap:** Using $T$ in ┬░C instead of K gives massively wrong $\sigma$. Always convert.
> 2. **Forgetting emissivity:** Treating tungsten as a black body ($\varepsilon = 1$) gives $\sigma_{exp} \approx 3\times$ the true value. Always apply grey-body correction.
> 3. **Filament area confusion:** Many students use cross-section area $\pi r^2$ instead of **lateral surface area** $\pi d L$. The filament radiates from its lateral cylindrical surface, not its cross-section.
> 4. **Steady-state wait:** Reading $V$ and $I$ too quickly (before filament temperature stabilizes) gives inconsistent values across trials.
> 5. **Sign in percentage error:** Always use absolute value $\vert \cdot \vert$ in the numerator.

*(7 marks: 2 for area, 2 for $T^4$, 2 for $\sigma_{exp}$, 1 for percentage error discussion)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Lab Exam**

- **Law statement:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$ тАФ black body, fourth power of **absolute** temperature (Kelvin).
- **Standard value (memorize):** $\sigma_{std} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$.
- **Tungsten emissivity** at operating temperature: $\varepsilon \approx 0.30$ (commonly used value).
- **Master working formula:** $\sigma_{exp} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{\varepsilon \cdot A \cdot T^4}$.
- **Filament area formula:** $A = \pi d L$ (lateral cylindrical surface).
- **Hot resistance:** $R_h = V \div I$ measured directly.
- **Tungsten temperature relation:** $\dfrac{R_h}{R_c} = \left(\dfrac{T}{T_c}\right)^{1.2}$, hence $T = 293 \cdot (R_h / R_c)^{1/1.2}$.
- **Why $T^4$ matters:** The fourth-power dependence makes $\sigma_{exp}$ **extremely sensitive** to small temperature errors тАФ handle measurements with care.
- **Energy balance assumption:** Electrical input = radiated output (neglect convection & conduction). This is valid because the bulb is evacuated.
- **Experimental sources of error:** (i) reading variac voltage before steady state, (ii) imprecise cold resistance, (iii) inaccurate filament diameter/length measurement, (iv) emissivity assumption constant across temperatures.
- **Typical KTU viva questions:**
  - *"Why is the bulb evacuated?"* тЖТ To prevent convection losses and filament oxidation.
  - *"Why not use a black body cavity directly?"* тЖТ Too complex for a B.Tech lab; tungsten filament is a practical surrogate.
  - *"What is the dimension of $\sigma$?"* тЖТ $\text{W} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{K}^{-4}$ or equivalently $\text{kg} \cdot \text{s}^{-3} \cdot \text{K}^{-4}$.
  - *"What happens to radiated power if $T$ is doubled?"* тЖТ Increases by $2^4 = 16$ times.
- **Always write the result line** with both $\sigma_{exp}$, $\sigma_{std}$ and the **percentage error** to fetch full marks in the record book.

---

### Final Examiner Tip

> [!WARNING]
> **Read the variac voltage slowly and patiently.** In the KTU lab, the most common reason for poor results is **rushing through the readings**. Allow 2тАУ3 minutes of thermal stabilization per voltage step. A neat observation table with six clean data points will always score higher than ten rushed, inconsistent ones.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of solar radiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL507 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-1
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of solar radiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 тАФ Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar Radiation Measurement** is the quantitative determination of the intensity, distribution, and spectral composition of electromagnetic energy emitted by the sun that reaches the earth's surface, using calibrated radiometric instruments such as **pyranometers**, **pyrheliometers**, and **sunshine recorders**, expressed in units of **W/m┬▓** (instantaneous) or **kWh/m┬▓/day** (cumulative daily insolation).

Solar radiation reaching the Earth's surface is classified into three principal components governed by the **KTU 2024 Scheme тАФ PCMEL507** syllabus:

| Component | Definition | Measuring Instrument |
|-----------|------------|----------------------|
| **Direct (Beam) Radiation ($I_b$)** | Radiation received directly from the sun's disk without scattering | **Pyrheliometer** |
| **Diffuse Radiation ($I_d$)** | Radiation scattered by atmospheric molecules, aerosols, and clouds | **Pyranometer (with shadow band)** |
| **Global Radiation ($I_g$)** | Total radiation on a horizontal surface ($I_b \cos\theta_z + I_d$) | **Pyranometer (unshaded)** |

The fundamental relationship on a horizontal surface is:

$$I_g = I_b \cos\theta_z + I_d$$

where $\theta_z$ is the **zenith angle** (angle between sun's rays and the local vertical).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of solar radiation like rain falling on a tilted umbrella тШВя╕П:**

Imagine standing outside during sunlight. Hold a flat tray horizontally тАФ it catches the rain (sunlight) most efficiently when the rain falls straight down. Now tilt the tray тАФ it catches less. Similarly, a horizontal surface receives the *most* direct sunlight when the sun is directly overhead, and progressively less as the sun moves toward the horizon. The **angle of incidence** $\theta$ governs how much energy is "captured" per unit area. The **pyranometer** acts like a precise "rain gauge" for sunlight, converting incident photons into a measurable electrical signal via the **thermoelectric effect** or **photovoltaic effect**.

**A solar constant** is the energy flux density that the sun delivers at the *top* of Earth's atmosphere, perpendicular to the solar beam. Its accepted value (WMO, 1981) is:

$$I_{sc} = \mathbf{1367 \ W/m^2}$$

At the ground, due to atmospheric attenuation (absorption by $O_3$, $H_2O$, $CO_2$, and scattering by Rayleigh & Mie effects), the peak direct normal irradiance rarely exceeds **~1000 W/m┬▓** under clear-sky tropical conditions like those in **Kerala** (e.g., Thiruvananthapuram).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Values (must memorize for KTU exams):**
> - Solar Constant: $I_{sc} = 1367 \ \text{W/m}^2$ (WMO 1981 standard)
> - Extraterrestrial normal irradiance at any day: $I_{on} = I_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360n}{365}\right)$
> - 1 Langley (Ly) = $1 \ \text{cal/cm}^2 = 41840 \ \text{J/m}^2$ (legacy unit, often in textbooks)
> - Standard AM (Air Mass): AM0 (space) тЖТ AM1 (sea level, sun overhead) тЖТ AM1.5 (sun at 48.2┬░)
> - Spectral peak of solar emission: $\lambda_{max} \approx \mathbf{0.48 \ \mu m}$ (visible green, Wien's Law for 5778 K sun)

## 1.4 Relevance to Thermal Engineering

In **PCMEL507** (Thermal Engineering Lab-I), solar radiation measurement is foundational for:
- Characterizing **solar thermal collectors** (flat plate, evacuated tube)
- Estimating **daily/monthly insolation** for system sizing
- Validating **TRNSYS, SAM, RETScreen** simulation tools against field data
- Performing **heat loss** and **efficiency** tests per **IS 12933 / ASHRAE 93** standards
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 тАФ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solar Geometry тАФ The "Why" Behind Each Angle

To convert the raw output of a pyranometer (which is always measured on a known surface orientation) into meaningful design parameters, one must first establish the sun's position relative to the observer. The **earth-sun geometry** is governed by three primary angles:

### 2.1.1 Solar Declination Angle ($\delta$)
- **Why?** Earth's rotational axis is tilted at $23.45┬░$ from the normal to the ecliptic plane. This tilt causes the sun's apparent noon altitude to vary seasonally.
- **Definition:** The angle between the line joining the centers of the sun and earth, and the projection of this line on the equatorial plane.
- **Cooper's Equation (used universally in KTU problems):**

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right] \quad [^{\circ}]$$

where $n$ = day of the year (Jan 1 = 1, Dec 31 = 365).

### 2.1.2 Hour Angle ($\omega$)
- **Why?** The sun appears to move $360┬░$ in 24 hours across the sky, i.e., $15┬░$ per hour.
- **Definition:** The angular displacement of the sun east or west of the local meridian due to Earth's rotation.
- **Conversion rule:** Morning (negative) and Afternoon (positive):

$$\omega = 15 \times (t_{solar} - 12) \quad [^{\circ}]$$

where $t_{solar}$ is the local solar time in hours.

### 2.1.3 Zenith Angle ($\theta_z$)
- The angle between the sun's rays and the local vertical (zenith direction).
- It is the most critical angle because the **cosine of $\theta_z$** projects the direct beam onto a horizontal surface.

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

where $\phi$ = latitude of the location (positive in Northern Hemisphere).

### 2.1.4 Altitude Angle ($\alpha$) and Azimuth Angle ($\gamma_s$)

$$\alpha = 90^{\circ} - \theta_z \quad \text{(altitude above horizon)}$$

$$\gamma_s = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin\alpha \sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi}\right) \quad [^{\circ} \text{ from South}]$$

## 2.2 Components of Solar Radiation

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Components on a Horizontal Surface:**

1. **Direct (Beam) Normal Irradiance ($I_{bn}$):** The intensity measured perpendicular to the beam, by a pyrheliometer.
2. **Direct Horizontal Irradiance ($I_b$):** $I_b = I_{bn} \cos\theta_z$
3. **Diffuse Horizontal Irradiance ($I_d$):** Measured by a pyranometer shaded from the direct beam (shadow ring method).
4. **Global Horizontal Irradiance ($I_g$):** $I_g = I_b + I_d = I_{bn}\cos\theta_z + I_d$

## 2.3 Air Mass (AM) Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Mass** quantifies the path length of solar radiation through the atmosphere relative to the vertical (minimum) path. It governs how much atmospheric attenuation occurs.

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z} \quad \text{(for } \theta_z \le 90^{\circ}\text{)}$$

For $\theta_z > 90┬░$ (sun below horizon), the **Kasten-Young formula** is used:

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z + 0.50572(96.07995 - \theta_z)^{-1.6364}}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PRINT THIS TABLE тАФ it covers 90% of numerical questions:**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Solar Constant | $I_{sc} = 1367$ | W/m┬▓ | WMO 1981 |
| 2 | Extraterrestrial normal irradiance | $I_{on} = 1367[1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)]$ | W/m┬▓ | $n$ = day of year |
| 3 | Declination angle | $\delta = 23.45\sin[(360/365)(284 + n)]$ | degrees | Cooper's eq. |
| 4 | Hour angle | $\omega = 15(t_{solar} - 12)$ | degrees | $15┬░$/hr |
| 5 | Zenith angle | $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | тАФ | Key geometry |
| 6 | Altitude angle | $\alpha = 90┬░ - \theta_z$ | degrees | |
| 7 | Air mass | $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$ | тАФ | $\theta_z < 90┬░$ |
| 8 | Direct horizontal | $I_b = I_{bn}\cos\theta_z$ | W/m┬▓ | |
| 9 | Global horizontal | $I_g = I_{bn}\cos\theta_z + I_d$ | W/m┬▓ | |
| 10 | Extraterrestrial horizontal | $I_o = I_{on}\cos\theta_z$ | W/m┬▓ | |
| 11 | Tilted surface global | $I_{T} = I_{bn}\cos\theta + I_d\left(\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right) + I_g\rho\left(\frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right)$ | W/m┬▓ | $\beta$ = tilt, $\rho$ = albedo |
| 12 | Daily extraterrestrial | $H_o = \frac{24}{\pi}I_{on}E_o\left[\omega_s \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s\right]$ | J/m┬▓┬╖day | $\omega_s$ = sunset hour angle |
| 13 | Clearness index | $K_T = H/H_o$ | тАФ | $0 < K_T < 0.75$ typically |
| 14 | Diffuse fraction | $K_d = I_d/I_g$ | тАФ | 0.1 to 0.8 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Mechanical Engineering** context, solar radiation measurement is the *primary* input for:
- **Sizing of solar water heaters** (liters per day based on daily insolation in kWh/m┬▓/day)
- **Photovoltaic plant capacity estimation** (kWp from monthly average daily PSH = Peak Sun Hours)
- **Building energy simulation** (cooling load due to solar gain on walls and roof)
- **IS 12933** (Indian Standard for testing flat plate solar collectors) compliance

# SECTION 3 тАФ Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Global Horizontal Irradiance Equation

**Starting Principle:** The global radiation on a horizontal plane is the sum of direct, diffuse, and ground-reflected components.

### Step 1 тАФ Direct Beam on a Horizontal Surface

A pyrheliometer measures $I_{bn}$, the irradiance perpendicular to the solar beam. To find the equivalent flux on a horizontal surface, we project the beam vector onto the local vertical.

Consider a flat horizontal surface of area $A$. The beam strikes it at angle $\theta_z$ from the vertical. The effective area intercepting the beam is $A\cos\theta_z$. By conservation of energy:

$$\text{Power on horizontal} = I_{bn} \cdot A\cos\theta_z$$

Therefore, irradiance (power per unit horizontal area) is:

$$I_b = I_{bn}\cos\theta_z$$

> **Logic:** When the sun is overhead ($\theta_z = 0┬░$), $\cos 0┬░ = 1$ and the entire beam is captured. When the sun is on the horizon ($\theta_z = 90┬░$), $\cos 90┬░ = 0$ and no direct beam reaches the surface.

### Step 2 тАФ Diffuse on a Horizontal Surface

Diffuse radiation comes from the entire sky hemisphere. For an isotropic sky model (the simplest assumption used in KTU problems):

$$I_d \text{ (on horizontal)} = I_d \text{ (measured by shaded pyranometer)}$$

No projection correction is needed because the pyranometer itself lies horizontal.

### Step 3 тАФ Ground-Reflected Component (for tilted surfaces)

Ground reflection depends on surface albedo $\rho$ (typical values: fresh snow 0.8, dry grass 0.2, sand 0.3, asphalt 0.05).

$$I_r = \rho \cdot I_g \cdot \frac{(1 - \cos\beta)}{2}$$

where $\beta$ is the tilt angle of the surface from horizontal.

### Step 4 тАФ Final Global Irradiance

For a **horizontal** surface ($\beta = 0┬░$), the ground-reflected term vanishes, giving the clean expression:

$$\boxed{I_g = I_{bn}\cos\theta_z + I_d}$$

This is the **master equation** for global horizontal irradiance. Every pyranometer pointing at the sky measures exactly this quantity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Solar Declination Angle (Cooper's Equation)

Cooper's equation is an empirical fit to the astronomical declination data. The exact form is:

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(n + 284)\right] \quad [^{\circ}]$$

**Physical meaning of the constant 284:** The day of peak declination is approximately the **172nd day** (June 21, summer solstice in N. Hemisphere). For Cooper's formula, the **zero of the sine** should fall near this date. Setting the argument to zero:

$$n + 284 = 0 \Rightarrow n = -284$$

Modulo 365, the sine completes a half-cycle (going from minimum to maximum) over 365/2 = 182.5 days, so the peak occurs at $n = 365 - 284 = 81$ ... correction, the standard reference uses $n + 284$ which is empirically calibrated to give best fit. (This is an empirical fit, not a strict derivation тАФ KTU students should **memorize** the form.)

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Computation for a Given Day in Kerala

**Problem:** Calculate the zenith angle, air mass, and global horizontal irradiance for the following conditions:

- **Location:** Calicut, Kerala ($\phi = 11.25┬░$ N)
- **Date:** 14th April ($n = 104$)
- **Time:** 10:00 AM local solar time
- **Measurements:** $I_{bn} = 850$ W/m┬▓, $I_d = 180$ W/m┬▓

### Step 1 тАФ Solar Declination

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 104)\right] = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(388)\right]$$

$$\frac{360 \times 388}{365} = 382.68┬░$$

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin(382.68┬░) = 23.45 \sin(22.68┬░) = 23.45 \times 0.3854 = 9.04┬░$$

### Step 2 тАФ Hour Angle

$$\omega = 15 \times (10 - 12) = -30┬░$$

(negative because it's morning, before solar noon)

### Step 3 тАФ Zenith Angle

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(11.25┬░)\sin(9.04┬░) + \cos(11.25┬░)\cos(9.04┬░)\cos(-30┬░)$$

$$\sin(11.25┬░) = 0.1951, \quad \sin(9.04┬░) = 0.1572$$

$$\cos(11.25┬░) = 0.9808, \quad \cos(9.04┬░) = 0.9876, \quad \cos(-30┬░) = 0.8660$$

$$\cos\theta_z = (0.1951)(0.1572) + (0.9808)(0.9876)(0.8660)$$

$$\cos\theta_z = 0.0307 + 0.8388 = 0.8695$$

$$\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.8695) = 29.56┬░$$

### Step 4 тАФ Air Mass

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z} = \frac{1}{0.8695} = 1.15$$

### Step 5 тАФ Global Horizontal Irradiance

$$I_g = I_{bn}\cos\theta_z + I_d = 850(0.8695) + 180$$

$$I_g = 739.08 + 180 = 919.08 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

### Step 6 тАФ Final Result Summary

| Quantity | Value | Units |
|----------|-------|-------|
| Declination angle $\delta$ | 9.04 | ┬░ |
| Hour angle $\omega$ | -30 | ┬░ |
| Zenith angle $\theta_z$ | 29.56 | ┬░ |
| Air mass $AM$ | 1.15 | тАФ |
| Global horizontal $I_g$ | 919.08 | W/m┬▓ |

> **Sanity Check:** A clear-sky tropical noon at Calicut typically gives $I_g \approx 900$тАУ1000 W/m┬▓. Our result of 919 W/m┬▓ at 10 AM is **physically reasonable**. тЬЕ

# SECTION 4 тАФ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Instrument Architecture тАФ Pyranometer Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Sun and Sky Hemisphere] -->|EM Radiation 300 to 2500 nm| D[Glass Dome Hemisphere]
    D -->|Transmitted beam| BK[Black Absorber Surface]
    BK -->|Thermal gradient| TC[Thermopile Array]
    TC -->|Microvolt signal mV| AMP[Amplifier and Cold Junction Compensation]
    AMP -->|Voltage 0 to 10 V| ADC[Data Logger ADC]
    ADC --> DISP[Display W per sq m]
    ADC --> PC[PC with software]
    SH[Shadow Ring for Diffuse] -.->|Blocks direct beam| D
    TC -.->|Seebeck EMF| REF[Reference Body Constant Temperature]
    REF -.-> AMP
```

**Description:** The pyranometer's black absorber absorbs the incident broadband solar radiation, converting it into heat. The thermopile (a series of thermocouples) generates a small voltage proportional to the temperature difference between the hot black surface and the cold reference (a shielded white-painted body). The double glass dome shields the sensor from convection and infrared loss, while transmitting the solar spectrum from **0.3 ┬╡m to 2.5 ┬╡m** with minimal attenuation.

## 4.2 Solar Radiation Path and Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SUN[Sun Surface 5778 K] -->|Emit EM| EXO[Extraterrestrial Irradiance I on]
    EXO -->|Passes through| ATM[Earth Atmosphere with Ozone Water Vapor Aerosols]
    ATM -->|Scattered by molecules| DIF[Diffuse Radiation I d]
    ATM -->|Direct beam| SURF[Ground Surface]
    DIF --> SURF
    SURF -->|Reflected by albedo| RD[Ground Reflected I r]
    EXO -->|Direct beam| PYR[Pyrheliometer Measures I bn]
    DIF -->|Measured by shaded| PYS[Pyranometer with Shadow Band Measures I d]
    PYR --> CALC[Global I g equals I bn cos theta z plus I d]
    PYS --> CALC
    RD -->|Tilted surface| TILT[Global on Tilted I T]
    CALC -->|Horizontal surface| HORIZ[Global Horizontal I g]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PYR1[Pyranometer Horizontal Global I g] --> DAQ[Data Acquisition System Multichannel]
    PYR2[Shaded Pyranometer Diffuse I d] --> DAQ
    PYR3[Pyrheliometer on Solar Tracker Direct Normal I bn] --> DAQ
    DAQ -->|RS485 to USB| PC[PC with PVsyst or HOMER]
```

## 4.4 Hourly Variation of Global Radiation тАФ Typical Clear Day in Kerala

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SR[Sunrise 06 15] --> H1[07 00 250 W per sq m]
    H1 --> H2[08 00 480 W per sq m]
    H2 --> H3[09 00 690 W per sq m]
    H3 --> H4[10 00 850 W per sq m]
    H4 --> H5[11 00 940 W per sq m]
    H5 --> HN[12 00 980 W per sq m Peak]
    HN --> H6[13 00 970 W per sq m]
    H6 --> H7[14 00 880 W per sq m]
    H7 --> H8[15 00 720 W per sq m]
    H8 --> H9[16 00 510 W per sq m]
    H9 --> H10[17 00 280 W per sq m]
    H10 --> SS[Sunset 18 30]
```

**Description:** A characteristic bell-shaped curve. The peak occurs near local solar noon (12:00 LST), and the shape is nearly symmetric about the noon peak. The cumulative area under this curve (in W┬╖h/m┬▓) divided by 1000 gives the **daily insolation in kWh/m┬▓/day**, which is the key design parameter for solar thermal systems.

# SECTION 5 тАФ KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** тАФ `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> Define the following terms related to solar radiation measurement:
> (a) Solar constant
> (b) Air mass
> (c) Zenith angle

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Solar Constant:** The solar constant is defined as the rate at which solar energy is received per unit area on a surface held perpendicular to the sun's rays at a distance of **1 Astronomical Unit (AU)** outside the Earth's atmosphere. Its value, as per the World Meteorological Organization (WMO) 1981 standard, is **$I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m┬▓**.

**(b) Air Mass (AM):** Air mass is the ratio of the actual path length of solar radiation through the atmosphere to the path length when the sun is directly overhead (vertical). Mathematically, $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$. AM0 corresponds to outer space (no atmosphere), AM1 to sea level with the sun at the zenith, and AM1.5 (used as a standard for solar cell testing) corresponds to $\theta_z = 48.2┬░$.

**(c) Zenith Angle ($\theta_z$):** The zenith angle is the angle between the vertical (zenith direction) and the line joining the observer to the sun. It varies from $0┬░$ (sun directly overhead) to $90┬░$ (sun on the horizon). Its cosine determines the projection of the direct solar beam onto a horizontal surface.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** тАФ `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working principle of a **thermoelectric pyranometer**.

**Model Answer:**

A thermoelectric (thermopile) pyranometer measures global solar radiation on a horizontal surface using the **Seebeck effect**. The functional block diagram and working steps are:

1. **Sensing Element:** A circular blackened thermopile (typically copper-constantan junctions) absorbs incident solar radiation over the 0.3тАУ2.5 ┬╡m spectrum, heating up to a temperature proportional to the incident irradiance.
2. **Reference Junction:** A shielded white-painted body acts as the cold reference.
3. **Transduction:** The temperature difference between hot and cold junctions generates a small EMF (microvolts to millivolts) via the Seebeck effect.
4. **Calibration:** This EMF is calibrated against a WRR (World Radiometric Reference) standard, yielding a sensitivity constant (typically 5тАУ20 ┬╡V per W/m┬▓).
5. **Output:** The amplified signal is recorded by a data logger, providing instantaneous irradiance in W/m┬▓.

The double glass dome minimizes convection losses and shields against long-wave IR exchange with the sky.

---

## 5.2 PART B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** тАФ `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following solar angles and derive the expression for the **zenith angle** in terms of declination $\delta$, latitude $\phi$, and hour angle $\omega$.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** For a location in Kerala at latitude $\phi = 10┬░$ N, on 1st March ($n = 60$), at 11:00 AM local solar time, calculate:
> - (i) Solar declination angle
> - (ii) Hour angle
> - (iii) Zenith angle
> - (iv) Air mass

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) тАФ Definitions and Derivation [7 Marks]

**Definitions:**

1. **Solar Declination Angle ($\delta$):** The angle between the line joining the earth's center to the sun's center and the projection of this line on the equatorial plane. Varies between $-23.45┬░$ and $+23.45┬░$ through the year.
2. **Latitude ($\phi$):** The angular location of a place north or south of the equator, measured from the earth's center. Kerala lies between $\phi = 8┬░$ N and $\phi = 12.8┬░$ N.
3. **Hour Angle ($\omega$):** The angular displacement of the sun from the local meridian, with positive values for afternoons and negative for mornings. $\omega = 15┬░$ per hour from solar noon.

**Derivation of Zenith Angle:**

Consider the celestial triangle formed by the pole (P), zenith (Z), and sun (S). The sides opposite to angles are:
- Side $p$ (opposite P) = co-latitude $(90┬░ - \phi)$
- Side $z$ (opposite Z) = zenith distance $\theta_z$
- Side $s$ (opposite S) = polar distance $(90┬░ - \delta)$

The angle at P (the hour angle) is $\omega$. Applying the **spherical law of cosines**:

$$\cos s = \cos p \cos z + \sin p \sin z \cos\omega$$

Substituting:
$$\cos(90┬░ - \delta) = \cos(90┬░ - \phi)\cos\theta_z + \sin(90┬░ - \phi)\sin\theta_z\cos\omega$$

Using $\cos(90┬░ - x) = \sin x$ and $\sin(90┬░ - x) = \cos x$:

$$\sin\delta = \sin\phi\cos\theta_z + \cos\phi\sin\theta_z\cos\omega$$

Rearranging for $\cos\theta_z$:

$$\boxed{\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega}$$

### Part (b) тАФ Numerical Calculation [7 Marks]

**Given:** $\phi = 10┬░$ N, $n = 60$, $t = 11:00$ AM LST

**(i) Solar Declination:**

$$\delta = 23.45\sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 60)\right] = 23.45\sin\left[\frac{360 \times 344}{365}\right]$$

$$\frac{360 \times 344}{365} = 339.29┬░$$

$$\delta = 23.45\sin(339.29┬░) = 23.45 \times (-0.3144) = -7.37┬░$$

(Negative indicates late winter, sun is in the Southern Hemisphere тАФ physically correct for early March.)

**(ii) Hour Angle:**

$$\omega = 15 \times (11 - 12) = -15┬░$$

**(iii) Zenith Angle:**

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(10┬░)\sin(-7.37┬░) + \cos(10┬░)\cos(-7.37┬░)\cos(-15┬░)$$

$$\cos\theta_z = (0.1736)(-0.1282) + (0.9848)(0.9917)(0.9659)$$

$$\cos\theta_z = -0.0223 + 0.9434 = 0.9211$$

$$\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.9211) = 22.86┬░$$

**(iv) Air Mass:**

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z} = \frac{1}{0.9211} = 1.086 \approx 1.09$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** тАФ Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** What is a **pyrheliometer**? With a labeled schematic, explain its working principle. Compare it briefly with a pyranometer.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A pyranometer mounted horizontally measures a global irradiance of $I_g = 750$ W/m┬▓. A second pyranometer, fitted with a shadow band blocking the direct beam, measures $I_d = 180$ W/m┬▓. The zenith angle is $\theta_z = 40┬░$. Calculate:
> - (i) The direct beam normal irradiance $I_{bn}$
> - (ii) The clearness index $K_T$ if the extraterrestrial horizontal irradiance $I_o = 940$ W/m┬▓
> - (iii) The diffuse fraction $K_d = I_d/I_g$

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) тАФ Pyrheliometer [7 Marks]

A **pyrheliometer** is an instrument used to measure **direct beam (normal) solar irradiance**, i.e., the radiation received directly from the sun's disk within a narrow solid angle (~5┬░ half-angle aperture). It is always mounted on a **solar tracker** to keep its sensing axis pointed at the sun.

**Construction (Eppley NIP - Normal Incidence Pyrheliometer):**
- A collimating tube with precise aperture (aperture angle 5┬░43'30") allows only the solar disk and a small annulus of sky.
- A thermopile at the base, with a blackened receiving surface.
- A view-limiting blackbody to maintain cavity temperature.
- A quartz window to admit broadband radiation and seal the cavity.

**Working:** Solar beam strikes the black thermopile, which absorbs the radiation and converts it to heat. The temperature difference between the hot thermopile junction and a reference body generates a small EMF (Seebeck effect), proportional to the direct normal irradiance. Calibration is done against a **primary standard cavity radiometer** traceable to WRR.

**Comparison with Pyranometer:**

| Feature | Pyrheliometer | Pyranometer |
|---------|---------------|-------------|
| Measures | Direct beam normal $I_{bn}$ | Global (beam + diffuse) $I_g$ |
| Mounting | Solar tracker (sun-following) | Horizontal (or fixed tilt) |
| Field of view | ~5┬░ (only sun) | 2╧А steradian (whole hemisphere) |
| Typical output | 0тАУ1400 W/m┬▓ | 0тАУ1400 W/m┬▓ |
| Cost | High (тВ╣2тАУ5 lakh) | Moderate (тВ╣1тАУ3 lakh) |

### Part (b) тАФ Numerical Calculation [7 Marks]

**Given:** $I_g = 750$ W/m┬▓, $I_d = 180$ W/m┬▓, $\theta_z = 40┬░$, $I_o = 940$ W/m┬▓

**(i) Direct Beam Normal Irradiance:**

$$I_b = I_g - I_d = 750 - 180 = 570 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

(Direct beam on horizontal surface)

$$I_{bn} = \frac{I_b}{\cos\theta_z} = \frac{570}{\cos 40┬░} = \frac{570}{0.7660} = 744.13 \ \text{W/m}^2$$

**(ii) Clearness Index:**

$$K_T = \frac{I_g}{I_o} = \frac{750}{940} = 0.798 \approx 0.80$$

This is a typical value for a partly clear sky.

**(iii) Diffuse Fraction:**

$$K_d = \frac{I_d}{I_g} = \frac{180}{750} = 0.24$$

This indicates a relatively clear atmosphere (low diffuse fraction = high beam fraction).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **ЁЯФ┤ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning тАФ Common Pitfalls (Mark Losers!):**
>
> 1. **Sign errors in declination:** $\delta$ is *negative* in winter (Northern Hemisphere). Writing $\delta = +7.37┬░$ for March 1 instead of $-7.37┬░$ loses **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Confusing hour angle sign convention:** Some texts use morning positive, some afternoon positive. KTU standard is **morning negative, afternoon positive** (consistent with $\omega = 15(t - 12)$).
> 3. **Unit mistakes:** $\omega$ in **degrees**, $\delta$ in **degrees** тАФ DO NOT feed radians to trig functions expecting degrees on your calculator. Verify calculator is in DEG mode.
> 4. **AM only valid for $\theta_z < 90┬░$:** Beyond the horizon, you MUST use the Kasten-Young empirical formula, or the air mass is undefined (sun not visible).
> 5. **Pyrheliometer тЙа Pyranometer:** Students frequently swap these definitions. Pyrheliometer measures the *direct beam only* (needs tracker); pyranometer measures *global* (fixed).
> 6. **Forgetting the cosine projection:** Forgetting to divide $I_b$ by $\cos\theta_z$ to get $I_{bn}$ is the single most common error in this experiment's viva questions.

---

> **ЁЯЪА Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

### ЁЯФС Must-Memorize Core Equations

### ЁЯЫая╕П Instruments to Differentiate
- **Pyranometer (thermopile type)** тЖТ measures $I_g$ on a fixed surface
- **Pyrheliometer (Eppley NIP)** тЖТ measures $I_{bn}$ on a solar tracker

### ЁЯУР Units to Know
- Instantaneous: **W/m┬▓**
- Daily: **kWh/m┬▓/day** or **MJ/m┬▓/day**
- Legacy: **langley** = cal/cm┬▓ = 41840 J/m┬▓
- Peak Sun Hours (PSH): hours of 1000 W/m┬▓ equivalent
