- Comparison/difference between pyranometer and pyrheliometer
- Definition of clearness index $K_T$ and its typical range
- Block diagram of thermopile pyranometer
- Derivation of $\cos\theta_z$ using spherical trigonometry (asked in 14-mark questions)

### тЪб Numerical Traps to Avoid
- $\cos\theta_z$ must be **positive** (sun above horizon); if negative, sun is below horizon
- Latitudes in Kerala: Trivandrum 8.5┬░, Kochi 10.0┬░, Calicut 11.25┬░, Kannur 11.9┬░
- Day number $n$: Jan 1 = 1, Feb 1 = 32, Mar 1 = 60, Apr 1 = 91, May 1 = 121, Jun 1 = 152, Jul 1 = 182, Aug 1 = 213, Sep 1 = 244, Oct 1 = 274, Nov 1 = 305, Dec 1 = 335, Dec 31 = 365
- Always carry **3тАУ4 decimal places** in trig calculations for full marks
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experimental study of dropwise and filmwise condensation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL507 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-1
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experimental study of dropwise and filmwise condensation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Condensation

> [!NOTE]
> **Condensation** is the process by which a vapor changes its phase to a liquid when its temperature is reduced below its saturation temperature corresponding to its pressure, with the **latent heat of vaporization ($h_{fg}$)** being rejected to a cooler surface.

In the context of the KTU Thermal Engineering Lab, condensation is experimentally studied on a **cooled metallic test surface** exposed to a stream of saturated steam. The mode in which the condensate forms and accumulates on the surface fundamentally dictates the overall **Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient (HTC)**, often denoted as $\bar{h}$ or $h_m$.

### 1.2 Two Distinct Modes of Condensation

| Mode | Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) |
|------|----------------------------------------------|
| **Filmwise Condensation** | A continuous, unbroken liquid film of condensate forms over the entire condensing surface. The condensate flows downward (gravity-driven) under the influence of body forces, growing thicker in the direction of flow. |
| **Dropwise Condensation** | The condensate forms as discrete, numerous discrete droplets of varying diameters on the surface. These droplets coalesce, grow, and roll/slide off the surface under gravity, exposing fresh surface area for further condensation. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Cold Drink Bottle Phenomenon**
>
> Imagine taking a chilled glass bottle out of a refrigerator on a humid Kerala monsoon day.
>
> - **Filmwise mode (in default glasses)**: A uniform, transparent sheet of water wraps the entire bottle. You cannot see the glass clearly because the water film completely smears the surface. This is because the glass is **wettable** (high surface energy interaction between water and glass). Heat must conduct through this increasingly thick water layer тАФ the rate falls off as the film thickens.
>
> - **Dropwise mode (on specially coated "anti-fog" glasses)**: Tiny spherical beads of water appear as discrete droplets. The droplets coalesce into larger drops and slide down, leaving **patches of dry glass** behind. Heat transfer is dramatically higher because the surface is mostly exposed to the vapor and only intermittently covered by tiny drops.
>
> In engineering terms, **dropwise condensation can yield a heat transfer coefficient 5 to 10 times greater** than filmwise condensation, but it is unstable and difficult to maintain in industrial practice.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Latent heat of vaporization of water at atmospheric pressure**: $h_{fg} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- **Acceleration due to gravity**: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Saturation temperature of steam at 1 atm**: $T_{sat} = 100^\circ\text{C}$
- **Typical filmwise HTC range**: $\bar{h}_{film} \approx 5{,}000$ to $11{,}000 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$
- **Typical dropwise HTC range**: $\bar{h}_{drop} \approx 40{,}000$ to $100{,}000 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The experiment is a comparative study. Students are expected to compute the **average heat transfer coefficient** for each mode and verify the theoretical Nusselt correlation for filmwise condensation. The dropwise case is qualitative in most lab setups because droplet physics is non-ideal.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Two Modes Exist тАФ The Physics of Wetting

The mode of condensation is governed by the **wettability** of the surface, characterized by the **contact angle ($\theta$)** between the liquid droplet and the solid surface.

- If $\theta < 90^\circ$ тЖТ surface is **wettable** тЖТ **filmwise** condensation occurs.
- If $\theta > 90^\circ$ тЖТ surface is **non-wettable** тЖТ **dropwise** condensation occurs.

The transition contact angle is approximately $90^\circ$. For pure water on clean polished copper or stainless steel, $\theta \approx 70^\circ$ to $85^\circ$, so filmwise condensation is the **natural, default mode** in most engineering heat exchangers. Special **promoters** (e.g., oleic acid, mercaptan-based coatings, or hydrophobic nano-coatings) are required to force dropwise condensation.

### 2.2 Nusselt's Analysis of Film Condensation (1916)

Nusselt (1916) developed the classical analytical solution for laminar film condensation on a vertical plate. The logic behind it is structured as follows:

**Step 1 тАФ Setup of the physical model**:
A vertical plate of height $L$ and width $W$ is exposed to saturated vapor at temperature $T_{sat}$. The plate is maintained at uniform wall temperature $T_w$. A thin liquid film of thickness $\delta(x)$ flows downward under gravity.

**Step 2 тАФ Force balance (momentum equation)**:
Within the film, viscous shear ($\mu \cdot \partial u / \partial y$) is balanced by gravity minus the buoyancy force, plus the pressure gradient (which is hydrostatic and cancels with buoyancy on a vertical plate). This gives the classic parabolic velocity profile of a falling liquid film.

**Step 3 тАФ Energy balance**:
The latent heat released by condensation at the liquidтАУvapor interface must conduct through the liquid film to the wall. The local heat flux is:

$$q''(x) = \frac{k_f (T_{sat} - T_w)}{\delta(x)}$$

**Step 4 тАФ Integration over film thickness**:
Integrating the velocity profile and matching the mass flow rate of condensate to the heat removed gives the film thickness profile:

$$\delta(x) = \left[\frac{4 \mu_f k_f (T_{sat} - T_w) x}{g \rho_f^2 h_{fg}'}\right]^{1/4}$$

where $h_{fg}' = h_{fg} + 0.68 c_{p,f} (T_{sat} - T_w)$ is the **modified latent heat**, accounting for subcooling of the condensate.

**Step 5 тАФ Local and average HTC**:
Substituting back, the local HTC is $h_x = k_f / \delta(x) \propto x^{-1/4}$, and integrating over $x = 0$ to $L$ yields the **average HTC**.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

This analysis underpins the design of:
- **Steam surface condensers** in thermal power plants (NTPC, KSEB stations)
- **Refrigerant condensers** in HVAC&R systems
- **Distillation column condensers** in petrochemical refineries (BPCL Kochi)
- **Waste heat recovery boilers** in process industries

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Perspective**: Every numerical problem on condensation in the KTU exam requires the student to recognize the geometry (vertical plate, vertical tube, or horizontal tube bundle) and apply the corresponding Nusselt correlation correctly.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Scenario | Governing Equation | Units | Key Assumptions |
|---|--------------------|--------------------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Local film thickness on vertical plate | $\delta(x) = \left[\frac{4 \mu_f k_f \Delta T x}{g \rho_f^2 h_{fg}'}\right]^{1/4}$ | m | Laminar, constant properties, $T_w$ uniform |
| 2 | **Average HTC, vertical plate** | $\bar{h}_L = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho_f^2 \, g \, h_{fg}' \, k_f^3}{\mu_f \, L \, \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$ | $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ | Nusselt, 1916 |
| 3 | **Average HTC, single horizontal tube** | $\bar{h}_D = 0.729 \left[\frac{\rho_f^2 \, g \, h_{fg}' \, k_f^3}{\mu_f \, D \, \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$ | $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ | Nusselt, 1914 |
| 4 | Average HTC, vertical tier of $N$ horizontal tubes | $\bar{h}_N = 0.729 \left[\frac{\rho_f^2 \, g \, h_{fg}' \, k_f^3}{\mu_f \, N \, D \, \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$ | $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ | Used in shell-and-coil condensers |
| 5 | Reynolds number of condensate film | $Re_\delta = \frac{4 \dot{m}}{\mu_f W} = \frac{4 \Gamma}{\mu_f}$ | dimensionless | $Re_\delta < 1800$ for laminar |
| 6 | Condensation mass flow rate from experiment | $\dot{m} = \frac{V_c \, \rho_c}{t}$ (volume collected in time $t$) | kg/s | Steady state |
| 7 | Heat transfer rate from condensation | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}'$ | W | At steady state |
| 8 | Heat transfer rate from cooling water | $Q = \dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} \, (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | W | Energy balance check |
| 9 | Average experimental HTC | $\bar{h}_{exp} = \frac{Q}{A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}$ | $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ | $\Delta T_{lm} = T_{sat} - T_{w,mean}$ |
| 10 | Log mean temperature difference (with subcooling) | $\Delta T_{lm} = T_{sat} - \overline{T_w}$ | K | When $T_w$ varies along the surface |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol Convention** тАФ Subscript $f$ refers to **liquid film properties** evaluated at the **film temperature** $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_w)/2$. The properties $\rho_f, \mu_f, k_f, c_{p,f}$ are **NOT** taken at $T_{sat}$ or $T_w$, but at $T_f$. KTU examiners often deduct marks for ignoring this.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Nusselt's Equation for Laminar Film Condensation on a Vertical Plate

**Geometry & Assumptions**:
1. Vertical plate of width $W$ and height $L$, wall temperature $T_w$ uniform.
2. Saturated vapor at temperature $T_{sat}$ floods the region $x > 0$ (downstream of the leading edge).
3. Laminar film flow, no waves, no vapor shear at the interface (zero interfacial velocity gradient).
4. Constant liquid properties $\rho_f, \mu_f, k_f, c_{p,f}$ evaluated at film temperature.
5. Heat transfer across the film is by **conduction only** (negligible convection in the thin film).
6. The kinetic energy and potential energy of the condensate are neglected in the energy equation.
7. The pressure inside the film is uniform (hydrostatic variations negligible).
8. The latent heat released at the interface is conducted to the wall.

**Step 1 тАФ Momentum (NavierтАУStokes) equation in the film**:

For steady, incompressible, laminar flow of a thin film on a vertical wall, the x-momentum equation reduces to a balance between gravity and viscous force:

$$\mu_f \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} = 0$$

This is because inertia, the pressure gradient, and the $\partial p / \partial x$ term all vanish in the thin-film limit (lubrication approximation).

**Step 2 тАФ Velocity profile**:
Integrating twice with respect to $y$, with boundary conditions:
- No-slip at the wall: $u(0) = 0$
- Zero shear at the liquidтАУvapor interface: $\left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right|_{y = \delta} = 0$

First integration:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = C_1 \implies \text{from BC 2: } C_1 = 0$$

Second integration:

$$u(y) = C_2 \implies \text{from BC 1: } C_2 = 0 \text{ ?}$$

> **Correction**: With gravity as the body force, the equation is $\mu_f \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} = -\rho_f g$ (the negative sign arises because gravity acts in the positive x-direction while $\partial p / \partial x = 0$ and inertia is neglected).

$$\mu_f \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} = -\rho_f g$$

Integrating once:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\frac{\rho_f g}{\mu_f} y + C_1$$

From BC 2 ($y = \delta$, $\partial u / \partial y = 0$): $C_1 = \dfrac{\rho_f g \, \delta}{\mu_f}$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\rho_f g}{\mu_f} (\delta - y)$$

Integrating again:

$$u(y) = \frac{\rho_f g}{\mu_f} \left(\delta y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right) + C_2$$

From BC 1 ($y = 0$, $u = 0$): $C_2 = 0$.

**Final velocity profile**:

$$u(y) = \frac{\rho_f g}{\mu_f} \left(\delta y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right)$$

This is a **parabolic** velocity profile with maximum velocity at $y = \delta$:

$$u_{max} = \frac{\rho_f g \delta^2}{2 \mu_f}$$

**Step 3 тАФ Mass flow rate of condensate at position x**:

The local mass flow rate per unit width, $\Gamma(x)$, is obtained by integrating the velocity profile across the film:

$$\Gamma(x) = \int_0^{\delta} \rho_f u(y) \, dy = \rho_f \int_0^{\delta} \frac{\rho_f g}{\mu_f} \left(\delta y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right) dy$$

$$\Gamma(x) = \frac{\rho_f^2 g}{\mu_f} \int_0^{\delta} \left(\delta y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right) dy$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$\int_0^{\delta} \left(\delta y - \frac{y^2}{2}\right) dy = \left[\frac{\delta y^2}{2} - \frac{y^3}{6}\right]_0^\delta = \frac{\delta^3}{2} - \frac{\delta^3}{6} = \frac{\delta^3}{3}$$

Therefore:

$$\Gamma(x) = \frac{\rho_f^2 g \, \delta^3}{3 \mu_f}$$

**Step 4 тАФ Energy balance at position x**:

The heat conducted through the film at $x$ must equal the latent heat released by condensation over the infinitesimal area $W \, dx$ upstream of $x$:

$$q''(x) \cdot W \, dx = h_{fg}' \cdot d\Gamma \cdot W$$

Here $d\Gamma$ is the increment in $\Gamma$ between $x$ and $x + dx$:

$$d\Gamma = \frac{d\Gamma}{dx} dx$$

So:

$$q''(x) = h_{fg}' \frac{d\Gamma}{dx}$$

By Fourier's law through the film:

$$q''(x) = \frac{k_f (T_{sat} - T_w)}{\delta(x)}$$

Equating both expressions:

$$\frac{k_f (T_{sat} - T_w)}{\delta(x)} = h_{fg}' \frac{d\Gamma}{dx}$$

**Step 5 тАФ Differentiate $\Gamma(x)$**:

From $\Gamma(x) = \dfrac{\rho_f^2 g \delta^3}{3 \mu_f}$:

$$\frac{d\Gamma}{dx} = \frac{\rho_f^2 g}{3 \mu_f} \cdot 3 \delta^2 \frac{d\delta}{dx} = \frac{\rho_f^2 g \delta^2}{\mu_f} \frac{d\delta}{dx}$$

**Step 6 тАФ Substitute and separate variables**:

$$\frac{k_f (T_{sat} - T_w)}{\delta} = h_{fg}' \cdot \frac{\rho_f^2 g \delta^2}{\mu_f} \frac{d\delta}{dx}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{k_f \mu_f (T_{sat} - T_w)}{h_{fg}' \rho_f^2 g} \cdot \frac{dx}{\delta^3} = \delta \, d\delta$$

Or equivalently:

$$\delta^3 \, d\delta = \frac{k_f \mu_f (T_{sat} - T_w)}{h_{fg}' \rho_f^2 g} dx$$

**Step 7 тАФ Integrate from $x = 0$ (where $\delta = 0$) to $x$**:

$$\int_0^\delta \delta^3 \, d\delta = \frac{k_f \mu_f (T_{sat} - T_w)}{h_{fg}' \rho_f^2 g} \int_0^x dx$$

$$\frac{\delta^4}{4} = \frac{k_f \mu_f (T_{sat} - T_w) x}{h_{fg}' \rho_f^2 g}$$

Therefore, the **local film thickness**:

$$\delta(x) = \left[\frac{4 k_f \mu_f (T_{sat} - T_w) x}{h_{fg}' \rho_f^2 g}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 8 тАФ Local heat transfer coefficient**:

By definition $h_x = q'' / (T_{sat} - T_w)$:

$$h_x = \frac{k_f}{\delta(x)} = \left[\frac{h_{fg}' \rho_f^2 g k_f^3}{4 \mu_f (T_{sat} - T_w) x}\right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 9 тАФ Average heat transfer coefficient over the entire plate**:

The average HTC is found by integrating $h_x$ over the plate from $x = 0$ to $L$ and dividing by $L$:

$$\bar{h}_L = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L h_x \, dx$$

Substituting $h_x$:

$$\bar{h}_L = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L \left[\frac{h_{fg}' \rho_f^2 g k_f^3}{4 \mu_f (T_{sat} - T_w)}\right]^{1/4} x^{-1/4} dx$$

Pulling out the constants:

$$\bar{h}_L = \frac{1}{L} \left[\frac{h_{fg}' \rho_f^2 g k_f^3}{4 \mu_f (T_{sat} - T_w)}\right]^{1/4} \int_0^L x^{-1/4} dx$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$\int_0^L x^{-1/4} dx = \left[\frac{x^{3/4}}{3/4}\right]_0^L = \frac{4}{3} L^{3/4}$$

Therefore:

$$\bar{h}_L = \frac{1}{L} \left[\frac{h_{fg}' \rho_f^2 g k_f^3}{4 \mu_f (T_{sat} - T_w)}\right]^{1/4} \cdot \frac{4}{3} L^{3/4}$$

$$\bar{h}_L = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{L^{1/4}} \left[\frac{h_{fg}' \rho_f^2 g k_f^3}{4 \mu_f (T_{sat} - T_w)}\right]^{1/4}$$

$$\bar{h}_L = \left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{1/4} \left[\frac{h_{fg}' \rho_f^2 g k_f^3}{\mu_f (T_{sat} - T_w) L}\right]^{1/4}$$

Numerically, $\left(\dfrac{4}{3}\right) \left(\dfrac{1}{4}\right)^{1/4} = 0.9428 \approx 0.943$.

**Final Nusselt Equation (Vertical Plate)**:

$$\boxed{\;\bar{h}_L = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho_f^2 \, g \, h_{fg}' \, k_f^3}{\mu_f \, L \, (T_{sat} - T_w)}\right]^{1/4}\;}$$

This is the cornerstone correlation for KTU 2024 exam numerical problems.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix тАФ Sample Experimental Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (Typical KTU 2024 Lab Viva Problem)**
>
> In an experiment to study film condensation of saturated steam at atmospheric pressure on a vertical copper plate of height $L = 0.30 \text{ m}$ and width $W = 0.10 \text{ m}$, the following data were recorded at steady state:
>
> - Steam temperature: $T_{sat} = 100^\circ\text{C}$
> - Average wall temperature: $T_w = 80^\circ\text{C}$
> - Condensate collected in $t = 60 \text{ s}$: $V_c = 9.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3$
> - Density of condensate (at $T_f$): $\rho_f = 971 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Latent heat: $h_{fg}' = 2306 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (modified for subcooling)
> - Other film properties at $T_f = 90^\circ\text{C}$: $k_f = 0.675 \text{ W/mK}$, $\mu_f = 3.15 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/ms}$

**Step A тАФ Mass flow rate of condensate**:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{V_c \rho_f}{t} = \frac{9.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 971}{60} = 1.537 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step B тАФ Heat transfer rate**:

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}' = 1.537 \times 10^{-3} \times 2306 \times 10^3 = 3544 \text{ W}$$

**Step C тАФ Heat transfer area**:

$$A = L \times W = 0.30 \times 0.10 = 0.030 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step D тАФ Experimental average HTC**:

$$\bar{h}_{exp} = \frac{Q}{A (T_{sat} - T_w)} = \frac{3544}{0.030 \times 20} = 5907 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step E тАФ Theoretical Nusselt HTC**:

$$\bar{h}_{th} = 0.943 \left[\frac{971^2 \times 9.81 \times 2306 \times 10^3 \times 0.675^3}{3.15 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.30 \times 20}\right]^{1/4}$$

Computing inside the brackets step by step:

- $\rho_f^2 = 971^2 = 9.428 \times 10^5 \text{ kg}^2/\text{m}^6$
- $\rho_f^2 g = 9.428 \times 10^5 \times 9.81 = 9.249 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^5$
- $\rho_f^2 g h_{fg}' = 9.249 \times 10^6 \times 2.306 \times 10^6 = 2.133 \times 10^{13}$
- $k_f^3 = 0.675^3 = 0.3075 \text{ W}^3/\text{m}^3\text{K}^3$
- $\rho_f^2 g h_{fg}' k_f^3 = 2.133 \times 10^{13} \times 0.3075 = 6.560 \times 10^{12}$
- $\mu_f L \Delta T = 3.15 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.30 \times 20 = 1.890 \times 10^{-3}$

$$\frac{6.560 \times 10^{12}}{1.890 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.471 \times 10^{15}$$

$$(3.471 \times 10^{15})^{1/4} = (3.471)^{1/4} \times 10^{15/4} = 1.3647 \times 10^{3.75} = 1.3647 \times 5623 = 7674$$

$$\bar{h}_{th} = 0.943 \times 7674 = 7237 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step F тАФ Percentage deviation**:

$$\%\text{ Deviation} = \frac{\bar{h}_{exp} - \bar{h}_{th}}{\bar{h}_{th}} \times 100 = \frac{5907 - 7237}{7237} \times 100 = -18.4\%$$

**Step G тАФ Interpretation**:
The experimental HTC is lower than theoretical due to ripples on the condensate film (which increase thermal resistance), non-uniform wall temperature, and heat losses to surroundings. A deviation within $\pm 20\%$ is acceptable per KTU lab rubric.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Experimental Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SG[Steam Generation Subsystem]
        A1[Electric Heated Boiler]
        A2[Water Level Indicator]
        A3[Safety Pressure Relief Valve]
    end

    subgraph TS[Test Section Subsystem]
        B1[Steam Inlet Manifold]
        B2[Vertical Test Plate or Tube]
        B3[Condensate Collection Trough]
        B4[Cooling Jacket Backside]
    end

    subgraph MS[Measurement Subsystem]
        C1[Thermocouples T1 T2 T3 T4]
        C2[Pressure Gauge at Inlet]
        C3[Rotameter for Cooling Water]
        C4[Measuring Cylinder and Stopwatch]
    end

    subgraph CS[Cooling Subsystem]
        D1[Cold Water Reservoir]
        D2[Centrifugal Pump]
        D3[Flow Control Valve]
    end

    subgraph DS[Data Acquisition]
        E1[Digital Temperature Indicator]
        E2[Manometer and Flow Meters]
    end

    A1 -->|Saturated Steam 100C| B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 -->|Latent Heat Released| B4
    B2 -->|Condensate Falls| B3
    B3 --> C4
    B4 -->|Cooling Water In| D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 -->|Controlled Flow| B4
    B4 -->|Cooling Water Out| D1

    C1 --> E1
    C2 --> E2
    C3 --> E2
    C4 --> E2

    A2 -.Monitors.-> A1
    A3 -.Safety.-> A1
```

### 4.2 Process Flow тАФ Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: Switch on Boiler and Pump] --> P2[Allow Steam to Purge Air from Test Section for 3 Minutes]
    P2 --> P3[Adjust Cooling Water Flow to Maintain Steady Wall Temperature]
    P3 --> P4{Is Steady State Reached?}
    P4 -->|No| P3
    P4 -->|Yes| P5[Record All Temperatures T1 to T4, Pressure, Flow Rate]
    P5 --> P6[Start Stopwatch and Collect Condensate in Measuring Cylinder for 60 Seconds]
    P6 --> P7[Stop Stopwatch and Record Condensate Volume]
    P7 --> P8[Repeat for 3 Different Cooling Water Flow Rates]
    P8 --> P9[Repeat Entire Setup with Dropwise Promoter Coating on Test Plate]
    P9 --> P10[Tabulate Data and Compute HTC for Both Modes]
    P10 --> P11[Compare with Nusselt Theoretical Correlation]
    P11 --> P12[Plot h_bar versus Delta T and Conclude]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Condensate Film Formation on Vertical Plate

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph vertical[Vertical Test Plate Section]
        direction TB
        VAP[Saturated Steam Region x greater than 0]
        FILM[Condensate Film thickness delta x increases with x]
        WALL[Cooled Wall at T_w]
        SUB[Subcooled Condensate]
    end

    VAP -->|Latent Heat Released| FILM
    FILM -->|Conduction through Film| WALL
    FILM -->|Gravity Driven Downflow| SUB
    WALL -->|Cooling Water Path| CW[Cooling Water Channels]

    note1[Note Film Thickness delta x grows with x] -.-> FILM
    note2[Note h_x decreases with x local HTC drops downstream] -.-> FILM
```

### 4.4 Decision Logic for Mode Selection in the Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the Test Plate Surface Clean Polished Metallic?}
    Q1 -->|Yes Default| M1[Filmwise Condensation Expected]
    Q1 -->|No Coated with Oleic Acid or Hydrophobic Layer| M2[Dropwise Condensation Forced]
    M1 --> R1[Expected HTC 5000 to 11000 W per m2K]
    M2 --> R2[Expected HTC 40000 to 100000 W per m2K]
    R1 --> C1[Compare with Nusselt Correlation 0.943 bracket term]
    R2 --> C2[Dropwise is Empirical No Closed Form Correlation]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2024 Model Question]**
>
> **Q1. Define dropwise and filmwise condensation. List three key differences between them.** `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer**:

**Filmwise condensation** is a mode in which the condensate forms a continuous liquid film that completely wets the condensing surface and flows downward under gravity. **Dropwise condensation** is a mode in which the condensate forms as discrete droplets that coalesce and roll off, leaving portions of the surface exposed.

| Parameter | Filmwise | Dropwise |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| Heat transfer coefficient | Low (5,000тАУ11,000 W/m┬▓K) | Very high (40,000тАУ100,000 W/m┬▓K) |
| Surface condition | Wettable ($\theta < 90^\circ$) | Non-wettable ($\theta > 90^\circ$) |
| Predictability | Well-predicted by Nusselt's theory | Empirical; no closed-form theory |
| Practical occurrence | Default in industry | Requires special coatings |

---

> **[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023 Model Question]**
>
> **Q2. State the assumptions made by Nusselt in deriving the equation for film condensation on a vertical plate.** `[3 marks]`

**Model Answer**:
1. Laminar flow of the condensate film with a smooth, wavy-free interface.
2. Constant liquid film properties evaluated at the mean film temperature.
3. No vapor shear at the liquidтАУvapor interface.
4. The pressure variation across the film is hydrostatic (negligible).
5. Heat transfer across the film is by pure conduction (no convection in the thin film).
6. Inertia terms in the momentum equation are negligible (lubrication approximation).
7. Subcooling of the condensate is accounted for through the modified latent heat $h_{fg}' = h_{fg} + 0.68 c_{p,f}(T_{sat} - T_w)$.

---

### 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam тАУ Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the Nusselt equation for the average heat transfer coefficient for laminar film condensation of a saturated vapor on a vertical plate. State all assumptions clearly. `[7 marks]`

**(b)** A vertical plate of height 0.5 m and width 0.15 m is used to condense saturated steam at atmospheric pressure. The wall temperature is maintained at 70┬░C. Using Nusselt's correlation, compute:
   1. The average heat transfer coefficient.
   2. The total heat transfer rate.
   3. The mass of condensate collected in one hour. `[7 marks]`

**Given Data (At film temperature $T_f = 85^\circ$C)**:
- $\rho_f = 968 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $k_f = 0.668 \text{ W/mK}$
- $\mu_f = 3.35 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/ms}$
- $h_{fg}' = 2317 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $T_{sat} = 100^\circ\text{C}, \quad T_w = 70^\circ\text{C}$

**Model Solution to Part (b)**:

**Step 1 тАФ Average heat transfer coefficient**:

$$\bar{h}_L = 0.943 \left[\frac{\rho_f^2 \, g \, h_{fg}' \, k_f^3}{\mu_f \, L \, (T_{sat} - T_w)}\right]^{1/4}$$

$$\bar{h}_L = 0.943 \left[\frac{(968)^2 \times 9.81 \times (2317 \times 10^3) \times (0.668)^3}{(3.35 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.5 \times 30}\right]^{1/4}$$

Computing the numerator:
- $\rho_f^2 = 9.37 \times 10^5$
- $\rho_f^2 g = 9.20 \times 10^6$
- $\rho_f^2 g h_{fg}' = 9.20 \times 10^6 \times 2.317 \times 10^6 = 2.132 \times 10^{13}$
- $k_f^3 = 0.298$
- Product: $2.132 \times 10^{13} \times 0.298 = 6.353 \times 10^{12}$

Computing the denominator:
- $\mu_f L \Delta T = 3.35 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.5 \times 30 = 5.025 \times 10^{-3}$

Ratio: $\dfrac{6.353 \times 10^{12}}{5.025 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.264 \times 10^{15}$

Fourth root: $(1.264 \times 10^{15})^{1/4} = 1.058 \times 10^{3.75} = 1.058 \times 5623 = 5950$

$$\bar{h}_L = 0.943 \times 5950 = \mathbf{5610 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}}$$

**Step 2 тАФ Heat transfer area and total rate**:

$$A = L \times W = 0.5 \times 0.15 = 0.075 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q = \bar{h}_L A (T_{sat} - T_w) = 5610 \times 0.075 \times 30 = \mathbf{12{,}622 \text{ W}}$$

**Step 3 тАФ Mass of condensate in one hour**:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{Q}{h_{fg}'} = \frac{12{,}622}{2317 \times 10^3} = 5.448 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$m_{1 hr} = 5.448 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = \mathbf{19.61 \text{ kg}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) тАФ Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the experimental setup used to study dropwise and filmwise condensation in the KTU Thermal Engineering Lab. List the instruments used and their purpose. `[7 marks]`

**Model Answer Outline**:

The setup consists of five subsystems:

1. **Steam generator (boiler)** тАФ provides dry saturated steam at a controlled pressure. Fitted with a water level gauge, pressure gauge, and safety relief valve.

2. **Test section** тАФ a vertical copper plate (or a vertical tube) mounted inside an insulated steam chamber. The back of the plate is water-cooled to remove the latent heat of condensation. The front face is exposed to steam.

3. **Cooling water circuit** тАФ a centrifugal pump draws water from a reservoir, sends it through a rotameter (for flow control) and the cooling jacket of the test plate.

4. **Condensate collection** тАФ the condensate dripping from the plate is collected in a graduated measuring cylinder over a fixed time interval (e.g., 60 seconds) measured with a stopwatch.

5. **Instrumentation** тАФ calibrated thermocouples (T1 at steam inlet, T2, T3 along the plate, T4 at cooling water outlet), digital temperature indicator, U-tube manometer for condensate level, and a pressure gauge for steam pressure.

**Procedure (summary)**:
- Purge air, allow steady state, record all temperatures, measure condensate volume, repeat for at least three cooling water flow rates.
- For dropwise condensation, repeat with the plate coated with **oleic acid** (a hydrophobic promoter).

**Step-by-step Calculations Required**:
- $\dot{m} = V_c \rho_f / t$
- $Q = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}'$
- $\bar{h}_{exp} = Q / [A (T_{sat} - T_w)]$

**(b)** Compare dropwise and filmwise condensation in detail. Why is filmwise condensation the more common mode in industrial condensers despite its lower heat transfer coefficient? `[7 marks]`

**Model Answer**:

**Comparison** (already tabulated in Part A Q1 above) тАФ expand on the following points:
- **Mechanism**: continuous film vs. discrete droplets
- **Theoretical predictability**: Nusselt's analytical model exists for filmwise; dropwise relies on empirical correlations (e.g., Tanasawa, 1991)
- **Heat transfer performance**: dropwise is 5тАУ10├Ч higher
- **Stability and durability**: dropwise coatings degrade with time, fouling, and repeated thermal cycling
- **Industrial prevalence**: filmwise is the natural mode on clean metal surfaces; promoters are expensive and short-lived
- **Operating conditions**: in the presence of non-condensable gases (e.g., air), filmwise is forced; in vacuum condensers, both modes can coexist

**Why filmwise dominates industry**:
1. Most engineering surfaces (stainless steel, copper, titanium) are intrinsically wettable by water and refrigerants.
2. Sustaining dropwise condensation requires continuous replenishment of hydrophobic coatings, which is impractical in large power plant condensers.
3. Non-condensable gases (always present even in well-deaerated systems) and fouling quickly force the system toward filmwise.
4. Predictable Nusselt correlation enables confident design; dropwise has no closed-form theory.
5. Filmwise condensers are mechanically simpler, easier to clean, and have longer service life.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Properties at the wrong temperature** тАФ always evaluate $\rho_f, \mu_f, k_f, c_{p,f}$ at the **film temperature** $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_w)/2$, not at $T_{sat}$ or $T_w$. Marks deducted if the property table used is for the wrong temperature.
> 2. **Forgetting $h_{fg}'$** тАФ the modified latent heat $h_{fg}' = h_{fg} + 0.68 c_{p,f} (T_{sat} - T_w)$ must be used, not $h_{fg}$ alone. Marks lost in numerical problems.
> 3. **Geometry confusion** тАФ vertical plate uses $0.943$, horizontal single tube uses $0.729$, vertical tier of $N$ tubes uses $0.729$ in the numerator and $N$ in the denominator. Mixing these constants loses 2тАУ3 marks.
> 4. **Sign convention** тАФ heat flux is from vapor to wall; HTC is positive by definition. Do not introduce a negative sign.
> 5. **Units inconsistency** тАФ if $h_{fg}'$ is in kJ/kg, multiply by 1000 to get J/kg before computing. Many students lose marks by mixing kJ and J.
> 6. **Nusselt's derivation order** тАФ examiners expect a clean logical sequence: assumptions тЖТ momentum balance тЖТ velocity profile тЖТ mass flow rate тЖТ energy balance тЖТ film thickness тЖТ HTC тЖТ average. Jumping straight to the formula earns partial credit only.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Condensation** is the phase change from vapor to liquid with the rejection of latent heat $h_{fg}$.
- [ ] **Filmwise condensation** is the default industrial mode; surface is **wettable** ($\theta < 90^\circ$).
- [ ] **Dropwise condensation** requires a non-wettable surface ($\theta > 90^\circ$); produces HTC 5тАУ10├Ч higher.
- [ ] **Nusselt's vertical plate equation**: $\bar{h}_L = 0.943 \left[\dfrac{\rho_f^2 g h_{fg}' k_f^3}{\mu_f L \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$.
- [ ] **Nusselt's horizontal tube equation**: $\bar{h}_D = 0.729 \left[\dfrac{\rho_f^2 g h_{fg}' k_f^3}{\mu_f D \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$.
- [ ] **Modified latent heat**: $h_{fg}' = h_{fg} + 0.68 c_{p,f}(T_{sat} - T_w)$.
- [ ] **Properties** $\rho_f, \mu_f, k_f, c_{p,f}$ are evaluated at the **film temperature** $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_w)/2$.
- [ ] **Laminar regime condition**: condensate Reynolds number $Re_\delta = 4\dot{m}/(\mu_f W) < 1800$.
- [ ] **Experimental HTC** computed from energy balance: $\bar{h}_{exp} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}' / [A (T_{sat} - T_w)]$.
- [ ] **Acceptable deviation** between experimental and Nusselt's theoretical HTC: typically within $\pm 20\%$.
- [ ] **Dropwise promoters** in the lab: oleic acid, benzaldehyde, mercaptans, hydrophobic nano-coatings.
- [ ] **Typical HTC ranges**: filmwise 5,000тАУ11,000 W/m┬▓K; dropwise 40,000тАУ100,000 W/m┬▓K.
- [ ] **Non-condensable gases** (air, COтВВ) drastically reduce condensation HTC by blanketing the surface тАФ known as the **"air-bound" effect**, a frequent viva question.
- [ ] **Effect of vapor velocity**: at low velocities, Nusselt's theory is valid; at high vapor shear, the condensate film is thinned and HTC increases (governed by $Re_v$ and $Re_\delta$ correlations).
- [ ] **In the KTU lab record**, the report must include: schematic, observation table, sample calculation, graph of $\bar{h}$ vs. $\Delta T$ on log-log axes (slope should be $-1/4$), and percentage deviation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiments on boiling heat transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL507 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-1
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiments on boiling heat transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Boiling Heat Transfer** is a mode of convective heat transfer in which a liquid undergoes a phase change to vapour due to the addition of heat at a heated surface, with the heat transfer coefficient being strongly dependent on the temperature difference between the surface and the fluid's saturation temperature.

In the context of **PCMEL507 тАУ Thermal Engineering Lab-1**, *Experiment 2* deals with the experimental investigation of saturated **pool boiling** of a liquid (commonly water or a refrigerant) over a horizontal cylindrical/platinum wire heater submerged in a stagnant pool of liquid. The experiment is used to plot the **Boiling Curve** (Nukiyama Curve), identify regimes such as *natural convection, nucleate boiling, transition boiling, and film boiling*, and compute the **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** as well as the **Boiling Heat Transfer Coefficient (h)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Terminology (Use exact phrasing in answers):**
> - **Pool Boiling**: Boiling where the bulk liquid motion is induced solely by natural convection and bubble-induced agitation.
> - **Saturated Boiling**: Boiling at a pressure equal to the saturation pressure corresponding to the liquid temperature.
> - **Nukiyama Curve**: The characteristic plot of heat flux $q''$ versus surface superheat $\Delta T_{sat}$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are heating water in a kettle on a stove. Initially, the water at the bottom warms up gently тАФ this is the **natural convection regime**, where warm water simply rises and cooler water sinks. As the bottom surface becomes hot enough, tiny vapour bubbles start to form at microscopic pits on the kettle bottom тАФ this is the onset of **nucleate boiling**. The bubbles detach and rise, carrying away a tremendous amount of heat (this is why boiling water heats food efficiently).

If you crank the stove to maximum, the heat flux reaches a peak тАФ the **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** тАФ beyond which the surface cannot generate bubbles fast enough. Instead, a stable **vapour film** blankets the heater (like a thin insulating blanket of steam). Heat transfer actually *drops* sharply even though the surface temperature skyrockets тАФ this dangerous condition is called **burnout** in nuclear reactors and **Departure from Nucleate Boiling (DNB)** in boilers.

The complete journey: Convection тЖТ Nucleate Boiling тЖТ Critical Point тЖТ Transition Boiling тЖТ Film Boiling тЖТ is what we experimentally plot as the Nukiyama curve.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorize:**
> - Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100┬░C: $h_{fg} \approx \mathbf{2257 \; kJ/kg}$
> - Saturation temperature of water at 1 atm: $T_{sat} = \mathbf{100 \; ┬░C}$
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$
> - Atmospheric pressure: $P_{atm} = \mathbf{101.325 \; kPa}$

### 1.3 Objectives of the Lab Experiment

1. To experimentally determine the **boiling heat transfer coefficient** as a function of surface superheat.
2. To plot the **boiling curve (Nukiyama curve)** for the test liquid.
3. To identify the **critical heat flux** at which burnout occurs.
4. To study the effect of surface roughness and orientation on boiling performance.
5. To compare experimental results with standard correlations (Rohsenow, Zuber, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Regimes of Pool Boiling (Nukiyama Curve)

The boiling phenomenon is classified into distinct regimes based on the magnitude of surface superheat $\Delta T_{sat} = T_s - T_{sat}$:

1. **Natural Convection Regime** ($\Delta T_{sat} < 4┬░C$):
   - Liquid is subcooled or slightly superheated.
   - Heat transfer occurs primarily through natural convection currents.
   - No bubbles are formed.
   - Heat flux follows the relation: $q'' \propto (\Delta T_{sat})^{1.25}$ approximately.

2. **Nucleate Boiling Regime** ($4┬░C < \Delta T_{sat} < 30┬░C$):
   - Bubbles nucleate at active cavities on the heated surface.
   - Two sub-regimes: **Isolated bubble regime** and **Slug/Vapor column regime**.
   - Heat transfer coefficient rises sharply.
   - Most efficient regime тАФ used in industrial boilers and heat exchangers.

3. **Critical Heat Flux (CHF) Point** ($\Delta T_{sat} \approx 30┬░C$ for water at 1 atm):
   - Maximum attainable heat flux $q''_{max}$ before transition.
   - Beyond this, surface gets blanketed by vapour.

4. **Transition Boiling Regime (Unstable Film Boiling)**:
   - Alternating patches of liquid contact and vapour film.
   - Heat flux *decreases* as $\Delta T_{sat}$ increases.
   - Practically avoided in design.

5. **Stable Film Boiling Regime** ($\Delta T_{sat} > 120┬░C$):
   - Continuous stable vapour film covers the surface.
   - Heat transfer by radiation and conduction through vapour.
   - $\Delta T_{sat}$ increases rapidly for small heat flux changes.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind the Boiling Curve

- **Why** does the heat flux decrease in film boiling? Because the vapour film has a very low thermal conductivity compared to liquid, acting as an insulator.
- **How** do bubbles enhance heat transfer? Each bubble, as it grows and detaches, agitates the thermal boundary layer, dramatically increasing the convective heat transfer coefficient.
- **Why** is CHF the design limit for nuclear reactors? Because operating beyond CHF leads to fuel rod cladding failure due to catastrophic surface temperature rise.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All symbols use SI units unless stated otherwise. Use $\vert$ for absolute value expressions inside tables to avoid markdown conflicts.

| **#** | **Formula / Parameter** | **Equation** | **Application / Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surface Superheat | $\Delta T_{sat} = T_s - T_{sat}$ | Driving potential for boiling |
| 2 | Heat Flux (Fourier's Law) | $q'' = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{A}$ | Instantaneous heat supplied per unit area |
| 3 | Boiling Heat Transfer Coefficient | $h_b = \dfrac{q''}{\Delta T_{sat}}$ | Performance metric of the experiment |
| 4 | Rohsenow Correlation (Nucleate Boiling) | $q'' = \mu_l h_{fg} \left[\dfrac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2} \left[\dfrac{c_{pl}\,\Delta T_{sat}}{C_{sf}\,h_{fg}\,Pr_l^{\,n}}\right]^3$ | Predicts nucleate boiling heat flux |
| 5 | Zuber Correlation (CHF) | $q''_{CHF} = 0.131\, h_{fg}\,\rho_v^{1/2}\,\left[\sigma\, g\, (\rho_l - \rho_v)\right]^{1/4}$ | Predicts critical heat flux on horizontal surface |
| 6 | Leidenfrost Temperature | $T_{Leid}$ | Minimum surface temp for stable film boiling |
| 7 | Effective Heat Flux (with losses) | $q'' = \dfrac{VI - Q_{loss}}{A}$ | Used when heater has finite losses to surroundings |
| 8 | Bubble Departure Diameter (Fritz Equation) | $D_d = 0.0208\, \theta \left[\dfrac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}\right]^{1/2}$ | Diameter of bubble leaving the surface |

Where:
- $T_s$ = Surface temperature, $T_{sat}$ = Saturation temperature
- $V$, $I$ = Voltage and current across the heater
- $\rho_l$, $\rho_v$ = Liquid and vapour densities
- $\sigma$ = Surface tension
- $\mu_l$ = Dynamic viscosity of liquid
- $c_{pl}$ = Specific heat of liquid at constant pressure
- $Pr_l$ = Prandtl number of liquid
- $C_{sf}$, $n$ = Surface-fluid constants (e.g., water-copper: $C_{sf}=0.0130$, $n=1.0$; water-platinum: $C_{sf}=0.0132$, $n=1.0$)
- $\theta$ = Contact angle in degrees

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nuclear Reactors**: Reactor core design uses nucleate boiling to transfer heat from fuel rods to coolant. Operating limit is always below CHF.
- **Steam Boilers**: Industrial fire-tube and water-tube boilers operate in nucleate boiling regime for maximum efficiency.
- **Electronic Cooling**: Two-phase immersion cooling of high-power GPUs/CPU chips uses boiling of dielectric fluids.
- **Refrigeration & Air-Conditioning**: Evaporators in refrigerators rely on nucleate boiling of refrigerants on tube surfaces.
- **Cryogenics**: LNG storage uses film boiling to minimize heat ingress.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Boiling Heat Transfer Coefficient from Experimental Data

**Given**: A horizontal cylindrical test heater of length $L$ and diameter $D$ immersed in a saturated liquid pool. The heater dissipates electrical power $P = VI$ when supplied with voltage $V$ and current $I$. Multiple thermocouples measure the heater surface temperature $T_s$ at various power levels, and the bulk liquid temperature $T_{sat}$ is recorded.

**Step 1 тАФ Express the heat supplied to the fluid (assuming negligible losses):**

$$
q'' = \frac{Q}{A_s} = \frac{V \cdot I}{\pi D L}
$$

**Explanation:** The electrical energy supplied to the heater is entirely used to vaporize the liquid at the surface (in ideal conditions). The cylindrical heater surface area is $A_s = \pi D L$.

**Step 2 тАФ Calculate the surface superheat (driving temperature difference):**

$$
\Delta T_{sat} = T_s - T_{sat}
$$

**Explanation:** $\Delta T_{sat}$ is the excess temperature of the surface above the saturation temperature, which provides the thermodynamic driving force for bubble nucleation.

**Step 3 тАФ Compute the boiling heat transfer coefficient using Newton's Law of Cooling:**

$$
h_b = \frac{q''}{\Delta T_{sat}} = \frac{V \cdot I}{\pi D L\,(T_s - T_{sat})}
$$

**Explanation:** This is the definition of convective heat transfer coefficient, applied to the boiling scenario.

### 3.2 Derivation: Critical Heat Flux (Zuber's Pool Boiling Correlation)

Zuber (1959) performed a hydrodynamic stability analysis on a vapour-liquid interface under a heated surface and derived the maximum sustainable heat flux.

**Step 1 тАФ Start from the Rayleigh-Taylor instability criterion:**

$$
\lambda_c = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}}
$$

**Explanation:** The critical wavelength $\lambda_c$ of instability is derived by equating destabilizing buoyancy to stabilizing surface tension.

**Step 2 тАФ The pool boiling velocity scale (Taylor wave velocity):**

$$
u_c = \sqrt{\frac{2\pi \sigma g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}}
$$

**Step 3 тАФ Substitute into the energy balance at the interface:**

The maximum heat flux is the energy carried by the vapour jets generated by the instability:

$$
q''_{CHF} = \rho_v \, h_{fg} \, u_c
$$

**Step 4 тАФ Combine and simplify using the critical wavelength expression:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q''_{CHF} &= \rho_v \, h_{fg} \, \sqrt{\frac{2\pi \sigma g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}} \\
&= h_{fg} \, \rho_v^{1/2} \, \sqrt{2\pi \sigma g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 тАФ Apply the empirical constant (Zuber's correction factor 0.131):**

$$
\boxed{q''_{CHF} = 0.131 \; h_{fg} \, \rho_v^{1/2} \, \left[\sigma \, g \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)\right]^{1/4}}
$$

**Explanation of the constant 0.131:** The hydrodynamic analysis gives an upper-bound estimate, but actual experimental observations yield slightly lower values; hence the empirical reduction factor.

### 3.3 Numerical Analytical Matrix тАФ Worked Example

**Problem Statement:** In a pool boiling experiment, a copper test heater of diameter $D = 12 \, mm$ and length $L = 80 \, mm$ is immersed in saturated water at atmospheric pressure ($T_{sat} = 100┬░C$). At a particular operating point, the voltmeter reads $V = 15 \, V$ and ammeter reads $I = 4.0 \, A$. The surface temperature recorded is $T_s = 118┬░C$. Calculate:
(a) The surface superheat
(b) The boiling heat flux
(c) The boiling heat transfer coefficient

**Given Data:**
- $D = 12 \times 10^{-3} \, m$
- $L = 80 \times 10^{-3} \, m$
- $V = 15 \, V$
- $I = 4.0 \, A$
- $T_{sat} = 100┬░C$
- $T_s = 118┬░C$

**Solution:**

**(a) Surface Superheat:**

$$
\Delta T_{sat} = T_s - T_{sat} = 118 - 100 = 18 \, ┬░C
$$

**(b) Boiling Heat Flux:**

Surface area of heater:

$$
A_s = \pi D L = \pi \times (12 \times 10^{-3}) \times (80 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
A_s = \pi \times 9.6 \times 10^{-4} = 3.016 \times 10^{-3} \, m^2
$$

Heat input rate:

$$
Q = V \times I = 15 \times 4.0 = 60 \, W
$$

Heat flux:

$$
q'' = \frac{Q}{A_s} = \frac{60}{3.016 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.989 \times 10^{4} \, W/m^2
$$

$$
\boxed{q'' \approx 19.89 \, kW/m^2}
$$

**(c) Boiling Heat Transfer Coefficient:**

$$
h_b = \frac{q''}{\Delta T_{sat}} = \frac{1.989 \times 10^{4}}{18}
$$

$$
\boxed{h_b \approx 1105 \, W/m^2K}
$$

**Interpretation:** This $h_b$ value falls in the nucleate boiling range for water (typically 2000 тАУ 50,000 $W/m^2K$), confirming the experiment is operating in nucleate boiling regime.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Table

| **Regime** | **$\Delta T_{sat}$ range** | **Heat Flux Behavior** | **Typical $h_b$ value** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Natural Convection | $0 - 4┬░C$ | Low, increases gently | $100 - 500 \, W/m^2K$ |
| Nucleate Boiling | $4 - 30┬░C$ | Sharp increase | $2000 - 50000 \, W/m^2K$ |
| Critical Heat Flux (CHF) | $\approx 30┬░C$ | Maximum peak | Variable |
| Transition Boiling | $30 - 120┬░C$ | Decreases rapidly | Drops to $500 - 1500$ |
| Stable Film Boiling | $> 120┬░C$ | Increases slowly | $200 - 800 \, W/m^2K$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Boiling Curve (Nukiyama Curve) тАФ Functional Flow Representation

The following Mermaid block represents the **regime transitions** in a typical Nukiyama boiling curve. It is rendered as a functional flow chart because Mermaid cannot produce physically continuous curves.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply ON] --> B[Low Voltage Applied]
    B --> C{Heat Flux less than CHF?}
    C -- Yes, low heat flux --> D[Natural Convection Regime]
    D --> E[Increase Voltage in Steps]
    E --> F[Bubble Nucleation Begins]
    F --> G[Nucleate Boiling Regime]
    G --> H[Heat Flux Increases Sharply]
    H --> I{Critical Heat Flux Reached?}
    I -- Not Yet --> G
    I -- Yes --> J[CHF Point Burnout]
    J --> K[Transition Boiling Regime]
    K --> L[Stable Film Boiling Regime]
    L --> M[Power Off Heater]
    M --> N[Data Acquisition Complete]

    style A fill:#4A90E2,color:#FFFFFF
    style D fill:#7ED321,color:#000000
    style G fill:#F5A623,color:#000000
    style J fill:#D0021B,color:#FFFFFF
    style K fill:#BD10E0,color:#FFFFFF
    style L fill:#9013FE,color:#FFFFFF
    style N fill:#50E3C2,color:#000000
```

### 4.2 Schematic of Pool Boiling Experimental Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWR[Variac AC Power Supply] --> V[V Voltmeter]
    V --> HTR[Horizontal Test Heater]
    A[Ammeter in Series] --> HTR
    HTR --> TC1[T1 Thermocouple Heater Surface]
    TC1 --> DAQ[Data Acquisition System]
    HTR --> TC2[T2 Thermocouple Liquid Pool]
    TC2 --> DAQ
    TC2 --> CND[Condenser Coil]
    CND --> RS[Reflux Reservoir]
    RS --> CND
    HTR --> POOL[Pyrex Glass Boiling Vessel]
    POOL --> LQ[Saturated Liquid Pool]
    LQ --> POOL
    DAQ --> PC[Computer / Plotter for Nukiyama Curve]

    style PWR fill:#4A90E2,color:#FFFFFF
    style HTR fill:#F5A623,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram тАФ Heat Flow Path in Boiling Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Electrical Energy Input V times I] --> CONV[Conduction Through Heater Wall]
    CONV --> SURF[Heat Reaches Heater Surface T sub s]
```

## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A тАФ Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define the term *Critical Heat Flux (CHF)* in pool boiling and explain its significance in the design of nuclear reactors.

**Model Answer:**
Critical Heat Flux (CHF) is defined as the maximum heat flux that a boiling surface can sustain before the transition from nucleate boiling to film boiling occurs. Beyond CHF, a stable vapour blanket forms on the surface, drastically reducing the heat transfer coefficient and causing a rapid rise in surface temperature. In nuclear reactors, CHF is the primary safety limit because exceeding it leads to fuel cladding failure тАФ a phenomenon known as Departure from Nucleate Boiling (DNB).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** What is the *Leidenfrost point* and how does it relate to film boiling?

**Model Answer:**
The Leidenfrost point is the surface temperature at which a stable continuous vapour film forms between a heated surface and a liquid. Below this temperature, the liquid contacts the surface directly (nucleate or transition boiling); above it, the vapour film acts as an insulator, dramatically reducing heat transfer. It marks the onset of *stable film boiling* and is critical in scenarios such as quenching of hot metal parts in industrial processes.

---

### 5.2 PART B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks)** тАФ *[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neatly labelled diagram, describe the experimental setup used to investigate pool boiling heat transfer. Explain the procedure to determine the boiling curve.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cylindrical copper heater of diameter $D = 10 \, mm$ and length $L = 100 \, mm$ is immersed in saturated water at 1 atm ($T_{sat} = 100┬░C$). The voltage and current readings at one steady state are $V = 20 \, V$ and $I = 5 \, A$. If the heater surface temperature is $T_s = 125┬░C$ and the heat loss to surroundings is 5% of the input power, calculate the boiling heat flux and the boiling heat transfer coefficient.

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The experimental setup (described schematically in SECTION 4.2) consists of:
- A **Pyrex glass vessel** containing the test liquid (saturated water).
- A **horizontal cylindrical test heater** (copper or platinum wire) immersed in the liquid, connected to a **Variac-regulated AC power supply**.
- **Voltmeter and Ammeter** to measure electrical input.
- **Thermocouples** (Type K or T) attached to the heater surface and within the liquid pool to record $T_s$ and $T_{sat}$.
- A **condenser coil** with reflux reservoir to maintain saturated boiling conditions by returning condensed vapour back to the vessel.
- A **data acquisition system** connected to a computer for plotting.

**Procedure:**
1. Fill the boiling vessel with distilled water and bring it to saturation by initial gentle heating.
2. Set the Variac to the lowest voltage and allow steady state (constant $T_s$ and $T_{sat}$).
3. Record $V$, $I$, $T_s$, and $T_{sat}$ at steady state.
4. Increment voltage in small steps (e.g., 0.5 V), wait for steady state, and record readings.
5. Continue until film boiling is clearly observed (large temperature jump).
6. Plot $q''$ vs $\Delta T_{sat}$ to obtain the Nukiyama curve.
7. Identify CHF, nucleate boiling region, and transition region.

**(b) Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 10 \times 10^{-3} \, m$, $\quad L = 100 \times 10^{-3} \, m$
- $V = 20 \, V$, $\quad I = 5 \, A$
- $T_{sat} = 100┬░C$, $\quad T_s = 125┬░C$
- Heat loss = 5% of input

**Step 1 тАФ Heater Surface Area:**

$$
A_s = \pi D L = \pi \times (0.010) \times (0.100) = 3.142 \times 10^{-3} \, m^2
$$

**Step 2 тАФ Electrical Power Input:**

$$
P_{input} = V \times I = 20 \times 5 = 100 \, W
$$

**Step 3 тАФ Effective Heat Used for Boiling (95% of input):**

$$
Q_{eff} = 0.95 \times 100 = 95 \, W
$$

**Step 4 тАФ Boiling Heat Flux:**

$$
q'' = \frac{Q_{eff}}{A_s} = \frac{95}{3.142 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.024 \times 10^{4} \, W/m^2
$$

$$
\boxed{q'' \approx 30.24 \, kW/m^2}
$$

**Step 5 тАФ Surface Superheat:**

$$
\Delta T_{sat} = T_s - T_{sat} = 125 - 100 = 25 \, ┬░C
$$

**Step 6 тАФ Boiling Heat Transfer Coefficient:**

$$
h_b = \frac{q''}{\Delta T_{sat}} = \frac{3.024 \times 10^{4}}{25} = 1209.6 \, W/m^2K
$$

$$
\boxed{h_b \approx 1210 \, W/m^2K}
$$

**Interpretation:** This $h_b$ value indicates the experiment is in the nucleate boiling regime (typical $h_b$ for water: 2000 тАУ 50,000 $W/m^2K$). The slightly lower value suggests it is at the lower end of nucleate boiling, possibly near the isolated bubble sub-region.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks)** тАФ *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive Zuber's correlation for the critical heat flux in pool boiling on a horizontal surface. List the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using Zuber's correlation, calculate the critical heat flux for water boiling at 1 atm. Given: $h_{fg} = 2257 \times 10^3 \, J/kg$, $\rho_v = 0.5956 \, kg/m^3$, $\rho_l = 957.9 \, kg/m^3$, $\sigma = 0.0589 \, N/m$, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (Refer Section 3.2 for detailed steps):**

Assumptions:
1. The liquid pool is stagnant (only natural convection).
2. The heated surface is horizontal and sufficiently large.
3. Vapour bubbles coalesce into jets at CHF.
4. The vapour-liquid interface behaves as a hydrodynamic instability.
5. Vapour properties are evaluated at saturation temperature.

Final Result:

$$
\boxed{q''_{CHF} = 0.131 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v^{1/2} \, \left[\sigma \, g \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)\right]^{1/4}}
$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 тАФ Substitute values:**

$$
q''_{CHF} = 0.131 \times (2257 \times 10^{3}) \times (0.5956)^{1/2} \times \left[(0.0589) \times (9.81) \times (957.9 - 0.5956)\right]^{1/4}
$$

**Step 2 тАФ Evaluate each term:**

$$
\rho_v^{1/2} = (0.5956)^{1/2} = 0.7718
$$

$$
\rho_l - \rho_v = 957.9 - 0.5956 = 957.30 \, kg/m^3
$$

$$
\sigma \cdot g \cdot (\rho_l - \rho_v) = 0.0589 \times 9.81 \times 957.30 = 553.21 \, N/m^3
$$

$$
[553.21]^{1/4} = (553.21)^{0.25} \approx 4.853
$$

**Step 3 тАФ Combine all terms:**

$$
q''_{CHF} = 0.131 \times 2257 \times 10^{3} \times 0.7718 \times 4.853
$$

$$
q''_{CHF} = 0.131 \times 2257000 \times 3.7457
$$

$$
q''_{CHF} \approx 0.131 \times 8.453 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
\boxed{q''_{CHF} \approx 1.107 \times 10^{6} \, W/m^2 \approx 1.11 \, MW/m^2}
$$

**Verification:** The standard experimental CHF for saturated water at 1 atm is approximately **1.0 тАУ 1.2 $MW/m^2$**, confirming that Zuber's correlation yields a reliable estimate.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch**: Forgetting to convert $mm$ to $m$ when calculating surface area тАФ this causes a 1000├Ч error in $h_b$.
> 2. **Ignoring heat losses**: Many students forget to subtract heat loss percentage from the electrical input; KTU examiner's key explicitly checks for the 0.95 multiplier.
> 3. **Mixing up $T_s$ and $T_{sat}$**: Surface superheat is $T_s - T_{sat}$, not the other way around.
> 4. **Forgetting the $0.131$ empirical constant in Zuber**: Writing $q''_{CHF} = h_{fg} \rho_v^{1/2} [\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)]^{1/2}$ instead of $^{1/4}$ with the 0.131 factor тАФ major deduction.
> 5. **Not labelling axes in the Nukiyama curve**: Plot must be $q''$ (y-axis) vs $\Delta T_{sat}$ (x-axis) with units clearly mentioned.
> 6. **Confusing sub-regimes of nucleate boiling**: Isolated bubble vs slug/vapour column тАФ examiners test this distinction.
> 7. **Skipping assumption statements in derivations**: Every derivation in Part B requires 3тАУ4 explicit assumptions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**
> - **Boiling** is a convective mode involving phase change at a heated surface.
> - **Pool boiling** occurs in stagnant liquid; **flow boiling** occurs with forced bulk motion (not covered in Module Exp 2).
> - The **Nukiyama curve** has five regimes: Natural Convection, Nucleate Boiling, CHF, Transition Boiling, Film Boiling.
> - **Nucleate boiling** is the most efficient regime тАФ used in all commercial boilers.
> - **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** marks the transition; exceeding it leads to burnout.
> - **Rohsenow correlation** predicts nucleate boiling heat flux (most tested).
> - **Zuber's correlation** with the 0.131 factor predicts CHF.
> - Heat transfer coefficient formula: $h_b = q'' / \Delta T_{sat}$.
> - Heat flux formula from electrical input: $q'' = (VI - Q_{loss}) / A_s$.
> - Bubble departure size given by **Fritz equation**.
> - **Latent heat of water at 100┬░C = 2257 kJ/kg** (must memorize).
> - **Surface-fluid constant $C_{sf}$ for water-copper тЙИ 0.0130** (commonly tested).
> - **Saturated boiling** vs **subcooled boiling** distinction is frequently asked in viva.
> - **Boiling incipience** begins at active cavities (microscopic pits) on the surface.
> - **Film boiling** heat transfer occurs via conduction through vapour + radiation.
> - **Leidenfrost point** is the minimum surface temperature for stable film boiling.
> - **DNB (Departure from Nucleate Boiling)** is the nuclear engineering equivalent of burnout.
> - **Two-phase cooling** is a modern application in electronics (CPU/GPU).
> - The boiling curve is **hysteretic**: Different paths on increasing vs decreasing heat flux тАФ examiners occasionally ask why.

---

**END OF NOTES тАФ PCMEL507 | Module Experiment 2 | Experiments on Boiling Heat Transfer**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance study on heat pipe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMEL507 - THERMAL ENGINEERING LAB-1 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Performance study on heat pipe # ЁЯУШ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Heat Pipe:** A heat pipe is a **passive, sealed, evacuated, two-phase heat transfer device** that transports large quantities of heat over relatively long distances with an exceptionally small temperature difference between its hot (evaporator) and cold (condenser) ends. It combines the principles of **thermal conductivity** and **phase change (latent heat)** to achieve an *effective thermal conductivity* several orders of magnitude higher than that of the best solid conductors like copper or silver. The heat pipe operates on a closed-loop **evaporationтАУcondensation cycle** driven by capillary action within an internal wick structure, requiring **no external power input** for fluid circulation. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy тАФ "The Super-Sponge for Heat" > [!NOTE] > Think of a heat pipe as a **"thermal super-sponge"**: > - Place one end of the sponge over a burning candle (the **evaporator**). > - The water inside the sponge at the hot end turns to steam. > - The steam naturally races to the cool end of the sponge. > - There, it condenses back into liquid (the **condenser**), releasing its hidden heat (latent heat) into the surroundings. > - The condensed liquid is then *automatically sucked back* to the hot end by the sponge's capillary pores (the **wick**), and the cycle repeats forever тАФ no pump, no battery, no moving parts! This is exactly how a heat pipe works. The "sponge" is the **wick**, the "water" is the **working fluid**, and the "steaming and condensing" is the **phase change cycle**. ## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board Highlights) - **Effective Thermal Conductivity (k_eff):** typically **5,000 тАУ 100,000 W/m┬╖K** (vs. copper тЙИ **400 W/m┬╖K**) - **Operating Temperature Range:** **тИТ200 ┬░C to +2000 ┬░C** depending on working fluid - **Heat Transport Capacity (Q):** ranges from a few watts to several kilowatts - **Thermal Resistance (R_th):** as low as **0.01 ┬░C/W** in well-designed heat pipes - **Isothermal Operating Mode:** nearly constant temperature along its length > [!TIP] > **Key Takeaway for KTU Viva:** A heat pipe is *NOT* a fin, *NOT* a heat exchanger, and *NOT* a thermosyphon in the classical sense тАФ it is a **hybrid** that uses **boiling** + **capillary pumping** + **condensation** in a single passive device. # ЁЯУШ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Heat Pipe A heat pipe consists of three functional zones: | Zone | Location | Function | |------|----------|----------| | **Evaporator Section** | Hot end (heat input) | Working fluid absorbs heat and **vaporizes** | | **Adiabatic (Transport) Section** | Middle | Vapour travels with negligible heat loss to the condenser | | **Condenser Section** | Cold end (heat rejection) | Vapour releases **latent heat** and condenses back to liquid | | **Wick Structure** | Lines the inner wall | Generates **capillary pressure** to pump liquid back | ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Principle 1. **Heat Input Stage** ЁЯФе External heat source (electrical heater in the lab) supplies energy to the evaporator. The working fluid absorbs this energy and its temperature rises until it reaches the **saturation temperature** corresponding to the internal pressure. 2. **Evaporation Stage** ЁЯТи Once saturation is reached, the liquid **boils** at nearly constant temperature. A large amount of heat (latent heat of vaporization, **h_fg**) is consumed in phase change. 3. **Vapour Transport Stage** ЁЯМмя╕П The high-pressure vapour rushes through the **hollow core** (no wick blockage) toward the cooler condenser end. Pressure drop is negligible due to very low vapour velocity. 4. **Condensation Stage** тЭДя╕П At the condenser, the vapour meets a cooler surface, releases its **latent heat (h_fg)**, and condenses into a thin liquid film that coats the wick. 5. **Capillary Return Stage** ЁЯТз The wick's capillary pores act like microscopic straws, generating a **capillary pressure (╬ФP_cap)** that draws the condensed liquid back to the evaporator. This completes the loop. 6. **Steady State** тЩ╛я╕П The cycle continues indefinitely as long as the capillary pressure is sufficient to overcome all pressure losses in the loop (viscous, gravitational, etc.). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains every formula you will need to solve KTU 2024 Scheme numerical problems on heat pipe performance. Memorize these! | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|-----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | **Heat Input (Electrical)** | $Q_{in} = V \cdot I$ | W | From voltmeter and ammeter | | 2 | **Heat Dissipated (Cooling Water)** | $Q_{out} = \dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$ | W | $\dot{m}_w$ = mass flow rate of cooling water | | 3 | **Heat Pipe Efficiency** | $\eta_{hp} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Should be > 80% for good heat pipe | | 4 | **Effective Thermal Conductivity** | $k_{eff} = \dfrac{Q \cdot L_{eff}}{A \cdot \Delta T}$ | W/m┬╖K | $L_{eff}$ = length between heater and cooler centers | | 5 | **Thermal Resistance** | $R_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{Q}$ | ┬░C/W | $\Delta T$ = T_evap тИТ T_cond | | 6 | **Capillary Pressure (LaplaceтАУYoung)** | $\Delta P_{cap} = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r_{eff}}$ | Pa | $\sigma$ = surface tension, $\theta$ = contact angle | | 7 | **Pressure Balance (operating limit)** | $\Delta P_{cap} \geq \Delta P_{liq} + \Delta P_{vap} + \Delta P_{grav}$ | Pa | All losses must be overcome | | 8 | **Wick Permeability (Darcy's Law)** | $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \dfrac{K \cdot A \cdot \Delta P_{w}}{\mu \cdot L}$ | kg/s | $K$ = permeability, $\mu$ = viscosity | | 9 | **Capillary Limit (Heat Transport)** | $Q_{cap,max} = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta \cdot \rho_l \cdot h_{fg} \cdot K \cdot A}{\mu_l \cdot L_{eff}}$ | W | Most common operating limit | | 10 | **Wick Thermal Resistance** | $R_{wick} = \dfrac{\ln(r_o/r_i)}{2 \pi k_{w} L_{wick}}$ | ┬░C/W | $r_o, r_i$ = outer/inner wick radii | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Spacecraft Thermal Control:** Satellite electronics are cooled by ammonia heat pipes (zero-gravity environment, where thermosiphons fail because gravity-driven return is absent). - **Laptop CPUs:** Copper heat pipes with sintered wicks transport heat from the processor to the cooling fins. - **Solar Thermal Collectors:** Heat pipes transfer collected solar energy to storage tanks without pump losses. - **Nuclear Reactor Cooling:** Sodium-potassium (NaK) heat pipes for high-temperature passive decay heat removal. - **Cryogenics:** Helium-charged heat pipes cool superconducting magnets in MRI machines. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Insight:** Because heat pipes are *passive*, they are inherently **fail-safe** тАФ this is why NASA uses them in critical spacecraft systems where any moving part would be a reliability risk. # ЁЯУШ SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 тАФ Capillary Pressure (LaplaceтАУYoung Equation) We begin with a cylindrical liquidтАУvapour interface inside a wick pore of effective radius $r_{eff}$. **Starting point:** A curved liquid surface under tension $\sigma$ produces a pressure jump given by the YoungтАУLaplace relation: $$\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2 \sigma}{r}$$ **Step 1:** For a perfectly wetted surface, the effective radius is reduced by the contact angle $\theta$: $$r_{eff} = \frac{r}{\cos\theta}$$ **Step 2:** Substituting $r_{eff}$ back: $$\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2 \sigma}{r / \cos\theta}$$ **Step 3:** Simplifying: $$\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r_{eff}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This capillary pressure is the **driving force** for liquid return. It must exceed the sum of all pressure losses in the loop for the heat pipe to operate properly. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 тАФ Effective Thermal Conductivity of a Heat Pipe **Given:** Heat $Q$ is transferred isothermally over an effective length $L_{eff}$ with a temperature difference $\Delta T = T_{evap} - T_{cond}$ across the heat pipe of cross-sectional area $A$. **Step 1:** Start from Fourier's law of heat conduction: $$Q = \frac{k_{eff} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{L_{eff}}$$ **Step 2:** Rearrange to solve for effective thermal conductivity: $$k_{eff} = \frac{Q \cdot L_{eff}}{A \cdot \Delta T}$$ **Step 3:** For a cylindrical heat pipe of outer diameter $D_o$ and wall thickness $t$: $$A = \frac{\pi}{4} (D_o - 2t)^2$$ **Step 4:** Final expression: $$k_{eff} = \frac{4 \cdot Q \cdot L_{eff}}{\pi (D_o - 2t)^2 \cdot (T_{evap} - T_{cond})}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is $k_{eff}$ so high?** Because the heat transport is governed by **latent heat** (h_fg), not sensible heat. For water, $h_{fg} \approx 2257$ kJ/kg, which is enormous compared to $C_p \cdot \Delta T$. The result is $k_{eff}$ values 10тАУ100├Ч that of solid copper. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 тАФ Capillary Heat Transport Limit **Step 1:** The capillary pump must overcome viscous losses in both the liquid and vapour phases. Using Darcy's law for laminar flow through the wick: $$\dot{m} = \frac{\rho_l K A}{\mu_l L_{eff}} \Delta P_{cap}$$ **Step 2:** The heat transported is related to the mass flow rate and latent heat: $$Q = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$$ **Step 3:** Eliminating $\dot{m}$: $$Q = \frac{\rho_l K A h_{fg}}{\mu_l L_{eff}} \cdot \Delta P_{cap}$$ **Step 4:** Substituting $\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r_{eff}}$: $$Q_{cap,max} = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta \cdot \rho_l \cdot h_{fg} \cdot K \cdot A}{\mu_l \cdot L_{eff} \cdot r_{eff}}$$ > [!TIP] > This is the **single most important design equation** in heat pipe analysis. If the actual heat load $Q > Q_{cap,max}$, the wick will dry out and the heat pipe will **fail catastrophically** (this is called *burnout*). --- ## 3.4 Solved Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A copper heat pipe has an effective length $L_{eff} = 0.30$ m, outer diameter $D_o = 12$ mm, wall thickness $t = 1$ mm. The evaporator temperature is 95 ┬░C and condenser temperature is 80 ┬░C. The electrical heater supplies $V = 12$ V, $I = 2.0$ A. Find (a) Heat input, (b) Effective thermal conductivity, (c) Thermal resistance. ### Part (a): Heat Input $$ Q_{in} = V \cdot I $$ $$ Q_{in} = 12 \cdot 2.0 = 24.0 \text{ W} $$ ### Part (b): Effective Thermal Conductivity **Step 1:** Cross-sectional area: $$ A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o - 2t)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.012 - 2 \times 0.001)^2 $$ $$ A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.010)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 10^{-4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 2:** Temperature difference: $$ \Delta T = T_{evap} - T_{cond} = 95 - 80 = 15 \text{ ┬░C} $$ **Step 3:** Apply the formula: $$ k_{eff} = \frac{Q \cdot L_{eff}}{A \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{24.0 \times 0.30}{7.854 \times 10^{-5} \times 15} $$ $$ k_{eff} = \frac{7.20}{1.178 \times 10^{-3}} = 6112 \text{ W/m┬╖K} $$ ### Part (c): Thermal Resistance $$ R_{th} = \frac{\Delta T}{Q} = \frac{15}{24.0} = 0.625 \text{ ┬░C/W} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result Validation:** $k_{eff} = 6112$ W/m┬╖K is approximately **15├Ч that of copper** (тЙИ 400 W/m┬╖K). This confirms the heat pipe is functioning as designed. If the student gets $k_{eff}$ less than 500 W/m┬╖K, it indicates a problem (e.g., wick dryout, air ingress, or non-condensable gases). --- ## 3.5 Heat Transfer Limits of a Heat Pipe (Analytical Matrix) | Limit | Cause | Typical Dominance | |-------|-------|-------------------| | **Capillary Limit** | Wick cannot pump enough liquid | Low-temperature, long heat pipes | | **Viscous Limit** | Vapour flow resistance too high at low pressure | Very low temperature operation | | **Sonic Limit** | Vapour velocity reaches Mach 1 | High heat flux, low temperature | | **Entrainment Limit** | Vapour shear strips liquid from wick | High vapour velocity, low surface tension | | **Boiling Limit** | Critical heat flux exceeded at evaporator | High heat flux applications | | **Burnout Limit** | Combination of dryout + overheating | Operating beyond all other limits | # ЁЯУШ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ##
