List any four advantages of a heat pipe over conventional heat transfer methods.** **Model Answer:** > A heat pipe is a passive, sealed, evacuated device that transports heat by the evaporation and condensation of an internal working fluid, using a capillary wick structure to return the condensate. It exhibits extremely high effective thermal conductivity. **Four advantages:** 1. Very high effective thermal conductivity (10тАУ1000├Ч copper). 2. No moving parts тЖТ high reliability and zero maintenance. 3. Passive operation тЖТ no external power required for fluid circulation. 4. Uniform temperature distribution along its length (isothermal operation). 5. Compact, lightweight, and capable of transporting heat over long distances with minimal temperature drop. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam тАУ Dec 2023]` **What is the role of a wick in a heat pipe? Name any three types of wick structures commonly used.** **Model Answer:** **Role of Wick:** The wick performs two essential functions: 1. Generates **capillary pressure** ($\Delta P_{cap} = 2\sigma \cos\theta / r_{eff}$) to pump the condensed liquid back to the evaporator. 2. Provides a **flow path** for the returning liquid against gravity and viscous resistance. **Three types of wick structures:** 1. **Grooved wick** (axial or circumferential grooves on inner wall) 2. **Sintered powder wick** (metal or ceramic particles fused together) 3. **Wire mesh wick** (fine woven metal screen) 4. **Artery wick** (separate liquid arteries along the pipe length) --- ## 5.2 Part B тАФ Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### ЁЯУЭ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam тАУ Jul 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat schematic diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a heat pipe. Discuss the function of each component. **(b) [7 Marks]** A copper heat pipe has the following specifications: - Effective length: $L_{eff} = 0.25$ m - Outer diameter: $D_o = 10$ mm, Wall thickness: $t = 1$ mm - Evaporator temperature: 100 ┬░C, Condenser temperature: 85 ┬░C - Heat input: $Q = 30$ W Calculate: (i) Effective thermal conductivity, (ii) Thermal resistance, (iii) Heat transport capacity per unit length. --- #### Model Solution for Question A ### Part (a) тАФ Construction and Working [7 Marks] **Construction:** A heat pipe consists of a sealed metallic container (usually copper, aluminum, or stainless steel) with three sections: 1. **Evaporator Section** тАФ wrapped with an electrical heater coil. Contains liquid working fluid (e.g., water, acetone). 2. **Adiabatic Section** тАФ the middle transport zone with minimal heat loss. 3. **Condenser Section** тАФ equipped with a cooling jacket (water-cooled fin in lab setups). The inner wall is lined with a **wick structure** (sintered powder, mesh, or grooves). The container is evacuated to a high vacuum (тЙИ $10^{-5}$ torr) and back-filled with a precise amount of working fluid. **Working Principle (4-step cycle):** 1. **Heat Absorption and Evaporation:** Heat from the source vaporizes the working fluid in the evaporator. The fluid absorbs its latent heat $h_{fg}$ at nearly constant temperature. 2. **Vapour Transport:** The low-pressure vapour flows rapidly to the condenser through the hollow central core (low pressure drop). 3. **Condensation:** At the cold end, the vapour condenses on the wick surface, releasing the latent heat to the cooling medium. 4. **Capillary Return:** The wick's capillary action pumps the condensate back to the evaporator, completing the loop. **Function of Each Component:** - **Container:** Provides vacuum-tight enclosure; often acts as part of the condenser surface. - **Working Fluid:** Carries heat via phase change; choice depends on temperature range. - **Wick:** Provides capillary pumping and liquid distribution. - **Charged Gas/Vacuum:** Sets the saturation temperature for the operating condition. ### Part (b) тАФ Numerical Solution [7 Marks] **Given:** $L_{eff} = 0.25$ m, $D_o = 0.010$ m, $t = 0.001$ m, $T_{evap} = 100$ ┬░C, $T_{cond} = 85$ ┬░C, $Q = 30$ W **Step 1 тАФ Compute cross-sectional area:** $$ A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o - 2t)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.010 - 0.002)^2 $$ $$ A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.008)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 6.4 \times 10^{-5} = 5.027 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 2 тАФ Temperature difference:** $$ \Delta T = 100 - 85 = 15 \text{ ┬░C} $$ **(i) Effective Thermal Conductivity:** $$ k_{eff} = \frac{Q \cdot L_{eff}}{A \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{30 \times 0.25}{5.027 \times 10^{-5} \times 15} $$ $$ k_{eff} = \frac{7.50}{7.54 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 9947 \text{ W/m┬╖K} $$ **(ii) Thermal Resistance:** $$ R_{th} = \frac{\Delta T}{Q} = \frac{15}{30} = 0.50 \text{ ┬░C/W} $$ **(iii) Heat Transport Capacity per Unit Length:** $$ \frac{Q}{L_{eff}} = \frac{30}{0.25} = 120 \text{ W/m} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Performance Verification:** $k_{eff} = 9947$ W/m┬╖K is approximately **25├Ч that of solid copper** (тЙИ 400 W/m┬╖K), confirming excellent heat pipe performance. --- ### ЁЯУЭ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam тАУ May 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** What are the different heat transfer limits of a heat pipe? Explain the capillary limit and burnout limit in detail with appropriate equations. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the experimental procedure to determine the effective thermal conductivity of a heat pipe in the laboratory. List the instruments used and the precautions to be observed. --- #### Model Solution for Question B ### Part (a) тАФ Heat Transfer Limits [7 Marks] A heat pipe fails when any one of several transport limits is exceeded: 1. **Capillary Limit** (most common): The wick's maximum capillary pressure must overcome the sum of all viscous and gravitational pressure drops: $$ \Delta P_{cap} \geq \Delta P_{liquid} + \Delta P_{vapour} + \Delta P_{gravity} $$ The maximum heat transport is given by: $$ Q_{cap,max} = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta \cdot \rho_l \cdot h_{fg} \cdot K \cdot A}{\mu_l \cdot L_{eff} \cdot r_{eff}} $$ If $Q > Q_{cap,max}$, the wick dries out, and the heat pipe fails. 2. **Viscous Limit:** Vapour pressure is too low at start-up; vapour cannot overcome viscous resistance. Important during cold start-up or in low-temperature cryogenic heat pipes. 3. **Sonic Limit:** Vapour velocity reaches Mach 1, choking the flow. Critical in low-temperature, high-heat-flux operations. 4. **Entrainment Limit:** Vapour shear stress overcomes the surface tension holding the liquid to the wick. Liquid droplets are torn off and entrained in the vapour, causing dryout. 5. **Boiling Limit (Critical Heat Flux):** The evaporator surface reaches critical heat flux; vapour bubbles coalesce into a continuous insulating film. 6. **Burnout Limit:** A combination of the above leads to catastrophic overheating of the evaporator surface. The wick completely dries out and the wall temperature spikes. ### Part (b) тАФ Experimental Procedure [7 Marks] **Apparatus Required:** - Heat pipe test rig (copper heat pipe with evaporator heater and water-cooled condenser) - Variac (variable AC transformer) for heater power control - Digital voltmeter and ammeter - 6 calibrated K-type thermocouples (T1тАУT6) with digital temperature indicator - Cooling water supply with rotameter (flow meter) and digital thermometers - Stopwatch and measuring scale **Procedure:** 1. **Setup Inspection:** Check all thermocouple connections, ensure cooling water lines are connected, and verify the variac is at zero output. 2. **Cooling Water Flow:** Start the cooling water pump and set a constant flow rate (e.g., 2 L/min) using the rotameter. Record inlet and outlet water temperatures ($T_{w,in}$, $T_{w,out}$). 3. **Initial Readings:** With heater OFF, record all six wall temperatures (T1тАУT6) as the baseline. 4. **Power ON:** Slowly increase the variac to apply a low voltage (e.g., 10 V) and let the system reach steady state (тЙИ 15тАУ20 minutes). 5. **Data Recording:** At steady state, record all six temperatures, voltage, current, water flow rate, and $T_{w,in}$, $T_{w,out}$. 6. **Increment Heat Load:** Increase the variac in steps (e.g., 12 V, 15 V, 18 V, 20 V). Allow 15 minutes for steady state at each step. Record all readings. 7. **Shutdown:** Reduce variac to zero, switch off heater, and continue cooling water flow until the pipe cools below 40 ┬░C. **Calculations:** - $Q_{in} = V \times I$ - $Q_{out} = \dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot (T_{w,out} - T_{w,in})$ - $k_{eff} = (Q \cdot L_{eff}) / [A \cdot (T_{evap} - T_{cond})]$ - $\eta_{hp} = (Q_{out}/Q_{in}) \times 100$ **Precautions:** 1. Always maintain a continuous flow of cooling water **before** switching on the heater тАФ otherwise the condenser will overheat. 2. Do not exceed the rated voltage of the heater coil (risk of burnout). 3. Ensure all thermocouples are firmly attached to the pipe surface using thermal paste and clamps. 4. Wait for true steady state (no temperature drift for 5 minutes) before recording data. 5. Do not touch the heat pipe surface during operation тАФ risk of severe burns. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert тАФ Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing cm with m in area and length calculations. Always convert to SI units first. > 2. **Forgetting $L_{eff}$ тЙа $L_{total}$:** Use the distance between heater coil center and cooler section center, not the full pipe length. > 3. **Ignoring Wall Thickness:** The area is the **vapour core area**, not the outer diameter. Use $A = \pi(D_o - 2t)^2/4$. > 4. **Not Comparing with Copper:** KTU often awards 1 mark for explicitly stating that $k_{eff}$ exceeds that of copper and quoting the ratio. > 5. **Confusing Capillary Limit with Burnout Limit:** Capillary limit is the *cause*; burnout is the *consequence*. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap тАФ Rapid Revision Checklist ЁЯУЛ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must Remember Before the Exam:** - тЬЕ Heat pipe = **passive + sealed + evacuated + two-phase** device. - тЬЕ Heat transport uses **latent heat (h_fg)**, not sensible heat тАФ this is why $k_{eff}$ is so high. - тЬЕ Three sections: **Evaporator, Adiabatic, Condenser**. - тЬЕ Working fluid selection: water (30тАУ200 ┬░C), ammonia (тИТ60 to +100 ┬░C), sodium (500тАУ1200 ┬░C), helium (cryogenic). - тЬЕ **Capillary pressure equation:** $\Delta P_{cap} = 2\sigma \cos\theta / r_{eff}$ тАФ memorize it. - тЬЕ **Effective thermal conductivity:** $k_{eff} = (Q \cdot L_{eff}) / (A \cdot \Delta T)$. - тЬЕ **Thermal resistance:** $R_{th} = \Delta T / Q$. - тЬЕ **Heat pipe efficiency:** $\eta_{hp} = (Q_{out}/Q_{in}) \times 100 > 80\%$. - тЬЕ **Capillary limit** = most common operating limit; wick dryout is the failure mode. - тЬЕ **Heat input** in lab = $V \times I$ (electrical heater). - тЬЕ **Heat output** in lab = $\dot{m}_w \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T_w$ (cooling water method). - тЬЕ $k_{eff}$ of a working heat pipe should be **> 10├Ч that of copper** (i.e., > 4000 W/m┬╖K). - тЬЕ Heat pipes are used in **laptop CPUs, spacecraft, solar collectors, cryogenics, and nuclear reactors**. - тЬЕ Always record readings only at **steady state** (тЙИ 15 min after each setpoint change). - тЬЕ Cooling water must be **ON before** the heater is switched ON.
